channel.title: SCMP + Caixin Deep Reports

item.title: [SCMP] Why Singapore PM Wong moved swiftly on Faishal Ibrahim’s resignation
item.description: Singapore’s acting minister-in-charge of Muslim affairs Muhammad Faishal Ibrahim resigned about a month after the prime minister learned of his improper interactions with a woman, a move observers say shows Lawrence Wong’s swift handling of the first scandal under his watch to contain any political fallout. They told This Week in Asia that while there was no affair in Faishal’s case, it made sense for the party to deal with the consequences quickly in case of leaks as the woman was a member of t
item.content:encoded:
Singapore’s acting minister-in-charge of Muslim affairs Muhammad Faishal Ibrahim resigned about a month after the prime minister learned of his improper interactions with a woman, a move observers say shows Lawrence Wong’s swift handling of the first scandal under his watch to contain any political fallout.

They told This Week in Asia that while there was no affair in Faishal’s case, it made sense for the party to deal with the consequences quickly in case of leaks as the woman was a member of the public, not a party member whom the ruling People’s Action Party (PAP) could counsel or discipline.

Furthermore, Faishal’s position as acting minister-in-charge of Muslim affairs meant Wong had to be sensitive to the community’s reaction – and potential embarrassment – if the matter was not swiftly extinguished and a replacement sought for the post.

On Monday, Faishal resigned from the cabinet and parliament after inappropriate interactions he had with a woman that resulted in harassment accusations by both parties became known to the prime minister.

No criminal action was found but Faishal, 58, who was also senior minister of state for home affairs, reflected and accepted that his conduct fell short of the standards “expected of a political office holder and MP”, Wong said in a statement on Monday.

Wong revealed that most of their interactions took place through online messages and they also met on the sidelines of public events. Both parties later made allegations of harassment against each other, and the matter was referred to police.

Faishal, who is married with two children, wrote in his resignation letter from government and his party: “While there was no physical relationship between us, and I did not intend for the interactions to develop into one, there was a lapse of judgment on my part in the way I handled the interactions, and in failing to establish clearer boundaries at an earlier stage.”

Faishal’s case inevitably drew comparisons with another explosive revelation in July 2023, when former speaker of parliament Tan Chuan-Jin and MP Cheng Li Hui, both from the PAP, resigned from their positions after being in an extramarital affair.

Political observers noted the much shorter timeline in Wong’s handling of Faishal’s resignation compared with the affair between Tan and Cheng, which then prime minister Lee Hsien Loong found out about after the 2020 general election, at least two years before they stepped down in 2023.

Lee said in a press conference in July 2023 that as recently as February that year, he had counselled the lawmakers involved to end it. Tan offered his resignation then, but Lee said he told him to ensure his constituents were taken care of first and that the affair had to end.

Lee added then that he had “very recently” come across information strongly suggesting the pair’s relationship had continued.

Teo Kay Key, senior research fellow at the Institute of Policy Studies, noted that the full details of Faishal’s interactions were unknown, making comparisons difficult. However, the difference in timelines was “quite clear”, perhaps reflecting lessons learned from the public reaction to Tan’s affair, she said.

“Of course, given that the woman had sent details to the PMO [Prime Minister’s Office] and also that both parties had actually made harassment allegations, there is a possibility that the news would have gotten out from other channels and that it would have been more expedient to deal with the whole matter swiftly,” Teo said.

While the details of the relationship were scant and what actually happened was unclear, Teo added that this incident, taken together with the previous resignations, revealed the PAP’s expectations for how its MPs and political office holders should behave in their personal relationships and how this would affect their political careers.

Independent political analyst Felix Tan raised concern that the lack of details could lead to greater public speculation online.

He said Wong was not being stricter than his predecessor but wanted to stem the damage early instead of letting it “evolve into something greater that he has to deal with in the future”.

“One possible question that may arise is whether Faishal’s decision to step down was intended to bring the matter to a close, particularly if there was concern that it could become public or be pursued further. While there is no evidence to support such a conclusion, it is the kind of question that may emerge in the absence of greater clarity,” Felix Tan said.

He also noted that Leader of the House Indranee Rajah had earlier this year stressed integrity and honesty regarding Workers’ Party chief Pritam Singh’s conviction for lying to a parliamentary committee, and this was a reflection of how the PAP believed parliamentarians should conduct themselves.

Former nominated MP (NMP) Calvin Cheng wrote on social media that Faishal should not resign since he did not have an affair. “One has to balance losing a good minister/MP with small failings that all human beings have,” Cheng said.

Walid Jumblatt Abdullah, an associate professor of public policy at Nanyang Technological University, said on social media that given what was known about the interactions, he did not believe Faishal had to resign and that impossible standards were being set that would deter young people from politics.

“The criteria I would use is: is Singapore better off today than yesterday? I think the answer is no. He was a good minister for the country,” Walid said.

Yet Zulkifli Baharudin, a former NMP, told This Week in Asia that the office of Minister-in-charge of Muslim Affairs had to be held to a higher standard.

“The institution of the Muslim minister is a very peculiar one,” he said. “It’s one that’s based on preserving the dignity and the position of Malay Muslims in Singapore and Lawrence Wong must treat the institution of Muslim minister very sensitively so even a minor indiscretion will not be tolerated.”

While some might not consider Faishal’s actions a big or serious deal, Zulkifli noted that the prime minister was taking an uncompromising and high standard expected of the minister-in-charge of Muslim affairs, which would strengthen the institution in the long run.

“It is a tragedy and the community is embarrassed and upset. But one, this is human failure and two, at the end of the day, this will strengthen the institution. This is not just another ministry, it reflects the standing and position of Malay Muslims in Singapore,” Zulkifli said.

Faishal, who has a PhD in management science, entered politics in 2006 in the then Marine Parade group representation constituency following an uncontested walkover.

He was appointed minister of state for national development and home affairs in 2020 and replaced Masagos Zulkifli as acting minister-in-charge of Muslim affairs following the 2025 election, when he was also co-opted into the central executive committee of the ruling PAP.

item.title: [SCMP] Can Europe win a trade war with China? It may depend on how victory is defined
item.description: As trade tensions with China reach boiling point, EU officials are ramping up engagement with Beijing in search of an off-ramp. Quietly behind the scenes, though, Europe is preparing for a trade war. In Brussels, a steely determination has set in to ensure the bloc’s manufacturing heartlands are not deindustrialised by the “China Shock 2.0”. With some industries already haemorrhaging jobs, the EU’s resilience-building and de-risking plans are now laced with deterrents as Brussels looks to acquir
item.content:encoded:
As trade tensions with China reach boiling point, EU officials are ramping up engagement with Beijing in search of an off-ramp.

Quietly behind the scenes, though, Europe is preparing for a trade war. In Brussels, a steely determination has set in to ensure the bloc’s manufacturing heartlands are not deindustrialised by the “China Shock 2.0”.

With some industries already haemorrhaging jobs, the EU’s resilience-building and de-risking plans are now laced with deterrents as Brussels looks to acquire leverage and credible retaliatory tools that would allow it to respond if China uses trade coercively.

Some analysts say the sides are already in a trade war, others think the two sides are still preparing for the inevitable storm – and some say that a trade war should be avoided at all costs.

But a question that is increasingly being asked is: could Europe actually win?

Few in Brussels share the hubris of US President Donald Trump, who in March 2018 said “trade wars are good, and easy to win”.

During a parliamentary hearing last week, the EU’s top trade enforcer, Denis Redonnet, said the purpose of engaging with Beijing was “to avoid sleepwalking at two levels”.

“One, not to sleepwalk into excessive dependencies any more but two, not sleepwalking into an unnecessary trade war with China. It’s going to be a combination of … navigating between these two extremes,” Redonnet said.

Beijing has spent the past eight years building an arsenal of weapons that one senior EU official described recently as “formidable”.

Yet one view is that Europe, if it wishes to hold on to its industry, has little choice but to get into the trenches.

“Something we really try to emphasise in the book is that Europe’s already in the trade war, and I think that’s what the public doesn’t understand,” said Chad Bown, from the Peterson Institute for International Economics and co-author with the journalist Soumaya Keynes of How to Win a Trade War, a book that will accompany many EU officials to the beach this summer.

He said it was not simply a question of “do we do tariffs or not?”, adding: “China is already doing all of these things and Europe has to respond. So then the question is, what’s the combination of policies?”

Plans are accelerating to exclude Chinese companies from procurement markets, to force investors in the electric vehicle supply chain to transfer technology and to compel European companies to diversify their supplier base to avoid over-concentration on Beijing.

Narrow product-based investigations into alleged Chinese dumping and subsidies are also gathering pace, and there are moves afoot to broaden those to subsectors using “safeguard” probes.

These would allow for emergency tariffs and quotas to be imposed on product groups, but would also target the whole world, rather than just Beijing.

For months, officials have been searching for chokepoints where China is dependent on Europe in an effort to “assess where we have leverage … we need to start trying to place ourselves in a situation where we can react as well”, according to a second senior official.

Meanwhile, Brussels is preparing for the expected Chinese blowback. Work is under way to establish a solidarity mechanism for companies caught in the crossfire by Chinese retaliation – something EU officials are certain will come.

The bloc has also been expanding its web of trading partners as it looks for alternative sources of mineral supplies following a spate of Chinese export controls – ranging from rare earths and graphite to gallium and germanium – but progress has been slow on stockpiling and building an indigenous supply chain in Europe.

Whether that is enough is debatable. But before assessing Europe’s chances, there is a more fundamental question about what victory would actually look like.

Nadine Godehardt, a specialist in China and geopolitics at the German Institute for International and Security Affairs, said “to some extent, we are in a trade war because everyone is positioning … both sides are more or less preparing”.

For Godehardt, victory would not be defeating China but surviving a decade of strategic competition while preserving enough of Europe’s industrial base, and ultimately its democratic model, to remain a viable geopolitical actor.

“The trade war is really not about winning in the sense that you can really gain something out of it. It’s a very long-term situation – more like the Hundred Years’ War … there’s lots of friction, and you have to be prepared for retaliation,” she said.

Virtually nobody believes the EU can win in an escalatory tit-for-tat conflict with Beijing. When Trump ramped up US tariffs on Chinese goods to 150 per cent last year, China forced an embarrassing climbdown by putting export controls on rare earth minerals and magnets. For Europe, this was a cautionary tale.

China knows Europe is a paper tiger … it won’t win a trade war

John Clarke , a former lead EU trade negotiator

“Realistically, will there be sort of a big bang type of assault? I’m not sure. The threat is so intense from China now, and they have made this so abundantly clear,” said Tobias Gehrke, a specialist in geoeconomics at the European Council on Foreign Relations (ECFR), pointing to industrial supply chain security and outbound investment rules adopted by Beijing in recent months.

Instead, Europe’s best chance of survival may be through the plethora of smaller actions – described in an ECFR paper as a “distributed swarm” – each of which would bolster resilience or reduce dependence at a lower risk of a response such as cutting off rare earth supplies completely.

“This works like a military swarm … it is far harder for Beijing to swat away 50 small, dispersed measures at once than one large and threatening one,” wrote the think tank’s Asia chief Andrew Small.

Many of the elements of this “swarm” – from trade cases and supply chain mapping to diversification and leveraging the giant EU market – are already happening, with varying degrees of speed and success. There is a feeling, in Brussels at least, that Europe is more likely to succeed with this strategy.

“We don’t have this one tool that rules them all, but should unleash across the board all the measures, whether it’s cybersecurity, strict market access, public procurement or foreign subsidy regulation … all of them taken together can amount to something meaningful,” Gehrke said.

But the other big question is whether Europe has the stomach for a fight.

It is indisputable that the European Commission is getting its ducks in a row, but also hard to argue that the 27 member states have the requisite pain threshold.

Divisions among capitals have proven to be the EU’s Achilles’ heel in skirmishes against the US and China in recent years and as trade becomes increasingly securitised, national governments have more of a say than before.

“The competence of trade policy is moving to the member states … they are famously divided when it comes to how to deal with China,” said John Clarke, a former lead EU trade negotiator with a long history of dealing with Beijing.

“China knows that. They exploit it. They divide and rule.”

For this reason, perhaps, the EU’s most potent trade weapon, its anti-coercion instrument, has lain unused despite several high-profile attempts at economic bullying from both superpowers.

“I don’t think there’ll be a consensus inside the EU to have a direct fight with China. China knows Europe is a paper tiger … it won’t win a trade war,” Clarke said.

If Clarke is right, Europe is unlikely to emerge from a prolonged trade war with China as an outright victor – but it is unclear whether that should be the goal.

As Bown and Keynes argue in their book “a winner might be the one who is best able to preserve their economic model”, or simply “the team that loses less than a rival”.

By that measure, Europe may be content if it emerges from this most confrontational of eras with enough industry surviving to ensure political cohesion.

item.title: [SCMP] Hong Kong restaurants score extra HK$500 million thanks to World Cup
item.description: The World Cup brought in an extra HK$500 million (US$63.8 million) for Hong Kong’s catering sector this year, an industry leader has said, after restaurants and shopping centres hosted overnight viewings of the final with dim sum, snacks and milk tea. Businesses were also urged on Monday to devise creative strategies and set competitive prices to maintain the momentum from the city’s World Cup fever. The final between Spain and Argentina drew crowds of football fans, including children and the e
item.content:encoded:
The World Cup brought in an extra HK$500 million (US$63.8 million) for Hong Kong’s catering sector this year, an industry leader has said, after restaurants and shopping centres hosted overnight viewings of the final with dim sum, snacks and milk tea.

Businesses were also urged on Monday to devise creative strategies and set competitive prices to maintain the momentum from the city’s World Cup fever.

The final between Spain and Argentina drew crowds of football fans, including children and the elderly, who flocked to shopping centres at 3am – packing every floor of Tuen Mun Town Plaza, Olympian City, MCP in Tseung Kwan O and Kai Tak Mall.

Simon Wong Ka-wo, president of the Hong Kong Federation of Restaurants and Related Trades, estimated that the catering industry had pocketed an additional HK$500 million between June 30 and Monday, representing an uptick of about 6 per cent from its initial estimate in July.

In Tuen Mun Town Plaza, cheers erupted as Lionel Messi and Lamine Yamal appeared on a 540-inch screen, building to a crescendo when Spain clinched victory 1-0 after extra time.

Stalls and restaurants sought to capitalise on the crowds, setting up booths nearby to offer bubble tea or delivering dim sum directly to the viewing area.

Supermarket chain ParknShop stayed open overnight, selling drinks and fruit, while some spectators ordered food deliveries to the venue.

Many vendors offered promotions such as “buy three, get one free” to attract customers. Taxis also lined up at the mall’s entrance, ready to pick up passengers after the match.

Nini Man, manager of Nap Tea’s Tuen Mun branch, said the World Cup pop-up stand had only started turning a profit in the last two matches after the spot opened for a trial run on July 3.

She said she was inspired by the strong turnout at the mall for the Brazil-Japan match earlier in the tournament. As more vendors joined, the screenings evolved into a plaza-wide event.

“It was worth continuing [during the World Cup],” she said.

Man added that the exposure had helped introduce the shop to nearby residents who had never visited before.

“We have become like old neighbourhood friends,” she said, referring to the rare opportunity to interact with customers face-to-face.

Terence Chan Tak-ming, general manager of the portfolio leasing department at Henderson Land, said MCP recorded double-digit growth in both foot traffic and sales turnover throughout the World Cup.

He noted that tenants offering sports, lifestyle retail goods, or food and drinks had performed particularly well, creating business opportunities outside traditional peak hours.

In addition to vendors offering snacks and drinks ranging from coffee to craft beer, MCP also arranged free shuttle buses to Central and Mong Kok to help fans travel directly to work on Monday morning after watching the final.

Catering sector leader Wong said that while the significant time difference between Hong Kong and North America had dampened the usual business boost over the match period, many fans still visited restaurants and shopping centres to watch the final.

Venues raking in extra business included traditional Chinese dim sum restaurants.

“Restaurants need new directions in operations to attract new clientele. For important matches in the future, now that large screens have been installed, restaurants can roll out different activities and discounts to attract customers,” Wong said.

Secretary for Culture, Sports and Tourism Rosanna Law Shuk-pui, who watched the final at Kai Tak Mall, said that even with mega-events, businesses need to think creatively about strategies to attract customers.

She highlighted the emerging trend of traditional dim sum restaurants live-streaming World Cup matches as a successful example.

“I used to think watching a match in a dim sum restaurant might seem out of place, but it is actually feasible,” Law said. “That is why everyone has to rack their brains and examine their own strengths to discover more business opportunities.”

She added that a proactive approach from these businesses would help boost the economy.

Economist Simon Lee Siu-po said he believed business among restaurants and retailers would return to baseline levels after the World Cup, given lukewarm nighttime spending sentiment.

He urged the catering industry to be transparent and creative to attract patrons, citing the example of Lin Heung Lau’s Sheung Wan branch.

The restaurant rolled out an HK$3,888 eight-person VIP package and a HK$368 set for singles for the final match, with tea and service charges included.

Lee said the operator addressed the two pain points of Chinese restaurants by setting out their prices clearly and catering to the needs of solo diners.

“Hong Kong has too many options. With a competitive price and quality food, patrons will be interested,” he said, adding that restaurants could offer smaller meal portions to attract those dining alone.

Among those drawn to the city’s shopping malls for the final match’s “vibrant” atmosphere was Tuen Mun resident Shannon Dias, 42, who came to support Spain.

“A lot of people are here for both teams. It feels like a stadium – it’s better to be here,” he said.

Although he had eaten before the match, Dias said services by vendors at the shopping centre were “pretty good”.

“They’re doing their best to set up shop, especially for an event like this,” he said. “It definitely helps boost the economy.”

Another fan, Kit Ching, attended with his family after seeing promotions on social media.

Despite the crowds, he said he enjoyed the atmosphere and spent about HK$50 on drinks and snacks from ParknShop before kick-off.

“It was good to have something to eat while watching,” he said.

But some Argentina supporters left disappointed after the final whistle.

McQueen Zeng, who watched the match with friends, said it was a “pity” Argentina could not defend their title.

“I applaud Spain, and I also applaud our Argentinian players because they kept making efforts and trying to score,” he said. “I’m grateful to them for that.”

Zeng added that he would continue to support the team and hoped to watch a future World Cup in person, noting that Argentina, Paraguay and Uruguay would co-host the opening three centenary matches for the 2030 Fifa tournament.

Additional reporting by Jess Ma

item.title: [SCMP] Malaysia aims for 600,000 South Korean arrivals in new travel drive
item.description: As Malaysia pushes to welcome 47 million international visitors under this year’s travel campaign, the country is setting its sights on bringing South Korean arrivals back above pre-pandemic levels by promoting authentic local experiences beyond its well-known beach resorts and city attractions. “South Korea is one of our priority markets,” Kamilia Hani Abdul Halim, director of Tourism Malaysia Seoul, said in an interview. Last year, about 558,000 South Koreans visited Malaysia, making it the co
item.content:encoded:
As Malaysia pushes to welcome 47 million international visitors under this year’s travel campaign, the country is setting its sights on bringing South Korean arrivals back above pre-pandemic levels by promoting authentic local experiences beyond its well-known beach resorts and city attractions.

“South Korea is one of our priority markets,” Kamilia Hani Abdul Halim, director of Tourism Malaysia Seoul, said in an interview.

Last year, about 558,000 South Koreans visited Malaysia, making it the country’s eighth-largest source market for international visitors. This year, Malaysia hopes to increase that figure to 600,000.

“Our arrivals from South Korea have yet to return to pre-pandemic levels,” Kamilia said. “Before Covid-19, we welcomed more than 600,000 South Korean visitors, so this is an important milestone for us.”

The tourism drive forms part of the government’s global Visit Malaysia 2026 campaign, which aims to attract 47 million international visitors despite growing uncertainties affecting the global travel industry.

At the heart of the campaign is the theme “Surreal Experiences” – reflecting a shift in travellers’ expectations following the pandemic.

“Nowadays, travellers are looking for authentic experiences,” the Malaysian tourism chief in South Korea said. “They don’t want crowded places any more. They want something new.”

To meet that demand, Malaysia has broadened its tourism offerings beyond traditional leisure travel, promoting nature, wellness, rural tourism, culture and heritage, gastronomy, sports and educational tourism.

The campaign also reflects changing travel patterns among South Koreans.

Kamilia said the way South Koreans travel had altered dramatically since the pandemic. South Korean tourists are becoming increasingly interested in exploring destinations at their own pace rather than following conventional itineraries like packaged tour programmes.

“In the past, many South Koreans came on package tours,” she said. “Now they prefer to explore on their own.”

Instead of relying on organised itineraries, travellers in the postpandemic era tend to book flights independently, navigate Malaysian cities using public transport and ride-hailing services and search online for unique local experiences.

“People who can speak English, or simply find information online, now arrange their own trips,” she said. “That’s why we are creating more themed packages and promoting activities that travellers may not have known about before.”

To reach these increasingly independent travellers, Tourism Malaysia has expanded its digital outreach through social media, South Korean influencers, bloggers and online platforms, while also partnering airlines including Malaysia Airlines, AirAsia, Batik Air and Jeju Air to promote direct routes to Kuala Lumpur and Kota Kinabalu.

New experiences

While golf holidays and resort holidays still remain popular among South Koreans, Tourism Malaysia is increasingly encouraging visitors to discover different sides of the country. “Now we’re promoting other experiences as well, such as educational tourism. Many South Korean families are sending their children to Malaysia to learn English.”

Besides widely known destinations among South Koreans such as Kuala Lumpur and the resort island of Langkawi, Tourism Malaysia is also highlighting lesser-known destinations – including Sarawak on the island of Borneo and the northern state of Perak.

Sarawak, famed for its ancient rainforests and diverse Indigenous communities, recently earned Unesco Global Geopark recognition for the Sarawak Delta Geopark. The destination could also become more accessible to Korean travellers should direct air links be established in the future.

“If you like nature, Sarawak is an ideal destination,” Kamilia said. “It is famous for its food as well, and we’re hoping to have direct flights from South Korea someday.”

Perak, meanwhile, offers a different appeal. “It is reachable by land from Kuala Lumpur, making it perfect for a day trip,” she said. “It’s a quieter destination, but it’s well known for its gastronomy and gives visitors a chance to experience another side of Malaysia.”

One area where Tourism Malaysia has also seen particularly strong momentum is gastronomy. South Korean interest in Malaysian cuisine has grown rapidly in recent years, helped by the opening of more Malaysian restaurants in Seoul and the increasing exposure of Malaysian dishes through Korean television travel programmes, according to Kamilia.

“We’ve noticed that many Koreans are becoming interested in Malaysian food,” she said. “When you watch South Korean TV programmes these days, many of them travel to Malaysia and introduce local dishes like roti canai and teh tarik.”

Food has therefore become one of Tourism Malaysia’s key promotional pillars at travel fairs and cultural festivals in Korea.

Durian diplomacy

One product receiving an especially warm response is Malaysia’s premium Musang King durian. “Many people think South Koreans don’t like durians because of the smell,” Kamilia said with a laugh. “But when we introduced Musang King here, we received a very good response.”

The agency has even begun promoting seasonal “durian tourism” packages that allow visitors to tour orchards during harvest season and sample freshly picked fruit. “You can find durian in chocolates, cakes, cookies and even McDonald’s serves a durian sundae during the season in Malaysia.”

Kamilia’s own connection to South Korea stretches back more than 15 years. Before joining Tourism Malaysia’s overseas network, she studied Korean language for a year at Seoul National University’s language institute.

Returning to Seoul as the head of Tourism Malaysia has allowed her to witness how much both countries’ understanding of one another has evolved.

“When I first studied here in 2010, many South Koreans didn’t really know Malaysia. People often asked whether I was from Indonesia,” she recalled. “Now it’s completely different. Many Koreans tell me they’ve visited Malaysia. They know nasi lemak, roti canai and our multicultural society. That has been a very pleasant surprise.”

She believes that background has also helped her better understand South Korean travellers and tailor Malaysia’s marketing strategy accordingly.

“It helps because I understand Korean culture and what Korean travellers are looking for,” she said. “If they have feedback or complaints, I can understand where they’re coming from and relay that to our headquarters.”

Tourism Malaysia plans to hold a Malaysia Fair at Dongdaemun Design Plaza in Seoul this November, featuring cultural performances and promotional events showcasing the country’s diverse heritage.

“We hope more South Koreans will come and discover that Malaysia offers much more than they may already know,” she said. “There are always new experiences waiting for them.”

This article was first published by The Korea Times

item.title: [SCMP] Bridging the divide: how AI scientists unite despite growing geopolitical rifts
item.description: For policymakers in Beijing and Washington, artificial intelligence (AI) has become a high-stakes arena in their strategic rivalry, closely tied to national interests and security. From export controls on high-end semiconductors to heavy scrutiny of foreign investment, the future of AI would appear to represent a stark binary choice: one country’s technological leap is seen as a direct threat to the other. Yet, far from the halls of power, some developers and scientists contend that the true pot
item.content:encoded:
For policymakers in Beijing and Washington, artificial intelligence (AI) has become a high-stakes arena in their strategic rivalry, closely tied to national interests and security.

From export controls on high-end semiconductors to heavy scrutiny of foreign investment, the future of AI would appear to represent a stark binary choice: one country’s technological leap is seen as a direct threat to the other.

Yet, far from the halls of power, some developers and scientists contend that the true potential of AI is in expanding global capabilities.

This view united a diverse group of AI innovators and entrepreneurs who gathered in Hong Kong this month to explore how global collaboration and knowledge sharing could forge an entrepreneurial ecosystem that transcends deepening geopolitical rifts.

For Pak-Sun Ting, co-founder and CEO of Votee AI – developer of the first open-source Cantonese large language model (LLM) – scientific progress and collaboration in AI are like a “small undercurrent to help go against geopolitics”.

“Geopolitical winds change from time to time,” Ting said. “Government to government, there could be some disagreements. But from civilian to civilian, technology to technology, as long as they allow us and it is doable, we will take it and work with it.”

Votee AI has built partnerships with universities in Hong Kong and the United States, and with Chinese tech giants. It has also set up an AI research lab in Toronto, as well as regional hubs in Kuala Lumpur and Ho Chi Minh City.

The company is on a mission to bring AI to underserved languages and replicate its foundry model to preserve languages in Southeast Asia, Africa and elsewhere.

“These collaborations validate the fact that geopolitics – if you take a big enough ruler, that is not going to matter much,” Ting said.

The former banking executive’s pragmatism is echoed by Votee AI’s technology adviser, John Whaley, who also serves as an adjunct lecturer in computer science at Stanford University and runs an accelerator for AI founders named Inception Studio.

Whaley said the commercial and productivity incentives for AI adoption were so immense that they pulled the global industry towards integration. “The size of the opportunity dwarfs even the governments and geopolitics. The amount of productivity boost and positive impact is much bigger than any individual country.”

“This is why everyone is trying to vie for who is going to be the leader in the AI world,” Whaley added, highlighting the focus on sovereign AI and the strategic importance of access to AI, data, and graphics processing unit (GPU) and computing power.

Global AI growth should not be a zero-sum game, he said, calling the creation of new AI technologies an expansion of the overall “pie” of global economic growth and productivity.

“A world where people have access, knowledge and capability – that is the world that we want to live in. It is going to help so much. There are a lot of things that humans should not be doing manually any more.”

Whaley also highlighted changes in the AI regulation landscape, saying the US government had been shifting from an unregulated stance on the matter to a “new era” in which certain models were flagged as national security risks or of strategic importance.

When it comes to oversight, some leading AI developers in the US have suggested an even more cautious approach.

For instance, Dario Amodei, CEO of American AI research company Anthropic, wrote last month that AI’s exponential growth necessitated government oversight.

Amodei called for legal frameworks that would grant governments the authority to “block or deter deployment of the model if it is determined … to present unacceptable risks”.

“US export controls on frontier chips and semiconductor manufacturing equipment to China have been a major contributor to the US’ overall lead in AI, and these policies need to be expanded, tightened and coordinated with other like-minded states,” he added.

Meanwhile, as innovators around the world work on AI, Whaley described the future of AI as global and “not going to be necessarily just centred in Silicon Valley”, where he said there was “a hyper concentration of capital and talent”.

The next great AI company could very well emerge from Hong Kong or the Greater Bay Area, he added.

Whaley voiced an eagerness to bring the founder and builder energy to the region, as his accelerator organises intense retreats for select AI founders to workshop their business ideas and pitch to potential investors.

“Silicon Valley is Silicon Valley because it is a place where [failure] is very socially accepted. People happily, proudly tell you about all the things that they tried and failed. Culturally, it may be a little bit harder to do that here in Hong Kong.”

He said the focus should be on fostering an environment with freedom to experiment and build, rather than replicating the Silicon Valley model and overlooking the robust local university system and available capital.

Chinat Yu, an AI researcher and strategist from Hong Kong, is working to bridge that gap, having graduated from Stanford’s master’s programme in learning, design and technology in 2024.

By leveraging Hong Kong’s status as a regional “human capital magnet”, the city could synthesise local research with an entrepreneurial spirit that encourages STEM students to pursue moonshot innovations, Yu said.

“Too many people ask if it is the US or China [that will win the AI race],” said Yu, who helped organise this month’s Hong Kong AI Summit. “The right question is to ask how you do it [with both] the US and China.”

“By building this bridge between the two bay areas and connecting those innovation hubs, we are able to hopefully transform the culture here such that this then becomes the future innovation hub globally.”

“A lot of companies from China that are thinking about going global are already naturally going through Hong Kong,” he added, pointing to Chinese AI start-ups like Zhipu and MiniMax going public and being listed in the city.

Yu said that by bringing in mentors who had been in the start-up trenches for decades, local founders and aspiring entrepreneurs could learn to define customer needs and problem-solve as innovators.

“We want to build local but dream global.”

And as AI collapses the barriers to building software, the pool of the individual creator who can take part in developing new technologies is broadening.

Quincy Larson, founder of online learning platform freeCodeCamp.org, said the focus for acquiring programming skills should not be rote syntax, but rather domain-specific expertise fused with technical literacy.

“I would encourage people to learn the fundamentals of programming, but you do not need to spend thousands of hours getting good at programming any more, unless you really want to focus on some area that is beyond what LLMs are capable of.”

Larson envisioned a future in which the next wave of builders would be industry experts – lawyers, accountants and managers of different fields.

“Everybody is going to need to become a technical product manager,” he said. “They need to understand the fundamentals. They also need to understand some domain that they have a lot of insight into.”

“They understand that world really well and now they can potentially build solutions within their existing organisation or create a company that builds those solutions.”

Larson said that as his platform provided children in California and western China access to the same learning resources for free, the future was “more about one person who holds all the moving parts in their head and shared memories in the computer”.

item.title: [SCMP] Why does the world distrust America? Just ask the Canadians
item.description: The world is giving up on America, according to Richard Wike, director of global attitudes research at Pew Research Centre. Writing in Foreign Affairs this month, he warns that this time, anti-Americanism is different. Often seen as holding the gold standard of public opinion surveys, Pew executives are usually circumspect in their public statements. But Wike is stating the obvious. In the past, anti-Americanism was often ideological. Today, it may be purely practical as the United States direct
item.content:encoded:
The world is giving up on America, according to Richard Wike, director of global attitudes research at Pew Research Centre. Writing in Foreign Affairs this month, he warns that this time, anti-Americanism is different.

Often seen as holding the gold standard of public opinion surveys, Pew executives are usually circumspect in their public statements. But Wike is stating the obvious. In the past, anti-Americanism was often ideological. Today, it may be purely practical as the United States directly threatens the security and interests of other countries, including its allies.

There is probably no closer ally of the US than Canada. But according to the Pew survey that Wike and his team conducted this summer, only 35 per cent of Canadians would describe the US as a reliable partner, compared with 83 per cent in 2022.

I am a Chinese-Canadian. Since this newspaper is from China, I usually write from a Chinese perspective. But this time, I will write purely as a Canadian.

In the past few days, my family and I have been confined at home in Toronto because the air quality has been rated the world’s worst. There are currently more than 950 wildfires burning in several Canadian provinces, with some of the worst in western Ontario. My inconvenience, though, is nothing compared to those whose homes have been destroyed while others have had to evacuate.

But in the country’s hour of need, Donald Trump and other leading US politicians have responded with the opposite.

The US president has threatened to hike import tariffs already levied on Canadian goods: “We are holding Canada responsible for the fact that they are not properly maintaining their Forests, and Brush therein, and the United States is being unnecessarily invaded by filthy, polluted, and unhealthy air.

“This is Willful Negligence, and becoming a yearly occurrence, costing the United States Billions of Dollars. [The] cost of this pollution must of necessity be added to the TARIFFS Canada is currently paying,” he wrote in a social media post.

Senator Bernie Moreno, the Ohio Republican, said he would table a sanctions bill against Canada this week for allowing smoke to drift across the border. I swear I am not making this up. “I’ll be introducing a bill … to sanction Canada and the responsible Canadian government officials for this atrocity,” he said in a statement.

Four Republican members of the US House of Representatives – Jack Bergman, John James, Lisa McClain and John Moolenaar – jointly wrote to Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney, “demanding immediate action from the Canadian government”.

“American lungs are paying the price for Canadian inaction, year after year,” the letter said. “Sovereignty comes with responsibility, and the responsibility to prevent a foreseeable disaster from crossing into another country’s airspace has not been met.”

Every Canadian understands the not-too-subtle reference to Canadian sovereignty after repeated threats by Trump to make their country the “51st state”.

Katie Miller – who is married to Stephen Miller, the White House deputy chief of staff – is more explicit. “This is why Canada should be the 51st state,” she wrote in a social media post.

Well, it’s not like the US never had wildfires with smoke drifting across the Canadian border.

Last year, James and Bergman wrote separately to Ottawa complaining that wildfire smoke was ruining the summer for Americans.

“We write jointly this time because a year has passed, the season has come around again, and nothing has changed except that our patience has run out,” their latest letter continued.

James, who is running for governor in Michigan, went even further on social media by posting an image saying, “Canada: No Excuses. Stop The Smoke.”

Affected Canadian provinces have been steadily training and hiring more specialised firefighters. In May, the federal government leased 10 new firefighting planes and helicopters to help provinces and territories prepare to fight wildfires. A new C$317 million (HK$1.77 billion) special budgetary allocation was set up to establish a nationwide “aerial firefighting surge capacity”. Since 2020, Canada has invested C$12 billion in forest sustainability and fire prevention.

If something like this was happening across the border in the US, Canadians would be rallying to help. During past uncontrolled wildfires in the US, Canadian firefighters, including specialised teams from British Columbia, routinely deployed to help out. Canadians often make generous donations of money and materials to Americans in need. At the very least, no Canadian public figure would ever blame the US for its troubles from natural disasters because those disasters also affect Canada.

Why? Because ordinary Canadians would not stand for such vindictive pettiness from their officials, and no one would elect such people to public office. Not so in today’s America under Donald Trump.

Mike Rogers, a Michigan politician running for a Senate seat, wrote on Facebook: “For the fourth summer in a row, Canada has made its wildfire smoke Michiganders’ problem.

“The implications are real – and hazardous for you and your family – yet zero accountability for the country that constantly pushes crippling environmental regulations. Canada has to get its act together – and as your senator, I’ll make sure of it.”

That’s part of his electioneering because blaming Canada clearly appeals to at least some American voters. Theirs is a very different attitude from Canadians regarding neighbourliness.

Canada has 369 million hectares of forest, accounting for close to 9 per cent of the world’s total forest. Much of it is not easily accessible. The world is warming. Across western Europe and much of North America, an unusually severe heat dome has caused havoc.

At least Canada is doing something about global warming while the US president and his Republican Party claim the worldwide phenomenon is a hoax and have been cancelling alternative energy projects across the United States.

According to the Pew survey, a 36-country median of only 35 per cent say the US contributes to peace and stability around the world. Only a median of 32 per cent think the US takes into account the interests of countries like theirs a great deal or a fair amount in its foreign policy decisions.

Canadians are a bellwether of global opinion about the US. And you know how we feel about America right now.

item.title: [SCMP] Fewer opportunities, less passion, no clue? How the US’ China expertise is fading
item.description: As the United States marks the 250th anniversary of its founding, it confronts a new world order dominated by its relationship with China. In this wide-ranging series, we examine the pressure points and possibilities in those ties, from hard tech to soft power. Here, Meredith Chen looks at how tensions and shrinking academic exchange may be eroding America’s deep, experience-based understanding of China. Long before China could be studied through satellite images, social media, big data or compu
item.content:encoded:
As the United States marks the 250th anniversary of its founding, it confronts a new world order dominated by its relationship with China. In this wide-ranging series, we examine the pressure points and possibilities in those ties, from hard tech to soft power. Here, Meredith Chen looks at how tensions and shrinking academic exchange may be eroding America’s deep, experience-based understanding of China.

Long before China could be studied through satellite images, social media, big data or computer modelling, American scholars learned about the country the slow way.

Many travelled to the country. Some went far beyond its major cities, reaching remote county towns and villages by train, bus and boat to conduct fieldwork.

They spent months, even years, knocking on doors, lingering over tea, combing through archives, and interviewing cadres and ordinary members of the public to build a deep understanding of a country undergoing extraordinary transformation.

This was the reform era. As China reopened and the two countries’ relationship normalised after prolonged Cold War estrangement, American scholars gained access to a society that had been closed to them for decades.

Many of those who entered China in those years would go on to become the backbone of American expertise on the country, shaping scholarship, training future generations and influencing how Washington dealt with Beijing.

As the United States marks its 250th anniversary, however, that intellectual tradition is under strain.

Observers caution that America’s ability to understand China is undergoing a generational rupture, as specialists with deep in-country experience are leaving the stage while the conditions that once nurtured such expertise are disappearing as ties become increasingly tense.

“America’s ability to understand and manage its most consequential strategic relationship is eroding,” according to a report released in March by the US-China Education Trust, a non-profit organisation.

It pointed to the dwindling number of Americans studying in China and warned that the US could face a “critical shortage of grounded China expertise within a decade”.

A fascinated generation

American expertise on China did not begin with reform and opening-up. Earlier generations, including heavyweights such as John King Fairbank, laid the foundations for modern China scholarship and influenced generations of scholars who later came to prominence.

But it was the cohort – political scientists, historians and anthropologists – that entered China in the 1980s and 1990s that gained unprecedented opportunities for sustained, on-the-ground study.

Many of them came of age during the Cold War and experienced the political and intellectual currents of the 1960s and 1970s, experts said.

Two movements were particularly influential in shaping their world view: the anti-Vietnam war movement and the Cultural Revolution.

For many of them, China was a subject of “both fascination and mystery”, and they developed a strong interest in the communist revolution and, “to varying degrees, felt sympathy for China’s socialist experiment”, said Guo Wu, a historian and former professor at Allegheny College in Pennsylvania.

According to Ding Xueliang, professor emeritus at the Hong Kong University of Science and Technology, when those American scholars began to conduct fieldwork in China after 1979, they confronted the realities of the Cultural Revolution and experienced a bitter process of “waking from a great dream” as they reassessed their beliefs.

This painful disillusionment was channelled into a sustained focus on ordinary Chinese society, including poorer and less-developed communities, he said in an article published online in May.

Cuts and restrictions

Ding is not optimistic about the future of China expertise in the US as the older generation of scholars gradually retires.

“Will there still be deeply knowledgeable China scholars after [this] generation leaves the stage? I certainly hope so, but the outlook is not encouraging,” he said.

Of the many factors behind this darkening outlook, analysts said, the most important was the rapid erosion of opportunities for Americans to conduct on-the-ground research in China as pressure increased from both sides.

The flow of American students into Chinese universities has sharply declined since the onset of the Covid pandemic in 2020 and the continued deterioration of US-China relations.

Today, fewer than 2,000 Americans are studying in China, down from roughly 15,000 a decade ago and still far below pre-pandemic levels, according to the US-China Education Trust.

In the US, growing political scrutiny and cuts to federal funding for China studies have limited research and exchange programmes, while China has tightened controls over visiting scholars and made visas harder to get.

While American scholars were once warmly welcomed and even treated as honoured guests, foreign researchers today are more likely to encounter suspicion about their intentions.

As Chinese society became more globally connected and confident, the West no longer possessed “the same aura of mystery or prestige that it once did”, which made the interpersonal and institutional dynamics that once facilitated scholarly engagement more complex, Wu noted.

For Dennis Wilder, a senior fellow for the Initiative for US-China Dialogue on Global Issues at Georgetown University, the loss of experiential knowledge that comes from living in China contributed to “a serious gap” in US understanding of US-China relations.

“It is not language skills that I worry about,” said Wilder, who formerly held senior roles related to China and East Asia at the US National Security Council and CIA. “But the ‘feel’ for new China and the societal ecosystem can only be learned with long-term immersion in China.”

Passing the torch

Beyond shrinking access, Wu said that US students studying China today increasingly viewed it through the lens of strategic competition and national security, a tendency he attributed in part to the tenor of mainstream discourse on China.

For scholars of previous generations, engagement with the country was often driven by “a personal and intellectual passion”, he explained.

The “deep sense of curiosity about China” had been ruptured, Wu said, along with “the aspiration to travel to China, immerse themselves in its society, and understand it on its own terms”.

Many younger scholars today approach China through well-established disciplinary frameworks, producing important scholarship but also contributing to a “more professionalised and specialised approach” to the field, he added.

“[A] more detached and ostensibly ‘rational’ assessment of China has also become increasingly influential among younger scholars and policy analysts,” he said.

Diao Daming, a professor at Renmin University of China’s School of International Studies, drew a similar contrast.

“The older generation … faced a China that needed to be continually understood,” he said. “While the younger generation confronts a China that is continually developing – a China they increasingly perceive as a so-called rival to the US.”

While the work of the older generation sought to explain China to the US and the wider world and generally favoured stable bilateral relations, he said many younger scholars approached China warily with an underlying orientation towards containing China’s rise and preserving America’s dominant position.

The earlier generation approached China through disciplines such as history, literature, sociology and anthropology, while many younger scholars approached China mainly through the lens of economics, political science and international relations, and often relied more heavily on data than long-term immersion, he added.

The current generation of American China experts “seems to have lost interest in Chinese culture and history”, and may not even possess good Chinese-language skills like their predecessors, Diao said.

Many had not spent enough time living in different parts of China and therefore lacked much of a “personal feel” for the country, he added.

For Ding, the issue was not only the passing of the torch to a new generation but also the marginalisation of older, established China scholars in Washington, which had left policy debates increasingly dominated by superficial assessments, he said in another article published in May.

Scott Kennedy, a senior adviser and trustee chair in Chinese business and economics at the Washington-based think tank Centre for Strategic and International Studies, however, said he had seen no evidence that younger Americans were broadly more critical of China than previous generations or less interested in studying it.

Rather, the changing environment surrounding China itself played a larger role in reshaping the field, argued Kennedy, who has travelled regularly to China for decades.

“China’s domestic politics are tighter. Its engagement with the world has more restrictions. This is less about Americans changing over the course of generations than changes in China that Americans and others are perceiving.”

Blocked pipeline

Neysun Mahboubi, who directs the Penn Project on the Future of US-China Relations at the University of Pennsylvania and regularly brings American students to China for study trips, said his biggest concern was not the current moment, but the pipeline crisis that could leave the country short of qualified specialists in the coming decades.

He also pushed back against framing the issue simply as a generational divide, arguing that a key challenge was increasingly one of material incentives. The erosion of career opportunities had made China studies a less attractive path for young Americans than it once was, he said.

These trends predated the pandemic, he noted, and even emerged before the first Trump administration.

Foreign firms retreating from China or increasingly hiring local talent were not a new phenomenon, Mahboubi explained, while “budget cuts in the federal government displacing many young China analysts is of a more recent vintage”.

“China is the most consequential bilateral relationship for the US right now, but some jobs that used to exist, whether in business or in government, don’t exist any more.”

He added that the picture remained dynamic and that there was “at least some cause for optimism” that US students’ interest in China might rebound.

“Maybe precisely because there’s such a clear sense now that China is the primary geopolitical relationship for the US to be concerned about, so certainly at some schools, especially elite American institutions, there seems to be an uptick in interest in China studies.”

The changing balance of expertise, some scholars said, was also visible from the Chinese side.

Wu added that Chinese studies on America had been greatly enhanced in the past few years, resulting in expanding research interest and output. He also pointed to the “demystification” of the US in contemporary China.

As more Chinese scholars, students and travellers gained first-hand experience of American society, the US had become less an object of “admiration, imagination, or ideological projection”, and more one of Chinese observation, analysis and evaluation, he said.

Chinese studies of the US had contributed to a more nuanced understanding of the strengths and weaknesses of the American political system, he added.

But Chinese scholars studying the US also faced growing challenges in conducting on-the-ground fieldwork, experts said.

Kennedy said he was worried that “we’re actually moving towards scholarly decoupling”.

“If the relationship between the two countries at the political level does not achieve some greater measure of genuine stability, then we will increasingly become strangers with each other,” he warned.

item.title: [SCMP] From Bruce Lee craze to regional hub: how China’s kung fu captured Ivory Coast
item.description: Meite Siaka wears many hats. By day, he is a senior tax official, a lecturer at the National School of Administration and deputy mayor of Kani in northern Ivory Coast. But outside government, he serves as the founding president of the Ivorian Federation of Chinese Martial Arts (FIAMC), a group he established in 2008. He also works as a master and instructor, running several martial arts clubs. Now he is a central figure in standardising the practice of kung fu, a Chinese martial art, and promoti
item.content:encoded:
Meite Siaka wears many hats. By day, he is a senior tax official, a lecturer at the National School of Administration and deputy mayor of Kani in northern Ivory Coast.

But outside government, he serves as the founding president of the Ivorian Federation of Chinese Martial Arts (FIAMC), a group he established in 2008. He also works as a master and instructor, running several martial arts clubs.

Now he is a central figure in standardising the practice of kung fu, a Chinese martial art, and promoting its growth.

Siaka, who is around 60, started his journey during Abidjan’s craze over the films of martial arts legend Bruce Lee.

During the 1970s, long before Ivory Coast and China established formal diplomatic ties in 1983, dubbed Hong Kong action films flooded local cinemas, fuelling a widespread fascination with kung fu and Lee’s films.

“Bruce Lee movies played a role, but it was not the only factor,” Siaka said in Abidjan recently. “Hong Kong films and local television exposure also contributed significantly to the popularity of martial arts.”

The films have offered a model of discipline and resistance that resonates with working-class youth in Abidjan, with ripples spreading across French-speaking West Africa.

The zeal even sparked the vibrant “Ziguehi” street movement, described by French anthropologist Alessandro Jedlowski as “one of the most influential in recent Ivorian history”.

It also shaped Nouchi, the country’s street language.

Alessandro Jedlowski, an associate professor in political sociology and African Studies at Bordeaux Institute of Political Science (Sciences Po Bordeaux), said that in crowded, stigmatised neighbourhood cinemas, kung fu films offered young spectators both escape and instruction.

They treated these spaces as a “social laboratory”, experimenting with the gestures and moral codes viewed on screen. “The films thus helped precipitate new historical experiences, shaping the ethos of groups like the Ziguehi and influencing the broader landscape of popular culture and street politics in [Ivory Coast],” Jedlowski said.

By the 1980s, the cinematic obsession of the previous decade gave way to a wave of formalisation. This grass-roots fascination turned serious as early Ivorian practitioners began travelling abroad, even to China’s famed Shaolin Monastery, for rigorous training.

Inspired by what they learned, these pioneers returned home determined to establish a structured practice, setting the stage for future leaders like Siaka.

“Everything started in the 1980s … with former masters,” Siaka said, giving examples such as Boni Yao Alfred, an Ivorian kung fu pioneer who had trained abroad and specialised in Nanquan (“Southern Fist”) martial arts.

In recent years, several Ivorians have travelled to China to advance their skills. Max Yollando, who went to university in Abidjan, spent six years training at the Shaolin Temple, where he took the name Yanming, and later became an instructor at the Shaolin Cultural Centre in Zambia.

His classmate, Jean-Philippe N’Dri – with help from a Confucius Institute in Abidjan – also trained at the temple before returning to the city to lead local Shaolin training camps.

“When these practitioners returned to Ivory Coast around 1985, they began organising and structuring martial arts practice,” Siaka said.

In the early years, Chinese martial arts was grouped under a multi-sport federation alongside judo and karate. The movement gained independent momentum by the mid-2000s through collaborations with visiting Shaolin masters from China.

That ultimately led to the formal creation of the FIAMC to standardise training and provide institutional oversight.

In the 2000s, renowned Chinese masters were invited to the country to ensure local practice met established technical standards, Siaka said.

Today, the sport has expanded across the country. There are now about 250 officially registered clubs organised into associations and regional leagues, with five leagues in Abidjan, the country’s most populous city, and 12 outside it.

Informal associations and clubs have also emerged, and the discipline continues to expand beyond officially registered structures, Siaka added.

Jedlowski said the shift from informal, film-inspired street practices to today’s institutionalised Wushu scene mirrored broader changes in Ivorian society.

During the 1970s–1990s, youths copied movements to integrate into gang dynamics. “The moral and aesthetic appeal of kung fu remains, but it is reframed within institutional pedagogies emphasising discipline, health and sporting excellence rather than street-level confrontation,” Jedlowski said.

He added that this “afterlife” of kung fu had evolved from improvised street cosmopolitanism into state-sanctioned Wushu, reflecting modern aspirations for global belonging and self-cultivation.

Ivory Coast has become a regional hub for the sport, evident in its hosting the eighth African Wushu Kung Fu Championships in 2023. The city aims to host the event again next year.

The federation has organised training trips to China, including to Shaolin, with support from the Chinese embassy in Abidjan.

“These exchanges helped develop practitioners locally,” Siaka said, adding that the federation has built a competitive record, winning some 115 medals in African contests – including 22 in recent years – across both technical disciplines and “sanda fighting”, also known as Chinese kickboxing.

Siaka described the federation’s relationship with the Chinese embassy as collaborative but uneven.

“There has been some support from the Chinese embassy in the past, and collaboration has existed,” he said.

“However, in recent years, the relationship has changed. We now approach them when we need assistance rather than receiving consistent or direct support.”

Siaka said funding was a persistent challenge.

“Financial support is a major need. We participate in international competitions, but we often have to borrow money due to a lack of sufficient funding. We need financial assistance.”

At the Abidjan Sports Activities Centre in the upscale neighbourhood of Cocody, Toure Ounar, an assistant martial arts trainer, said the sport’s minimal infrastructure requirements drove rapid growth but that the scene remained fragmented.

“There are many practitioners because it’s a discipline that doesn’t require much infrastructure.”

The sector struggles with cohesion owing to a lack of standardisation across schools, he said, adding that “each master has their own style. That’s what makes things complicated”.

Nevertheless, across Abidjan, in the neighbourhoods of Cocody and Abobo, as well as in the Ivorian capital Yamoussoukro, formal and informal clubs continue to crop up, often led by enthusiasts.

“Some are not affiliated … they gather young people and organise themselves,” Ounar said, underscoring the grass-roots energy and the need for stronger coordination in Ivory Coast’s growing kung fu community.

item.title: [SCMP] Why data may become China’s most durable advantage in the AI race
item.description: The global AI race is no longer just about chips or models. Increasingly, it is also about data – namely, the language resources that feed and train them. Beijing has identified that layer as a new strategic frontier, accelerating the buildout of a national ecosystem of Chinese-language data for artificial intelligence (AI). The push covers everything from foundational text corpora and high-quality training datasets to technical standards and governance frameworks. Language data has emerged as a
item.content:encoded:
The global AI race is no longer just about chips or models. Increasingly, it is also about data – namely, the language resources that feed and train them.

Beijing has identified that layer as a new strategic frontier, accelerating the buildout of a national ecosystem of Chinese-language data for artificial intelligence (AI). The push covers everything from foundational text corpora and high-quality training datasets to technical standards and governance frameworks.

Language data has emerged as a “more winnable” arena for China in the global AI race despite intensifying US restrictions on advanced chips and technologies, analysts say, with continuing challenges over data scarcity, quality, standards and governance.

The drive comes amid China’s growing conviction that language resources are no longer simply research assets but strategic infrastructure that underpins the country’s AI ambitions, digital governance and cultural influence.

The efforts have also become increasingly visible in recent months.

On July 5, the Communist Party newspaper Guangming Daily devoted an entire page to the topic, with three articles stressing its strategic significance and calling for faster and more coordinated progress.

The urgency stems from a stark imbalance in the world’s language resources available for AI training.

Large language models learn by ingesting vast amounts of data, and the availability, quality and diversity of language resources have become increasingly important in shaping AI performance.

English overwhelmingly dominates the data used to train most popular models in the world, while Chinese remains structurally under-represented.

According to a 2024 report by AliResearch, Alibaba’s research institute, English accounts for nearly 60 per cent of global web content, while Chinese makes up just about 1.3 per cent, even though it is spoken by more than a billion people.

Alibaba owns the South China Morning Post.

That imbalance gives today’s leading AI systems a “native tongue”, and those who do not speak it are being left behind in subtle and consequential ways, according to Wesley Wu-Yi Koo, an assistant professor of management and organisation at Johns Hopkins University.

Koo said past research had shown that some Chinese open-source models relied heavily on English-dominated web datasets, inheriting English-centric nuances and biases that “might not be suitable for Chinese usage contexts”.

His own research found that AI-generated content in Chinese and Arabic consistently scored lower than English across all quality dimensions, leading non-English speakers to produce “less actionable and less creative” output, with the gap most pronounced in technical tasks.

“A model’s native tongue is a deliberate, consequential allocation decision, and countries that don’t make that investment will accept structural quality deficits they may not fully see,” Koo said.

Chinese AI developers have already begun responding.

Chinese-language data made up more than 60 per cent of the training data used by most Chinese AI models, with some models reporting shares of up to 80 per cent, according to the National Data Administration in August last year.

Official guidelines announced in 2025 on strengthening the construction of “digital Chinese” called for promoting the digitalisation of Chinese-language resources and expanding Chinese-language data resources, with the aim of establishing a national corpus and strategic language resources information database by 2027.

By 2035, China hopes to have significantly expanded the presence of the Chinese language in global digital and generative AI scenarios.

Koo said Beijing now viewed data as “the domain where it can compensate for its chip disadvantage”.

As US export controls keep constraining China’s access to advanced semiconductors, language data had become “a more winnable territory”, supported by China’s vast domestic user base and the state-coordinated capacity to mobilise data across enterprises and institutions that few other countries could match, he added.

“I’d argue data is becoming more decisive than chips in some cases.”

As model architectures converge and open-weight models make advanced AI designs more widely accessible, high-quality language resources may become a more durable source of competitive advantage because the depth of a language’s technical literature and domain-specific corpora cannot be replicated easily, according to Koo.

Building high-quality language resources was far more than data collection, he added, saying it required decisions over access, processing, standards and governance.

In May, China’s official People’s Daily Online launched a media corpus service platform designed to help media organisations transform their content into AI-ready datasets through data processing, standardisation, and copyright and security management.

High-quality news data is an important component of language resources, as professionally produced journalism provides well-sourced and informative materials for AI systems.

According to analysts, while the English ecosystem benefits from decades of organically accumulated digital footprint – from scholarly literature and Wikipedia to programming forum posts – many high-value Chinese knowledge resources are still difficult to access or have yet to be systematically incorporated into AI-ready datasets.

Governance of language resources remains a major challenge, Tsinghua University professor Sun Maosong and assistant researcher Kong Cunliang wrote in Guangming Daily.

They said China still needed a more coherent governance framework to address issues including copyright and data sharing, fragmented technical standards, and privacy and security risks.

Technical standards – including character encoding, semantic annotation and multimodal data integration – had become increasingly important in global AI competition, wrote Rao Gaoqi, an associate professor at Beijing Language and Culture University, also in Guangming Daily.

Falling behind in foundational standards for Chinese-language resources would have direct implications for “national cultural security, data sovereignty and China’s ability to shape international standards”, Rao said.

“If these core standards are controlled by others, China risks losing ground in global digital governance and limiting the reach of Chinese culture in the digital space.”

Koo said governance mode was one of the main differences between Chinese- and English-language AI ecosystems, which faced “different trade-offs”.

Chinese-language resource development involved “ideological curation” and not just technical curation, he added, as Beijing required training data to adhere to “socialist core values”.

Meanwhile, governance in the English-language AI ecosystem was shaped more by constraints such as copyright litigation, transparency obligations and consent concerns.

“These operate through litigation and markets rather than state-directed content filtering, leading to a more pluralistic data landscape,” Koo said.

As AI increasingly becomes the interface through which people access knowledge, solve technical problems and make decisions, differences in language resources risk translating into differences in economic opportunity and technological capability, he warned.

Non-English speakers also bore a higher safety risk, Koo said, with research showing models produced more harmful content and followed instructions less faithfully in low-resource languages, yet major international AI safety frameworks scarcely addressed multilingual safety.

As many governments around the world launch sovereign AI initiatives to strengthen their domestic capabilities, a national model alone would not be enough, he added.

“AI is often touted as a technology that levels the playing field,” Koo said. “My research suggests that, cross-lingually, it may do the opposite unless there is deliberate, sustained investment in language-inclusive models and data.”

item.title: [SCMP] Indonesia’s Danantara must ‘outlast’ presidencies to prove worth: investment chief
item.description: Indonesia’s Danantara must outlive political administrations to prove its worth to investors and most importantly to the people, according to the sovereign wealth fund’s chief investment officer. Pandu Sjahrir said the fund was focused on the demutualisation of Jakarta’s stock market and to support the establishment of the first financial centre in Bali to boost investor confidence. “Investment is almost everything for us. Confidence is the name of the game, and we have to create long-term confi
item.content:encoded:
Indonesia’s Danantara must outlive political administrations to prove its worth to investors and most importantly to the people, according to the sovereign wealth fund’s chief investment officer.

Pandu Sjahrir said the fund was focused on the demutualisation of Jakarta’s stock market and to support the establishment of the first financial centre in Bali to boost investor confidence.

“Investment is almost everything for us. Confidence is the name of the game, and we have to create long-term confidence,” Pandu said at the Nusa Dua Forum in Bali on Friday, organised by the South China Morning Post with Danantara Indonesia as partner.

“So, for me personally, Danantara has to outlast presidencies, and for this to happen, we have to be very, very successful,” he said.

Success was the best insurance in guaranteeing continuity from one political administration to another and the core leadership of Danantara had to be disciplined in its focus to deliver high and sustainable returns on its mandate.

“For us, our focus is strictly on profit,” Pandu said at a panel discussion with Tengku Zafrul Aziz, chairman of the Malaysian Investment Development Authority.

Pandu said Danantara drew inspiration from other regional sovereign wealth funds, such as Malaysia’s Khazanah, by establishing rigorous underwriting standards.

“There’s so much downside protection and layering to ensure that at least we get a good return of capital.”

“We call it work-work balance in Danantara, seven days a week, honestly, because we have to make sure that the institution is stronger than the individual,” Pandu said later at a closing dialogue.

Since its inception last year, Indonesia’s second sovereign wealth fund has now pivoted from implementation to ensuring consistency, efficiency and stability, according to Pandu.

Hailing the importance of “quick wins”, he said the investment vehicle aimed to complete items on its agenda before the year ended. “Maybe six months. I don’t have one year … It’s like a dog year, one year is equal to six or seven years,” he said when asked about Danantara’s priorities for 2027.

Danantara has since January backed the government’s efforts to demutualise the stock market, which would turn the member-owned bourse into a share company owned by external investors. The fund described it as an important effort to strengthen governance, transparency and Indonesia’s equities in the long term.

“In terms of stability, because family capital lasts longer than any presidency, consistency is also very important … So you need to have quick wins,” Pandu said.

The fund will also support the launch of an international financial centre in an economic zone in Bali, expected to be formalised in parliament by this month.

To a room of sovereign fund operators, family office leaders and institutional investors, Pandu said: “You can use Bali as a gateway to invest in Indonesia.”

Danantara, which oversees more than 1,000 companies and a trillion US dollars in assets, is poised to be one of the main vehicles driving President Prabowo Subianto’s ambition to raise Indonesia’s economic growth from 5 per cent to 8 per cent by 2029.

Its launch sparked fears over transparency and accumulated risks by one overarching fund and concerns that politics would dilute its mandate to be profitable and maximise the country’s wealth and resources.

In an interview with This Week in Asia after the forum, Pandu said an audit report would be released in September, calling the process of putting it together arduous and a “massive exercise”.

While he acknowledged concerns around the fund, Pandu said Danantara’s successful bond sale and its admission into the International Forum of Sovereign Wealth Funds (IFSWF) have boosted confidence.

The fund’s investment unit raised US$1.5 billion in its debut US dollar bond sale, experiencing strong global demand with US$4.6 billion in orders. Danantara was granted admission to the IFSWF as an associate member this month. Pandu said Danantara hoped to achieve full membership status within a year.
