“The fact that we’re able to issue our bond sale very well, three times oversubscribed, and also in terms of IFSWF, because IFSWF’s main issue is all about governance and transparency … It’s kind of the standard agreed on by everyone,” he said.

“We will use the standard, but also will not release some information, because I think we also have to balance between transparency, openness, as well as national security.”

Against the backdrop of Danantara’s moves to deploy capital, co-invest and rationalise the country’s 1,000 state-owned enterprises down to 250, other parts of the Indonesian economy have not fared as well.

Its financial markets have been undergoing a severe rout as investors pull billions out of Jakarta’s assets, driven by concern over the conflict in the Middle East and the Prabowo administration’s policies.

Global index provider MSCI, which in January warned it would reclassify Indonesia from an emerging to a frontier market due to a lack of transparency over shareholder structures by May, has put off the decision until November.

Credit ratings agency S&P reaffirmed Jakarta’s sovereign credit rating last Monday, stating that it expected recent financial strains to be temporary and mitigated by higher commodity prices and spending cuts.

“I think all the intentions are excellent, it’s just sometimes, the execution and how it gets translated are different,” Pandu said of the country’s financial climate, adding that time was required for some policies to bear fruit.

“I think there’s been noise. One thing we need to do better is to be able to sell ourselves better, not just domestically but also outside,” he said, pointing to Friday’s forum as a launch pad.

“This year is not tough, it is just a bit illusory,” Pandu said, recalling that Indonesia had weathered economic crises before.

He added that the country’s tycoons rode out the storm, refused to leave and even “doubled down”.

“The leading businesspeople here … stayed because they believe in the country and they believe that, actually, we just have to work closer to the government to build Indonesia,” Pandu said.

item.title: [SCMP] Did the Ukraine war spark China’s quiet ban on most civilian drone flights in cities?
item.description: For years, aerial drones frequently buzzed above the Huangpu River in Shanghai, capturing breathtaking views of one of China’s most iconic central business districts and the lights of thousands of homes. But the spectacular aerial photos that once defined one of the world’s largest commercial drone markets have now been heavily restricted along this picturesque stretch of the river, reflecting a nationwide tightening of rules on civilian drone use. Security concerns magnified by Ukraine’s drone
item.content:encoded:
For years, aerial drones frequently buzzed above the Huangpu River in Shanghai, capturing breathtaking views of one of China’s most iconic central business districts and the lights of thousands of homes.

But the spectacular aerial photos that once defined one of the world’s largest commercial drone markets have now been heavily restricted along this picturesque stretch of the river, reflecting a nationwide tightening of rules on civilian drone use.

Security concerns magnified by Ukraine’s drone attacks behind Russian lines are believed to have prompted China to enact the strict new regulations.

The sweeping changes have disrupted casual drone use and increased compliance hurdles for industrial applications like power grid inspections, complicating the country’s growing low-altitude economy.

Interviews and research by the South China Morning Post have revealed that nearly 20 of China’s biggest cities have put in place a blanket ban on civilian drone flights in central areas, with some changes implemented without policy announcements.

Fu Qianshao, a military analyst and former PLA Air Force officer, said the repeated drone attacks against Moscow and other important cities during the Ukraine war had created a sense of urgency in China about security in low-altitude areas.

“As long as a basic drone has a two-way data link for control, using it to launch an attack is highly accessible, and the threshold is extremely low,” Fu said.

That threat was highlighted recently in Beijing.

On June 26, a light sport aircraft that took off from a Beijing suburb crashed into the 108-storey Citic Tower, the tallest skyscraper in the capital, killing the pilot and injuring 13 others.

Authorities said the incident was a case of “endangering public safety caused by personal reasons”. They have not explained how the aircraft flew from a suburb to one of the most sensitive and densely populated areas of Beijing.

Li Wei, director of the Centre for Counterterrorism Studies at the China Institutes of Contemporary International Relations, a government think tank in Beijing, said such incidents left authorities with almost no time to react.

“Once the civilian aircraft veered towards the city, its progress would have left air traffic control and air defence little time to react,” Li said.

Determining the pilot’s “intent” in the crash would be difficult, he added, particularly because the aircraft’s transponder had stopped transmitting.

Li said this left authorities with a critical dilemma: attempting to shoot down a rogue aircraft over a densely populated city like Beijing risked causing severe casualties on the ground and widespread panic.

Policy change

According to the Civil Aviation Administration of China (CAAC), a regulatory body, China had 3.287 million registered drones by the end of 2025, logging a total annual flight time of 45.3 million hours.

In 2025, the scale of China’s low-altitude economy exceeded 1.5 trillion yuan (US$221.5 billion).

But after years of rapid market expansion, policy winds shifted last year when local and central regulators issued multiple warnings about industry risks.

Xie Maosong, a senior research fellow at the National Institute of Strategic Studies at Tsinghua University, described the policy shift as a paradigm for China’s handling of new technologies.

“When China encounters a new technology, it will first allow it to develop freely for a period of time,” Xie said. “When the technology develops on a larger scale, it will begin to consider the issue of risk regulation and subsequently seek a balance between development and security.”

In January 2025, Shanghai released administrative regulations redefining flight areas for civilian drones. The rules designated many urban areas as restricted zones, meaning pilots must secure approval before flying, which is usually very difficult to obtain.

In May, Beijing went further, implementing a sweeping ban that prohibits the sale, rental and transport of drones and their core components into or within the capital city without public security approval.

Citing “low-altitude security” concerns, authorities have effectively blocked online deliveries to Beijing addresses, banned physical retail sales and mandated strict public security registration for existing devices.

At least 15 other major Chinese cities have tightened restrictions on drone use since then.

Drone policies have become more stringent at the national level as well.

Two new mandatory national standards for civilian drones officially took effect on May 1, following their initial release late last year. Under the new rules, all drones must be registered using the owner’s real name, and remain software-locked from flying until activated – and locked again if they are later deactivated.

Drones must also be equipped with an operational identification module that continuously broadcasts real-time data – including the drone’s identity, location and speed – directly to regulatory databases, from the moment they are switched on until they land.

Further, drones must be modified to comply with the new real-name activation and real-time data transmission rules within a strict transition period, or face being locked out of operation.

Also in May, CAAC established a dedicated department to coordinate low-altitude aviation safety and development, as well as build low-altitude flight service dispatch platforms and service stations.

Xie of Tsinghua said the new Low-Altitude Safety Department was “a clear signal that China is re-coordinating development and safety in the low-altitude economy and strengthening safety regulations”.

The decision reflected China’s perception that the development of its low-altitude economy and the drone industry driving it faced structural risks requiring stricter regulation, he added.

That perception may look familiar as Beijing has shored up national security on all fronts over the past 10 years.

Chinese President Xi Jinping first proposed the concept of “coordinating development and security” in 2014. As part of China’s holistic national security framework, the principle emphasises balancing national development with security needs, establishing risk baselines and strengthening resilience.

“Coordinating development and security” was also incorporated as one of six development principles under China’s latest five-year plan. Adopted at the National People’s Congress (NPC) in March, the 15th five-year plan outlines China’s economic and social development blueprint from 2026 to 2030.

According to the plan, China must “strengthen the management of low-altitude operations and advance the research, development and application of technologies and products for protecting against security risks posed by low-cost drones”.

Fears from Ukraine

The shift in drone policy came as asymmetric warfare kept escalating in the Ukraine war.

In June last year, under a highly coordinated covert operation code-named “Spiderweb”, Ukraine smuggled more than 100 strike drones deep into Russian territory by concealing them within cargo trucks.

Once positioned near key Russian airbases – some thousands of kilometres from the Ukrainian border – the drones were launched and remotely guided to their targets, to devastating effect.

The operation inflicted massive losses on Russia’s long-range aviation, destroying and damaging dozens of high-value reconnaissance aircraft and strategic bombers parked on tarmacs.

Moscow and its surrounding regions have also faced unprecedented waves of drone strikes, including last month, when Ukraine launched its largest-ever drone attack on the Russian capital.

The bombardment breached Moscow’s air defences, striking the critical Moscow Oil Refinery and igniting a massive blaze that severely disrupted the region’s fuel supply and grounded hundreds of flights.

Beijing has reason to be concerned, according to a Chinese radar expert who spoke on condition of anonymity due to the sensitivity of the matter.

“Drones can fly as low as just over 10 metres (about 33 feet) and can even fly close to the ground, while moving relatively slowly,” the expert said. “Under such flight conditions, it is very difficult for radar to distinguish threatening drones from background clutter.”

“The low-altitude environment in cities is even more complex, creating greater pressure on drone detection and defence.”

According to Fu, the former PLA Air Force officer, regulations are still inadequate.

“Security loopholes remain if someone modifies a drone or directly assembles a modified drone from components,” Fu said.

Beijing has stepped up its warnings for those who seek to fly drones without gaining approval.

In June last year, the NPC Standing Committee – China’s top legislative body – passed a heavily revised Public Security Administration Punishments Law.

It officially lists the illegal use of drones as a threat to public safety, with operators in serious cases subject to administrative detention. If the violations constitute a formal crime, offenders may face criminal prosecution.

And in December last year, China’s Ministry of Public Security launched a nationwide “clean skies” campaign aimed at combating the illegal use and modification of drones.

Since then, the ministry has published details of cases involving illegally modified drones on multiple occasions to warn the public against such activities.

Confusion

For the owners of the country’s estimated 3 million drones, the policy change has caused considerable confusion and shock.

At least 15 major cities, including Chengdu, Fuzhou, Guangzhou and Nanjing, have designated most of their urban areas as restricted zones. Approval is required for drone flights on the CAAC’s civil uncrewed aircraft operation management (UOM) platform.

The SCMP has learned that most of the changes to restricted flight zones were made between January and March, and many were not formally issued as administrative regulations.

The SCMP reached out to local authorities in Fuzhou, Guangzhou and Nanjing regarding the adjusted restricted flight zones but they had yet to respond by publication time.

John Liu, a Shanghai-based drone pilot with six years of aerial photography experience, said he spent the last six months shuttling between drone shops and public security bureaus just to modify and register his three devices.

The SCMP reached out to eight drone pilots from various cities who shared urban aerial photographs taken between 2021 and 2025.

Today, the exact locations where those photos and videos were shot have become restricted flight zones that require official approval.

Daniel Wei, a drone pilot from Fuzhou with seven years of experience in aerial photography, said: “In 2019, there were almost no restricted zones, and people could freely photograph urban landscapes from altitudes of 200 to 300 metres, although some restrictions were introduced later.”

In 2024 and 2025, Wei recalled, as long as a drone pilot was not flying in a sensitive military area or around a transport hub, take-offs were permitted on the same day a flight application was filed.

Under the new regulations, even within the now much smaller designated “blue zones”, small civilian drones must fly no higher than 120 metres above ground level unless they receive special approval.

“The 120-metre altitude limit means that at least half of the visual effects sought in aerial photography are no longer achievable,” Wei said.

“It is now very difficult to conduct urban aerial photography in Fuzhou. Almost all areas are restricted, and even forests and mountainous scenic areas near the city require approval.”

He added that following the policy changes, the approval process had become extremely opaque.

Individual drone pilots can submit applications on the UOM platform before noon for flights the following day, but the outcomes appear to be consistently disappointing, according to Wei.

“My approval rate is extremely low, no matter how detailed my flight plan is,” he said.

“Sometimes the reason given for rejecting an application is even, ‘Please fly in government-designated suitable flight areas.’ If so, what is the point of applying for permission to fly?”

“Sometimes no one even processes the applications, and after midnight the system automatically rejects my applications.”

Xu Ying, an assistant professor at the school of government of the Central University of Finance and Economics (CUFE), said the move might reflect deeper policy uncertainty.

“Against the backdrop of nationwide policy adjustments, making changes directly through the system instead of issuing administrative regulations may indicate that some local governments have not yet reached a formal decision or policy consensus on the use and development of civilian drones, and have therefore first tightened controls at the administrative level,” Xu said.

Backsliding

The regulatory crackdown has done more than just render a hobby obsolete. In some cases, it has set urban management back by years.

Drones have become deeply integrated into daily operations across China’s agricultural, manufacturing and service sectors. They play an essential role in a wide variety of tasks, including food delivery, crop monitoring, industrial inspections, pesticide spraying, aerial photography and light-show performances.

The use of drones to replace manual labour in infrastructure inspections has been a notable development in China’s power system since 2022. However, major compliance hurdles have now emerged under the stringent new civil aviation and real-name drone registration laws.

An inspector at the State Grid Shanghai Municipal Electric Power Company who spoke on condition of anonymity described inspection methods as temporarily going backwards, shifting from using drones back to relying on helicopters and manual checks.

“Drones can carry infrared thermal-imaging equipment and conduct automated and detailed inspections of transmission towers and power lines,” the inspector said. “They are much safer and more efficient than manual inspections.”

“But because of the policy changes, obtaining approval for drone use has become very difficult, so we cannot use them for the time being.”

“Approval for helicopters is even much easier to obtain than approval for drones, but helicopter take-offs and landings are more complicated than those of drones, making them less convenient to use,” the inspector added.

According to Xu, who is also a fellow at CUFE’s Low-Altitude Economy Research Centre, difficulties in applying for drone flight approvals highlight a lack of administrative capability in coordinating the airspace needs of different departments, companies and individuals within some local governments.

“The new zoning and approval methods adopted by some local governments have effectively raised the threshold for flying, and these changes have not gone through a relatively transparent process,” Xu said.

“Public participation in airspace planning remains insufficient, which may leave drone pilots and companies frustrated about the development and in their expectations of the low-altitude economy at this time.”

Xu said local governments should provide a clear development road map and establish more coordination mechanisms to give non-governmental participants involved in the low-altitude economy sufficient confidence.

Hope on the horizon?

Among the major Chinese cities the SCMP looked into, southern tech hub Shenzhen still appeared to broadly permit the flight of small civilian drones.

China’s largest drone manufacturer, DJI, is headquartered in Shenzhen, which is also the country’s most advanced city for low-altitude economy trials.

Shenzhen is estimated to produce – or export components for – about 70 per cent of the world’s consumer drones and roughly half of its industrial drones.

Observers suggested that these examples proved local governments had enough regulatory freedom to support the industry’s development.

“Cities like Shenzhen, which have a strong industrial foundation, need to maintain a balance between security and applications, preserving space for test flights, logistics deliveries and commercial scenarios,” said Zhang Weishi, an associate professor at Tianjin Normal University.

“Compared with Beijing, Shenzhen places greater weight on development,” she said.

Zhang noted that major cities typically mimicked each other’s stricter approaches in a typical example of policy convergence amid uncertainty.

Citing Shenzhen as an example, Zhang added: “Urban areas do not necessarily need to impose flight restrictions. The standard is whether additional security demands or key facilities are present in the surrounding areas.

“A more reasonable approach would be to implement dynamic, tiered management based on the area, time period and flight mission, rather than imposing a blanket policy.”

Xie of Tsinghua said policies at the local level could still change.

“Local governments also hope to retain some flexibility in decision-making through these temporary measures before the policy is fully settled,” he said. “They will continue to make adjustments as the measures are implemented.”

“In the long term, management may develop in a more refined direction.”

item.title: [SCMP] Negeri Sembilan snap poll threatens to chip away at Malaysian state’s tech hub goals
item.description: Negeri Sembilan’s bid to turn itself into Malaysia’s next hi-tech industrial hub is heading into an unusual political stress test, as a royal dispute spills into a snap election on August 1. The vote matters not because existing semiconductor and aerospace projects are expected to flee but because it could slow future commitments, reshape the state’s policy environment and sharpen wider uncertainty over the prospect of a snap national poll, analysts say. Long regarded as an affordable base for K
item.content:encoded:
Negeri Sembilan’s bid to turn itself into Malaysia’s next hi-tech industrial hub is heading into an unusual political stress test, as a royal dispute spills into a snap election on August 1.

The vote matters not because existing semiconductor and aerospace projects are expected to flee but because it could slow future commitments, reshape the state’s policy environment and sharpen wider uncertainty over the prospect of a snap national poll, analysts say.

Long regarded as an affordable base for Klang Valley commuters, Negeri Sembilan has recently sought to capitalise on its proximity to Kuala Lumpur, Selangor and major transport links to attract higher-value semiconductor and aerospace industries.

Last year, it recorded 19.1 billion ringgit (US$4.7 billion) in approved investments across 295 projects, almost three times the US$1.77 billion logged in 2024. The investments were expected to create more than 3,800 jobs, according to Chief Minister Aminuddin Harun.

While capital-intensive projects were unlikely to be derailed, given the cost and complexity of moving them, analysts warned political uncertainty could still test the momentum behind the state’s industrial push.

The turmoil began after four territorial chiefs, known as undang, claimed they had removed Tuanku Muhriz Tuanku Munawir as the state’s ruler over alleged misconduct and named his cousin and son of the late ruler, Tunku Nadzaruddin Tuanku Ja’afar, as the 12th monarch. Negeri Sembilan is the only royal state in Malaysia where the ruler is chosen by undangs through a customary process rather than by simple hereditary succession.

Both the federal and state government continue to recognise Tuanku Muhriz, but the dispute spilled into politics when all 14 state legislators from Barisan Nasional (BN) withdrew their support for Aminuddin, resulting in the snap election.

Unusual and confusing

Constitutional disputes could affect economies by causing businesses to postpone investments and other major decisions, said Geoffrey Williams, an economist and business consultant based in Malaysia.

“In Negeri Sembilan, the constitutional issue is quite unusual and confusing to investors,” he said.

While a state election would normally provide clarity, Williams said BN’s recent landslide victory in the southern state of Johor had added another layer of risk.

Pakatan Harapan (PH) – Prime Minister Anwar Ibrahim’s multiethnic coalition – won 17 of Negeri Sembilan’s 36 seats in 2023 and governed with BN’s support, but that pact has unravelled after PH received a drubbing in Johor, winning only eight of 56 seats while federal partner BN swept 48.

BN’s announcement that it would contest only 25 seats in the poll has also fuelled expectations of cooperation with Islamist party PAS and its ally, Parti Wawasan Negara, in the remaining constituencies. Such a partnership could concentrate the anti-PH vote, making it much harder for the coalition to hold on to the state, Williams said.

Businesses did not like change – something an election might bring about, he said. “A snap GE16 [general election] is the main risk to the Malaysian economy in the second half of 2026. For now, the Negeri Sembilan election has become the biggest challenge in economic terms.”

However, semiconductor projects were likely to proceed as planned, unless the political crisis caused a major change, said Ooi Tze Howe, a supply-chain executive.

“Semiconductor facilities require heavy capital expenditure, moving an expansion to another state would mean restarting land and utility approvals, and also [finding] proximity to existing plants and supply networks,” Ooi said. “As long as the situation is under control, it is very rare that any investment will be cancelled or put on hold.”

In the existing climate, companies were more likely to accelerate efforts to create manufacturing capacity, he said. “Nevertheless, land and water supply are still critical … for semiconductor plants, so the state governments’ responsiveness on land, water and infrastructure can still influence where companies place subsequent investments.”

When asked what investors would want to hear after the election, Ooi’s answer was simple: “The incoming state leader should say it is ‘business as usual’.”

Bigger issues

On the aerospace side, Shamsul Kamar Abu Samah, the chief executive of the National Aerospace Industry Corporation Malaysia, said his organisation had not received any reports of delays or hesitation as a result of Negeri Sembilan’s political situation.

Negeri Sembilan has been described as an emerging aerospace location given its proximity to more mature clusters in Selangor. Shamsul estimated there were about nine aerospace companies operating in the state – including Safran Landing Systems and the National Aerospace Fasteners Corporation, both of which are expanding.

Most investment, trade and industrial policies were administered at a federal level, allowing operations and approved projects to continue despite changes in political leadership, he said. “Current state policies are enough to support ongoing development activities.”

The industry was more worried about larger issues. “Industry concerns are focused on the conflicts in the Middle East and Ukraine, such as their effects on raw materials, logistics and fuel prices,” Shamsul said.

The Johor election had “woken up” Negeri Sembilan’s business community, said a local businessman.

“There was a lot of discontent because of federal policies like the fuel subsidy rationalisation, and e-invoicing, which meant a lot of people cannot manipulate their tax returns,” said Philip Liu, who is based in the state capital of Seremban. E-invoicing is a federal digital tax-reporting mandate for businesses above a certain revenue threshold.

But after BN’s landslide victory in Johor and the possibility of a state government formed with PAS in the mix, many local businesses were now moving towards supporting PH, Liu said.

“Even e-invoicing is something we know we would have to adopt eventually, and it comes from federal policy. But locally, the state government has actually been very business-friendly even for local small and medium-sized enterprises, such as involving local businesses in global giants’ service chains and providing financing and digitalisation support.”

If PAS got involved, businesses were worried they could become bogged down by increased state bureaucracy, he said. Paperwork has been streamlined since 2018, to improve business efficiency.

“Other friends in the hospitality industry are also worried about the introduction of more restrictive social policies,” Liu added.

item.title: [SCMP] Japan scapegoats foreigners as mountain rescue numbers rise
item.description: On a clear summer morning at Mount Fuji’s fifth-station trailhead, the crowds look more beach ready than alpine prepared with canvas slip-ons, bare legs and lightweight jackets. Above them, Japan’s most famous peak rises to 3,776 metres (12,389 feet) and into sub-zero temperatures, even in July. For Tatsuo Nanai, a veteran climber and until recently secretary general of the Fuji‑san Club, the scene has become a source of dread. Japan’s official climbing season has only just begun, but he says he
item.content:encoded:
On a clear summer morning at Mount Fuji’s fifth-station trailhead, the crowds look more beach ready than alpine prepared with canvas slip-ons, bare legs and lightweight jackets.

Above them, Japan’s most famous peak rises to 3,776 metres (12,389 feet) and into sub-zero temperatures, even in July.

For Tatsuo Nanai, a veteran climber and until recently secretary general of the Fuji‑san Club, the scene has become a source of dread.

Japan’s official climbing season has only just begun, but he says he is already “very worried” about the number of people who will need rescuing – and the lives that will be lost – before the trails close again in mid-September.

“Too many people underestimate Mount Fuji,” Nanai told This Week in Asia. “For anyone who takes it lightly, it can be very dangerous.”

Last year, Japan recorded a new high in mountain emergencies, with 3,623 people having to be rescued and 332 deaths, according to National Police Agency data.

The biggest problem is the tourists who are under‑equipped for the rigours of hiking at altitude, according to Nanai. They overestimate their own abilities and do not realise that it can be below freezing at the summit, even when it is a hot day in Tokyo.

On a clear day, Fuji is visible from the capital and its iconic status, appearing in guidebooks and Instagram feeds the world over, means many visitors treat it as just another must-see to tick off.

“I’ve seen people setting off from the fifth-station trailhead in canvas beach shoes and shorts,” Nanai said. “They don’t have a hat, rain gear or enough food … These are the people who typically end up needing to be helped off the mountain.”

Foreign tourists are easy to single out when things go wrong, but rescue statistics do not support the notion that foreigners are the main group needing help, regardless of what local media reports might say.

Less than 2 per cent of last year’s fatalities involved a foreign national – six deaths in total – with just 264 foreigners involved in accidents, emergencies, or roughly one in 14 of the incidents recorded.

Still, any helicopter evacuations of ill-equipped visitors that do happen are virtually guaranteed to make headlines, feeding into narratives about “misbehaving” tourists and overtourism.

In April, a Chinese university student became a lightning rod for online anger after being rescued from Mount Fuji twice in four days – first after losing his crampons and mobile phone and suffering altitude sickness, then after a fall at around 3,000 metres (9,840 feet) that left him needing to be carried down the mountain on a stretcher.

In May, a police helicopter was dispatched to pluck two British hikers from Mount Yotei in northern Hokkaido after they called an emergency hotline, saying they were too cold to continue. Television footage later showed the pair in shorts and thin jackets, fuelling accusations that they had treated a hike up an active volcano like a stroll in the park.

After a series of recent off‑season incidents, Hidetada Sudo, mayor of Fujinomiya – one of the main gateway towns to Mount Fuji that operates and funds local rescue teams – publicly called for a blanket ban on climbing the mountain outside the recognised summer window.

Sudo later backed away from demanding an outright ban, throwing his support instead behind calls for hikers to be forced either to pay the cost of emergency evacuations themselves or to prove they have insurance that will cover their rescue.

Nanai also supports such measures. “Having insurance, especially if someone has to use a helicopter, seems like a sensible precaution,” he said. “I do not think that people take hiking in Japan seriously enough.”

Naoki Kobayashi, an official with the prefectural government of Shizuoka that manages three of the four paths to the top of Mount Fuji, said the main cause of accidents on the mountain was “a lack of basic knowledge of the climb or a failure to properly prepare”.

“A failure to observe the rules and etiquette can also inconvenience others,” he said. “Even in summer, Mount Fuji has unique geographical conditions and even foreigners who have lived in Japan for a long time may find themselves in life-threatening situations if they lack the appropriate knowledge.”

Shizuoka’s prefectural government was working with neighbouring Yamanashi prefecture on a proposal to introduce mandatory insurance, Kobayashi said.

“But human life takes absolute priority,” he said. “If a system was introduced that requires individuals to bear the cost, there is a risk that a rescue would be delayed depending on whether payment could be paid or not.”

Seasoned mountaineers argue that access to better information on the risks involved would help prevent emergencies, especially among tourists.

Kentaro Hirokawa, vice-president of the Japan Mountaineering and Sport Climbing Association, said the growing number of foreign hikers on Fuji, and off-piste skiers in the winter months, were working from patchy, inconsistent guidance.

“Foreigners utilise information about Japanese mountains and backcountry areas in their respective languages, but a major problem is that there are significant differences in the information regarding required time, technical difficulty and the perceived risks,” Hirokawa said.

“This is not just a problem for beginners or foreigners,” he added. “We are seeing mountaineering accidents among middle-aged and older Japanese people. I do not think we can apply restrictions only to foreigners, but we do need to provide them with better information in multiple languages.”

Recent incidents in Nagano prefecture, home to some of Japan’s most famous peaks, show how quickly public sentiment can turn against foreign visitors.

On July 12, three Malaysian and Singaporean women had to be rescued from Mount Hakuba in Japan’s Northern Alps after becoming so fatigued at around 2,600 metres (8,500 feet) that they could no longer move, local broadcaster NBS reported.

Online commenters were quick to criticise, accusing the women of attempting climbs beyond their capabilities, lacking proper equipment and not treating the country’s mountains with the respect they deserve.

“It’s time to seriously consider implementing mandatory climbing plans, mandatory insurance and high entrance fees,” one wrote, blaming such incidents on “irresponsible behaviour”. Another post stated that “climbing high mountains in summer attire after coming from a tropical country is suicidal” and called for rescue services to charge such “reckless climbers” or insist on mandatory insurance.

Yet when NBS reported days later that a 49‑year‑old Japanese man had broken his ankle in a fall on Mount Hotaka and summoned a rescue helicopter, the tone online was markedly different. The trail was described as “tough”, and calling for help was praised as “a wise decision”.

For Paul Christie, founder of specialist tourism operator Walk Japan, that contrast helps explain why foreign hikers often draw the internet’s fury.

Japanese hikers were presumed to “always abide by the rules” and be more responsible, he said.

Christie believes the issue has attracted so much attention that new regulations are now inevitable and, in some respects, overdue.

“In France, anyone who needs to be rescued by helicopter from a mountain has to cover that cost and I think that would be the easiest thing to do here as well,” he said. “People should be clearly warned of the dangers and the possible costs of being evacuated.”

But he was wary of framing the problem as a strictly foreigners vs locals debate.

“This is not unique to Japan,” Christie said. “How many Japanese are helped off mountains in other parts of the world every year? Both sides just need to be better prepared.”

item.title: [SCMP] Can Philippines turn reforms into gains in Southeast Asia’s FDI race?
item.description: Not so long ago, the Philippines was bedevilled by warning signs to investors, from its corruption to its groaning infrastructure and a fuel crisis born of a war fought thousands of miles away. Those problems have not simply disappeared, but a flurry of business-friendly reforms – paired with ambitious plans to upgrade the nation’s railways, ports and power grid – now puts it in a stronger position to capture some of the billions of dollars seeking a home outside the region’s recent supply chain
item.content:encoded:
Not so long ago, the Philippines was bedevilled by warning signs to investors, from its corruption to its groaning infrastructure and a fuel crisis born of a war fought thousands of miles away.

Those problems have not simply disappeared, but a flurry of business-friendly reforms – paired with ambitious plans to upgrade the nation’s railways, ports and power grid – now puts it in a stronger position to capture some of the billions of dollars seeking a home outside the region’s recent supply chain winners, such as Vietnam and Malaysia.

With companies still fleeing Washington’s tariff war on China, Philippine President Ferdinand Marcos Jnr’s government has pushed through new laws on tax, governance and extended land leasing to entice global capital seeking a safe harbour in troubled times.

Meanwhile, its English-speaking population of around 117 million offers a ready labour force for companies considering relocation.

Taken together, these factors give the Philippines all the hallmarks of a global supply chain “rising star”, according to risk intelligence and data analytics firm Verisk Maplecroft.

“Despite lower infrastructure quality and governance challenges, including recent corruption scandals, the Philippines’ opportunities are knocking in electronics, auto parts and food manufacturing,” the company said in its recent 2026 Supply Chain Risk Outlook report.

It praised the country, alongside Thailand, for their “market openness, regulatory strength and labour rights – structural, slow-moving factors that are critical to manufacturing supply chains”.

Southeast Asia attracted a record US$244 billion in foreign direct investment last year. While regional leader Singapore captured the majority, the remainder was concentrated in Malaysia’s semiconductors, Vietnam’s electronics and communications and Indonesia’s renewable energy.

But Verisk Maplecroft warns that Vietnam and Malaysia are increasingly the subjects of investor concern over shifting global tariffs and complex regulatory compliance risks.

Indonesia, meanwhile, has seen its market openness decline amid what the firm called “rising resource nationalism”, in reference to government proposals to channel strategic exports through a state-owned entity.

While the Philippines trails its neighbours in total FDI, its geopolitical position has opened doors that analysts say could help it climb the global value chain.

These include the Luzon Economic Corridor – a connectivity project with the US and Japan that could inject US$100 billion into the economy by transforming the country’s main island into a manufacturing and logistics hub – and a 1,620-hectare AI hub under the Pax Silica initiative.

Pax Silica is a US-led coalition that aims to secure critical supply chains in artificial intelligence and semiconductors. The Philippines and Singapore are its sole Southeast Asian members.

Beneath the ground lies another prize: an estimated US$1 trillion in mineral wealth, including the world’s second-largest nickel reserves – a resource crucial for EV batteries and coveted by both China and the US.

“The Philippines has been at the receiving end of foreign direct investment from other like-minded or allied countries, as well as from competitors and supply chains for these hi-tech industries that have shown interest in the Luzon Economic Corridor,” said Michael Ricafort, chief economist at the Rizal Commercial Banking Corporation.

Recent laws extending foreign investor lease terms to 99 years and cutting income tax aim to spur further investment.

Climbing the chain

Semiconductors already account for 40 per cent of the Philippines’ exports and industry leaders sense a pivot point approaching.

“The next challenge is to move beyond traditional assembly and test operations towards higher-value activities across the semiconductor value chain,” said Dan Lachica, president of the Semiconductor and Electronics Industries in the Philippines Foundation.

“As countries seek to diversify production and reduce concentration risk, the Philippines could position itself as a trusted and reliable partner within this emerging network.”

Get the Luzon Economic Corridor right and it could “lower the cost of doing business, improve supply chain efficiency and make the Philippines a more attractive destination for high-value manufacturing investments”, he added.

The Philippines’ electronics exports hit US$49.64 billion last year, the bulk of which was semiconductors. Yet this pales in comparison when set against Malaysia’s 711 billion ringgit (US$174.3 billion) in exports, a figure projected to top 800 billion ringgit this year as the neighbouring nation pushes into front-end wafer testing and other higher-value work.

Lachica’s foundation has been advocating for a national semiconductor wafer lab to build similar capabilities at home.

What the Philippines lacks in hi-tech infrastructure, it more than makes up for with an abundant labour force that has helped it build the world’s second-largest business process outsourcing industry after India.

“The Philippines’ competitive advantage is at the higher end of the global value chain,” Ricafort said, pointing to industries that required “highly skilled professionals” such as business process outsourcing and emerging hi-tech sectors as possible growth areas.

Jack Madrid, president and CEO of the IT and Business Process Association of the Philippines, agreed that the country was “well-positioned to support companies that are redesigning their global operations”.

“Our value proposition goes beyond cost. We offer scale, a strong service culture, an English-proficient workforce and growing capabilities in AI-enabled services,” he said.

Yet for all the hype, experts urge caution. The Philippines still has huge infrastructure gaps, a sluggish bureaucracy and a declining skills base, with AI threatening to eliminate hundreds of thousands of back-office jobs.

A government-backed education commission report released in January found that 70 per cent of Filipino children “continue to struggle with foundational skills” by the third grade, with proficiency worsening at every subsequent level.

“That impacts the ability of the country to create a credible workforce for critical and emerging technologies,” said Mark Manantan, a technology researcher at La Trobe University’s Centre for Global Security.

“And if you add artificial intelligence into the mix … it’s already threatening the business process outsourcing.”

Observers warn that the government must anchor its long-term plans on building a resilient economy, rather than one shaped merely to fit the current chaotic global moment through tax incentives that ultimately share little benefit with local communities.

“I have suspicions that the Luzon Economic Corridor alone will not propel the Philippines up the value chain,” said Maria Theresa Anna Robles, an assistant professor of political science at the University of the Philippines Diliman.

“Strategies need to take a more long-term perspective instead of being swayed by geopolitical vacillations. If the policy mix includes strengthening domestic capabilities alongside investment incentives … then that could be a step in the right direction.”

item.title: [SCMP] Asia’s AI boom has a dirty secret: the coal that powers it
item.description: Asia sits atop vast amounts of coal, nearly three-fifths of the world’s known reserves by some estimates. Cheaper and more reliable than imported oil and gas, and far less vulnerable to disruptions caused by foreign wars, coal is also dirty, polluting – and the region’s No 1 “stopgap source” to power the AI infrastructure roll-out. “AI demand is materialising faster than clean energy generation can be commissioned,” said Alexander Kheder, a market research analyst at BMI who tracks global AI inf
item.content:encoded:
Asia sits atop vast amounts of coal, nearly three-fifths of the world’s known reserves by some estimates.

Cheaper and more reliable than imported oil and gas, and far less vulnerable to disruptions caused by foreign wars, coal is also dirty, polluting – and the region’s No 1 “stopgap source” to power the AI infrastructure roll-out.

“AI demand is materialising faster than clean energy generation can be commissioned,” said Alexander Kheder, a market research analyst at BMI who tracks global AI infrastructure spending and data centre expansion.

Solar and wind, for all their recent progress, still could not deliver the relentless, round-the-clock baseload that the data centres powering artificial intelligence demanded, he said.

In Malaysia’s pioneering smart city of Cyberjaya, dozens of data centres are already in operation, with more planned for the future.

“The impact is going to be very, very visible,” said Adit Rahim, 49, a local resident and communications executive who worries about the strain all this development is placing on utilities.

“What no one is really addressing is whether resources like water and electricity are keeping pace,” he said.

Data centres never sleep. Their servers hum around the clock, training and running the artificial-intelligence models that are transforming the global economy – and their appetite for electricity is voracious.

That hunger has been a boon for companies such as Johnson Controls, whose Asia-Pacific vice-president for project delivery and data centre solutions, Ali Badreddine, has been fielding a flood of urgent requests from his base in Singapore.

“Customers are telling us they need everything faster,” he told This Week in Asia.

His company’s backup power systems – essential to prevent catastrophic data loss at AI data centres, where an unexpected power outage can destroy months of model-training progress – used to ship within months, but now come with waiting times of two years or more.

“Even if you are willing to pay, there simply isn’t enough manufacturing capacity available,” Badreddine said.

Southeast Asia’s AI workload demand will, in all likelihood, continue outpacing renewable energy supply for some years yet, according to Kheder, which is why coal has become the No 1 “stopgap source to bridge this gap”.

War woes

Asia imports the bulk of its oil and gas, much of it through a chokepoint strait that has been largely sealed off amid the US-Israel war on Iran.

Oil prices have whipsawed this year from lows of around US$60 a barrel in January to nearly US$120 at the height of the conflict in April. A ceasefire agreed in June helped stabilise the market for a few weeks until US President Donald Trump declared the truce “over” on July 8 following a fresh round of tit-for-tat attacks, sending prices rising again.

Soaring natural gas prices, meanwhile, have driven electricity tariffs to all-time highs of nearly 35 cents (27 US cents) per kilowatt-hour in Singapore, while sky-high prices in the Philippines have pushed households towards solar and Vietnam has rolled out peak-hour energy pricing to manage the strain.

Malaysia’s rates have held steady so far, largely thanks to government subsidies, but those living in the shadow of Cyberjaya’s server farms are nervous about the future.

“At the end of the day, there will be a choke point in electricity supply,” Rahim said of Malaysia’s proliferating data centres. “And there will definitely be an impact on water supply.”

Big data centres had two key questions when it came to power, said Ashwin Balasubramanian, a partner at management consultancy firm McKinsey: how soon can we get it and how reliable is the supply?

“That is much more important than just green power. Less than 40 per cent have actually made those [green] commitments,” he said, predicting a short‑term increase in fossil fuel emissions.

Mayank Maheshwari, an energy analyst for India and Southeast Asia at multinational investment bank Morgan Stanley, said the priority for policymakers across the region would be securing steady energy supplies.

“Energy consumers worldwide are price-sensitive and discerning. They always gravitate towards the most affordable and reliable forms of energy,” he said – adding that Asia, with its enormous coal reserves, had an easily accessible option close at hand.

Morgan Stanley projects that the region’s coal consumption will climb by 130 million tonnes between now and 2030, which equates to an 8-10 per cent increase.

It also forecasts Asia’s annual energy spending will double over that same period, with more than US$5 trillion being spent on clean and fossil fuel investments, making it the region’s second-largest funding destination after AI itself.

Separately, Asian buyers are expected to snap up a decent chunk of the more than 100 million tonnes of natural gas that analysts predict the US will release onto global markets this year alone.

“Asia is preparing to absorb the supply, as it is expected to be cheaper than the imported natural gas prices Asian consumers have paid in recent years,” Maheshwari said.

In a coal hole?

Fossil fuels offer a quick fix to power the AI boom when demand outstrips supply, but not everyone is convinced that a full-blown coal revival is inevitable.

“A major new coal wave would be a blunt and expensive answer to a more complex problem,” said Aditya Lolla, interim managing director at energy think tank Ember.

He argues that the sums simply do not add up, as new coal plants take years to build and would not come online until long after the current AI-driven demand spike has passed.

In the longer term, Lolla said more coal and gas would only lead to increased air pollution while saddling the region with power plants ill-suited to grids increasingly built for renewables.

Renewables are certainly more cost efficient, according to the International Renewable Energy Agency, which found that more than 90 per cent of all newly commissioned utility-scale renewable projects globally last year delivered power cheaper than the lowest-cost new fossil-fuel alternative.

In Indonesia, Thailand and the Philippines alone, renewable generation has already displaced an estimated US$5.7 billion of coal and gas imports that would otherwise have been bought, the agency found in a recent report.

“In many markets, it is already the most cost-competitive source of new electricity generation and is increasingly the preferred procurement option for major technology and industrial companies,” said Joe Phelan, executive director for the Asia-Pacific at the World Business Council for Sustainable Development.

Yet analysts say there exists a divergence between the region’s most advanced, supply-constrained economies such as Singapore – which is increasingly positioning itself as a regional hub for clean-power imports – and less constrained markets that tend to lean more heavily on coal and gas because they are so readily available.

Michael Goh, vice-president and general manager for the Asia-Pacific and other regions at Iron Mountain Data Centres – one of the world’s largest operators, with a facility in Singapore – said higher electricity costs had indeed affected the industry, though he credited the city state’s government with working to contain them.

He said his own group had focused on boosting energy efficiency and tapping resources otherwise considered waste output, such as heat recovered through the regasification of liquefied natural gas, rather than drawing more heavily on water and power.

Singapore had also met additional demand through partnerships with Indonesia and Australia to import renewable energy, he added.

Cross-border connectivity may be the key to solving the region’s energy challenge, according to specialist project management consultancy TBH.

The Association of Southeast Asian Nations’ long-stalled integrated power grid plan, first sketched out decades ago, could offer such a path to sharing surplus clean power across borders rather than each country going it alone.

“Southeast Asia’s energy transition is entering a new phase,” said Ali Nami, a director and energy lead at TBH.

“The ability to coordinate these investments and translate them into reliable outcomes will play a major role in shaping the region’s long-term energy future.”

Additional reporting by Ushar Daniele

item.title: [SCMP] China’s tech giants fuel the rise of AI tokens as a new corporate currency
item.description: Every month, a Beijing-based ByteDance employee says he burns through close to a billion units of a new corporate currency – one that cannot buy a coffee or pay rent. Yet, as he noted, that staggering volume “doesn’t even put me near the top of the consumption rankings in my department”. That currency is not yuan, but artificial intelligence tokens. “Using AI has become part of almost every task now,” said the employee, who requested anonymity as he was not authorised by the company to speak to
item.content:encoded:
Every month, a Beijing-based ByteDance employee says he burns through close to a billion units of a new corporate currency – one that cannot buy a coffee or pay rent.

Yet, as he noted, that staggering volume “doesn’t even put me near the top of the consumption rankings in my department”.

That currency is not yuan, but artificial intelligence tokens.

“Using AI has become part of almost every task now,” said the employee, who requested anonymity as he was not authorised by the company to speak to the media.

A token – dubbed ciyuan in mainland China – is the basic unit through which AI services are measured and billed. Every prompt submitted to a model and every response generated consumes tokens, much like electricity is measured in kilowatt-hours.

As Chinese tech giants from ByteDance to Alibaba Group Holding and Tencent Holdings rush to encourage staff to integrate AI into daily workflows, tokens have become a new kind of corporate allowance. Employees are no longer just budgeting their time; they are also expected to budget their token use as proof of their AI literacy.

This surge in AI use in the corporate sector is being fuelled by a brutal price war. When Hangzhou-based AI unicorn DeepSeek slashed the price of its flagship V4-Pro model by 75 per cent in May, it highlighted the defining feature of China’s AI industry: an accelerating race to make intelligence cheap.

The overarching logic is that as token costs plummet, a broader economy built around ubiquitous AI adoption can finally take shape.

The token surge

For decades, Chinese companies bought software through fixed, predictable subscriptions – often called “per-seat” licensing. If a company bought 500 licences for an office software suite, the monthly bill remained the same whether an employee typed one sentence or 1,000 pages.

AI billing, however, scales proportionally with usage. While a short question might cost a regular user a few dozen tokens, a software engineer scanning an extensive corporate spreadsheet or building a computer program can burn through millions at a time.

The exact price of a token varies by the model. DeepSeek, for instance, later reversed course and raised its prices, charging 12 yuan (US$1.77) for every million output tokens during peak hours and 6 yuan during non-peak time for API access to its latest V4 models.

To adapt, Chinese tech companies are experimenting with new allocation methods.

In May, cybersecurity firm 360 Security Technology launched a companywide initiative to directly distribute a total of 100 million AI tokens across employee accounts, entirely eliminating managerial approval and reimbursement procedures.

Founder Zhou Hongyi has repeatedly argued publicly that AI tools must be integrated into ordinary workflows, rather than restricted mostly to specialised engineering teams.

In March, Tencent, developer of the Hunyuan model, handed out free tokens to employees through products such as QClaw, an internal AI tool. During testing, some individual quotas reportedly reached as high as 40 million tokens per day.

Alibaba, the Hangzhou-based e-commerce giant, launched a similar internal incentive scheme the same month, granting token allowances for in-house tools like its agentic platform Wukong and coding assistant Qoder.

Internal adoption is expected to climb further after Alibaba’s security team banned the use of Anthropic’s Claude Code earlier this month, citing data security risks.

Alibaba owns the South China Morning Post.

Rise of the token economy

This major corporate push has contributed to a massive explosion in token usage across the country.

According to the National Data Administration (NDA), China’s annual AI token consumption surpassed 140 trillion in March, up from 100 billion two years earlier. That is equivalent to roughly 100,000 tokens per person based on the country’s population of about 1.4 billion.

The [Chinese] digital economy has gone through the stages of the data economy and the computing economy. Today, the token economy is emerging

Yin Hao, Chinese Academy of Sciences

In May, Premier Li Qiang said average daily token consumption in the country had reached hundreds of trillions by the end of the month.

Top Chinese policymakers have openly touted the emergence of a “token economy”.

When NDA head Liu Liehong convened executives from top domestic companies including Alibaba, Tencent and China International Capital Corporation at a token-economy symposium in May, he described tokens as the new accounting and settlement units for AI services.

At the China Internet Conference in Beijing last week, Yin Hao, an academician at the Chinese Academy of Sciences, said, “The [Chinese] digital economy has gone through the stages of the data economy and the computing economy. Today, the token economy is emerging.”

Tokens were more than just a technical metric, they had become a new production factor connecting AI model capability, computing resources and commercial value, he said.

China Unicom viewed “token operation” as an important driver of efficiency across its businesses, company vice-president Qiu Baohua said in a speech at the conference.

Qiu said Unicom’s growing AI token consumption mirrored the expanding use of AI across its business.

AI agents had improved document drafting and contract retrieval efficiency by 70 per cent, he said, while AI now handled more than 85 per cent of calls to Unicom’s customer service hotline, reducing annual operating costs by more than 100 million yuan (US$14.8 million).

Other industry leaders also expressed optimism about the future of the token economy, with Huawei Technologies vice-president and head of data communication products Wang Lei saying the company believed it was “the greatest opportunity we can be certain of in an otherwise uncertain era”.

The developments reflect a growing belief among policymakers and technology companies that AI output is becoming an economic resource in its own right. As model usage spreads across coding, research, customer service and content creation, tokens are increasingly being used to measure, allocate and price access to that resource.

“Tokens are effectively the energy unit of the AI era,” said Zhou Zhengang, research vice-president at IDC China.

“Just as companies in the industrial era budgeted for electricity, companies in the AI era will have to budget for tokens,” added Lu Yanxia, research director at the market intelligence firm. “They are becoming a financial metric rather than simply a technical one.”

IDC’s latest market tracking report, published in May, projected that China’s model-as-a-service (MaaS) market would process 40,000 trillion tokens in 2026, an increase of about 20 times versus 2025.

The surveillance shift

The rapid expansion of token allowances, however, has also created a new flashpoint for corporate strife: unprecedented managerial surveillance. As AI usage surges across entire companies, bosses have gained unprecedented visibility into how employees work.

Some companies are now linking token consumption directly to employee performance evaluations.

Beijing-based internet company Kunlun Tech announced in February it would provide engineers access to top US coding AI agents, including OpenAI’s Codex and Anthropic’s Claude Code, while evaluating employees based on how many tokens they consumed.

This mirrors controversial productivity tracking methods seen in the US.

According to internal reports from late last year, Meta Platforms, the parent of Facebook and Instagram, briefly used an internal dashboard known as the “Claudeonomics” leaderboard to track AI token consumption across more than 85,000 employees before shutting it down after internal statistics were leaked.

Amazon similarly retired “KiroRank”, an internal leaderboard that ranked developers by AI activity. Some employees had been inflating token usage through unnecessary agent tasks to fake productivity, spiking compute costs without delivering real output, the Financial Times reported in late May.

Similar tensions are beginning to emerge in China.

Since June, Tencent has tightened internal AI allowances, requiring employees to seek fresh approval every two weeks for additional tokens. Quotas have been slashed to less than half of previous levels and are now increasingly tied to specific job functions and workloads, according to an employee working on AI agents.

Tencent did not respond to a request for comment.

For tech enterprises in both the US and China, the primary drivers are the same: intense financial pressure to increase engineering efficiency, speed up product releases and drive down development costs.

While corporate executives often pitch the token economy as a way to liberate workers from boring, repetitive tasks, some employees describe a more complicated reality. Part of the reason is that AI tools are not yet reliable enough to fully automate entire workflows.

Employees from various Chinese companies have reported a distinct gap between corporate optimisation targets and reality. AI tools remain prone to hallucinations – instances where models generate incorrect or fabricated data – meaning heavy token usage often translates to more oversight work for humans, not less.

“Cursor and Codex are basically used by everyone,” said a software engineer in Nvidia’s IT Software Engineering group in Shanghai, referring to popular AI-assisted coding tools. “But that does not mean the workload is lighter. I spend more time reviewing AI-generated output now.”

China’s state-owned telecoms giants are looking to monetise the AI boom by introducing tokens as a new unit of measurement in monthly bills, after having billed users by the gigabyte of data for years.

In May, China Telecom unveiled nationwide, token-based pricing packages on its official app, targeting everyone from enterprise developers to casual smartphone users.

Business packages, which support coding and AI agent deployment, range from 39.9 yuan to 299.9 yuan per month, with token allowances from 15 million to 250 million. Consumer packages, tailored for everyday tasks, start at 9.9 yuan per month for 10 million tokens and increase in price and volume to 49.9 yuan for 80 million tokens.

Beyond ‘big tech’

As the cost of running AI models continues to drop, the token economy is reshaping ecosystems beyond corporate cubicles – namely, venture capital and higher education.

In the start-up world, the traditional practice of exchanging cash for corporate equity is being supplemented by a more immediate need: access to raw compute.

In March, prominent Chinese venture capital firm ZhenFund, in partnership with start-up developer community Crossing, launched an initiative dubbed “Token Grant”.

Instead of offering standard cash financing, the programme awards early-stage AI developers 50,000 yuan worth of direct model-access credits. The initiative specifically targets solo founders and prototype-stage teams who are building AI agents or multimodal products before they approach institutional investors for cash.

“For early-stage developers, token credits often matter more immediately than cash itself,” said an angel investor, who requested anonymity because they were not authorised to speak to the media. “A small team with sufficient compute access can now prototype agents, generate code, process video and test products without hiring large engineering teams.”

The replacement of cash with tokens has even reached the halls of higher education. In May, during a public panel discussion with Moonshot AI president Zhang Yutong, Tian Xuan, the dean of Peking University’s Guanghua School of Management, suggested that universities might eventually “stop giving scholarships and distribute more tokens instead”.

Additional reporting by Ben Jiang

item.title: [SCMP] Why buyout funds, not IPOs, may define China’s next capital market cycle: Zhang Yichen
item.description: Zhang Yichen is chairman and CEO of Citic Capital Holdings, one of the leading players in China’s capital market, and the chairman of Trustar Capital. He heads several companies, including the McDonald’s master franchise business in mainland China and Hong Kong, as well as Harbin Pharmaceutical Group. He also sits on the board of the Hong Kong Exchanges and Clearing (HKEX) as an independent non-executive director. In this interview, conducted during the “two sessions” – the annual meetings of Ch
item.content:encoded:
Zhang Yichen is chairman and CEO of Citic Capital Holdings, one of the leading players in China’s capital market, and the chairman of Trustar Capital. He heads several companies, including the McDonald’s master franchise business in mainland China and Hong Kong, as well as Harbin Pharmaceutical Group. He also sits on the board of the Hong Kong Exchanges and Clearing (HKEX) as an independent non-executive director.

In this interview, conducted during the “two sessions” – the annual meetings of China’s top legislature and advisory body – the veteran investor and government adviser discussed the role of buyout funds, the impact of geopolitical tensions on cross-border deals and why global brands are increasingly relying on local partners to succeed in China. He also shared his views on Hong Kong’s strategic role as a global financial centre.

This interview first appeared in SCMP Plus. For other interviews in the Open Questions series, click here.

Regulators have recently stressed that government-backed investment funds should lead the way in serving as “patient capital” – funding provided from a more long-term perspective and with a higher tolerance for risk – as a contrast to what they view as the pursuit of rapid and maximum returns. In your opinion, how should buyout funds respond to this?

Patient capital is not simply about holding assets for longer. It requires both long-term capital and the capability to create value within companies.

China has historically lacked long-duration capital due to its reliance on bank-led, indirect financing. Increasing the share of direct and equity financing is essential to building a foundation for patient capital.

At the same time, institutions managing this capital must be able to empower businesses through governance improvements, operational support and strategic guidance.

China’s market evolves rapidly, and remote control from global headquarters is often too slow and insufficiently attuned to local dynamics

You are a member of the national committee of the Chinese People’s Political Consultative Conference – the country’s top political advisory body. At this year’s two sessions, you said the role of buyout funds in capital market restructuring is an area of focus for you. With the private equity industry holding more than 10 trillion yuan (US$1.47 trillion) in legacy assets and facing mounting exit pressures, what role should buyout funds play in addressing this challenge? How do you assess the long-term significance of the new national-level buyout funds?

Relying solely on initial public offerings (IPOs) to exit such a large pool of private equity assets is unrealistic. Even with the recent recovery in the A-share and Hong Kong markets, IPO capacity remains limited. In mature markets, nearly 80 per cent of exits are achieved through buyout funds rather than IPOs.

China’s mergers and acquisitions (M&A) market is still underdeveloped. Many companies continue to focus on listing, while the market needs more consolidation to support industrial upgrades and innovation. Buyout funds could play a key role by integrating fragmented assets and linking the primary and capital markets.

The launch of national-level buyout funds sends a clear policy signal: the government is placing greater emphasis on asset consolidation after years of “early-stage, small-scale, innovation-focused” investments. These funds are complementary to market-driven buyout funds and have a stronger strategic orientation.

The Citic Capital-led buyout of McDonald’s China under your leadership is widely regarded as a landmark deal. In carve-outs involving the China operations of multinationals, what are the critical factors for success? Do you expect more deals of a similar scale in the coming years?

Multinational companies in China are facing two main pressures: intensifying local competition and geopolitical headwinds, which have made China a more challenging and, in some cases, a riskier market in the eyes of global investors.

Against this backdrop, many have come to realise the core issue is not operational capability, but decision-making. The core lessons are threefold: the importance of a localised strategy, aligned incentive mechanisms and speed of execution. In many cases, multinational operations in China are fundamentally sound. The issue is not the “body”, but the “brain” – decision-making that is not sufficiently localised.

China’s market evolves rapidly, and remote control from global headquarters is often too slow and insufficiently attuned to local dynamics, particularly given the country’s highly digitalised and distinct ecosystem. As a result, there’s a growing need for more localised decision-making at the strategic level.

This is why more multinational companies are turning to local partners. In China, many have chosen to work with financial investors rather than industry players, partly because successful cases such as McDonald’s have shown this model can be more straightforward.

Bringing in a strategic partner often introduces additional complexities, such as potential competition and management integration issues, whereas working with an investment fund allows companies to retain operational continuity while improving strategic alignment.

In our experience, multinational businesses in China are generally well run, with capable local teams. What is needed is not a wholesale overhaul of operations but a shift in strategy, stronger management incentives and faster execution.

The McDonald’s China transaction illustrates this approach. After our investment, we largely retained the existing management team and focused instead on strategic adjustments. One key change was upgrading the company’s digital capabilities, including appointing a new chief technology officer to build systems tailored to China’s unique IT environment. This significantly improved efficiency and responsiveness, without disrupting the core organisation.

Looking ahead, as competition intensifies and geopolitical factors persist, we expect more multinational companies to consider similar carve-out transactions or partnerships with local investors.

You mentioned earlier that AI is being integrated into your operations. Could you explain how the technology is reshaping your overall strategy and share specific examples of its use?

AI is already being applied across both customer-facing and operational functions. It is used to analyse consumer behaviour, enhance customer engagement and improve membership activity.

On the operational side, AI supports decision-making in areas such as demand forecasting, staffing and inventory management. For example, predicting peak-hour traffic helps optimise food preparation, reducing waste while maintaining efficiency.

Rather than relying on a single large model, the company uses multiple models in parallel and continuously tests and refines them to improve performance.

How do you assess the outlook for China’s M&A market over the next two to three years? As Chinese companies accelerate their global expansion, how is Citic Capital positioning itself to support outbound M&A and global market development, and what are the biggest opportunities and challenges for Chinese companies pursuing overseas acquisitions?

We remain highly optimistic about the outlook for China’s M&A market over the next two to three years. This is largely driven by a structural shift in the economy, as China moves from a phase of high-speed growth to one focused on high-quality development.

At this stage, economic growth increasingly depends on upgrading existing industries rather than simply creating new ones. While emerging sectors are still driven by venture capital, a much larger part of the economy now consists of mature companies that require consolidation, restructuring and efficiency improvements – which is where M&A becomes essential.

In that sense, M&A is not just an option but a natural outcome of this stage of development. Policy support is also reinforcing this trend. Regulators have introduced measures to facilitate M&A activity, including financing tools such as M&A loans, while the establishment of state-backed buyout funds signals a stronger role for government guidance in driving consolidation. Taken together, both the economic fundamentals and the policy environment point to a more active M&A market ahead.

On outbound M&A, we’ve been involved in cross-border transactions for more than two decades, primarily in the United States, Europe and Japan. In recent years, however, geopolitical factors have made deals in the US increasingly difficult. Europe has also been affected, though to a lesser extent, while Japan remains relatively more accessible.

There are also structural challenges beyond geopolitics. In cross-border acquisitions, financial investors and strategic buyers often have different priorities – particularly regarding exit timing and long-term objectives – which can make alignment difficult. This is not unique to China, but a global issue, and it partly explains why successful joint acquisitions remain relatively limited.

For Chinese companies going global, the biggest challenge is still geopolitics. At the same time, many companies are still in the process of building [the full capabilities of a multinational] – particularly in branding, product development and end-to-end operations –rather than relying primarily on manufacturing [strength].

As more companies expand overseas, they also face cultural differences and operational mismatches when applying domestic business models in foreign markets. In addition, as developed markets become more difficult to enter, many are turning to emerging markets, which come with higher risks.

That being said, the opportunities are significant. China’s manufacturing and industrial capabilities are highly competitive globally. In many sectors, Chinese companies have clear advantages in efficiency and execution. The potential for international expansion is therefore substantial – but it will take time to realise fully.

Building China into a “financial powerhouse”, a major policy goal, requires attracting more long-term foreign capital to Chinese assets. From your communications with overseas investors, how do they currently view the China market and what factors matter most in their assessment? In the current geopolitical environment, how can China stabilise expectations and strengthen long-term investor confidence? Do you see a reversal of an earlier view that China was “uninvestible”?

Foreign investors’ views on China have undergone a significant adjustment over the past few years, but it’s important to differentiate by region.

US investors, particularly large long-term institutions, have sharply reduced their exposure to China, largely for geopolitical reasons. This has also affected capital from countries closely aligned with the US, such as Canada and Australia. European and Japanese investors have also been affected, though to a lesser extent.

Asian investors, by contrast, are less constrained by geopolitical considerations. But their allocation to China has also declined in recent years due to factors such as the pandemic, the property sector downturn and policy uncertainties, making it harder for them to fully understand the market. In addition, weak exit conditions have limited their ability to redeploy capital.

That being said, sentiment has begun to improve since last year alongside the recovery in capital markets. We are seeing renewed interest from Asian investors, particularly in the Middle East, where allocations to China have historically been low compared with those to the US. As a result, there has been a noticeable increase in collaboration with these investors in recent transactions.

More broadly, we are also seeing a reversal of that earlier view that China was “uninvestible”. Investors are once again recognising that China remains investible and that it continues to have strong economic fundamentals. But geopolitical constraints mean that capital flows are unlikely to return to pre-2020 levels.

In terms of what investors are focused on today, geopolitics remains the key overarching risk, but it is not easily controlled. More fundamentally, investors are concerned about the balance and sustainability of China’s economic growth – in particular, the weakness in domestic demand and consumption.

Much of this is linked to the property sector. As a result, a clear and sustained stabilisation of the real estate market would be a critical turning point. Once investors see that the property sector has genuinely bottomed out and begun to recover, confidence in China’s broader economic outlook – and in turn, long-term capital allocation – is likely to strengthen significantly.

As more mainland Chinese institutions expand into Hong Kong, how can the city strengthen its role as a “superconnector”? What further steps can Hong Kong take to bridge mainland capital with global assets and attract more international capital into China?

Hong Kong should make full use of its “one country, two systems” advantage and continue to reinforce its position as an international financial centre, while integrating more closely with the country’s broader development agenda, including initiatives such as the 15th five-year plan.

Its role as an international financial hub is irreplaceable and represents a unique advantage for both Hong Kong and the country. This advantage has become particularly evident over the past year, as Hong Kong has helped revive its own economy while also supporting the development of mainland China’s capital markets. To sustain this role, it is important to preserve and further strengthen the institutional and legal foundations under “one country, two systems”.

At the same time, Hong Kong is beginning to explore a more proactive development model. The establishment of the Hong Kong Investment Corporation (HKIC) is a useful step in this direction. The city has long relied on a “small government” approach, but some of the deeper structural challenges it faces today cannot be addressed by the free market alone.
