While the HKIC is still relatively small and should not be expected to do too much too quickly, it can serve as an important starting point.

As Hong Kong deepens its integration into national development and works more closely with mainland institutions – particularly large state-owned enterprises – there’s a need for a platform similar to Singapore’s Temasek. In this sense, the HKIC could evolve into such an institution over time.

More broadly, Hong Kong’s most important role lies in supporting the internationalisation of the yuan. The city should actively, and in some areas even boldly, introduce policies that strengthen its position as a true offshore yuan centre. This requires balancing financial stability – given that the yuan is not yet fully convertible – with greater openness, while leveraging Hong Kong’s advantages as a free port and international financial hub.

If China is to meaningfully increase the share of direct financing, a well-functioning bond market is essential

What do you consider to be the most pressing issue in China’s capital markets today, and why? What solutions do you envision?

China’s capital markets remain relatively underdeveloped, particularly in terms of structure. The financial system has long been dominated by banks, with a heavy reliance on indirect financing.

For many years, there’s been a policy push to increase the share of direct financing – especially equity financing – but progress has been relatively limited. As a result, much of the economic growth has been funded by debt, leading to high leverage across the system.

The key challenge, therefore, is how to shift more capital from debt-based financing into true direct investment, particularly equity. There’s been some progress on the equity side, especially in the stock market, where development has gradually moved onto a more stable track.

The next critical step is the bond market. In previous years, disruptions in the credit environment led to a loss of confidence and numerous defaults. However, this should not derail its development. If China is to meaningfully increase the share of direct financing, a well-functioning bond market is essential.

What is needed now is to establish a more complete institutional and regulatory framework tailored to the bond market, so that it can develop in a more stable and sustainable way. This is one of the most urgent priorities for the future development of China’s capital markets.

The recent AI boom has reignited investor interest in the sector, as well as downstream industries. How is this changing the way Citic Capital evaluates investment opportunities? Are you seeing more M&A or buyout opportunities emerging as companies race to adopt AI, or are you adopting a “wait and see” approach?

Driven by strong market sentiment, valuations in certain AI segments have risen rapidly. In response, we have maintained a relatively cautious stance, believing that both solid fundamentals and reasonable valuations are essential.

We have not been overly aggressive in deploying capital at the earlier infrastructure stage. Instead, we are more inclined to wait until commercial opportunities at the AI application layer gradually emerge and achieve initial validation before investing. Ultimately, we hope the market does not become overheated, allowing us to preserve room for continued deployment in the second half of the year.

Looking ahead to the second half of this year, what do you see as the biggest opportunities and challenges for China’s capital markets? How will these shape the outlook for M&A and private equity investment?

China’s capital market is shifting from “scale expansion” to “quality enhancement.” The greatest opportunities lie in policy-supported industry consolidation and controlled buyouts. Buyout funds are becoming a key bridge connecting patient capital, the upgrading of listed companies, and new quality productive forces.

Specific opportunities include M&A and restructuring of listed companies, carve-outs by multinational corporations, scaling up of small and medium-sized enterprises, and so on. The main challenge is the shortage of long-term capital and the need to further optimise the fundraising structure.

item.title: [SCMP] Philippines’ Pax Silica AI hub plan slammed for mineral ‘plunder’
item.description: On a 1,620-hectare (4,000 acres) site north of Manila, Philippine and US officials are pitching a hi-tech hub as a global gateway to AI, semiconductors and critical minerals. For critics, however, the proposed development – part of the US-backed global Pax Silica project – is concrete proof of another infrastructure push over contested land at the expense of local communities, threatening their livelihoods and scarce water supplies. It is, they argue, a further example of ambitious investment pi
item.content:encoded:
On a 1,620-hectare (4,000 acres) site north of Manila, Philippine and US officials are pitching a hi-tech hub as a global gateway to AI, semiconductors and critical minerals.

For critics, however, the proposed development – part of the US-backed global Pax Silica project – is concrete proof of another infrastructure push over contested land at the expense of local communities, threatening their livelihoods and scarce water supplies.

It is, they argue, a further example of ambitious investment pitches racing ahead of environmental safeguards and proper cost-benefit analysis.

Manila is seeking to finalise a framework agreement with Washington for the hub within two to three months, according to Joshua Bingcang, president and CEO of the state-run Bases Conversion and Development Authority (BCDA).

The BCDA is tasked with turning former US military bases into centres of economic growth, including the one in New Clark City near Capas, Tarlac province – about 100km (62 miles) north of Manila – where the hub is set to be located.

Pax Silica aims to secure critical supply chains for artificial intelligence, semiconductors and other strategic technologies. The Philippines and Singapore are the only Southeast Asian members of the coalition.

Bingcang said the hub would likely start operations within two years, attract an initial US$10 billion in investment and generate “hundreds of thousands of jobs”.

The hub forms part of the Luzon Economic Corridor, a US-Japan-Philippines project linking four cities on Luzon Island that is projected to generate US$100 billion for the local economy.

Bingcang confirmed that Manila and Washington were exchanging draft agreements and denied earlier reports that the hub would operate under US legal jurisdiction despite being sited on Philippine soil.

In a television interview on Wednesday, he said Washington was aiming to conclude the deal before US President Donald Trump’s potential visit to the Philippines to attend the 49th Association of Southeast Asian Nations summit in November and mark 80 years of US-Philippine ties.

The US State Department has described Pax Silica as an effort to build “a secure, resilient” technology supply chain, as Washington looks to reduce its reliance on Chinese suppliers, especially for critical technologies.

Opposition to the planned hub has grown, with critics expressing concerns about the continued marginalisation and displacement of indigenous communities and farmers, environmental degradation and resource exploitation.

IBON Foundation has challenged claims that the hub would accelerate industrial, saying in a social media post in May that the Philippines “doesn’t have a strategy for national industrialisation”.

“Pax Silica isn’t about industrialising the Philippines but about deeper integration into US-controlled semiconductor and AI supply chains,” the non-profit think tank said, adding that industrialisation required building “national productive power” through local industries, labour, science and technology.

On Tuesday, around 3,000 members of the indigenous Aeta community in Capas marched in protest against restrictions they said authorities had imposed on their lands, including limits on access and basic supplies, which they claimed were partly related to the hub.

“The single most dangerous thing about Pax Silica is what it adds to a crisis that was already exploding before the announcement was made,” said Cathleen De Guzman, national coordinator for the Kalikasan People’s Network for the Environment, a Philippines-based environmental campaign group.

De Guzman said the Aeta community had fought for decades to secure titles to its ancestral domains, with the government repeatedly declining to issue them.

“The absence of a paper document was used to erase the existence of a community that has lived there since before the Spanish arrived,” she said, adding that an estimated 20,000 indigenous people and 15,000 farmers were at risk of displacement from the New Clark City development.

She also raised concerns about the Sacobia watershed – the primary water source for areas across New Clark City – warning that the risk of it drying up had been flagged as far back as 2020.

This Week in Asia has contacted the BCDA for comment.

Middle ground?

The backlash has exposed gaps in transparency, timing and legal process, according to environmental analysts.

Paulo Burro, an environmental policy and government affairs consultant and managing partner of Climate Balanced Business Advisors, said land conversion and the displacement of farmers, fisherfolk and indigenous people remained the biggest concerns surrounding the Pax Silica hub.

“With the drive to consolidate the supply chain, there may be increased mineral demand … questions remain around its actual economic impact on displaced farmers and fisherfolk,” Burro said.

Jonas Marie Dumdum, an energy and sustainability researcher and consultant, said such concerns were understandable given the scale of the project, noting the likely demand for minerals such as nickel, cobalt and copper to supply the hub.

Companies at the hub would not only draw an estimated 5,000 megawatts from Luzon’s coal-dependent grid, but also be located near identified watersheds and northeast of Mount Pinatubo, he said, referring to the active volcano whose 1991 eruption caused hundreds of deaths.

“The real pressure points in Capas come down to three things: carbon emissions, water and waste,” Dumdum said.

Industries would draw on the same water systems used to irrigate farms in central Tarlac, while mineral processing would produce heavy-metal waste in an area vulnerable to both flooding and volcanic eruption, he said.

“These points raise a question not just about our climate commitments but about whether there’s enough power, fresh water, and enough space and capacity to treat e-wastes in the area.”

However, Burro said that compromise remained possible, provided the interests of businesses, environmental groups and local communities were all protected.

The question was “how the project gets built and who gets a say, not necessarily over whether it should exist at all”, he said.

Dumdum said the public should be informed about the sources of the hub’s water and power supply, as well as the waste treatment process for mineral extraction.

He added that there should be a proper assessment of the potential environmental impact and “real consent” from farming and indigenous communities before the finalisation of any deal.

De Guzman said she “outrightly rejects Pax Silica”, calling it “plunder” of the country’s critical minerals and energy sources, as well as an attack on national sovereignty and patrimony.

She also questioned the authorities’ commitment to community engagement, calling the process a “pattern of consultation theatre”.

“This unequal agreement that the Philippines has joined opens our lands and vulnerable sectors to further exploitation and displacement,” she said.

item.title: [SCMP] Xi Jinping warns against creating ‘new historical injustices’ in AI era
item.description: President Xi Jinping warned against creating “new historical injustices” in the era of artificial intelligence on Friday, calling for greater support for the technology’s use and adoption in the Global South and a more open, inclusive approach to AI development. Addressing the 2026 World Artificial Intelligence Conference in Shanghai, Xi’s speech came shortly after his US counterpart, Donald Trump, delivered an unprecedented attack against Beijing during a prime-time national broadcast, accusing
item.content:encoded:
President Xi Jinping warned against creating “new historical injustices” in the era of artificial intelligence on Friday, calling for greater support for the technology’s use and adoption in the Global South and a more open, inclusive approach to AI development.

Addressing the 2026 World Artificial Intelligence Conference in Shanghai, Xi’s speech came shortly after his US counterpart, Donald Trump, delivered an unprecedented attack against Beijing during a prime-time national broadcast, accusing China of orchestrating the “largest compromise of election data in history”.

Xi also urged countries to strengthen risk awareness and ensure that AI is “secure and controllable”, while taking aim at the growing securitisation of the technology, at the opening ceremony of China’s largest annual event for the AI industry.

“We should jointly oppose overstretching the national security concept in the field of AI or placing one country’s security over that of others,” he said.

Xi’s speech, his first in-person appearance at the event since its launch in 2018, was viewed as a signal of Beijing’s intention to lead global AI governance amid an intensifying US-China tech rivalry.

The Chinese leader’s message also positioned Beijing as an advocate of a more open approach to AI development, at a time when access to advanced technologies is increasingly constrained by export controls and corporate blacklists.

During his speech, Xi paired his call for a more open and inclusive global AI order with a package of Chinese commitments aimed at helping developing countries bridge the AI divide and avoid what he called “new historical injustices”.

Over the next five years, China will provide 5,000 AI training and seminar programmes for developing countries and establish AI cooperation centres with Asean, the Arab League, the African Union, Latin American countries, the Shanghai Cooperation Organisation and Brics, he said.

Just moments before Xi’s speech, Trump accused Beijing of orchestrating the “largest compromise of election data in history” in a fiery prime-time address to the US and vowed to end what he called years of complacency towards “Chinese interference”. The duelling speeches underscored the deepening geopolitical and technological rivalry between the world’s two largest economies.

Kassym-Jomart Tokayev, the president of Kazakhstan, Cambodia’s Prime Minister Hun Manet and Thai Prime Minister Anutin Charnvirakul spoke at the opening ceremony of the conference, as did United Nations chief Antonio Guterres.

Xi added that China’s new five-year plan covering 2026-30 would provide “immense opportunities” for the international community, adding that the country’s core smart economy industries were now worth at least 1 trillion yuan (US$147.61 billion).

Citing a Chinese proverb that “a single tree does not make a forest”, Xi said AI development should not be “a solo performance by a single country, but a symphony of international cooperation”.

“China is ready to be more open, take more practical actions, and assume a more visionary perspective,” he said.

Xi’s personal attendance elevates AI development from a technological imperative to a cornerstone of China’s geopolitical strategy. As Washington maintains strict export controls on advanced semiconductors and restricts overseas access to top AI models, Beijing is accelerating its drive for tech self-reliance.

Also on Friday, the country’s National Development and Reform Commission released an action plan that proposed measures to facilitate cross-border data flows, expand access to computing power for developing countries and foster a global open-source AI ecosystem.

The eight-point plan called for greater international coordination on technical standards, safety governance and talent training, while promoting AI applications in fields such as healthcare, education, agriculture and manufacturing. It also pledged to contribute public scientific goods to the Global South and support the UN’s 2030 Sustainable Development Goals.

Beijing has been seeking a greater role in shaping international AI rules in recent years. In 2023, Xi proposed the Global AI Governance Initiative, calling for “equal rights” on AI development regardless of political systems.

According to Xinhua, 29 countries became founding members of the World AI Cooperation Organization after signing an agreement in Shanghai on Thursday to establish the body. Foreign Ministry spokesman Lin Jian said on Friday that China would work with the founding members to bring the organisation into operation as soon as possible.

China has also rejected criticism that its promotion of open-source AI models overseas amounts to a “trap”. Responding to a question on the subject at a press conference on Thursday, spokesman Lin said that Beijing opposed drawing ideological lines and imposing technological blockades.

During his speech, Xi also underscored the need to ensure AI’s development is “for positive, for good and for humanity.”

“We must make its oversight and governance precise and effective, and constantly refine measures to forestall loss of control,” he said.

This year’s World Artificial Intelligence Conference came as national security considerations increasingly shape competition across the entire AI supply chain, extending beyond advanced chips to companies, software and models.

The Pentagon added major Chinese technology groups, including Alibaba, Baidu, BYD, Unitree Robotics, CXMT and YMTC, to its list of “Chinese military companies” in June, allegations that the firms have denied. Alibaba owns the South China Morning Post.

Security concerns have also spilled into the use of foreign AI tools. Alibaba restricted employees’ access to Anthropic’s Claude Code after Chinese researchers raised concerns about code used to identify users in China and those linked to Chinese AI laboratories. Anthropic said the mechanism was designed to enforce its existing access restrictions.

A similar tension is playing out in AI hardware. Washington has sought to restrict China’s access to Nvidia’s most advanced processors, while still permitting sales of selected chips amid commercial pressure and strong demand from Chinese customers.

Beijing, meanwhile, is promoting domestic alternatives and has raised security concerns about Nvidia products, but local processors still cannot fully meet near-term demand for model training and inference. China has therefore continued to allow some purchases of Nvidia chips even as it accelerates efforts to build a more self-reliant computing ecosystem.

According to organisers, this year’s WAIC – set to run from Friday to Monday – would feature more than 1,100 exhibitors and over 300 global product debuts.

Held annually in Shanghai, the event gathers top scholars and entrepreneurs from the global AI community and has become a critical platform for China to voice its ambition in AI governance as it gains increasing influence.

Premier Li Qiang opened the conference in both 2024 and 2025, calling for an international centre to better coordinate global cooperation on AI.

At last year’s WAIC, China announced a 13-point action plan on global AI governance, stressing the need to step up cooperation, particularly with developing countries, to improve innovation capacity.

“This year’s WAIC is China’s latest showcase of its AI strengths to the world,” said Lian Jye Su, chief analyst at technology research firm Omdia. He said many emerging markets still faced severe shortages of AI talent, high-quality data, infrastructure and industry expertise, creating an opening for China’s broader technology ecosystem.

Chinese advancements in AI chips, token-based business models and open-source models could help fill those gaps, offering an alternative to Western suppliers for countries seeking to diversify their AI supply chains, he added.

Additional reporting by Ann Cao

item.title: [SCMP] China’s survival strategies written in flavours
item.description: The first time I ate proper Sichuan food in Chengdu, my mouth went numb. Not spicy-numb. Anaesthetised numb, like a dentist had been at my tongue with lignocaine. The waiter saw my face and laughed. “Málà,” he said. Numbing spicy: the specific sensation of Sichuan peppercorn meeting chilli oil, a flavour combination so particular to this basin-locked province that there’s no English word for it. I look Chinese and I should appreciate this, except I am not quite. What I didn’t understand then, ea
item.content:encoded:
The first time I ate proper Sichuan food in Chengdu, my mouth went numb. Not spicy-numb. Anaesthetised numb, like a dentist had been at my tongue with lignocaine. The waiter saw my face and laughed. “Málà,” he said. Numbing spicy: the specific sensation of Sichuan peppercorn meeting chilli oil, a flavour combination so particular to this basin-locked province that there’s no English word for it.

I look Chinese and I should appreciate this, except I am not quite. What I didn’t understand then, eating my way through a bowl of mapo tofu while my face leaked involuntary tears, was that I wasn’t experiencing cuisine. I was experiencing climate adaptation disguised as dinner.

Sichuan is a basin. Humid, isolated, subtropical. Before refrigeration, before modern preservation, the question wasn’t “what tastes good?” The question was “how do we stop food rotting in 90 per cent humidity while we’re eating it?” The answer: you overwhelm. You use so much chilli and peppercorn that bacteria doesn’t stand a chance. You pickle everything that can be pickled. You dry what can be dried. You ferment what’s left. The intensity isn’t indulgence, it’s necessity turned into culture over 2,000 years.

This is what Chinese food actually is, once you strip away the romanticisation and the “gastroporn” and the idea that cuisine is primarily about pleasure. It’s a historical record of what people needed to survive, written in flavour.

The basin: Sichuan’s humidity problem

Sichuan sits in a depression surrounded by mountains, with the Tibetan Plateau to the west, the Qinling range to the north. Moisture gets trapped. The air stays thick. In summer, it’s a greenhouse. In winter, it’s a damp cold that seeps into your bones and won’t leave. This creates a preservation crisis that coastal and northern China don’t face. Food spoils faster, mould is constant, storage is a battle against biology. So, Sichuan cooking became aggressive: doubanjiang (fermented broad-bean paste) by the handful, and hotpot, where everything gets boiled in chilli oil hot enough to sterilise on contact.

The food overwhelms your senses because it had to overwhelm microbes first.

I’ve eaten mapo tofu in London, in Singapore, in Chinese restaurants across three continents. It’s never the same as eating it in Chengdu, where the humidity is 85 per cent and your clothes never quite dry and the málà makes sense as something other than a flavour preference. Twice-cooked pork, dan dan noodles, fish-fragrant aubergine – every signature Sichuan dish follows the same logic: bold flavours that mask spoilage, high heat that kills bacteria, preservation techniques that buy time.

This is survival cuisine that became so successful it convinced the world it was always about pleasure.

The coast: Cantonese confidence

If Sichuan cuisine is about fighting humidity, Cantonese food is about not needing to fight anything.

Guangzhou sits at the Pearl River Delta, where river meets ocean, where trade brought wealth and wealth brought abundance, and abundance produced restraint. When you can get fresh fish daily, when vegetables grow year-round, you don’t need to preserve anything because tomorrow will bring more.

Cantonese cooking is quiet. Steaming, not frying. Light soy, not heavy bean paste. Ginger and spring onion, not chilli oil. The food doesn’t shout because it doesn’t need to. I’ve watched how Cantonese cooks prepare fish; steamed whole, soy sauce drizzled, nothing else. The point is showing off the fish, proving it’s so fresh that anything more would be vandalism. This is prosperity cuisine, abundance cuisine, cuisine that says, “we’re so secure, we can afford to do nothing”.

Dim sum, the elaborately folded dumplings, the delicate shrimp har gow, the translucent rice rolls, is restraint dressed as complexity. It is maximum technique applied to minimal ingredients, showing skill through precision rather than force. The Cantonese saying goes, “eat in Guangzhou”, not because the flavours are loudest, but because they’re truest. Coastal confidence written in steamed fish and morning yum cha.

The plateau: Shaanxi’s arid survival

Shaanxi province is characterised by yellow earth, cold winters, not enough water, wheat instead of rice because rice needs irrigation, the plateau can’t provide. This is northern China and the food reflects it: heavy, durable, built to fuel physical labour in cold, dry air where every calorie counts.

It gives us biang biang noodles, hand-pulled, wide as belts, served with chilli oil and vinegar because that’s what you’ve got and that’s what stores; roujiamo, flatbread stuffed with slow-cooked meat, eaten by hand, portable fuel; yangrou paomo, mutton soup with torn bread, eaten for breakfast when you need 2,000 calories before you start work.

I ate biang biang noodles in a tiny restaurant in Xian’s Muslim Quarter, watching the cook slap dough against a metal table until it stretched into noodles 60cm (two feet) long. The sound echoed off the walls – biang biang biang – which is supposedly how the dish got its name. The character for biang (𰻝) has 58 strokes and isn’t in standard dictionaries because it was invented by illiterate noodle sellers who needed a word for the sound. The noodles were thick, chewy, coated in chilli oil and vinegar, topped with vegetables and a bit of pork. Not delicate or subtle. Just: here’s wheat, here’s heat, here’s what keeps you upright when the plateau cold tries to put you down.

Shaanxi food is northern Chinese pragmatism rendered edible. It values sustenance over pleasure, durability over delicacy, survival over refinement.

The delta: Jiangnan’s sweet abundance

South of Shaanxi, east of Sichuan, you get Jiangnan, which sits “south of the Yangtze”, centred on Suzhou and Hangzhou – waterways, fertile land, relative peace for centuries. The scholar-official class developed here. The imperial examination system produced more successful candidates from Jiangnan than anywhere else. This is where Chinese literati culture comprising painting, poetry, gardens and aesthetic refinement grew the deepest roots.

The food reflects that. Jiangnan cuisine is subtly sweet, using sugar to balance rather than dominate. Think of xiaolongbao – soup dumplings in which the broth is delicately flavoured, not aggressively seasoned, and West Lake vinegar fish from Hangzhou – sweet-sour, restrained. This is cuisine developed by people who had time to think about aesthetics, who had enough security to value harmony and balance over intensity. When you’re not fighting for survival, you can afford elegance.

I ate xiaolongbao in a Shanghai restaurant where each dumpling was folded with 18 pleats, not because 18 is functionally necessary, but because 18 is the traditional number and tradition matters when you’re performing refinement rather than just feeding people.

Jiangnan food is what happens when prosperity lasts long enough to become culture.

The freeze: Dongbei’s preservation imperative

Finally, there’s the northeast: Manchuria, where winter lasts six months and temperatures hit minus 40 degrees Celsius (minus 40 degrees Fahrenheit), and the question isn’t “how do we keep food fresh?” but “how do we keep food at all?” The answer: you pickle. You ferment. You preserve everything possible in autumn because nothing grows between November and April.

Pickled cabbage, fermented in massive jars, stored for winter, the base of dozens of Dongbei dishes. Stews that cook all day because when it’s minus 30 degrees Celsius outside, the stove stays on anyway. Dumplings filled with pork and cabbage, boiled in vast quantities, frozen outside, retrieved as needed.

This is the cuisine of cold-climate survival. The food is heavy, repetitive, calorically dense. You’re not eating for pleasure, you’re eating to generate body heat, to maintain function, to survive until spring.

I visited Harbin in January, when everything was frozen solid. Beautiful and profoundly cold. The locals were eating hotpot at outdoor tables, wearing three layers, drinking baijiu to stay warm. The food was pure fuel. When I asked a Harbin native what defined Dongbei cuisine, she said: “We eat what doesn’t freeze.”

What the food reveals

Together, these five cuisines cover the fundamental climates and geographies that shaped how a billion people eat.

Each cuisine is a survival strategy that worked so well it became culture. Once it’s culture, wrapped in tradition and nostalgia and regional pride, it stops looking like adaptation and starts looking like identity.

Chinese food isn’t mysterious. It’s just geography rendered edible, climate written in flavour, history you can taste if you know what you’re tasting for. Every dish becomes a climate map, every regional speciality a survival footnote, every meal a reminder that culture is just successful adaptation given enough time to forget it was adaptation in the first place.

This is an exclusive essay by Lu-Ann Ong, whose forthcoming book, Experience China: What Maps Don’t Tell, explores contemporary China through landscape, cuisine, history and memory.

item.title: [SCMP] The human faces of China’s belt and road strategy, through Liu Heung-shing’s lens
item.description: It is barely light when Liu Heung-shing starts the day in his art-filled Happy Valley apartment. But then, the photojournalist has always believed in getting to the story before everyone else. Liu, 74, wakes at 5am to spend three hours reading the news, coffee in hand. Despite being “long retired”, the Pulitzer Prize-winning photographer takes note of where he might want to visit next. “There are so many ways to tell a story. Sometimes, the most difficult decision is when not to press the shutte
item.content:encoded:
It is barely light when Liu Heung-shing starts the day in his art-filled Happy Valley apartment. But then, the photojournalist has always believed in getting to the story before everyone else.

Liu, 74, wakes at 5am to spend three hours reading the news, coffee in hand. Despite being “long retired”, the Pulitzer Prize-winning photographer takes note of where he might want to visit next.

“There are so many ways to tell a story. Sometimes, the most difficult decision is when not to press the shutter, rather than when to press the shutter,” he says, while cooking and sipping a glass of Chablis. He has invited me to his home for dinner and to discuss his most recent book, Old Road New Connections: China Builds a Different World, for which his shutter charted a global odyssey following the tentacles of China’s Belt and Road Initiative. If the initiative sounds abstract in policy papers – six economic corridors, hundreds of billions in cumulative investment, more than 150 participating countries and 230 cooperation agreements – Liu’s book is an attempt to humanise that scale.

“I knew it was also a human story of Chinese going around the world, doing things, changing things and putting a uniquely Chinese stamp on different parts of the world.”

In many ways, the project is typical Liu: ambitious, self‑assigned and pursued with a mixture of stubbornness and curiosity.

“I think I’m somebody whose brain works 24 hours a day,” he laughs.

He likes to say that his books all begin with a simple editorial question: “If this is going to be one of the defining stories of the 21st century, and nobody has framed it properly yet, what can I do?”

Born in Hong Kong in 1951, Liu spent his early childhood in Fuzhou, Fujian province, with his mother, witnessing famine during the Great Leap Forward. His family eventually returned to Hong Kong.

In 1969, Liu left for New York, in the United States, where he studied politics at Hunter College. He remembers the year clearly because it was then that he saw Jimi Hendrix and Janis Joplin perform up close at the Woodstock festival.

More specific education came when his mentor, Albanian‑born photographer Gjon Mili, took him to the 28th floor of the Time & Life Building, which was where the photographers at Life magazine and other Time Inc titles were based.

“On that floor,” Liu says, “the world unfolded through photography.”

It was for Time Inc that Liu first returned to mainland China, in 1976, to photograph a nation coming to terms with the death of Mao Zedong.

As a foreign correspondent in China until the early 90s, he documented the pivotal decades that followed. Later, as the executive vice-president of News Corp China, Liu shuttled between tycoon Rupert Murdoch and party cadres.

Fundamentally, the photographer divides his understanding of modern China into three eras: China 1.0, the Maoist times he glimpsed as a child; China 2.0, launched by Deng Xiaoping’s 1978 reforms; and China 3.0, the country that is developing at breakneck speed, with the belt and road a defining initiative.

“This book is my exploration of that new world,” he writes in Old Road New Connections. “I used my camera to capture a resurgent nation spreading its wings.”

Doing so involved more than 270,000km of travel, “roughly the distance of seven times around the globe”, on multiple self-funded journeys over a three-year period. He covered five continents, 21 countries and 54 regions, spending a great deal of time in factories, mines, ports, deserts and train drivers’ cabins, as well as on the road, and that was after months of negotiation for access to the businesses, state-owned and otherwise, operating along the routes.

The resulting book turns one of this century’s most ambitious infrastructure projects into something unexpectedly intimate and personal: a portrait of a world quietly reordering at China’s direction.

“It’s capturing the contradictions sometimes, the smallest details that lend poetic meaning to life,” says Liu. “That is much more enduring than what’s in the latest news cycle.”

Following new lines of steel in Indonesia, he rode the Jakarta-Bandung high‑speed Whoosh, photographing Chinese and Indonesian operators shoulder to shoulder in the cab as Southeast Asia’s first bullet train, launched in 2023, zipped at 350km/h past villages and roads still heaving with traffic jams.

“You can show [a picture of] a high‑speed train once,” he says. “You show it twice, it’s already a repeat. After that, what interests me is who’s riding it, who’s driving it, who’s being moved and who finds their village cut in half.”

His journey through western China began on the old Silk Road: Xian’s terracotta warriors; camel caravans re‑enacted in Dunhuang. In complete contrast, he then visited the country’s first 100-megawatt molten‑salt solar thermal power station, which uses 12,000 mirrors and looks like a sci‑fi sun.

Some of Liu’s favourite photos from this project emerged along the sand ridges of the Gobi: young dancers from the Beijing Modern Dance Company performing in the desert, their body language, movement and skin tones coalescing with the sand dunes.

In Thailand, he watched workers construct concrete pillars that will one day connect Kunming, in China’s Yunnan province, to Bangkok and then Malaysia and Singapore, a rail artery replacing old Maritime Silk Road routes.

In Kazakhstan’s Aktau port, Liu slept uncomfortably “in a small converted shipping container”, he recalls, while sharing a worksite with engineers of the Citic Environmental Group, who were trying – not without problems – to hand over a water-treatment plant to a local team.

He met a couple in the old city of Kashgar, in the Xinjiang Uygur autonomous region; Hadiya Msham Abdulla from Tanzania studied at Fujian Medical University and now runs a cafe with her Uygur partner, Dilshat Tursun. As their staff made a latte for Liu, the couple proudly introduced him to their baby daughter.

“What is the most important factor in storytelling? I believe it’s trust,” he says. “I’m a humanist to the core, because I’m interested in everything. I’m interested in why this woman wants to look beautiful. I’m interested in athletes, how this man can run so fast.”

He’s also interested in the Kenyan manager at the Mombasa-Nairobi Standard Gauge Railway control centre who dispatches instructions in fluent Mandarin. In the Democratic Republic of Congo, Liu and his camera descended down a suffocating 1,150-metre-deep underground lift into the Kamoa-Kakula copper mine, which has Chinese investors. He snapped portraits of local miners, South African technicians and Angolan labourers working side by side with Chinese engineers.

Elsewhere in Africa, he encountered a Chinese lion conservationist, Xing Ba, in the Masai Mara wildlife reserve and a young Chinese engineer who has no plans to “go back” – Africa, for her, had become home.

His photographs from Iran date from just before the current conflict in the Middle East. While in the country, his interpreter asked him when more much-needed trains would be coming from China for Tehran’s metro. China helped build several of the city’s lines.

Liu captured a ladies’ Zumba class in Xinjiang; Brazilian cowboys; and a courier making AliExpress deliveries on horseback in a satellite town outside Moscow, Russia. He even peeked inside a Shanghai factory producing satellites deployed in the US-China space race.

A post-dinner snoop around his apartment finds a small framed picture of Liu’s, above his desk. It’s of Richard Nixon holding a bucket of Tsingtao beers and play-acting as a waiter on a Hangzhou-to-Shanghai train. The former US president was acting goofy on returning to China in 1982, to commemorate the 10th anniversary of the signing of the Shanghai Communiqué, which paved the way to normalising bilateral relations between China and the US.

On another wall is a picture that helped earn Liu his shared Pulitzer – as well as a punch from a KGB guard inside the Kremlin: a 1991 shot for Associated Press of Mikhail Gorbachev announcing his resignation and the dissolution of the Soviet Union. It shows the departing president sitting at a table, his shoulders slightly slumped, a blur of paper – an empire dissolving between one breath and the next.

“I was the only foreign photojournalist inside the great hall at the time,” recalls Liu. “I was really running around like crazy that day.”

His contributions to culture grew in 2015, when he founded the Shanghai Centre of Photography, which quickly became a mainstay of the West Bund art district before closing in December 2023. Running a non-profit, fundraising and training 10-plus staff proved rewarding but ultimately too taxing. He now divides his time between Hong Kong, Europe and mainland China.

With Old Road New Connections, Liu shows he is still more interested in being on the road than at the podium. Although conceived as a China 3.0 travelogue of sorts, a book on how China’s infrastructure push translates into lived experience is perhaps a logical addendum to a lifelong project of bearing witness to world-defining tipping points.

His photographs and anecdotes do not resolve into a neat verdict on the belt and road, or China’s far-spanning cross-border development. Instead, they register the speed of progress and the human ways by which capital and culture, Chinese or otherwise, flow. It’s not the panoramas of bridges, trains or ports that are most poignant, but the images of people absorbed in daily life as the world around them is being rewired.

“If the 21st century is to be China’s, as the last was America’s, I wanted to see the making of that century where it was happening, no matter how far away,” he writes.

“I’ve photographed so many people, in so many stories, in so many different countries, but I’m still interested,” he says, finishing the Chablis, “because my next picture could just be my best.”

Old Road New Connections: China Builds a Different World will be officially launched at Liu Heung-shing’s talk with Bernard Chan, chairman of the West Kowloon Cultural District Authority board, on Thursday July 16, at the 36th Hong Kong Book Fair, which runs until July 21, at the Hong Kong Convention and Exhibition Centre

item.title: [SCMP] Why the West has an uphill task challenging China in pharmaceutical ingredients
item.description: From rare earth elements to commercial drones, Washington has become all too familiar with the strongest weapons in Beijing’s manufacturing arsenal – the materials and products where China holds a strategic advantage in production, extraction or any other link in the supply chain. After those near-monopolies provided Chinese policymakers considerable leverage in last year’s trade dispute, US think tanks are asking whether pharmaceuticals represent the next critical dependency. Their concern is n
item.content:encoded:
From rare earth elements to commercial drones, Washington has become all too familiar with the strongest weapons in Beijing’s manufacturing arsenal – the materials and products where China holds a strategic advantage in production, extraction or any other link in the supply chain.

After those near-monopolies provided Chinese policymakers considerable leverage in last year’s trade dispute, US think tanks are asking whether pharmaceuticals represent the next critical dependency.

Their concern is not over the finished pills and capsules lining pharmacy shelves, but the upstream pharmaceutical ingredients, where petroleum-derived materials and biological feedstocks are transformed into the chemical building blocks of modern medicine. There, China has quietly built a strong foothold.

China now supplies 40 per cent of global active pharmaceutical ingredients (APIs) – the core chemicals that give medications their therapeutic effect, according to Chinese government data.

Further upstream, nearly 41 per cent of the key starting materials (KSMs) – basic chemical inputs for APIs – for drugs approved for use in the United States are sourced exclusively from China, according to the Washington-based Brookings Institution.

Those figures have unsettled policy circles in Washington. The Council on Foreign Relations, an American think tank, warned in a report last month that dependence on Chinese pharmaceutical ingredients could provide Beijing with leverage comparable to its dominance in rare earths.

In November, the Atlantic Council, another US think tank, urged the country and its partners, including the European Union, India and Mexico, to work together to strengthen medical trade and supply chain resilience and reduce reliance on China.

Despite these growing entreaties, overcoming China’s edge in pharmaceutical ingredients may prove difficult. Analysts said Beijing’s advantage was rooted not only in decades of industrial scaling and low costs, but also in a dense, deeply integrated manufacturing ecosystem and continuous policy guidance.

“Market forces alone are unlikely to rebuild these supply chains quickly, because China’s scale advantages are so entrenched,” said Lizzi Lee, a Chinese economy fellow at the Asia Society Policy Institute’s Centre for China Analysis.

“Rebuilding them requires a lot of deliberate policy support,” she said, pointing to procurement guarantees, regulatory coordination, subsidies, tax incentives and investment in chemical and advanced manufacturing capacity.

API manufacturing is fundamentally a chemical industry

Zhao Heng, Latitude Health

Building an edge

However, this level of structural capacity takes generations to build. China was a latecomer to the game, only manufacturing its first batch of domestic penicillin in the 1950s – a time when the US still held technological and manufacturing edges in APIs. But by the late 20th century, shrinking margins and rising regulatory costs in the West triggered successive waves of relocation, with production first shifting to Europe and eventually to the emerging pharmaceutical hubs of China and India.

In 1992, European manufacturers supplied 75 per cent of US antibiotic API imports, but by 2024, China was supplying 70 per cent and India 2 per cent, according to a study led by Mariana Socal at Johns Hopkins University published in October.

The manufacture of APIs for established drugs did not depend on scientific breakthroughs, said Zhao Heng, the founder of Shanghai-based healthcare consultancy Latitude Health, because there was limited room for innovation in the ingredients themselves.

“API manufacturing is fundamentally a chemical industry,” Zhao said, adding that competition centred instead on refining production processes to improve speed, capacity and cost efficiency – precisely where China’s advantage was most evident, even compared to India, which has been dubbed the “pharmacy of the world”.

Chinese API manufacturers enjoy an estimated 20 per cent cost advantage over their Indian counterparts, according to a China Securities report released two years ago, largely driven by cheaper upstream chemical and biological inputs. It said raw materials accounted for roughly two-thirds of total production costs, leaving Indian producers at a significant disadvantage.

Even though labour costs in India were roughly half those in China, higher productivity offset much of that advantage. The brokerage estimated that on a per-worker basis, output in China was about 1.5 times greater than in India, making overall labour cost differences relatively modest.

Consequently, India – which supplies 61 per cent of the generic drugs in the US by volume, based on Brookings data – has become a major buyer of Chinese pharmaceutical ingredients. It imports about 70 per cent of its ingredients from China “as it’s a cheaper option than manufacturing them domestically”, according to a report by India’s Ministry of Chemicals and Fertilisers released three years ago.

India bought US$6.26 billion worth of pharmaceutical ingredients from China last year, remaining the largest export destination, followed by the US, according to data from the China Chamber of Commerce for Import and Export of Medicines and Health Products.

China’s total exports of APIs and upper-stream intermediates last year were worth US$42.9 billion, almost double the amount in 2016.

It now has a tight grip on the ingredients for everyday bulk drugs in the US, controlling the KSMs for 94 per cent of amoxicillin, 74 per cent of heparin, and 70 per cent of acetaminophen, according to the Council on Foreign Relations.

Climbing the ladder

However, China’s rapid rise to dominance in pharmaceutical ingredients has been accompanied by significant environmental costs.

In 2013, China Youth Daily reported that two major pharmaceutical ingredient manufacturers in northwestern China’s Ningxia Hui autonomous region – both significant contributors to the local tax base – had caused severe air pollution, forcing some residents to wear masks at home due to strong odours, and had contaminated waterways linked to the Yellow River.

Public concerns following such reports prompted tighter environmental regulations. In 2015, Beijing introduced a strict new environmental protection law, with fines for illegal discharges of chemical waste that accumulated by the day – with no upper limit. It also mandated that environmental violations be recorded in corporate credit files, affecting access to financing.

In 2021, Beijing issued guidelines to encourage the sustainable development of the pharmaceutical ingredients industry, calling for upgrades in green production technologies and faster development of specialty and high-end customised pharmaceutical ingredients.

The regulatory push accelerated a broader shift in the industry, prompting many pharmaceutical ingredient producers to move up the value chain towards higher-margin segments, coinciding with the rise of China’s biotech sector and increasingly limited growth potential for bulk drug ingredients.

“China has repositioned itself upstream as a source of innovation, and that is what is pulling the entire API value chain into higher-value territory,” said Diederik Stadig, healthcare and technology economist at ING Research.

He said that beyond their long-standing strength in APIs for oncology drugs, Chinese companies were increasingly producing ingredients for next-generation therapies, including antibody-drug conjugates (ADCs), bispecific antibodies, GLP-1-based obesity drugs, and cell and gene therapies.

Pharmaceutical data provider PharmCube said Chinese API suppliers for GLP-1 drugs had expanded rapidly since 2022, shifting the balance in what had been a Europe-centred production network.

In 2022, there were only four Chinese suppliers for the API of semaglutide. Two years later, that number had surged to 33, accounting for roughly 80 per cent of global production.

That structural change is also evident at the company level. In its 2025 annual report, Zhejiang-based Huahai Pharmaceutical said intense competition and price wars among traditional pharmaceutical ingredient producers – driven by industry-wide homogenisation – had slowed growth momentum and continued to put downward pressure on prices.

The company, a leading Chinese supplier of ingredients for commodity drugs and a growing player in high-end generic and innovative medicines, said market demand was “rapidly shifting from traditional bulk APIs towards higher-value specialty APIs”.

Stadig said the key drivers behind China’s movement up the API value chain were heavy investment in research, a deepening scientific talent pool and a rapid increase in out-licensing to Western pharmaceutical firms, which had further integrated Chinese companies into global pharmaceutical innovation and manufacturing value chains.

He said that according to ING estimates, around 33 per cent of new pharmaceutical molecules now originated in China, compared with roughly 4 per cent in 2014. He added that the value of cross-border licensing deals involving Chinese companies was expected to reach US$250 billion this year, up from US$3 billion a decade ago.

A report by Guosen Securities in May said the average research and development investment of China’s A-share-listed API manufacturers last year was 5.86 billion yuan (US$863.3 million), up 21 per cent from 4.84 billion yuan in 2023.

Lee, from the Centre for China Analysis, said the country’s dense pharmaceutical ecosystem had provided a strong foundation for its move up the API value chain.

“API producers are not operating in isolation,” she said. “They sit inside clusters that connect research institutions, hospitals, clinical trial networks, companies that provide contract development and manufacturing services, regulators and so on.”

Such proximity mattered a lot, Lee said, because higher-value APIs required fast iteration, strong process know-how, and close coordination with drug developers.

China is the fastest place to scale up complex drug production

Tom Hancock, Gavekal Technologies

Transpacific entanglement

The speed of execution that such an ecosystem enables was evident during the global shortage of weight-loss drugs in 2024. WuXi AppTec, China’s leading provider of contract pharmaceutical research, built and staffed a new API production facility for US drug giant Eli Lilly within a year – far faster than would typically be possible in the US, according to a note by Gavekal Technologies’ analyst Tom Hancock that was issued in April.

“China is the fastest place to scale up complex drug production,” Hancock said, adding that capability was what “Chinese peptide manufacturers offer that cannot be beaten elsewhere”.

Despite the concerns in the West, he said the interdependence between China and the US meant that China’s significant position in the global pharmaceutical supply chain did not necessarily equate to the potential for weaponisation.

“As Chinese contract manufacturers’ ability to produce complex molecules grew, so did their reliance on imported equipment and inputs,” Hancock said.

He added that China also had a significant reliance on imported patented drugs. The value of China’s biomedicine imports hit US$16 billion last year, up 33 per cent from 2019, driven by cancer immunotherapies – the main US pharmaceutical export to China.

“In the short run, US-China pharma trade faces headwinds,” he said, pointing to policies such as America’s 100 per cent tariffs on patented pharmaceuticals and associated pharmaceutical ingredients.

“But such moves will not eliminate bilateral trade or mutual dependency: they will simply prompt pushback and workarounds,” Hancock said. “The complementarity of the US and Chinese pharmaceutical industries is growing, and neither side can live without the other.”

item.title: [SCMP] Tuscan, Korean and Cantonese: 3 chefs reimagine classic beef dishes in Hong Kong
item.description: It’s the same animal. In a Tuscan kitchen, it’s carved tableside as a ritual. In a Korean kitchen, it’s sliced thin and gone in seconds. And in a Cantonese kitchen, it’s been braising since morning. In Hong Kong, three chefs are bringing their love of beef from home, each cooking through the lens of a childhood tradition that shaped them long before they ever stepped into a professional kitchen. Here is how to eat beef well, because whether in Tuscany, Seoul, or Hong Kong, the rule is simple: wh
item.content:encoded:
It’s the same animal. In a Tuscan kitchen, it’s carved tableside as a ritual. In a Korean kitchen, it’s sliced thin and gone in seconds. And in a Cantonese kitchen, it’s been braising since morning.

In Hong Kong, three chefs are bringing their love of beef from home, each cooking through the lens of a childhood tradition that shaped them long before they ever stepped into a professional kitchen. Here is how to eat beef well, because whether in Tuscany, Seoul, or Hong Kong, the rule is simple: when it comes to beef, do as the locals do.

Tuscan at Carna by Dario Cecchini

Dario Cecchini is a proud Tuscan – and when an eighth-generation butcher from Panzano in Chianti tells you how to eat beef, you listen.

For Cecchini, the Bistecca alla Fiorentina is the centrepiece of Italian beef. The steak arrives whole, close to 2kg, still shimmering from the grill. It is 4cm thick, which allows the surface to develop a deep, mahogany crust while the inside stays rare and cool. What you get in a single bite is a series of contrasts – char and blood, fat and lean, smoke and minerality. Cecchini calls this “a conversation of textures”.

He brings that conversation to Mondrian Hong Kong, where his eponymous Carna by Dario Cecchini serves the same Bistecca by the same rule: never season the steak before it touches fire. Salt and Tuscan olive oil are applied only after cooking. Salt draws moisture to the surface, which then steams rather than sears, robbing the crust of its depth. But the rule runs deeper than food science. “You are saying to the steak, you are already complete,” Cecchini says. “I am only here to finish your essence.”

If you want a deeper cut, Cecchini recommends the spider steak. Known in Italian as ragnolino, it is a small, humble cut from the inner thigh. A lean and muscular cut, it is often overlooked in favour of more famous names. Grilled simply on the bone and dressed with extra virgin Tuscan olive oil and Cecchini’s own herb salt, Profumo del Chianti, it proves his philosophy better than any sermon could: no cut is tough if treated with care. It is only ever waiting for the right hands.

That philosophy traces back to cucina povera – the cooking of the poor – that has shaped Tuscan tradition for centuries. Cecchini’s grandmother made one of his favourite dishes from boiled beef knees – Tenerumi in Insalata. “Those memories shaped me,” he says. “It is knowledge passed down through generations, and what we are doing is keeping that conversation alive.”

On Carna’s menu, each muscle is named: the scamone, the costata, the cappello del prete. Diners can look at an illustration of the cow marked with numbers and pick the part they want to try. “It is an act of respect,” Cecchini says. “To the animal that gave its life, to the farmer who raised it, and to the guest, whom we trust to care about where their food comes from.”

Korean at Moo-Lah by Born and Bred

The grill is already hot. Thin slices of beef hit the metal and curl at the edges within seconds, smoke rising as chopsticks dart across the table. The meat goes from raw to perfect in the time it takes to grab a spoonful of rice. If Tuscany celebrates beef with stately reverence, this is the Korean rhythm, where speed matters.

For Jung Sang-won, co-founder of Born & Bred in Seoul and Moo-Lah in Hong Kong, the etiquette of the grill is one he learned young. He remembers eating tteok-galbi patties made from rib finger meat as a child: the meat’s aroma slowly grilling, the texture tender yet structured, and the way the fat caught the edge of the fire.

“For me, this memory is not just about food,” Jung says. “It was my first deep experience of understanding texture and flavour in beef.”

That memory now travels to Hong Kong, where Jung is developing a wonton inspired by tteok-galbi, using hand-chopped Hanwoo finger meat to intentionally preserve structure and bite.

Hanwoo, Korea’s native cattle, is at the centre of Jung’s kitchen. But it is more than just a breed of cow; it is “the result of farming environments, the philosophy of producers, and long-developed rearing practices”, the chef says.

Historically, cattle in Korea were not just livestock – they were partners in farming. Because of this, Korean beef culture developed around the idea of using the whole animal rather than focusing only on the premium cuts. The Korean saying, Il-dubaekmi, literally translates to “one cow, one hundred flavours”. While Western butchery divides a cow into about 10 primary cuts, Korean butchers can break a cow down into over 120 distinct parts.

Ask Jung to name one cut most non-Koreans may have never heard of, and his answer is immediate: jebichuri. Jebichuri (neck chain) comes from the area between the neck and chest, a heavily used muscle that develops a deep flavour and a distinctive chew. “Many people assume that highly marbled cuts define quality,” Jung says. “But once they experience jebichuri, they realise that the beef’s flavour is not defined only by fat content.”

Because it is lean, jebichuri demands a fast hand and tastes best when thinly sliced and quickly grilled over high heat, allowing the surface to caramelise fast while the inside stays juicy. It is the perfect expression of the Korean preference for quick cooking, tied to communal dining. Meat is shared at the table, cooked together and never eaten in isolation. “Korean food culture is deeply tied to rice,” Jung explains. “Thin slicing allows for the ideal balance of temperature and texture when eaten with rice.”

Cantonese at Duddell’s

The pot starts cooking long before service. Inside are beef brisket tendon and white radish, submerged and simmering; the liquid barely moving as the hours pass. You’ll know it’s ready when the meat yields and absorbs the sweetness of the radish. As much as the brisket is the star, time is the ingredient that cannot be rushed. And for chef Chan Yau-leung of Duddell’s, this patience is the foundation of his cooking.

Chan grew up in Hong Kong in the 1960s, in modest circumstances. Beef brisket and tendon (ngau gan lam) noodles were a rare luxury reserved for festivals or birthdays. He still remembers the weight of those bowls. “It left an indelible impression,” he says.

He holds ngau gan lam to an exceptionally high standard because he knows what it meant to him and others like him even before he stepped into a professional kitchen. The tendons must be thick and unctuous. The brisket must have a robust beef flavour and be soft and aromatic – elevated into what he calls the signature “radish brisket” profile that defines comfort in Cantonese cuisine.

The preparation demands patience, and the cut must be subjected to slow, gentle heat. This is where Chan sees home cooks go wrong. Cuts that require time, like brisket, are rushed, while delicate cuts that need a flash of heat, like rib-eye or Wagyu, are overcooked. “The key is to match the cooking time and method precisely to the cut’s characteristics,” he says.

Chan has honed this precision in the kitchen for more than four decades. At Duddell’s, his Spicy Mala Beef Shank is marinated, boiled for two to three hours, then infused again with Sichuan peppercorns and chilli for depth. He takes a different approach to the tender rib-eye, choosing to mince and steam it with 20-year-old tangerine peel for his steamed minced beef patty.

His own favourite cut is one that rarely appears in the spotlight: ngau lei sam (ox tongue heart). It is also the most challenging to cook properly, according to the chef. “The texture varies significantly throughout the muscle,” Chan says. “Some parts are soft while others are tough. It requires immense precision, care, and experience to handle correctly.” To honour it, he slowly simmers the tongue to infuse it with flavour, then isolates only the tenderest core, preparing it with port wine and vine tomatoes. Time does the rest.

item.title: [SCMP] Why a Chinese academic claim over Batanes has unsettled the Philippines
item.description: A university symposium in Guangzhou has placed the Philippines’ northernmost province of Batanes at the centre of a new argument over history, geography and sovereignty, after scholars at the event claimed the islands belonged to Taiwan and, on that basis, fell under overall Chinese sovereignty. The claim, made at a June 30 symposium hosted by Jinan University in Guangzhou, has been rejected by Philippine historians and officials, who dispute the scholars’ reading of geography, dynastic administ
item.content:encoded:
A university symposium in Guangzhou has placed the Philippines’ northernmost province of Batanes at the centre of a new argument over history, geography and sovereignty, after scholars at the event claimed the islands belonged to Taiwan and, on that basis, fell under overall Chinese sovereignty.

The claim, made at a June 30 symposium hosted by Jinan University in Guangzhou, has been rejected by Philippine historians and officials, who dispute the scholars’ reading of geography, dynastic administration, cultural links and post-war treaties.

Beijing sees Taiwan as part of China, to be reunited by force if necessary. Most countries, including the United States, do not recognise Taiwan as an independent state, but Washington opposes any attempt to take the self-governed island by force and is committed to supplying it with weapons.

Beijing has not formally adopted the scholars’ position on Batanes, but the discussion has fuelled concern in Manila that an apparently academic argument could be used to test or shape a wider narrative around the islands, which sit near one of the most important waterways between the South China Sea and the Pacific.

The Guangzhou symposium was convened in reaction to a May 28 joint statement by the Philippines and Japan that the two would negotiate to delimit their exclusive economic zones and continental shelves, including areas east of Taiwan also claimed by mainland China.

Symposium participants said the Philippines-Japan agreement infringed on China’s sovereignty, and that the Batanes islands belonged to China.

“The islands are not merely closer to Taiwan in distance, but constitute a natural geographical extension of Taiwan,” said Ju Hailong, dean of the School of International Studies at Jinan University.

In a statement issued on Friday rejecting the symposium’s claims, the National Historical Commission of the Philippines challenged that geographical argument, saying “current satellite and oceanographic data by the Philippines and other nations show a continuous shelf extending from northern Luzon through the Babuyan and Batanes Islands, and into parts of the Taiwan archipelago”.

It said “the Philippines claims a greater right over the subject territories from this perspective”.

Ju had claimed that during the Ming and Qing dynasties, the islands were under the jurisdiction of the Taiwan prefecture.

But the commission said “the earliest known extensive documentation of the Batanes Islands by British explorer William Dampier in 1687 noted no trace of Chinese governance over the islands”.

It noted that “since 1783, the Philippine government and its legal predecessors have held continuous and undisputed sovereignty over Batanes”.

The symposium also claimed the Ivatan residents of Batanes could be linguistically and culturally linked with Taiwan’s Tao people, with heritage originating from mainland China.

The commission noted instead that the Ivatan people “lived in protected communities and traded with other maritime trading nations”.

According to the Chinese scholars, post-World War II treaties required Japan to return all territories it had seized from China and, because they regarded Batanes as part of Taiwan, the islands should have been included.

The commission rejected that premise, saying the islands could not be returned because “Japan cannot give to China what clearly belongs to the Philippines”.

In its statement, the commission rejected the Chinese scholars’ assertions, saying they had “no rational basis in substantive research and operate from evident bad faith. They would never stand under scrutiny in academic discourse outside of the People’s Republic of China.”

Philippine foreign affairs department acting spokeswoman Analyn Ratonel said on Friday: “Flights of fancy should not be dignified with a response. Nevertheless, the Philippines’ sovereignty over Batanes is settled and not up for debate.”

She pointed out that when Manila approved a consular district of the Chinese consulate general in the northern city of Laoag, the Chinese request explicitly included Batanes.

Beyond academia?

Some Philippine officials and analysts said the symposium should not be viewed only as an academic event, warning that it could help test or shape public perceptions around Batanes.

Renato de Castro, a distinguished professor in the international studies department of De La Salle University Manila who once taught at Jinan University in 2007, said the symposium was unlikely to have taken place without political space from Beijing, arguing that “this is probably a prelude to a possible offensive in terms of psychological warfare”.

Speaking in a news forum on Saturday, retired rear admiral Roy Vincent Trinidad, spokesman for Philippine operations in the South China Sea, described what was happening as “salami-slicing in the information domain”.

He said it could begin with a simple statement before moving towards a point where “it’s officially adopted by the government”.

Former congressman Robert Ace Barbers, who once offered his province of Surigao del Norte to host US troops, told This Week in Asia that claiming Batanes “is another part of bullying, this is what we call creeping invasion”.

Raymond Powell, director of SeaLight, a maritime transparency project of Stanford University’s Gordian Knot Centre, cautioned during the news forum on Saturday that dismissing such claims would be “playing directly into Beijing’s hand”, as they served a broader purpose.

“The Chinese government needs the ‘possibility’ that it owns Batanes to justify” its actions in waters claimed by the Philippines, Powell said in a social media post on Monday, adding that Beijing “isn’t ready to say it directly” when it came to Batanes, therefore it “left it to academics”.

Philippine Defence Secretary Gilberto Teodoro called the Chinese scholars’ assertions a “quasi-state statement”.

The concern is sharpened by Batanes’ location near Taiwan and the Luzon Strait, a strategic corridor between the South China Sea and the Pacific.

Batanes comprises 10 islands, with the northernmost one, Mavulis, a mere 142km southeast of Taiwan. It is being built up by the Philippine military as part of its Comprehensive Archipelagic Defence Concept, which refocuses the country’s security strategies to include defence of its exclusive economic zone.

Teodoro has described the islands as “the spearhead of the Philippines as far as the northern baseline is concerned”.

In 2024, a Naval Forward Operating Base was constructed on the main island of Batan, while structures were built in Mavulis to house members of the Naval Forces Northern Luzon, a unit dedicated to defending the area.

Military drills have also taken place there in the two recent Balikatan, or shoulder-to-shoulder, exercises with the US and other nations, including Philippine and US marines rappelling from Black Hawk helicopters to the remote Batanes town of Itbayat.

Dr Ricardo Jose, an emeritus professor of history specialising in World War II, told This Week in Asia in 2024 that Batanes was strategically important in any conflict involving Taiwan.

Japanese forces had seized the airfield in Basco, Batanes, “because from Taiwan, Basco is almost halfway between Taiwan and the northern Luzon tip”.

De Castro said in a forum by think tank Stratbase on Friday that “the northern Philippines is key terrain – the control of which will give you a marked advantage over your adversary.”

item.title: [SCMP] Why and how the media industry can fit into Hong Kong’s first 5-year plan
item.description: Mainstream media, particularly in Hong Kong, have historically been conservative in their research and development spending. Media executives prefer to shop for off-the-shelf solutions, treating technology as back-office support rather than a core growth engine. This mode of operation now faces an existential challenge amid the explosive growth of artificial intelligence (AI), large language models and Web3 technologies. Concurrently, these developments offer Hong Kong’s media industry fresh opp
item.content:encoded:
Mainstream media, particularly in Hong Kong, have historically been conservative in their research and development spending. Media executives prefer to shop for off-the-shelf solutions, treating technology as back-office support rather than a core growth engine.

This mode of operation now faces an existential challenge amid the explosive growth of artificial intelligence (AI), large language models and Web3 technologies. Concurrently, these developments offer Hong Kong’s media industry fresh opportunities, particularly as such innovations have been identified as key components of China’s 15th five-year plan and, by extension, a focus of Hong Kong’s alignment with the national strategy.

One of the bottlenecks in AI development is the lack of high-quality, verified data that encompasses local culture and nuances. Professional journalism, in-depth reports and historical archives held by traditional media can be a gold mine for good model training.

International publishers have already pivoted to monetise these assets. News Corp recently signed a deal worth a reported US$250 million over five years with OpenAI. The Associated Press, Reuters and a growing list of publishers are building recurring revenue from content they already own.

Hong Kong has remained largely an idle observer. This passivity risks more than missed financial opportunities. It brings security and cultural concerns. As younger generations increasingly rely on AI models to synthesise information and understand the world, the data used to train these models becomes the new frontier of the global narrative war. If local media companies do not actively invest in processing, protecting and commercialising their data, the digital narrative of Hong Kong will be written by external models trained on unverified or biased sources.

Addressing this requires a fundamental shift from both the industry and the government. The Hong Kong government encourages AI adoption in traditional sectors through programmes such as the Enterprise Support Scheme (ESS) under the Innovation and Technology Fund (ITF). Yet as of March 2024, the ESS Assessment Panel had approved 220 projects out of 762 applications. The beneficiaries were almost exclusively deep-tech start-ups, biomedical firms, microelectronic companies and software-as-a-service developers. Not a single media outlet was among them.

Industry inertia is the main reason. Hong Kong media outlets traditionally invest in tech applications rather than genuine innovation, rarely designing proprietary tools with the intention of spinning them off as commercial software products for global markets. Consequently, media applications struggle to prove commercial product viability under strict innovation and technology (I&T) definitions.

However, there is also the issue of bureaucratic silos. For decades, Hong Kong’s media and creative industries have been isolated in a different administrative ecosystem from tech start-ups. Media is grouped with film, sports and entertainment under bureaus that prioritise artistic creativity, cultural impact and audience reach, while the ITF focuses on hard tech.

This division is outdated. Media operations – including speech-to-text, real-time multilingual translation, automated video generation, smart content recommendation and blockchain-based copyright protection – offer the most natural, high-density sandbox for developing and testing advanced AI technologies. With the right policy support, the media industry can branch out beyond the narrowly defined “culture industry” into the media-tech sector. Nurturing such a new industry would broaden professional horizons for local tech graduates, who currently face limited options.

For this transformation to occur, we must dismantle institutional barriers. We could consider classifying media-tech, generative AI content and digital language infrastructure as strategic technology industries. This reclassification would grant media organisations legal and administrative parity when applying for tech funding and resources, effectively moving them from the periphery of the cultural sector to the core of the I&T ecosystem.

For instance, Hong Kong currently offers a 300 per cent tax deduction for R&D expenditure, but media companies are excluded because content creation is not recognised as technical R&D. Policymakers should explicitly clarify that capital spent by media companies on developing proprietary software, algorithm engineering and data cleaning for AI training qualifies for these deductions.

Even more critically, the government must establish a modern legal and technical framework to protect local intellectual property from predatory mining. Hong Kong is updating its copyright laws by introducing a text and data mining exception into the Copyright Ordinance. While this amendment aims to allow AI developers to use copyrighted data for training without prior permission under specific conditions, it must include stringent safeguards, such as legal protections for paywalls and mandate clear opt-out mechanisms for local media outlets.

Because most traditional media companies lack the technical staff to build sophisticated digital walls against global AI scrapers, the government’s Digital Policy Office should step in. The DPO can help build and deploy practical shared infrastructure to explicitly signal to global web crawlers that Hong Kong media archives are protected by copyright.

This regulation is not an invitation for the media industry to become free riders. Rather, it corrects a massive market failure where small local content creators face global tech monopolies. In this struggle between David and Goliath, the government cannot be just a passive referee.

Only by establishing a regulated and fair market can the industry stand a chance of investing in and commercialising data for AI development. Traditional media cannot fund multimillion-dollar research on declining advertising revenues. They need structured government matching and equity investments for these changes to take place. We do not need to invent new funds; rather, we should loosen bureaucratic constraints to make existing ones accessible to the media sector. Over time, the government can champion locally developed media-tech by becoming the “first customer” that adopts tools such as public sentiment analysis and multilingual content creation.

By treating media-tech with the same financial and policy weight currently afforded to fintech or biotech, the Hong Kong government can convert a legacy industry into a vibrant node of new quality productive forces. This strategy would preserve local journalism while aggressively expanding Hong Kong’s digital cultural footprint under the 15th five-year plan.

In the age of AI, impactful storytelling requires technological leverage. For Hong Kong to cement its role as China’s international centre for technological and information innovation over the next decade, a vibrant media industry is critical.

item.title: [SCMP] What happens when Indonesia’s top corruption buster is suspected of graft himself?
item.description: Rows of gold bars, stacks of US and Singapore dollars and seven suitcases found inside a locked safe have become the defining images of one of the biggest scandals to hit Indonesia’s law-enforcement establishment in years. Police said they recovered 74kg (160lbs) of gold, plus more than US$15 million in multiple currencies from a house linked to Febrie Adriansyah, who until last week served as Indonesia’s deputy attorney general for special crimes, and is now suspected of corruption. Authorities
item.content:encoded:
Rows of gold bars, stacks of US and Singapore dollars and seven suitcases found inside a locked safe have become the defining images of one of the biggest scandals to hit Indonesia’s law-enforcement establishment in years.

Police said they recovered 74kg (160lbs) of gold, plus more than US$15 million in multiple currencies from a house linked to Febrie Adriansyah, who until last week served as Indonesia’s deputy attorney general for special crimes, and is now suspected of corruption.

Authorities estimate the bullion and cash found at the Sentul residence in Bogor, southeast of Jakarta, are worth about 476 billion rupiah (US$26 million). Images of neatly arranged gold bars and bundles of banknotes have featured prominently across Indonesian television and news websites since the raids.

The scandal raises serious questions for Indonesia, where the rupiah tumbled 10 per cent in the past year to become Asia’s worst-performing currency, over concerns about institutional integrity and policymaking. A big question is whether the discovery of the riches shows that safeguards against corruption are working, or whether a succession of scandals indicates corruption is worsening.
