I think he would like to do that again – a big, grand meeting that Trump will think of as a historic event, that showcases he’s able to do negotiations and deals with China in a way that other presidents haven’t been able to do. I think he would want to push quite hard in that direction. That’s my guess.

Are the two presidents likely to get along?

I think that’s perfectly possible. You might remember that when Trump invited Xi to Mar-a-Lago, he said that he gave Xi a big piece of chocolate cake. He never talked about giving chocolate cake to German chancellor Angela Merkel.

That’s a sign that he feels there’s some sort of ability to speak leader to leader in that sense. Xi has said less about Trump, but he hasn’t said any very publicly hostile things personally about him.

Will it stop competition? No. Will it stop conflict? Maybe. In some areas – I would single out technology – I think the US and China are going to be increasingly in competition and there will be less sharing of intellectual property or scientific development.

That would mean other countries could benefit from both, or they may have to choose a side. This will not change because of a meeting.

But can the two leaders talk about lowering the temperature and avoiding misunderstandings that could lead to military confrontation? That might be more possible.

Turning to China’s security environment, how might Beijing’s relations with its neighbours change, given the uncertainties of Washington’s policies during the Trump administration?

My guess is that China will be assessing how solid and secure American security guarantees are in Asia. There’s some evidence that the United States might want to maintain and maybe deepen its relationship with standing allies Japan, Australia and South Korea.

We know that Trump is interested in the Korean peninsula. In his first term, he wanted to do personal diplomacy with North Korean leader Kim Jong-un. But even when South Korea is going through political change and a lot of turmoil, there’s no sense that I’ve seen that the United States is stepping back from its relationship.

I would say that if you are looking from Beijing, the conclusion to draw at the moment is that things will change much less in Asia than in Europe.

Supposing American security guarantees, naval presence and alliances were withdrawn from Asia – the first thing that would be likely to happen is that Japan and South Korea would think hard about getting their own nuclear weapons. They have the capacity, science and the technology. They could do it, but they won’t do it at the moment.

in a strange way, Beijing’s best bet for keeping the region relatively nuclear-free is to encourage the Americans to stay. And most of its neighbours would share that analysis.

They want the Chinese market, but they also want American security. They don’t want either one to disappear. I think that’s what makes the calculation different in Asia and that will affect the way that China and the US deal with other countries in the region.

What about the South China Sea?

The South China Sea is probably one of the most potentially dangerous flashpoints, along with Taiwan.

The South China Sea has more of a chance of running out of control because it’s not just between two or three entities – seven or eight are involved. The US is not so directly involved in that.

But I would say that China is missing a big soft power opportunity, which it could take up at a time of uncertainty about American security intentions, of being able to provide reassurance to regional neighbours like the Philippines.

If China is not looking to increase its maritime and territorial claims, it could create a much better basis of trust in the region. But right now there’s much more military activity from China, not just near the Philippines but also off the coast of Australia, and that’s heightening tensions in the region.

How is the importance of Taiwan to Beijing shaping the security environment?

One of the most interesting things is that after repeatedly trying to take the island, Mao Zedong basically said to then-US president Richard Nixon, when they met in Beijing in 1972, that the Taiwan problem could be left to the side for 100 years.

That’s a reminder that actually the Taiwan question has not been a constant one at the forefront of China’s relationship with the world.

Right now, there are a whole variety of reasons that China needs to think about its changing place in the world. It has an opportunity to influence the world order at a time when the United States is changing its role.

It also has significant economic problems at home, in terms of creating new forms of employment, dealing with the way technology has changed labour markets, and stimulating domestic consumption, which the party has repeatedly said is a really important domestic priority.

In that context, you could say that with all those important problems, Taiwan doesn’t appear as a major solution or issue that will help to resolve any of them.

If you look at Chinese policy priorities, it’s very clear that the single most important task for the government is the economy.

China needs the Asia-Pacific region to be economically productive to get its economy going again. That means the attention has to be on the domestic economy and how it relates to a peaceful, calm Asia that does not have any sort of confrontation.

In five to 10 years, if current trends continue, the Asia-Pacific region could be even more of a driver of the global economy than it is now. It can become the most economically dynamic region in the world, with lots of foreign direct investment and expansion.

However, for that vision of Asia to emerge, the region needs to stay peaceful and stable and absolutely avoid wars. Any regional confrontations could put that economic future in danger and that would be very bad for China and its neighbours.

Alongside the nationalism that comes with the promise of building a powerful China, there is also a revival of traditional Chinese culture in both society and the official narrative. How do you think this will shape China’s governance and foreign policy?

This is one of the most interesting and important elements of what’s going on in thought and ideology in China today.

First of all, there is a real interest in what some people call traditional thought, or pre-modern thought, which includes the Confucian tradition but also lots of other areas, such as Taoism.

The Chinese government has been sponsoring publication of China’s canon of official writings, with the parts that suit contemporary politics tending to be stressed.

For instance, Confucian thinking does have an interest in proper hierarchy and this is something that is obviously very attractive to the Communist Party, because it wants people, essentially, to be stable and ordered.

But there are other ideas – such as individual self-development or self-cultivation, which could lead more to the idea that people should have individual rights – that are less popular with the party.

China’s political culture since 1949 has been heavily shaped by the Marxism and Leninism that underpins the Chinese Communist Party.

Many of the ideas are still there and you can hear them in the language of Xi and other top leaders. Even while they are praising traditional Chinese culture and wisdom, they are also pointing out that Marxism and Leninism still have significance.

How are these two things compatible? Actually, it’s not easy.

There was a TV show broadcast in Hunan province that got a lot of criticism on Chinese social media. It was called When Marx Met Confucius and was a way of trying to talk about the difficulties in combining these ideas.

There is actually quite an interesting and difficult tension in the way that these two thought systems operate in China today, but I think that they are both very much part of the debate.

How has the Chinese intellectual landscape changed in the past 10 years? Do you share the view that it can be summarised as a rise in nationalism, Sinification of ideas and declining critical voices? What other trends are you seeing and what are their effects?

In some ways, China’s intellectual sphere has narrowed, at least in public, but in other important ways, it has expanded.

The narrowing is real, and probably the biggest obstacle that China places in its own path to developing new ideas at home and being able to exert influence abroad. It’s difficult to persuade the wider world that you’re a force for new thinking if new thinking at home is heavily restricted.

In terms of subjects, including history, most people would say that the room for publication and discussion is much more limited than it was 10, 15 or 20 years ago. Many aspects of modern history are harder to research and write about in China today than in the past.

The most productive recent time intellectually in China was the late 1990s and early 2000s, when there was the most capacity to be able to speak more freely. That time will need to come again as China comes to grips with its new role in the world. It’s not possible to have a very narrowed intellectual sphere and a very wide influence.

But there are some areas where I think things are broader, and I’m particularly thinking about social media and the way it has expanded and grown so significantly in China in the past decade.

Lots of ideas actually are discussed at a popular level, even when there’s censorship a lot of ideas still get out there – not about top-level politics as that’s too sensitive to permit – but if you think about things like concerns about economic change, people’s concerns about their own financial situation, ideas about the position of women, society, feminism, those are quite visible in Chinese social media.

That is not the same thing as two or three scholars sitting in a think tank writing some big paper, but in terms of understanding social trends and how they are shaping China today, social media has been very valuable to reflect those changes.

How has the China studies community been affected by the changes of recent years? What will the subject look like, with a growing percentage of scholars outside the country unable to conduct interviews and on-the-ground research in China?

You have put your finger on one of the most problematic issues – the number of students studying Chinese languages and doing higher-level research in the West on China is going down significantly. It was never very high, but it was going up, and now the numbers are shrinking.

One reason is the sense that doing serious research in China – particularly for social scientists but also for researchers who need to use archives and libraries – is much more difficult than it used to be.

What real China researchers need is to be able to travel and set up their own agendas, to be able to do interviews at the local level that enable them to understand how local government works, or to collect large amounts of data that enables them to see how effective or ineffective particular policies are.

This is the work of real social science, and it involves transparency and openness on data and a willingness to welcome the gathering of data, rather than assuming that it has to be banned for security reasons.

So, although more scholars are coming back to China, opening up more capacity to be able to do real research in China is the next barrier that needs to be overcome.

There are a lot of brilliant Chinese students in many countries in the West who are doing significant social science and humanities research. But their Western equivalents who study China can’t do that kind of work in China, so it becomes very unbalanced.

What about your own experience with the Asia studies and Chinese history students you taught at Oxford and now Harvard?

When it comes to postgraduate students doing PhDs, a significant number are Chinese. We do still have some Westerners, but the problem is that they have to be enthusiastic about China, willing to learn its language and culture, and then be able to find a meaningful project to research.

It’s harder now to assure students that they can find a pathway to doing really good social science research inside China, so a lot of students just leave China out. If people say China is a very difficult place to do research and South Korea is easy, maybe people just decide they want to learn Korean instead.

The sense among Western researchers that it’s hard to do serious research in China has transmitted to students finding it difficult to find a career path if they do study China. I think that’s a great shame.

item.title: [SCMP] Asian-American groups fear Trump’s escalating rhetoric will fuel prejudice, racism in US
item.description: Asian-American groups on Friday slammed US President Donald Trump’s speech delivered this week as a dangerous threat to citizens of US minority communities already facing attacks and hostility during troubled and deeply partisan times, even as China hawks praised its message and tone. On Thursday during a prime-time address from the East Room of the White House, Trump repeated unsubstantiated claims that Beijing was interfering in US elections, citing his suspicion of what he termed “China’s sin
item.content:encoded:
Asian-American groups on Friday slammed US President Donald Trump’s speech delivered this week as a dangerous threat to citizens of US minority communities already facing attacks and hostility during troubled and deeply partisan times, even as China hawks praised its message and tone.

On Thursday during a prime-time address from the East Room of the White House, Trump repeated unsubstantiated claims that Beijing was interfering in US elections, citing his suspicion of what he termed “China’s sinister election meddling” and “the largest compromise of election data in history”.

Much of the voter registration data in the previously classified reports that the president cited is disputed and is not new, analysts said.

The speech – coming four months before a high-stakes midterm election amid an unpopular war and record-low polling numbers – raised concern that he could attempt to alter or overturn the results even as he repeated unproven claims that he won the 2020 election.

China’s foreign ministry dismissed Trump’s comments as factually wrong, aimed at “vilifying China”, while Chinese-American groups said his conspiracy theories threaten to fan an already tense domestic situation.

“This is an extension of racism and xenophobic behaviour,” said Joanna Yang Qing Derman, civil rights and national security director with civic group Asian-American Advancing Justice, part of a grass roots network of over 300 community groups.

“This kind of escalating rhetoric, this kind of fearmongering, has a real potential backlash on the Asian-American community today.”

Irresponsible language has implications, other groups said.

“Our research this year found that a majority of Americans believe the rhetoric used by President Trump about China and US-China relations has a negative impact on the treatment of Chinese-Americans,” said Paul Cheung, president of the Committee of 100, a group of prominent Chinese-Americans founded in 1990 by architect I.M. Pei and cellist Yo-Yo Ma.

C100 research released in March found that 54 per cent of Asian-Americans experienced some form of “everyday discrimination” at least once a month, ranging from insults and harassment to outright violence, with “perpetual foreigner” bias fuelling higher levels of stress and isolation in the community.

“Every time that tensions worsen, Chinese-Americans are the ones who encounter backlash,” added Cheung.

“That is why it’s so important to ensure that any claims are accurate, precise, and well-substantiated.”

Trump appeals to Maga base with anti-Chinese rhetoric amid polling slump

Several political polls peg Trump’s disapproval rate at around 60 per cent, among the lowest for any US president in recent memory, even as he retains fierce loyalty among core “make America great again” supporters.

“The president’s speech was so off the wall and weird. He’s just crazy every time he talks about things,” said Ted Gong, president of the 1882 Project Foundation.

The name of the anti-discrimination education group references a 19th century exclusion act that outlawed Chinese immigration.

“But there is still that raw Maga group,” he added. “What it does is maybe reinforce those guys that already want to be anti-Chinese, that have an excuse for what they’re doing.”

A key part of Trump’s televised speech was a call to pass his Save America Act, which he claims would prevent non-citizens from voting.

Critics counter that such voting is already illegal under federal law and the real intent is to deny a voice to millions of eligible citizens, including many Asian-American without easy access to passports or birth certificates.

“Don’t be fooled. Trump is using conspiracy theories as a pretext for attacking voting rights,” the Congressional Asian Pacific American Caucus wrote on social media.

The bipartisan caucus, made up of 85 lawmakers, is led by Democrat Grace Meng from New York. “If he really cared about foreign election interference, he wouldn’t have cut funding for agencies tasked with protecting our cybersecurity infrastructure,” she said.

Caucus member Ro Khanna, a Democrat from California, called foul.

“The president is creating a false and convenient narrative,” said Khanna. “He is using China to advance a political end, not to raise meaningful national concerns. He is weaponising the US-China relationship to control the elections.”

Asian-Americans push back publicly, encourage voter turnout

Asian-American civic groups say among the best ways to counter efforts to silence, sideline and demonise their community include education, awareness building, vocal pushback and voting.

Asian-Americans, who make up around seven per cent of the country’s 342.6 million population, have been the fastest-growing group of US voters over the past two decades, albeit still on a relatively small base.

Between 2020 and 2024, their numbers expanded by 15 per cent, or about two million eligible voters, compared to a three per cent growth rate nationwide.

“Last night, President Trump used a prime-time presidential address to continue spreading lies and misinformation about our elections and immigrant communities,” said Katrina Eames, digital manager with Asian Pacific Islander Political Alliance, a Pennsylvania-focused advocacy group for Asian-American voters.

“Trump’s claims aren’t based in reality – they are just a cover-up for a desperate power grab in 2026,” she added.

“We’re not giving Trump and Maga an opportunity to call foul on our elections this November before votes are cast and ballots are counted.”

Civic groups said criticising foreign policy issues is fair game, but all too often citizens of Asian descent become collateral damage.

“We have to distinguish between the actions of the Chinese government and Chinese-Americans,” said Derman. “It’s never appropriate to hold an entire community responsible.”

On Friday, meanwhile, the hardline House Select Committee on China praised the president’s speech and his attacks on the Communist Party [CCP], which it said were overdue.

“The newly declassified documents by President Trump confirm that the CCP is continuing its efforts to influence our elections and undermine the American way of life,” said John Moolenaar, a Republican from Michigan and chairman of the advisory committee.

“The CCP seeks to weaken us, turn us against one another, hollow out our economy, and undermine our resolve.”

The Chinese embassy in Washington said Moolinaar’s allegations were a “serious smear” long proven to be unfounded.

“We never interfere in US elections and have zero interest in that,” said spokesman Liu Chang. “The world has actually been witnessing who exactly has wantonly interfered in other countries’ internal affairs … We urge the US to reflect on its behaviour.”

Trump’s speech on Thursday was only the latest manifestation of a pattern of discrimination and the denial of land ownership, civil rights and a deserved place in American society stretching back centuries, groups said, including the incarceration of Japanese-Americans during World War II and the racially motivated killing of Vincent Chin in Detroit in 1982.

Trump’s hardline comments towards China come as he is preparing to host a September summit in Washington with Chinese President Xi Jinping, which Beijing has not confirmed.

“I feel like he doesn’t know what he’s talking about. He can’t make a coherent argument. He’ll talk about something, and then the next day [Secretary of State] Marco Rubio says something different,” Gong said.

“He can’t do so many things negative towards China. It will prevent him from having a nice conversation with Xi. I don’t know what he’s going to do if he goes further.”

Han Li contributed to this story

item.title: [SCMP] Many allegations, little evidence: unpacking the China election-interference records
item.description: Newly declassified US intelligence records show Chinese actors collected or acquired voter information covering millions of Americans and that Beijing developed capabilities that could be used to influence political opinion, but the documents do not substantiate US President Donald Trump’s claims that China stole the records of 220 million voters or manipulated the 2020 election. In a nationally televised address on Thursday, Trump accused China of carrying out a sweeping campaign to interfere i
item.content:encoded:
Newly declassified US intelligence records show Chinese actors collected or acquired voter information covering millions of Americans and that Beijing developed capabilities that could be used to influence political opinion, but the documents do not substantiate US President Donald Trump’s claims that China stole the records of 220 million voters or manipulated the 2020 election.

In a nationally televised address on Thursday, Trump accused China of carrying out a sweeping campaign to interfere in the election he lost to Joe Biden, alleging that Beijing illicitly obtained about 220 million American voter records and sought to fabricate ballots in favour of Biden.

The documents released by the White House provide new details about China’s collection and analysis of US voter information, as well as an internal dispute among American intelligence officials over whether Beijing’s broader political activities amounted to election influence.

But they offer no evidence that China altered voter rolls, produced fraudulent ballots or interfered with vote counting, tabulation or certification in 2020.

Several of the records describe the voter information as publicly available, commercially obtained or drawn from data sets that may previously have been leaked, complicating the White House’s assertion that the records were “compromised” by China.

One intelligence report says an unidentified Chinese entity possessed a document listing data sets believed to have been leaked or compromised. Among them was an unspecified collection containing about 204.8 million US voter records from 2016, including names, ages, telephone numbers and addresses.

Documented interest but no specific action

The document does not establish that the records represented 204.8 million individual voters. The data set could contain duplicate, historical or aggregated entries associated with the same people across different jurisdictions or periods.

Nor does the report identify who originally obtained the data, explain how it was acquired or demonstrate that a Chinese actor penetrated a government election system.

The intelligence assessment says only that China had shown a “newly identified interest” in US elections. It says the information could, “in theory”, be used to support future cyber or influence operations, mine personal information about US citizens, conduct identity matching and analyse American public opinion.

However, the report does not describe any specific action China took based on the information and explicitly states that the motivation for its collection was unknown.

Another intelligence document says a Chinese “computer network exploitation” or Cyber Network Exploitation actor downloaded publicly available voter-registration information from commercial websites in January 2022.

The data covered Colorado, Connecticut, Florida, Michigan, Oklahoma and Rhode Island and included information dating from 2013 to 2021. The actor also unsuccessfully attempted to download an Ohio voter registration application from a state government website.

Some of the data included names, birth dates, addresses, telephone numbers, party affiliations, voting histories, polling locations and, in certain cases, military affiliations.

The distinction between acquiring voter information and penetrating an election system is significant because many US states make portions of their voter files available to political parties, campaigns, researchers or commercial vendors, although access rules and the information disclosed vary.

Large-scale collection of such material can nevertheless pose national security risks. Voter records can be combined with other personal information to identify government officials, military personnel and politically influential individuals, or to facilitate spear-phishing, identity matching and highly targeted influence operations.

Intelligence community split over China’s intentions

The documents also shed new light on a long-standing disagreement within the US intelligence community over China’s intentions during the 2020 presidential campaign.

A newly released CIA note summarising sensitive reporting from 2018 to 2020 says the Communist Party sought to prevent Trump’s re-election and considered using economic and political pressure against his supporters.

According to the note, Beijing studied states and economic sectors that supported Trump in 2016, and considered imposing financial costs to encourage businesses and political constituencies to lobby his administration.

The reporting also described efforts to influence business executives, campaign donors, academics, former government officials and journalists.

Two additional intelligence summaries assess that China sought to portray the Trump administration negatively by highlighting racial tensions, immigration, gun violence, military spending, Washington’s handling of the coronavirus pandemic and US policy in the South China Sea.

The summaries also allege that China had developed a social-media influence apparatus capable of distributing such themes through TikTok, Facebook, Twitter, YouTube and other platforms.

According to the documents, Beijing could use overt and covert influencers, as well as contributors to mainstream media, to shape public opinion, including perceptions of senior US officials.

But the heavily redacted documents provide limited information about the evidence underpinning those assessments.

Other contemporaneous intelligence records released by the administration reflect more cautious judgments.

A CIA assessment published in July 2020 said Chinese state-sponsored cyber actors had targeted Biden campaign personnel and collected information from voter databases, polling companies and political organisations.

But the agency assessed at the time that Beijing did not intend to intervene covertly to sway the election’s outcome. The document also says that spear-phishing attempts against campaign personnel, as disclosed by Google, were unsuccessful.

An August 2020 National Intelligence Council assessment similarly said China preferred Trump’s defeat but believed a concerted attempt to influence the presidential election could backfire.

The assessment concluded that Beijing was collecting political intelligence and evaluating the consequences of either candidate’s victory, while leaving open the possibility that it could take more direct action if Chinese leaders believed existing tools were insufficient.

The disagreement was ultimately reflected in the intelligence community’s public assessment of foreign threats to the election, released in March 2021.

Most agencies concluded with high confidence that China had considered but not deployed influence operations intended to change the outcome. The National Intelligence Officer for Cyber dissented, assessing with moderate confidence that Beijing had taken some steps to undermine Trump’s re-election prospects.

Both positions agreed, however, that there was no evidence China interfered with the technical conduct of the election.

The declassified files add evidence supporting the minority view that at least some Chinese activities could be classified as election influence. They do not overturn the broader finding that China did not manipulate voting processes.

Questionable claims of FBI cover-up

Trump also accused FBI and intelligence officials of suppressing information about an alleged Chinese operation to produce fraudulent votes for Biden.

That allegation centres on a raw FBI report from September 2020 claiming the Chinese government planned to manufacture fraudulent US driver’s licences and use them to create tens of thousands of mail-in votes.

The newly released internal correspondence shows that FBI personnel recalled the report and debated whether to reissue it. One employee expressed concern that the report contradicted congressional testimony by FBI director Christopher Wray and worried that political considerations could influence its handling.

But the same records document substantial concerns about the intelligence itself.

The allegation had passed from an unidentified purported Chinese government contact, via a sub-source, before reaching an FBI confidential source. An FBI employee described its veracity as questionable, while other officials said portions of the source’s reporting veered into conspiracy theories and did not appear authoritative.

An inquiry involving US Customs and Border Protection also found that the overwhelming majority of counterfeit licences intercepted at the time appeared to have been ordered by people aged between 18 and 20 seeking to evade alcohol restrictions, not to register fraudulent voters.

After further discussions, FBI Albany personnel agreed with the bureau’s Foreign Influence Task Force that the report should not be reissued.

The documents therefore reveal internal concerns about how politically sensitive intelligence was handled, but do not verify the alleged Chinese ballot operation or establish that evidence of such a scheme was deliberately concealed.

China rejected Trump’s accusations on Friday, with foreign ministry spokesman Lin Jian describing them as fabricated and urging Washington to stop using election-related allegations to vilify Beijing.

Targeting Trump specifically makes little strategic sense

Derek Scissors, a senior fellow at the American Enterprise Institute who focuses on China, said Trump’s remarks suggested he was not fully confident in his own claims.

Scissors said Beijing could have an interest in sowing instability in the US, but targeting Trump specifically would make little strategic sense, given the president’s relatively dovish approach towards China.

“China trying to get him out of office would make it as big a political enemy as there could be. And yet the president didn’t mention a single action in retaliation,” Scissors said.

“[Trump’s] actions and inactions make it clear that he doesn’t believe what he just said.”

Trump’s claims come as his administration presses Congress to adopt stricter voter-identification and proof-of-citizenship requirements ahead of November’s midterm elections.

The declassified material could strengthen arguments that the United States needs to protect voter information from foreign collection and establish clearer security standards for data held by commercial vendors.

It does not, however, show that Chinese activity changed the result of the 2020 presidential election.

item.title: [SCMP] US lets Hong Kong emergency expire but keeps preferential treatment restrictions
item.description: The Hong Kong government on Friday welcomed the Trump administration’s decision to allow a national emergency declaration concerning the city to expire, although Washington said the executive order that ended the city’s preferential treatment remains in effect. The declaration, which underpinned Washington’s decision to end Hong Kong’s preferential treatment in areas including trade and export controls, expired this week after US President Donald Trump did not renew it for another year. “The pre
item.content:encoded:
The Hong Kong government on Friday welcomed the Trump administration’s decision to allow a national emergency declaration concerning the city to expire, although Washington said the executive order that ended the city’s preferential treatment remains in effect.

The declaration, which underpinned Washington’s decision to end Hong Kong’s preferential treatment in areas including trade and export controls, expired this week after US President Donald Trump did not renew it for another year.

“The president has allowed the national emergency to end, but Executive Order 13936 otherwise remains in effect,” a State Department spokesperson said.

The spokesperson said Washington continued to regard Hong Kong as “no longer sufficiently autonomous to justify differential treatment in relation to the [China]” under the US laws and provisions specified in the order.

The clarification indicates that the expiration of the emergency will not automatically restore Hong Kong’s special treatment in areas including trade and export controls.

The US Treasury noted on its website that the expiration did not apply to or affect two related US laws, the Hong Kong Human Rights and Democracy Act of 2019 or the Hong Kong Autonomy Act of 2020.

The Hong Kong government called the decision “an important step” towards implementing a consensus reached between China and the United States, amid signs of easing tensions between the world’s two largest economies.

“Safeguarding Hong Kong’s prosperity and stability serves the common interests of China and the United States and is also in line with the international community’s shared expectations,” a government spokesman said.

The statement welcomed what it described as a positive adjustment to Washington’s Hong Kong policy and urged the United States to respect Chinese sovereignty and the rule of law in the city while restoring normal economic and trade ties.

China’s commerce ministry also said the US had confirmed that it would not extend the measure, which had been renewed annually since Trump issued it during his first term.

“The US action is an important step in fulfilling the consensus reached in the bilateral trade talks,” the ministry said, referring to meetings in May.

The ministry said maintaining Hong Kong’s prosperity and stability served the interests of both countries and that Washington’s policy shift reflected the international community’s expectations.

The US Treasury Department noted the change in dry technical language.

“The national emergency declared in Executive Order (E.O.) 13936 of July 14, 2020, “The President’s Executive Order on Hong Kong Normalization,” has expired,” it said on its website.

It also noted that people and property sanctioned under the Hong Kong Autonomy Act remain so and have been added to an updated Office of Foreign Assets Control sanctions list.

US analysts said Friday’s move was a modest step but one that may signal a shift in direction.

“It’s significant at a symbolic level in terms of perhaps ever so slightly but tangibly the US is lightening up on its negativeness about Hong Kong,” said Kurt Tong, a former US consul general to Hong Kong and Macau who is now a partner with the Asia Group consultancy.

“The US is now leaning less heavily in the direction of wanting to apply punishment for Hong Kong’s changes and leaning a bit more in the direction of doing what is good for the US in practical terms on a case-by-case basis. It is a subtle but significant change.”

China’s commerce ministry urged the US to respect Chinese sovereignty and the rule of law in Hong Kong, restore normal economic and trade exchanges with the city, and contribute to a “constructive and strategically stable” relationship with Beijing.

The State Department, meanwhile, said the administration was focused on improving “strategic stability” with China, including in its approach to Hong Kong.

“The United States expects Beijing and Hong Kong authorities to engage constructively and reciprocally on US political, economic and national security priorities,” the spokesperson said.

Tong said under the complicated rule change, sanctions have been lifted on Hong Kong Secretary for Justice Paul Lam Ting-kwok. But they remain in place for other top officials, including Chief Executive John Lee Ka-chiu, Security Secretary Chris Tang Ping-keung and former chief executive Carrie Lam Cheng Yuet-ngor.

“It’s open to interpretation whether the US will be flexible about sanctions going forward,” he added. “I don’t know is the short answer on whether the US is going to consider sanctions relief for the chief executive, probably the thing that is most troublesome for Hong Kong authorities.”

Lau Siu-kai, a consultant for the semi-official Chinese Association of Hong Kong and Macau Studies think tank, said the US’ move was intended to stabilise US-China relations following the summit between Trump and Chinese President Xi Jinping.

“The move will benefit both sides economically and financially. American businesses and capital can gain greater access to the mainland Chinese market and seek opportunities there through Hong Kong,” he said.

“This move will also facilitate economic, legal and cultural exchanges between Hong Kong and the US. It also implies that, in the foreseeable future, the US will not impose sanctions on Hong Kong officials because of Hong Kong’s actions to safeguard national security.”

The State Department did not endorse that interpretation and referred questions about sanctions to the Treasury Department.

US analysts said they will be watching for clues on the direction of US policy. One obvious indicator will be whether sanctions are lifted on Lee. Another would be whether US Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent travels to the Asia Pacific Economic Cooperation finance ministers meeting in Hong Kong in October.

“The move confirms that Hong Kong is part of broader US-China trade and investment negotiations, with the Trump administration willing to trade away Hong Kong-related pressures. This is a major departure from bipartisan US policy of the past six years,” said Dominic Chiu, senior analyst with the Eurasia Group.

“It is a further signal of an ongoing shift in the administration away from viewing values-driven policy as non-negotiables, and towards a more transactionally driven diplomacy.”

But Chiu added that any future sanctions rollbacks are likely to be modest given that the cohort of officials designated exclusively under the executive order is relatively small.

“Export control alignment with China will also likely continue, given the administration’s priority on national security and technology competition,” said Chiu. “A reversal of these would be a much more meaningful signal of a sea change in the administration’s treatment on HK’s status.”

The analyst added that statutory sanctions are likely to remain given bipartisan support in Congress for Hong Kong-related human rights measures.

Trump issued Executive Order 13936 in July 2020, declaring a national emergency following Beijing’s imposition of the national security law that came after months of anti-government protests in Hong Kong.

The order suspended the city’s preferential treatment under several US laws, paving the way for Washington to treat Hong Kong in the same manner as mainland China in areas including export controls, defence shipments and the marking of imported goods.

Washington said the security law had eroded Hong Kong’s autonomy and restricted rights and freedoms promised to the city after its return to Chinese rule in 1997. Beijing and Hong Kong authorities have maintained that the legislation restored stability after the 2019 protests.

item.title: [SCMP] How South Korean hypermarket chain’s fall reflects ‘time-poor’ consumer demands
item.description: Homeplus was once a retail haven for millions of South Korean families who shopped for groceries and household goods regularly at its cavernous hypermarkets. But that ritual over almost three decades ended this week when the company closed all its remaining 67 operating outlets nationwide after suspending 37 others earlier as part of its restructuring. The sudden closure of South Korea’s second-largest hypermarket chain reflects not only its financial distress, but also a decline of the shopping
item.content:encoded:
Homeplus was once a retail haven for millions of South Korean families who shopped for groceries and household goods regularly at its cavernous hypermarkets.

But that ritual over almost three decades ended this week when the company closed all its remaining 67 operating outlets nationwide after suspending 37 others earlier as part of its restructuring.

The sudden closure of South Korea’s second-largest hypermarket chain reflects not only its financial distress, but also a decline of the shopping model it helped popularise.

A 200 billion won (US$135 million) emergency loan approved on Thursday has given Homeplus a temporary lifeline, allowing the company to challenge a court decision that had pushed it closer to liquidation. However, it still faces questions about unpaid wages, supplier payments and above all, its business status.

The Homeplus crisis signals how online shopping and smaller households are adversely affecting the hypermarket model, according to retail analysts.

Nearly 80 per cent of South Koreans shop online, according to a 2025 survey by Statista. A separate report in May by the country’s trade ministry shows that online platforms accounted for more than 60 per cent of total sales of the country’s major retailers earlier this year.

Evolving shopping experience

Lee Do-hyeon lives with his wife and one-year-old daughter in Unjeong, a suburb north of Seoul populated mostly by young families. A Homeplus megastore in the suburb’s central area used to be the neighbourhood’s main shopping destination.

“While the news was shocking because I’ve been going to Homeplus since I was a child, in another way it felt inevitable. It wouldn’t really affect our lives if it closed today. My wife and I don’t have much time, so we do most of our shopping online,” said the 30-year-old employee at a logistics company.

Lee and his family have increasingly spent their weekends at the nearby Starfield Village Unjeong, a sprawling shopping and entertainment complex operated by department store giant Shinsegae.

The complex attracted more than 2.1 million visitors in its first 100 days after opening in December, with visitors drawn to its attractions including trendy restaurants, cooking-class studios and children’s libraries.

“If we have to choose between the two, we’d rather spend time at Starfield. There’s much more to do,” Lee said.

Jeon Da-hyen, a researcher and co-author of the bestselling Trend Korea series, said the physical retail model had transformed in response to changing shopping patterns.

“Rather than simply buying products, shoppers now visit physical stores for experiences, entertainment and atmosphere. Retailers have to give consumers a reason to visit,” she said.

Promotional events such as pop-up stores and exclusive product launches have also become increasingly important for retailers.

“Because opportunities for families to spend time together have become much rarer, people increasingly choose destinations that offer broader leisure experiences rather than simply visiting a hypermarket,” Jeon said.

Recent data shows the changing fortunes of South Korea’s retail segments.

Department stores in the country were the only major retail segment to post higher sales in 2025, according to data from the Korean Statistical Information Service. In comparison, hypermarkets recorded their first annual sales decline while convenience stores also slipped.

According to data from the trade ministry, department store sales rose 14.7 per cent while online sales grew 8.1 per cent in March from the same month a year earlier. Hypermarkets and supermarkets posted a decrease in sales of 15.2 per cent and 8.6 per cent, respectively.

Tech-driven retail

Suh Yong-gu, a marketing professor at Sookmyung Women’s University in Seoul, said the Homeplus experience partly reflected the changing demographics of South Korean society.

“The decline of the hypermarket is, in many ways, the decline of the traditional family. As family structures have changed, the hypermarket model has become much less relevant,” Suh said.

South Korea has the world’s lowest birth rate, with one-person households accounting for 36 per cent of all households, followed by two-person households at 29 per cent.

Spending patterns have also been affected by rising inequality.

Suh noted income growth did not rise across all households while the wealthiest 20 per cent recorded a strong rise in their income and assets.

Nonetheless, South Korea’s appetite for retail consumption remains strong. The country’s spending in high-end segments such as food, clothing and accessories was among the highest in the world, according to a report by Deloitte Korea’s Consumer Signals that was released in April 2025 and based on that year’s first-quarter data.

“South Korea has a large and growing population of what we call ‘money-rich, time-poor’ consumers – people who have purchasing power but very little time”, Suh said, citing the success of Coupang, the largest online retailer in South Korea.

Coupang controls about one-third of South Korea’s e-commerce market and accounts for more than half of online grocery sales, according to several surveys. The company’s other major market is in Taiwan, where its delivery service now covers 70 per cent of its geography.

Suh said South Korea was a “global laboratory” reflecting how retail trends were evolving in Asia.

“Many Asian countries are becoming increasingly urbanised, household sizes are shrinking and online retail is increasingly becoming the norm even in traditionally family-oriented societies,” he added.

Despite the rise of online sales, major South Korean retailers are still expanding their physical presence in the region, with the likes of hypermarket and discount store giant Lotte Mart and convenience-store chain Emart expanding in markets such as Indonesia, Vietnam, Thailand and Mongolia.

One market that has caught the attention of retail analysts like Jeon is China, where she notes drone deliveries have become part of daily life in cities such as Shenzhen.

“China has taken delivery culture to another level. In some cities, delivery times are measured in minutes, with companies facing financial penalties if they miss strict deadlines,” Jeon said.

Similarly, Jeon believed it was a matter of time that South Korea’s retail scene would be revolutionised by technology.

“I expect South Korea’s delivery ecosystem to become increasingly integrated with advanced technologies. You may see robots delivering your groceries to your doorsteps”.

item.title: [SCMP] China blasts ‘unfounded smears’ as Trump’s election claims threaten diplomatic thaw
item.description: US President Donald Trump launched a sharp attack on China late on Thursday (Washington time), accusing Beijing of interfering in US elections in a move that has strained a fragile thaw in ties and cast doubt on future high-level engagements. In a prime-time address from the White House that lasted nearly 30 minutes, Trump accused China of orchestrating “what is believed to be the largest compromise of election data in history”. He claimed Beijing stole 220 million US voter files, including name
item.content:encoded:
US President Donald Trump launched a sharp attack on China late on Thursday (Washington time), accusing Beijing of interfering in US elections in a move that has strained a fragile thaw in ties and cast doubt on future high-level engagements.

In a prime-time address from the White House that lasted nearly 30 minutes, Trump accused China of orchestrating “what is believed to be the largest compromise of election data in history”.

He claimed Beijing stole 220 million US voter files, including names, addresses, phone numbers, political party preferences and other sensitive data. He also accused intelligence agencies of downplaying the scale of China’s “sinister” meddling.

On Friday, China’s foreign ministry dismissed Trump’s allegations as having “no factual ground”, saying Beijing adhered to the principle of non-intervention in other states’ internal affairs and had no interest in interfering in US elections.

Ministry spokesman Lin Jian urged Washington to reflect on its actions, stop its “unfounded smears” against China and “do more to benefit China-US relations”.

Trump’s claims of Chinese interference in US elections are not new – he made similar assertions in 2018 and 2020 – but the latest were his most sweeping and pointed yet. The national address barely touched on other issues such as the Iran war.

He also alleged that the Chinese government was seeking to turn US business leaders against him and paying US journalists “large sums of money” to write negative articles.

“China and other countries have been trying to meddle in our elections. Evidence of fraud has been buried,” he said.

Trump’s speech comes as his administration braces for the November midterm elections amid waning popularity, driven in part by the US-Israel war on Iran, which has pushed up the cost of living.

The fiery address also came two months after Trump’s May visit to China, when he and Chinese President Xi Jinping agreed to forge a “constructive relationship of strategic stability” to manage the rivalry between the powers.

That thaw might now be tested by Trump’s latest speech, which risks derailing high-level engagements between the two leaders, including Xi’s trip to the US in September, the first state visit by a Chinese leader in more than 10 years.

Domestically, [Trump] benefits from portraying China as an implacable enemy. Internationally, he may need China to behave as a pragmatic intermediary

Hilton Root, international politics professor

Michael Kugelman, a resident senior fellow at the Washington-based Atlantic Council think tank, said Trump had long taken an ambiguous approach to China, signalling at different times a preference for cooperation and competition.

“That has kept the relationship fraught, but relatively stable. But these new allegations could make Trump’s careful approach to relations with Beijing untenable,” he said.

“They’re explosive, and could provoke an angry response in Beijing that carries over into upcoming bilateral diplomacy.”

Tensions have simmered in recent weeks. The Pentagon added dozens of Chinese firms to a list of entities it says are linked to the Chinese military, prompting Beijing to respond by adding 10 American firms to its export control list. The US has also redeployed coastguard vessels from the Middle East to the South China Sea.

On Thursday, the White House tightened visa rules for foreign students, media personnel and exchange programme participants, including stricter limits on Chinese journalists.

In Beijing, officials are likely to recognise that Trump’s Thursday speech was driven by domestic politics, but there could still be cause for concern.

Zhao Minghao, a professor at Fudan University’s Institute of International Studies, said Trump’s interference claim clearly reflected that domestic political factors were exerting growing influence on US-China relations.

“China has reason to worry that Trump – facing unfavourable election prospects – might further intensify these sorts of attacks on China, including blaming Beijing for America’s economic difficulties … or accusing Beijing of other things,” Zhao said, adding that such attacks could have an impact on recovering US-China ties and cloud Xi’s Washington visit.

There was also a real strategic tension involving China, said Hilton Root, a professor of international politics at George Mason University in Virginia.

He said Washington was simultaneously accusing Beijing of the largest compromise of American election data while relying on its leverage over Tehran, creating friction that could “reduce Trump’s diplomatic space”.

“Domestically, [Trump] benefits from portraying China as an implacable enemy. Internationally, he may need China to behave as a pragmatic intermediary. Those two requirements pull in different directions,” Root said.

“Tonight’s speech was the announcement of a governing narrative. It did not establish that China changed votes or that the cited registrations changed an election outcome. It demonstrates how Trump intends to connect cyber threats, voter-roll disputes, intelligence-community distrust, immigration, media hostility and foreign conflict.”

In the US, Trump’s charged speech has drawn support from his Republican base but others have rejected his claims. The Congressional Asian Pacific American Caucus (Capac), a group of 85 members of the US Congress, accused Trump of using conspiracy theories to justify his voting agenda.

Before Trump spoke, former vice-president Kamala Harris said the US leader would “peddle lies and conspiracy theories” about the 2020 election, adding that he was attempting to distract Americans from rising costs and the Iran conflict.

Mark Warner, a Democratic senator from Virginia, described Trump’s allegations about Chinese election interference as “totally bogus”.

“The fact is our intelligence agencies unanimously agreed that China did not even try to change a single vote in the 2020 election,” he wrote on social media.

Trump and his allies had repeatedly cast China, not Russia, as the greatest foreign threat to the vote’s integrity in the run-up to the November 2020 presidential election.

At the time, Trump told Reuters he believed China would do “anything they can to have me lose this race” and that Beijing wanted Joe Biden, his Democratic opponent, to win the election to ease trade pressures.

Biden went on to win 306 electoral votes to Trump’s 232, along with a roughly 7-million-vote edge in the popular tally. But Trump refused to concede, alleging widespread fraud and foreign meddling, and has frequently pointed the finger at Beijing.

But a declassified Intelligence Community Assessment released in March 2021 concluded that it was Russia that ran an influence campaign to support Trump while China had weighed similar efforts but ultimately held back.

In 2018, Trump told a UN Security Council meeting that Beijing did not want him to win that year’s midterm elections because he was “the first president to ever challenge China on trade”.

“Regrettably, we found that China has been attempting to interfere in our upcoming 2018 election, coming up in November, against my administration,” he said.

Beijing issued a similar rebuttal then, urging the US to stop making “groundless accusations” against China and halt actions and rhetoric that harm bilateral relations.

item.title: [SCMP] Malaysia’s Network School probe raises questions over openness to tech talent
item.description: A controversy in Malaysia over a foreign tech community in Johor state has raised questions on whether immigration scrutiny and political sensitivities could slow the country’s push to attract digital nomads and other global talent. Johor authorities are investigating Network School, a private co-living and co-working community based in Forest City and founded by American tech investor and former Coinbase chief technology officer Balaji Srinivasan, following online allegations that Israelis were
item.content:encoded:
A controversy in Malaysia over a foreign tech community in Johor state has raised questions on whether immigration scrutiny and political sensitivities could slow the country’s push to attract digital nomads and other global talent.

Johor authorities are investigating Network School, a private co-living and co-working community based in Forest City and founded by American tech investor and former Coinbase chief technology officer Balaji Srinivasan, following online allegations that Israelis were involved in the project and had entered Malaysia using secondary passports.

For 38-year-old Malaysian digital nomad Harinder Singh, however, the episode is unlikely to deter global remote workers from considering living and working in Malaysia. What matters is whether the country is affordable, connected and transparent about its rules, according to Harinder.

“Digital nomads don’t really make decisions based on news cycles like this,” said Harinder, who has worked remotely for more than a decade between cities in Malaysia and elsewhere in Southeast Asia.

“Incidents like this get attention, but the real decision factors are visa clarity, cost of living, internet reliability and community.”

Malaysia does not have diplomatic relations with Israel and generally prohibits Israeli passport holders from entering the country without special approval.

The country has spent the last three years attracting remote workers through its DE Rantau Nomad Pass, competing with destinations including Thailand, Indonesia and Vietnam.

As of December 2024, the Malaysia Digital Economy Corporation (MDEC) had approved 2,344 DE Rantau applications, allowing visitors to stay between three and 12 months, with an option to renew for an additional year.

Should the global tech community continue investing in Malaysia?

Given recent events, I raise this question respectfully for the consideration of Prime Minister Yang Amat Berhormat Dato’ Seri Anwar bin Ibrahim (@anwaribrahim), for the people of Malaysia, and for our friends in… pic.twitter.com/0kvDSfF1z9

— Balaji (@balajis) July 16, 2026

The investigation has raised questions about whether the investigation could prompt tighter scrutiny of foreign tech communities based in Malaysia and undermine confidence in the country as a viable destination for global workers and investors.

Srinivasan said the controversy had disrupted Network School’s plans in Malaysia and that further investment was suspended pending assurances from the government.

“At this point, all further investment we were planning to make in Malaysia is on hold until we get sufficient assurance that such issues won’t recur,” he said in a video posted on social media on Thursday.

Srinivasan said false claims of harbouring illegal aliens by an anonymous social media account had prompted a visit by authorities days later, during which hundreds of passports were checked, including those belonging to dual passport holders.

He added that immigration officials were polite and professional, but added that “the process is the punishment”.

The Immigration Department inspected 266 foreigners from 40 countries at the site and found that all held valid travel documents. It said further checks were ongoing into any misuse of identities, passports, immigration facilities or permit conditions.

Network School claimed it had invested more than US$25 million in its Forest City campus and was preparing further expansion worth more than US$123 million.

Srinivasan said Forest City was chosen for its proximity to Singapore, inexpensive space and Malaysia’s policies aimed at attracting international technology workers and investors.

He called for a meeting with Prime Minister Anwar Ibrahim’s office and proposed a memorandum of understanding to commit Network School to Malaysian law and sovereignty.

“If Malaysia still wants continued global tech investment, if it wants to be a top 20 tech hub, if it wants us to revitalise Forest City, then we request an audience with the Prime Minister’s office,” he said, warning that Network School could redirect its capital elsewhere if confidence were not restored.

Johor has benefited from a data centre investment boom, as the state aims to draw more cross-border capital and technology activity.

Farlina Said, director of cyber and technology policy at the Institute of Strategic and International Studies Malaysia, said the controversy could lead to stricter scrutiny of dual nationals and immigration documents. Such enforcement would require clearer policies, greater resources and stronger cross-border co-ordination, she said.

Restrictions on technology communities could undermine exchanges of knowledge and talent, Farlina warned. “An overcorrection that does not acknowledge the overall role of community in innovation could be short-sighted.”

Johor’s Chief Minister Onn Hafiz Ghazi said in a statement on Tuesday that the state would not allow any party to be utilised as a base for an ideology or movement in contravention of Malaysian law.

“The investigation must cover the nationality of those involved, travel documents, as well as possible usage of second passports and passes used to enter the country, while ensuring the purpose of the visit aligns with the activities conducted,” Onn said.

Anwar said at a news conference on Wednesday that Malaysia would not allow any Israeli nationals found to have entered and stayed in the country.

“If there are Israeli nationals, since we do not recognise Israel, they will be deported immediately.”

The Home Ministry has said it takes seriously any matter involving national security, compliance with immigration laws and abuse of Malaysia’s immigration facilities, and is conducting a comprehensive review and investigation.

Country Garden Pacificview, the developer of Forest City, said it had zero tolerance for any breach of law and would cooperate fully with authorities while enforcing its legal and contractual rights in the event of any breaches.

For Harinder, the controversy has not affected Malaysia’s attraction as a base for global tech professionals.

“I don’t think this moves the needle for someone weighing their options next quarter,” he said.

A greater challenge for local authorities was ensuring that remote workers understood how long they were permitted to stay in Malaysia and the activities they could undertake legally, he added.

“The biggest deterrent isn’t one headline,” Harinder said. “It’s whether the rules are clear and whether the place still works for the life you’re trying to build.”

This Week in Asia has reached out to the Digital Ministry and MDEC for comments.

item.title: [SCMP] Why South Korea is racing to build a sovereign AI model for cybersecurity
item.description: South Korea plans to develop its own cybersecurity-focused artificial intelligence model by the end of this year, after a brief US clampdown on advanced systems exposed the risks of relying on foreign technology for national cyber defence. But Seoul’s more ambitious, longer-term goal of building a frontier model capable of competing with the world’s most advanced systems would require it to overcome a persistent gap in AI software, computing capacity and large-scale training, analysts said. The
item.content:encoded:
South Korea plans to develop its own cybersecurity-focused artificial intelligence model by the end of this year, after a brief US clampdown on advanced systems exposed the risks of relying on foreign technology for national cyber defence.

But Seoul’s more ambitious, longer-term goal of building a frontier model capable of competing with the world’s most advanced systems would require it to overcome a persistent gap in AI software, computing capacity and large-scale training, analysts said.

The initiative reflects a broader push for “AI sovereignty”, with governments in Japan, Britain, Canada and across the European Union investing in domestic models and computing infrastructure to reduce their reliance on foreign providers.

Science and ICT Minister Bae Kyung-hoon unveiled the plan on Thursday after Washington imposed new export restrictions on several advanced AI models, including Anthropic’s Mythos 5.

“We are pushing to create an AI model specialising in cybersecurity within this year by training our existing sovereign AI models on security-related data,” Bae said during a policy briefing chaired by President Lee Jae Myung.

Bae warned that AI-powered cyber threats were becoming increasingly sophisticated.

“AI can be used to identify security vulnerabilities and exploit them with ease, so we need to prepare our defences accordingly,” he said.

Although several South Korean companies have developed their own large language models, Bae acknowledged that none yet matched the capabilities of the world’s leading frontier AI systems.

“They are not yet capable of responding effectively at that level,” he said.

Bae outlined a two-pronged strategy: build a cybersecurity-focused AI model in the short term by further training existing sovereign AI models on security-related data, then explore developing a frontier AI model comparable to Anthropic’s Mythos.

“Although Mythos was not developed specifically for cybersecurity, it has become capable of solving complex security problems with ease,” he said. “We therefore need to seriously consider developing similarly advanced frontier AI models.”

The policy briefing also focused on uncertainty surrounding US restrictions on access to advanced AI systems.

President Lee said South Korea must prepare for the possibility that access to foreign AI models could be suspended.

“We need to assume that access could be blocked,” Lee said. “We cannot allow a situation in which another country is effectively protecting our cybersecurity and then suddenly leaves the gate.”

Bae echoed those concerns. “The United States restricted access to advanced AI, then relaxed the restrictions and has recently reintroduced limited controls. China appears to be moving in a similar direction, so we don’t know when access could be cut off,” he said.

Lagging behind

A senior Science Ministry official estimated that South Korea trailed the US by about six months in AI technology.

“The government has been supporting five selected pioneers by providing them with AI computing resources, including GPUs, and data to develop our own frontier AI model,” the official, who asked for anonymity, told This Week in Asia, noting that the government had been pursuing a sovereign AI foundation model project since August 2025.

Professor Kim Jang-hyun, an AI expert at Sungkyunkwan University, said South Korea’s weakness was in software rather than hardware.

“As far as AI software is concerned, South Korea is considerably behind not only the United States but China as well,” Kim said.

One visible sign of that gap, he said, was the lack of widely adopted domestic generative AI platforms capable of competing internationally with ChatGPT, Google’s Gemini, Anthropic’s Claude or China’s DeepSeek, although companies including Naver, LG and Upstage have developed their own models.

Kim attributed this partly to years of underinvestment.

“In the past, the Korean government was not very enthusiastic about investing money and resources in developing software that did not generate tangible short-term returns, while local companies had to compete within a relatively small domestic market,” he said.

“South Korea has the human resources and technological capability to develop a top-tier AI. It depends on how much the government is willing to invest,” Kim added. “I wouldn’t be surprised if it produces a frontier AI capable of competing with the world’s leading systems within a year or two.”

Another AI specialist working at a Korean technology company was less optimistic.

“There are still extremely difficult final stages in developing generative AI, including large-scale training, evaluating outputs and integrating models into enterprise workflows. No one can say with confidence how long it will take South Korea to overcome those challenges,” said the specialist, who asked for anonymity as he was not authorised to speak to the press.

Kim said South Korea’s push to develop security-focused frontier AI went beyond technological competition. “It represents an extension of the country’s broader effort to reduce its dependence on foreign AI giants and secure national AI sovereignty.”

While the global AI landscape has largely been dominated by the US and China, South Korea has relied heavily on overseas companies for foundation models, cloud infrastructure and AI software, exposing itself to risks should foreign governments or companies restrict access to critical technologies, according to Kim.

“Although South Korea may have entered the race later than some competitors, it must now pursue broad research and development initiatives to strengthen its domestic AI capabilities rather than relying unilaterally on foreign technology companies,” he said.

Kim said AI-powered cybersecurity should become a national strategic priority as generative AI and autonomous agents created new security risks.

AI could already automate malware development, vulnerability discovery, phishing campaigns, social engineering attacks and attack planning, he said.

“Looking ahead, fully autonomous cyberattacks – where AI independently plans and executes multiple stages of an attack – are increasingly becoming a realistic possibility,” he warned.

Against that backdrop, Kim said governments should be cautious about relying heavily on foreign AI systems to protect critical national infrastructure.

“No matter how capable a security solution may be, if the government cannot fully control how data is processed, how the model operates or how updates are managed, it effectively entrusts critical national information and security infrastructure to external entities,” he said.

Rather than excluding foreign technologies altogether, Kim said South Korea should build domestic capabilities while continuing to use overseas technologies where appropriate.

“The objective is not to reject foreign technology,” he said. “It is to ensure sovereign control over critical data, cybersecurity decision-making and essential infrastructure.”

He added that achieving that goal would require long-term cooperation among government, industry, universities, research institutes and local authorities.

“A country that lacks the ability to choose and control the AI technologies underpinning its critical systems cannot truly be considered an AI leader,” Kim said. “Developing security-centred frontier AI is therefore a strategic imperative that advances both industrial competitiveness and national security.”

item.title: [SCMP] China’s second-quarter GDP growth misses mark, with lowest reading since end of 2022
item.description: China reported 4.3 per cent gross domestic product growth in the second quarter of 2026 shortly after recording strong, sustained momentum in exports – a contrast underscoring the need for policy support to address weak domestic demand and persistent economic imbalances. The closely watched GDP figure, released by the National Bureau of Statistics (NBS) on Wednesday, was the slowest quarterly growth rate since the end of 2022. The number fell below the 4.48 per cent forecast from economists surv
item.content:encoded:
China reported 4.3 per cent gross domestic product growth in the second quarter of 2026 shortly after recording strong, sustained momentum in exports – a contrast underscoring the need for policy support to address weak domestic demand and persistent economic imbalances.

The closely watched GDP figure, released by the National Bureau of Statistics (NBS) on Wednesday, was the slowest quarterly growth rate since the end of 2022. The number fell below the 4.48 per cent forecast from economists surveyed by Chinese financial data provider Wind.

The second-quarter growth – which marked a deceleration from the 5 per cent expansion recorded in the first three months of 2026 – brought China’s first-half growth to 4.7 per cent, according to the bureau.

China’s exports remained resilient despite disruptions to global shipping routes and energy supplies caused by the US-Israel war on Iran, with outbound shipments rising 27 per cent in June and 17.6 per cent for the first half of 2026, both on a year-on-year basis.

Beijing has set this year’s growth target at “4.5 to 5 per cent” – a slight shift from the “around 5 per cent” goal that had been the recent norm – as the economy grapples with numerous headwinds, including weak domestic demand, a prolonged downturn in the property market and rising external risks.

Officials said that the GDP growth in the first half of the year laid the groundwork for achieving China’s full-year target.

“The central government will also introduce targeted, more proactive and effective policies in response to changing circumstances,” said Mao Shengyong, deputy director of the statistics bureau.

“There are solid conditions and strong support for achieving the main annual targets, especially the economic growth target.”

Retail sales, a major gauge of consumption, rose by 1 per cent year on year in June, beating the 0.09 per cent growth projection from the Wind poll. The metric fell by 0.6 per cent year on year in May, the first decline since China lifted Covid-19 lockdowns at the end of 2022.

Overall fixed-asset investment – covering major items such as infrastructure, manufacturing and property construction – dropped by 5.7 per cent year on year in the first six months of 2026, widening the 4.1 per cent decline recorded in the January-May period.

This rebound can’t be sustained without swifter policy action

Tan Junyu, Coface

“Overall, the second-quarter data highlights a deepening divergence between domestic and external demand,” said Tan Junyu, regional economist for North Asia at trade credit insurer Coface.

While export performance was being underpinned by artificial intelligence hardware and green-tech demand, he said, domestic demand was being weighed on by the property slump. However, he added, last month’s improvement in retail sales could be a sign of stabilisation, with consumption supported by accelerated trade-in subsidies.

“But this rebound can’t be sustained without swifter policy action,” Tan said.

Zhang Zhiwei, president and chief economist at Pinpoint Asset Management, said he was not sure whether the slower economic growth in the second quarter would prompt the government to “significantly” change its policy stance in the coming months.

Citing robust growth in the first quarter and China’s resilient exports, he said Beijing was “still on track to deliver growth in line with the official target.”

Zhang pointed to this month’s meeting of the Politburo, a high-level decision-making body of the ruling Communist Party, as an event to watch.

“The Politburo meeting in the last week of July will shed light on the policymakers’ guidance,” he said.

At its July meetings, the Politburo typically assesses the country’s first-half economic performance and charts the course for the remainder of the year.

Following the release of the second-quarter GDP data, several banks trimmed their forecasts for China’s economic growth this year. Morgan Stanley cut its full-year GDP forecast by 0.2 percentage points to 4.6 per cent, while ANZ also revised its 2026 projection down to 4.6 per cent from 4.8 per cent.

Su Jian, an economics professor at Peking University, said Beijing was unlikely to roll out broad-based, aggressive stimulus measures in the coming months.

That said, as investment growth continues to weaken and authorities seek to prevent systemic risks, Beijing could unveil further support measures centred on “new infrastructure” in the second half of the year, he added.

Property investment, an entrenched drag on economic growth, fell 18 per cent in the first half of the year, compared with a fall of 16.2 per cent in the first five months, according to the NBS.

Industrial output grew by 5.3 per cent year on year in June, compared with an increase of 4.5 per cent in May.

Elsewhere, China’s overall urban unemployment rate stood at 5 per cent in June, compared with 5.1 per cent in May.

“We expect China’s growth to strengthen modestly in the second half of the year, supported by a more proactive fiscal stance,” said Su Yue, principal economist for China with the Economist Intelligence Unit.

She said reconstruction spending after recent floods and a favourable base effect from last year should help stabilise growth, while technology-related exports are likely to remain resilient despite mounting external headwinds.

Su said “rebuilding household confidence will become increasingly important” ahead of the party’s next national congress, a high-profile event in next year’s political calendar, adding she expected policymakers to place greater emphasis on boosting consumption.

At a separate press conference on Wednesday afternoon, officials from the People’s Bank of China noted that credit was shifting from bank lending towards bond financing, reflecting the economy’s ongoing transition from property and infrastructure construction to new growth drivers.

The central bank pledged to “where necessary, increase the quotas of [structural monetary policy] tools based on market demand”, while optimising policy elements to boost support for domestic demand, tech innovation and small businesses.

Sheng Laiyun, former deputy head of the NBS, said more attention should be paid to the gap between official economic data and households’ lived experience at a forum hosted by Tsinghua University’s Academic Centre for Chinese Economic Practice and Thinking last week.

He also called for additional policy measures to be rolled out as early as possible in the second half of the year, prioritising investment, consumption and income distribution reform, according to a social media post by the organiser.

item.title: [SCMP] Meet CXMT’s Zhu Yiming: the engineer building China’s answer to global memory-chip giants
item.description: When ChangXin Memory Technologies (CXMT) – China’s attempt to build a home-grown rival to the world’s top makers of memory chips – kicked off one of the country’s most anticipated initial public offerings (IPOs) of 2026 last week, the spotlight fell on its founder Zhu Yiming. The Shanghai offering, set to start taking subscriptions on Thursday, aims to raise 29.5 billion yuan (US$4.4 billion). And some analysts predict that CXMT could eventually command a market value of 3 trillion yuan, placing
item.content:encoded:
When ChangXin Memory Technologies (CXMT) – China’s attempt to build a home-grown rival to the world’s top makers of memory chips – kicked off one of the country’s most anticipated initial public offerings (IPOs) of 2026 last week, the spotlight fell on its founder Zhu Yiming.

The Shanghai offering, set to start taking subscriptions on Thursday, aims to raise 29.5 billion yuan (US$4.4 billion). And some analysts predict that CXMT could eventually command a market value of 3 trillion yuan, placing it among China’s most valuable listed technology companies.

The listing will be a milestone for Zhu, who reportedly pledged in 2018 not to draw a salary from CXMT until the memory-chip project became profitable – which it did last year.
