Yet Zhu’s wager on Chinese memory chips began long before CXMT. More than two decades ago, when he was a Silicon Valley engineer with a chip design but little money, Zhu predicted that the centre of the global memory industry would eventually shift towards mainland China.

In emails retained by Li Jun, one of his earliest investors, and later reproduced in a Tsinghua University alumni publication, Zhu observed that the industry had migrated from the US to Japan, and then to South Korea.

“It is time for China to play [a] role in this industry,” he wrote.

His long-term ambition was even bolder: “to be the biggest memory designer and maker in China”.

Zhu’s interest in technology had already paid off. While studying physics at Tsinghua, he took programming jobs rather than the tutoring work common among students, making 300,000 yuan a year by the late 1990s – when, according to Tsinghua’s alumni magazine, the average annual income was about 10,000 yuan. As the software he wrote moved closer to the underlying hardware, he became fascinated by the integrated circuits that China largely relied on foreign companies to supply.

He later studied electronic engineering at Stony Brook University in New York and worked for technology companies in the United States, including memory-chip developer Monolithic System Technologies.

By the summer of 2004, Zhu had decided to start his own company.

At a Starbucks in Silicon Valley, he made a second pitch to Li, a fellow Tsinghua alumnus and technology investor. Their first meeting had produced little interest. This time, Zhu had quit his job, registered GigaDevice Semiconductor and prepared a design for a memory chip that promised lower costs and power consumption.

The pitch persuaded Li to introduce Zhu to another investor, who provided US$100,000 and rented his garage to the fledgling company for cheap. Zhu and fellow engineer Shu Qingming turned their design into a working sample there and applied for a patent.

While the garage origins followed a familiar Silicon Valley script, what came next was more characteristically Zhu: move quickly, even when the money was not quite enough.

In 2005, a group of investors, many of them Tsinghua alumni, assembled US$920,000 for GigaDevice’s first major fundraising round. The target had been US$1 million, but Zhu decided it was close enough and returned to Beijing to start the company.

The firm’s first technology struggled to win over large electronics makers, which were reluctant to rely on an unknown Chinese supplier. GigaDevice changed course and secured its first domestic order from chip designer Rockchip. The contract, worth only 100,000 yuan, put its memory technology into chips for MP3 players.

GigaDevice eventually grew into one of China’s leading chip-design companies and listed in Shanghai in August 2016.

For many founders, that would have been the capstone of a successful entrepreneurial career. For Zhu, it coincided with the start of a more ambitious second act.

That year, he joined forces with the Hefei government to launch the project that became CXMT. While GigaDevice could design chips and outsource their production, CXMT would have to build multibillion-dollar factories, master manufacturing processes and compete with companies that had decades of expertise.

In 2018, Zhu stepped back from GigaDevice to focus on CXMT – and made his no-salary pledge.

The most important thing in entrepreneurship is to stay alive

Zhu Yiming

CXMT began producing its first self-designed mainstream memory chip in 2019, giving mainland China a foothold in a market that was almost entirely supplied by foreign manufacturers.

One of Zhu’s important early moves was to secure licences to patents originally developed by Qimonda, a failed German memory maker spun out of Infineon Technologies. This gave CXMT an established intellectual-property base and reduced the risk of disputes with overseas rivals.

CXMT nevertheless remained loss-making for years as it built factories, recruited engineers and strived to improve its production yield.

Its fortunes changed dramatically as artificial intelligence data centres drove demand for memory to record levels. The boom also attracted Apple, which has reportedly tested CXMT chips for use in devices sold in China.

The IPO has also cast an earlier investment by Alibaba Group Holding in a new light. Its Hong Kong-listed shares jumped as much as 17.1 per cent last week amid a broader rally in Chinese AI stocks, just as its exposure to CXMT drew renewed attention.

Subsidiaries of Alibaba own just under 5 per cent of the chipmaker after investing about 7.6 billion yuan since 2021, making Alibaba its largest industry investor. Alibaba is the owner of the South China Morning Post.

Despite CXMT’s success, Zhu has not yet won his original wager. South Korea’s Samsung Electronics and SK Hynix, along with US firm Micron Technology, still control more than 90 per cent of the global DRAM market and remain ahead in scale and advanced products. CXMT must continue spending heavily on manufacturing technology while navigating cyclical prices and tightening restrictions on China’s access to semiconductor equipment.

The engineer who once handled technology, fundraising and early customer development now chairs a manufacturer that employed nearly 19,300 people at the end of 2025.

In an interview published by Tsinghua’s alumni magazine after GigaDevice’s listing, Zhu attributed the company’s success to choosing the right market and the hard work of his employees. He also expressed a dogged determinism that is likely to serve him well as he continues working on his prediction about the industry’s migration to China.

“The most important thing in entrepreneurship is to stay alive,” he said.

item.title: [SCMP] Rolex Oyster celebrates 100 years of revolutionising watchmaking
item.description: Few timepieces can genuinely claim to have changed the course of watchmaking. As it celebrates its centenary this year, the Oyster stands not merely as Rolex’s longest-lasting creation, but also as the conceptual blueprint for the modern mechanical wristwatch. Its influence is embedded so deeply into contemporary horology that its once-radical ideas – water resistance, everyday durability – are now taken for granted. Until the late 19th century, wristwatches had been regarded as feminine accesso
item.content:encoded:
Few timepieces can genuinely claim to have changed the course of watchmaking. As it celebrates its centenary this year, the Oyster stands not merely as Rolex’s longest-lasting creation, but also as the conceptual blueprint for the modern mechanical wristwatch. Its influence is embedded so deeply into contemporary horology that its once-radical ideas – water resistance, everyday durability – are now taken for granted.

Until the late 19th century, wristwatches had been regarded as feminine accessories while men relied on pocket watches that were protected from dust and moisture. Warfare transformed that convention. Soldiers, needing easy access to the time in combat, began strapping pocket watches to their wrists, giving rise to the “wristlet”. That practicality caught on beyond the battlefield, and, by the first world war, the wristwatch had firmly established itself among men, too, even though functional drawbacks still existed.

In 1926, Rolex was still far from the global force it would become. Founded in London in 1905 as Wilsdorf & Davis, by German businessman Hans Wilsdorf and his brother-in-law, Alfred Davis, the company had spent its early decades building credibility in precision timekeeping while harbouring ambitions to compete as an equal with long-established, prestigious Swiss manufacturers such as Vacheron Constantin, Blancpain and Patek Philippe.

In 1910, a Rolex became the first wristwatch to receive a chronometric certificate from the Official Watch Rating Centre in Switzerland. Four years later, another achieved the Class A certificate from Britain’s Kew Observatory, an honour previously reserved for marine chronometers. Precision was no longer the question. Durability was.

Wilsdorf understood that accuracy meant little if a watch could not survive real-life challenges. His answer arrived in 1926, by which time Rolex had relocated its base to Switzerland. The innovation that went into producing the Oyster was not simply water resistance – waterproof watches had existed in experimental forms before – but the creation of a hermetically sealed case designed for daily, long-term wear.

Rolex achieved this by engineering a system in which the bezel, case back and winding crown were screwed tightly against the middle case, forming an airtight architecture that protected the movement from water, dust and debris. The name captured the idea; like an oyster protecting the pearl within, the case became a shell safeguarding the movement. The watch could accompany its wearer through rain, travel, sport and work.

The new watch case’s first serious test came in 1927, when British swimmer Mercedes Gleitze attempted to cross the English Channel wearing a Rolex Oyster. After more than 10 hours in freezing waters, she and the watch emerged, the latter still running.

From there, the Oyster became a companion to other adventurers. On Malcolm Campbell’s land-speed record attempts in the 1920s and 30s, it endured extreme vibration, further proving that a wristwatch could maintain accuracy far beyond the controlled conditions of the watchmaker’s bench.

It accompanied pioneering aviators such as Chuck Yeager, surviving the thin air, freezing temperatures and physical strain of early high-altitude flight. It reached the highest peaks with mountaineers, proving that mechanical precision could endure where human performance itself was pushed to its limits.

Still, one challenge remained. Like all watches of its era, a Rolex required daily winding. That changed in 1931, with the Perpetual rotor. Using a freely oscillating weight powered by the motion of the wrist, Rolex created a self-winding mechanism that continuously replenished the mainspring. It eliminated the need for manual winding, reducing wear on the crown and preserving the integrity of the waterproof case.

The Oyster case and Perpetual rotor transformed the wristwatch into something new: an object that not only endured, but maintained itself, and in the process formed the foundation for an entire ecosystem of timepieces.

The Rolex Explorer, launched in 1953, was born from it. Shaped by high-altitude expeditions, it prioritised reliability and legibility – essential in low-oxygen conditions, freezing temperatures and reduced visibility.

The Submariner (also 1953) extended these principles beneath the waves, combining water resistance – initially rated to 100 metres and later 200 metres – with a rotating bezel that enabled divers to track elapsed immersion time. Its unidirectional 60-minute bezel became a safety feature, preventing accidental adjustment during a dive. High-contrast luminous markers and hands ensured legibility in low-light conditions.

The 1950s also saw the launch of the Rolex GMT-Master. Designed for Pan American World Airways pilots, it displays two time zones simultaneously and has a 24-hour rotating bezel and dedicated GMT hand that transform the watch into a navigation tool.

The Rolex Day-Date was launched in 1956 exclusively in precious metals – gold or platinum – setting it apart from the brand’s tool watches. It would become associated with political and cultural figures, most notably a United States president, Lyndon B. Johnson.

Later came the Rolex Cosmograph Daytona (1963), which was designed for racing drivers, combining a tachymeter bezel for calculating average speed, a precision timing mechanism for lap measurement and high-legibility sub-dials for use under pressure. Named after the Daytona International Speedway in Florida, its identity was amplified by celebrities such as actor and racing-car driver Paul Newman.

Other brands noticed how the watches of the Oyster family had become companions built to travel, endure and participate in life, and rose to the challenge. Omega refined water-resistant sports models and professional timing tools; Blancpain defined early modern diving capability with the Fifty Fathoms collection; IWC developed robust, engineer-led pilot’s watches; and Jaeger-LeCoultre explored durable, utility-driven complications such as those of the Memovox series.

But it had been the ingenuity of the Oyster case, 100 years ago, that opened those doors for others to follow.

To mark the centenary, Rolex has introduced commemorative Oyster Perpetual models that remain faithful to the design’s restrained identity. A 41mm version pairs a slate dial with yellow-gold detailing while a 36mm model references classic dial textures with subtle anniversary markings. Discreet engravings, including a “100” crown detail, acknowledge the milestone without disrupting the Oyster’s defining understatement.

The celebration has extended beyond product launches. In Shanghai, Rolex presented the “Oyster Story”, an immersive exhibition at the West Bund Dome that ran from June 10 to 28. The exhibition traced the Oyster’s evolution through archival pieces, technical components and interactive installations that revealed the engineering behind the design.

One hundred years on, the Oyster’s innovations have become industry standards, absorbed into the very language of watchmaking.

item.title: [SCMP] Philippines’ plan to fortify coastal defences: deterrence or target?
item.description: A Philippine coastal defence project in Palawan province could complicate Chinese operations near the Spratly Islands, but is unlikely to close Manila’s military gap with Beijing and could turn one of the country’s most important naval hubs into a more prominent target in a crisis, analysts have said. On Monday, the Puerto Princesa City Council approved a Department of National Defence proposal allowing the Armed Forces of the Philippines to establish Coastal Defence Regiment assets and logistic
item.content:encoded:
A Philippine coastal defence project in Palawan province could complicate Chinese operations near the Spratly Islands, but is unlikely to close Manila’s military gap with Beijing and could turn one of the country’s most important naval hubs into a more prominent target in a crisis, analysts have said.

On Monday, the Puerto Princesa City Council approved a Department of National Defence proposal allowing the Armed Forces of the Philippines to establish Coastal Defence Regiment assets and logistical facilities at Oyster Bay in Bahile village, one of the Philippine Navy’s most strategic bases.

Oyster Bay is a key staging and resupply hub for Philippine outposts in the Kalayaan Island Group – Manila’s name for the features it occupies in the disputed Spratly Islands in the South China Sea.

The defence ministry has not publicly disclosed what assets will be deployed, though local officials said during council deliberations on Monday that the project could involve missile interceptor or shore-based anti-ship missile systems.

During the session, Councillor Herbert Dilig voiced support for the proposal but sought clarification on the military assets planned for the site.

“To be clear, I support this. Our country needs it, especially given the current security situation,” he said. “But the purpose is dual: to protect and to invite something that needs protection.”

The Philippines operates the Indian-made BrahMos supersonic shore-based anti-ship missile system, acquired under a US$375 million deal signed in 2022, and has also bought the Israeli-made Spyder air defence system, designed to counter aircraft, drones and incoming missiles.

Experts said deploying BrahMos missiles and interceptor systems in Palawan would strengthen deterrence without fundamentally altering the Philippines’ defence posture in the South China Sea.

“It expands it to the west, closer to the Spratlys, but will not meaningfully address the country’s asymmetric gap with China or its ability to seriously threaten Chinese military assets in the Pacific,” said Hunter Marston, director of the Lowy Institute’s Southeast Asia programme and lead on its Asia Power Index.

While Marston said the deployment would strengthen deterrence, he added that it would make a frontline location more exposed in a future crisis. He also warned it could heighten sensitivities with Vietnam, which has overlapping claims in the Spratlys, unless Manila reassured Hanoi the move was purely defensive.

Sylwia Monika Gorska, a political analyst at Britain’s University of Central Lancashire specialising in international relations, said the plan would strengthen the Philippines’ ability to put Chinese surface combatants and support vessels at greater risk around the Spratlys.

Palawan played a critical operational role as the main staging area for sustaining Philippine outposts in the Kalayaan Island Group, she said, with Oyster Bay serving as the key logistics and support hub for those missions.

Gorska added that the deployment would force China’s navy to allocate additional surveillance, targeting and strike resources so its surface forces could operate with lower risk, raising the military costs Beijing would have to weigh in any future use of force around the Spratlys.

The trade-off, she said, was that Oyster Bay itself would become a more valuable military target in a potential conflict.

“Once a conventional conflict begins, fixed infrastructure such as Oyster Bay inevitably becomes a higher-priority target. The key question then is whether the AFP [Philippine military] can restore the force’s combat effectiveness after the base comes under attack,” she said.

The Philippines is accelerating its 10-year Re-Horizon 3 military modernisation programme, prioritising an integrated air and missile defence system, advanced missile systems, mid-range launchers and enhanced radar capabilities to strengthen deterrence amid growing regional security challenges.

American and Philippine troops have previously tested the M142 High Mobility Artillery Rocket System in Palawan, which US military officials have described as “key terrain” because it controls access between the Sulu Sea and the South China Sea.

Vina Nadjibulla, vice-president of research and strategy at the Asia Pacific Foundation of Canada, said BrahMos was more relevant to deterring a high-end military escalation than managing day-to-day incidents at sea.

She said the system had to sit within a broader architecture that included coastguard capacity, maritime surveillance, legal and diplomatic pressure, alliance support, resilient resupply and credible crisis-management channels.

For Nadjibulla, the goal is not simply to place missiles closer to the front line but to create uncertainty for any adversary.

“If an adversary cannot be sure where the launchers are, whether they can be destroyed quickly, or what other assets are feeding them targeting data, then deterrence becomes more credible,” she said.

“The Philippines is still building that ecosystem. The BrahMos deployment is important, but its real strategic value will depend on whether Manila can connect missiles, sensors, logistics, command-and-control and partner support into a resilient archipelagic defence network.”

Marston agreed that additional missile deployments would enhance the Philippines’ deterrent posture, noting that while Manila already had BrahMos batteries in Luzon, closer to China’s growing presence around Scarborough Shoal, a new site in Palawan would expand the armed forces’ operational coverage across the South China Sea.

“⁠The new missile sites are a net positive for the Philippines as forward deployment and expanded deterrence outweigh the risks of high-value targets,” he said. “By spreading out its missile sites, the Philippines also makes it harder for the PLA [China’s military] to wipe out all of those potential targets.”

Gorska said the deployment’s greatest contribution might be time, because slowing Chinese naval operations could create opportunities for Manila to reinforce exposed positions, coordinate with allies and make political decisions before a crisis developed further.

She said long-range anti-ship missiles were only as effective as a commander’s ability to detect and track enemy vessels, making maritime domain awareness just as critical as the missiles themselves.

Manila’s security partners could provide this service, Gorska added, with the United States contributing intelligence sharing and alliance cooperation, and Japan strengthening Philippine surveillance through coastal radars and information sharing.

More broadly, Nadjibulla said the proposed missile deployment reflected a shift in Manila’s defence strategy towards investing in national capabilities that reinforced its legal claims and raised the costs of coercion.

“This is not a magic solution,” she said. “BrahMos changes the military equation at the margins, especially in a crisis or high-end contingency. It does not eliminate the asymmetry with China, and it does not remove the need for diplomacy, crisis-management mechanisms and careful signalling.”

item.title: [SCMP] US-China AI rivalry becomes a battle to export competing tech governance visions
item.description: The global race for artificial intelligence has transformed into a direct contest of governance, with the United States and China actively exporting rival models of technology, funding and regulation to the rest of the world. In the West African state of Burkina Faso, dozens of young people gather at a training centre in the capital, Ouagadougou, to learn the basics of digital technology and AI, in a US-sponsored effort to develop local digital skills based on the American tech ecosystem. At the
item.content:encoded:
The global race for artificial intelligence has transformed into a direct contest of governance, with the United States and China actively exporting rival models of technology, funding and regulation to the rest of the world.

In the West African state of Burkina Faso, dozens of young people gather at a training centre in the capital, Ouagadougou, to learn the basics of digital technology and AI, in a US-sponsored effort to develop local digital skills based on the American tech ecosystem.

At the Suzhou Industrial Park Institute of Vocational Technology in eastern China’s Jiangsu province, trainees from Indonesia and Laos learn about the digital transformation and intelligent industrial automation, along with the Chinese perspective on AI.

As the AI race intensifies, these two leading nations in the field are competing over whose approach will dominate globally. It is a trajectory that raises pertinent questions about the future of AI governance and the risks posed by the technology.

China’s push to address the global gaps in AI governance is in the spotlight at a high-level forum that opened in Shanghai on Friday with a personal appearance by President Xi Jinping. The four-day event concludes on Monday.

Xi urged the world to adopt an inclusive approach, encouraging collaboration without rivalries in his Friday speech.

“AI development should not be a solo performance by a single country, but a symphony of international cooperation,” he said, adding that safety risks must be contained.

He also promised 5,000 “opportunities in AI training and seminar programmes”, and pledged to work with global organisations, including the Association of Southeast Asian Nations, the League of Arab States and the African Union to develop international AI application cooperation centres.

It was the first time that the Chinese leader attended the World AI Conference and High-Level Meeting on Global AI Governance since the annual event was first held in 2018.

Ahead of the conference, Kyle Chan, a fellow with the Brookings Institution’s John L. Thornton China Centre in Washington, pointed out that the two countries faced similar challenges.

“Both the US and China are trying to find a balance between supporting AI development and mitigating AI-related risks,” he told the South China Morning Post.

“Xi’s appearance at this year’s World AI Conference in Shanghai signals the importance that China places on AI.”

Chan said that China had been active in pushing for a global AI governance framework, even going as far as setting up the World AI Cooperation Organisation in Shanghai.

Though AI cooperation was among the issues US President Donald Trump was supposed to discuss with Xi during his May visit to Beijing, many details of that conversation remain unknown.

Gaps are likely to persist between the US and China as they engage in formal dialogues on AI governance, with each trying to win the other over to their viewpoint, according to Chan.

“AI is absolutely central to both Beijing and Washington,” he said. “The question will be whether the two AI superpowers can cooperate on this transformative technology that is reshaping the world.”

Washington and Beijing have adopted distinct strategies to the development of AI, with the US pursuing a market-driven approach while China’s preference is an open-source environment.

In the US, AI development is backed by huge outlays of venture capital in an approach that brings together the country’s technology giants and researchers in top universities with the backing of the government.

The flagship US$500 billion Stargate Project – a government-supported joint venture that includes SoftBank, OpenAI, Oracle and investment company MGX – is designed to give the US global leadership in the AI race through a national network of data centres.

China’s approach, articulated in its five-year plans, emphasises “AI for good” and “AI for all” as undergirding principles and favours cost-effective models such as the domestically developed DeepSeek. It also wants to see a quick deployment of AI to industries and services.

In both models, regulation and governance remain a work in progress. So far, the US has no federal law that addresses all the issues concerning AI. Applicable rules are vested in several federal agencies that swing between excessive restriction and deregulation.

In China, regulations and governance are run by the Cyberspace Administration of China. It has taken a targeted approach that applies specific measures to given scenarios, aimed at making AI useful and safe, without threatening social stability.

Rival visions

Both countries are exporting their rival visions for the technology to the rest of the world, with the US treating AI as a national security asset and banning chip and software exports to certain countries, such as China.

Beijing has responded to these export controls by turning to open-source software and cheaper hardware, seeking partnerships in the Global South with few or no political strings attached, and positioning itself as a responsible AI user.

For both countries, the competition is unfolding as a global race to build more data centres and expand the power grids needed to sustain them.

US companies including Amazon, Google, Microsoft and Meta are working with infrastructure companies such as Equinix and Digital Realty to roll out new data centres in all regions of the world.

China is doing the same under its Belt and Road Initiative, driven by the needs of Huawei, ZTE, Alibaba, Tencent and other tech companies, which are working with data centre builders including Chindata Group and GDS Holdings.

Alibaba owns the South China Morning Post.

While the US and China have acknowledged the need for some cooperation to mitigate AI risks, they have yet to produce modalities they can agree upon.

China stresses multilateral cooperation

Both tech leaders were among the countries represented at the inaugural UN Global AI Dialogue in Geneva on July 7 to discuss governance. AI-assisted cyberattacks, along with its misuse in biological and chemical research, were among the concerns raised, along with the need for guidelines.

At the conference, China’s Industry and Information Technology Minister Li Lecheng stressed the importance of multilateral cooperation on AI safety. While innovation should be encouraged, collaboration was vital to strengthen governance and ensure that AI benefited all, he said.

Katie Strickland, White House senior adviser for international science and technology policy, told the conference that the US government was working with lawmakers on a national regulatory policy to protect children, discourage censorship and respect intellectual property.

In doing this, it was relying on the leadership of the tech industry, she said.

“Industry is intimately aware of the state, scope, and trajectory of AI developments. Government is not. Voluntary cooperation between the two is the only AI security approach agile enough to meet challenges as they arise without stifling innovation.”

In some Washington policy circles, Beijing’s push to strengthen AI governance is viewed with suspicion – an attempt to impose its vision on the world.

In an analysis published by the Carnegie Endowment for International Peace on May 21, Arindrajit Basu observed that “China views the governance of AI through the lens of entrenching state control, rather than safeguarding individual liberties”.

China’s aim was a global diffusion of its values and standards, he wrote.

item.title: [SCMP] ‘Gateway to Asean’: South Korea bets on Vietnam hub for food expansion
item.description: South Korean food exporters are no longer treating Vietnam as just another market for kimchi, instant noodles and premium fruit. As Southeast Asia emerges as one of South Korea’s most important trading regions, Seoul is positioning Vietnam as a logistics and distribution base for a wider push to turn the popularity of Korean culture into sustained demand for Korean food across the region. But analysts said the strategy would depend on more than hallyu-driven curiosity, with exporters needing to
item.content:encoded:
South Korean food exporters are no longer treating Vietnam as just another market for kimchi, instant noodles and premium fruit.

As Southeast Asia emerges as one of South Korea’s most important trading regions, Seoul is positioning Vietnam as a logistics and distribution base for a wider push to turn the popularity of Korean culture into sustained demand for Korean food across the region.

But analysts said the strategy would depend on more than hallyu-driven curiosity, with exporters needing to pair cultural appeal with cold-chain reliability, local adaptation and a clearer premium proposition for increasingly selective Southeast Asian consumers.

South Korea’s Ministry of Agriculture, Food and Rural Affairs said on July 7 that it had selected Vietnam as a regional hub for expanding the logistics, distribution and supply chains of Korean food exports to Southeast Asia, following the Asean K-Food Fair 2026 in Hanoi.

The plan includes a new multimodal logistics hub in Hanoi, built jointly by the ministry and the Korea Agro-Fisheries and Food Trade Corp – its distribution agency – to help Korean food exporters cut transport costs, preserve product quality and serve growing regional demand linked to the hallyu wave.

The Hanoi hub will feature advanced systems to store and maintain the quality of fresh agricultural and livestock products, including rapid cooling and freezing rooms and cold-chain packaging.

The facility will allow Korean exporters to bring “not only high-value fresh Korean agricultural products like strawberries, grapes and pears but also premium livestock products to local consumers in Vietnam”.

The fair also gave Seoul a platform to deepen commercial links: 45 Korean food exporters met 107 Asean importers and signed 46 memorandums of understanding worth US$21 million.

Food is still a smaller part of South Korea’s export machine, but it is growing quickly: K-Food Plus exports reached a record US$13.62 billion in 2025, including US$10.41 billion in agri-food products, and Seoul has set a US$16 billion target for 2026.

Southeast Asia is one of South Korea’s biggest trading partners and its fourth-largest consumer goods export market, accounting for 15 per cent of total exports.

Korean exports of agri-food, cosmetics, household goods, fashion and pharmaceuticals to the region reached US$6.9 billion last year.

‘Hub and spokes’ network

Seoul’s partnership with Hanoi reflects Southeast Asia’s importance as a strategic growth region for Korean food companies, according to Brian JY Yoo, CEO of CareerLab and director of trade analysis firm Dallant Consulting Group.

“From my consulting experience, Korean seafood companies are no longer viewing Vietnam solely as an end market. Increasingly, they see Vietnam as their operational gateway into Asean,” Yoo said, drawing on his work with seafood exporters expanding into Vietnam and the wider region.

Vietnam’s growing middle class, modern retail channels, strong tourism industry and proximity to neighbouring markets made it especially important, he said.

The growing Vietnamese community in South Korea also strengthened cultural exchange, creating demand for authentic products after people returned home, while K-pop, K-dramas, K-beauty and social media continued to support long-term demand among younger consumers, Yoo said.

Hyun-je Woo, CEO of export company Mirako Trade, described Southeast Asia as a “rapidly evolving” region for South Korean businesses.

Woo said while demand had once been driven largely by the cultural spillover of the hallyu wave, Southeast Asian consumers increasingly viewed K-food “as a premium segment that guarantees rigorous quality control”.

The real opportunity, he said, lay in a structural shift across the region that pushed consumers from price-sensitive buying towards value-based consumption, particularly in health and wellness.

“This makes the region not just a large market, but a highly scalable one for those of us who focus on quality and efficient logistics,” said Woo, who focuses on exports to emerging markets such as Southeast Asia.

Woo said the partnership with Vietnam would help South Korean exporters move away from costly direct shipping to individual Southeast Asian markets, and towards a “hub and spokes” network that could consolidate goods before regional distribution.

For temperature-sensitive products, he said, direct less-than-container load shipments were often too expensive, but a Vietnam hub would allow exporters to achieve “full-container-load economies of scale”.

Vietnam’s improving cold-chain infrastructure would also help shorten lead times, reduce costs and extend shelf life, Woo said.

Yoo cited Vietnam’s improving logistics network, with distribution centres around Ho Chi Minh City and the country’s north, that would allow South Korean exporters to consolidate shipments to regional destinations including Thailand, Cambodia, Malaysia and Singapore.

Increasing premium

Korean exporters are also looking beyond familiar staples such as instant noodles, snacks and kimchi.

As Korean food marts and convenience stores grow in popularity across Southeast Asia, the region could see stronger demand for Korean-style meal kits, according to Kyung-yeon Koh, a trade consultant and adviser for How2Go Korea.

“With meal kits being increasingly popular in Korea, I think there are also opportunities for these kits to reach Southeast Asia, where there is demand for not only K-food but also K-experience of enjoying a Korean meal,” Koh said.

While consumers valued authentic flavours, especially when Korean food was linked to the culture they encountered through entertainment, companies also aimed to broaden appeal by adapting products to local markets, including through halal certification, she added.

Beyond ready-to-eat meals, Yoo said premium seafood, seaweed, laver products, sauces and healthy beverages showed strong growth potential in Southeast Asia.

Korean exporters, he said, “increasingly differentiate themselves with processing technology, strict food safety systems, premium packaging and sustainability rather than competing only on price”.

Woo predicted the strongest growth would come from a broader health and wellness category, including functional foods, dietary supplements and inner-beauty items.

Such products had a high value-to-volume ratio, he said, which helped absorb logistics costs and deliver higher margins for exporters and local distributors.

Yoo was confident that Korean products would continue to command premium positioning because consumers associated them with food safety, high standards and innovation.

“Consumers purchasing Korean food are often buying trust and authenticity as much as the product itself,” he said.

item.title: [SCMP] Can Malaysia’s PAS deliver the votes in Negeri Sembilan despite Johor wipeout?
item.description: Malaysia’s largest Islamist party was wiped out in Johor’s state election last weekend, but PAS is now trying to turn that defeat into bargaining power. Its argument is that supporters who had no PAS candidate to vote for helped deliver a landslide victory to Barisan Nasional (BN), the coalition led by Umno, its long-time rival for Malay-Muslim voters. The two parties have spent years competing for influence among Malaysia’s Malay majority, but they have also explored cooperation when it serves
item.content:encoded:
Malaysia’s largest Islamist party was wiped out in Johor’s state election last weekend, but PAS is now trying to turn that defeat into bargaining power.

Its argument is that supporters who had no PAS candidate to vote for helped deliver a landslide victory to Barisan Nasional (BN), the coalition led by Umno, its long-time rival for Malay-Muslim voters.

The two parties have spent years competing for influence among Malaysia’s Malay majority, but they have also explored cooperation when it serves their political interests.

Fresh constituency-level data, however, raises doubts about whether PAS can reliably transfer its support to Umno when the two parties choose coordination over competition.

That question now matters in neighbouring Negeri Sembilan, where voters go to the polls on August 1 and BN has left 11 of the state’s 36 seats open for potential partners.

The opening became more pointed after BN announced on Wednesday night that it would contest only 25 constituencies, fuelling speculation that the remaining seats could be used to make room for PAS or other Malay-Muslim allies.

At the candidates’ announcement, Malaysia’s Foreign Minister Mohamad Hasan, who leads the state BN machinery, said the coalition would enter the election with an “understanding with friends”, but pointedly ruled out cooperation with Pakatan Harapan (PH), Prime Minister Anwar Ibrahim’s multi-ethnic reformist coalition and BN’s nominal ally at the federal level.

That has been read as an opening to other Malay-Muslim nationalist parties such as PAS and the rebranded Wawasan Negara Party, led by Opposition Leader Hamzah Zainuddin.

PAS President Abdul Hadi Awang had on Monday cited positive discussions with BN, but BN Chairman Ahmad Zahid Hamidi denied concluding any formal agreement.

“What was stated [by PAS] should not be accepted as an accurate statement, because we don’t have any agreement with PAS. Even if there are discussions, it’s just an understanding, we have not finalised anything,” Ahmad Zahid told reporters on Monday.

Dr Sivamurugan Pandian of the Universiti Sains Malaysia’s School of Social Sciences described the conflicting messages as a “strategic divergence”, with PAS promoting cooperation and Umno avoiding commitment to formally reviving the Muafakat Nasional pact – the parties’ brief 2019 alliance aimed at consolidating the Malay vote to counter PH.

“Both parties are keeping their options open,” Sivamurugan said.

The manoeuvring has triggered a rupture within Perikatan Nasional (PN) – PAS’ formal coalition – with Bersatu also revealing on Wednesday that it would contest under its own logo, after accusing PAS of pursuing talks with BN without consulting its partners.

Saturday’s nominations are expected to be the first test of whether PAS can deliver a transferable bloc of Malay-Muslim votes.

Inconclusive data

BN won 48 of 56 constituencies in the Johor election on July 11, a result PAS told reporters justified its strategy of asking supporters to vote for BN in seats where PN did not contest.

Yet, the voting data provided limited support for PAS’ claim to being the decisive factor. According to tabulations by electoral consulting group Tindak Malaysia, of the 23 seats that PN did not contest in 2026, only five changed hands – four to BN and one from Muda to PH.

Danesh Chacko, director of Tindak, said BN’s four gains had all been won by PH on pluralities, rather than outright majorities, in the 2022 state election. PAS had not contested any of those seats; Bersatu had stood in one and Gerakan in the other three.

“The result could reflect several factors, such as higher turnout, opposition fragmentation and former PN supporters choosing to vote BN independently, rather than a direct transfer of PAS votes,” Chacko said.

“At this stage, however, there is no clear evidence to conclude that former Gerakan or Bersatu voters shifted to BN because of PAS’s endorsement.”

Tindak data, sourced from Election Commission information, also showed that results in the four constituencies PAS contested in 2022 were inconclusive because BN already held all four, making it difficult to separate any vote transfer benefiting BN from PAS.

BN’s majorities in 2026 were far higher than PAS’ own vote share in 2022, while turnout increased in 2026 for some constituencies.

“Mathematically, a PAS transfer would have been nice to have, but it was not essential,” Chacko said.

PAS’ disciplined grass-roots network could nevertheless help BN, particularly in rural and semirural Malay-majority seats where relatively small shifts could determine the outcome, Sivamurugan said, describing Johor as a PAS-assisted “Malay tsunami”.

Chacko, however, pointed to the higher turnout in 2026 and seats where BN’s majorities exceeded PAS’ vote share.

“This means BN’s strength was strong enough to convert the previous group of voters who sat out from elections,” he said.

Longer-term strategy

For Ahmad Fauzi Abdul Hamid, a political-science professor at Universiti Sains Malaysia’s School of Distance Education, PAS’ “sacrifice” narrative should be seen as a longer-term coalition strategy.

“PAS recognises that its strongest support remains concentrated in northern and northeastern Peninsular Malaysia, limiting its ability to capture federal power independently,” he said.

Cooperation with Umno, Ahmad Fauzi added, offered access to constituencies and government beyond PAS’ traditional strongholds.

“The party is therefore prepared to accept Umno’s leadership and surrender immediate electoral opportunities for future seats, cabinet positions or political influence,” he said.

“Why not sacrifice now? These are just some seats, but we can get something bigger later.”

The move to present electoral retreat as service to Muslim unity also reflected PAS’ political adroitness in changing partners and accepting secondary status to advance its longer-term influence, Ahmad Fauzi noted.

Negeri Sembilan will provide a more direct test. BN enters the election holding 14 seats and needs 19 to govern alone. Contesting 25 seats would give the party a safety margin, Ahmad Fauzi noted.

“Meanwhile, they can then test the waters on cooperation with PAS and other parties in the other 11 they’re not contesting,” he said.

Danesh identified five Negeri Sembilan state seats – including the Chennah constituency held by Transport Minister Anthony Loke – as possible pathways for BN to gain the five additional seats it requires.

“The challenge here is most of the [five identified] seats were previously contested by non-PAS parties,” Danesh said, noting that PAS influence in these areas would be “limited” due to conflict with other PN component parties.

Observers say the Negeri Sembilan poll will determine whether PAS’ strategy will work, and the first signs could come on nomination day.

Indications of informal coordination could include PAS and Umno avoiding direct contests in selected seats, restrained campaigning or late candidate withdrawals that benefited one side, Sivamurugan noted.

Fauzi said surprises remained possible until nominations closed.

Ahmad Zahid, too, urged caution, warning on Monday that PAS “can change goalposts any time”.

item.title: [SCMP] Why did only 2 Hong Kong developers put up their hands for Northern Metropolis project?
item.description: Stretches of green farmland and shimmering ponds, winding village roads and sprawling brownfield logistics yards all lend Hung Shui Kiu in the New Territories a patchwork look of rustic Hong Kong standing in stark contrast to its glistening skyscrapers. This rural landscape is slated for radical transformation under the government’s ambitious Northern Metropolis blueprint and is primed to be reinvented as a modernised logistics hub in the near future. However, the premise of this transformation
item.content:encoded:
Stretches of green farmland and shimmering ponds, winding village roads and sprawling brownfield logistics yards all lend Hung Shui Kiu in the New Territories a patchwork look of rustic Hong Kong standing in stark contrast to its glistening skyscrapers.

This rural landscape is slated for radical transformation under the government’s ambitious Northern Metropolis blueprint and is primed to be reinvented as a modernised logistics hub in the near future.

However, the premise of this transformation relies heavily on market buy-in. The project faced its first litmus test with the closing of the site tender last Friday, which was also the government’s first new large-scale land disposal tender.

The tender has defied conservative expectations by drawing two submissions, comprising one solo bid and one massive joint venture.

“Even with only one bid, the government will treat it as a success,” said Hannah Jeong, head of valuation and advisory services at CBRE Hong Kong.

Others have hailed the turnout as a success given the current property downturn and noted that large consortiums made up of local and mainland Chinese firms would become the norm for Northern Metropolis developments due to the sheer scale and complexity of these multi-use plots.

This outcome, they said, had also set a reassuring precedent for the government’s coming mega-tenders, while providing a vital blueprint that would bolster investor confidence as future development parcels were rolled out in San Tin and Fanling North.

The results raise questions about whether momentum can be sustained for future tenders and what permutations may work well to optimise development. Will more players be encouraged to take on the risk or will they continue to hold back? Will the government adapt the terms of future tenders based on the recent outcome? Analysts say these are issues that will decide the development of the megaproject.

No market precedent

The Hung Shui Kiu pilot site marked the first land sale under the government’s new large-scale land disposal model for the Northern Metropolis. The authorities’ blueprint is to transform 30,000 hectares (74,130 acres) of land near the mainland border into an economic and residential hub.

As part of the first batch of the most construction-ready land within the megaproject, the roughly 11-hectare pilot site is designed for double duty, boosting the city’s housing supply while anchoring innovation and technology (I&T) industries right at the border.

Beyond this specific tender, the wider Hung Shui Kiu area is being strategically positioned as a high-end professional services and logistics hub. The master blueprint features dedicated zones for commerce, recreation and conservation, alongside a newly announced roughly 22-hectare university town area.

The cluster as a whole is designed to cultivate a mature, self-sustaining community by seamlessly connecting different sites within the megaproject.

The Northern Metropolis has been touted repeatedly as a top priority for the current administration, capped most recently by a visit by Xia Baolong, director of the Hong Kong and Macau Affairs Office (HKMAO), who underscored Beijing’s call to accelerate construction.

The initiative serves as a critical strategic test of whether Hong Kong can successfully reinvent its role within the country’s broader I&T landscape. City leader John Lee Ka-chiu is in the midst of a large-scale consultation exercise to draft Hong Kong’s own five-year plan that will align with national development, specifically Beijing’s blueprint covering 2026 to 2030.

Hence, last week’s tender was closely watched as a test of whether the government can persuade property companies to take on a broader role in delivering its economic development agenda.

“The central government was watching how the developers play their part,” an establishment source told the South China Morning Post, without elaborating.

The 10.5-hectare site under the tender comprises three residential plots and three enterprise and technology park sites, with capacity for about 3,120 homes and 280,000 square metres (3.01 million sq ft) of industrial floor space.

Unlike traditional land sales, bids are assessed under a two-envelope system, with 30 per cent weighting given to the land premium offered and 70 per cent to non-price factors, including proposals for technology park development and plans to attract strategic industries, investment and jobs.

Henderson Land Development submitted a stand-alone bid, while Sino Land partnered with five companies – China Merchants Land, China Resources Land (Overseas), China Overseas Land & Investment, CTG Investment and Chinese e-commerce firm JD.com.

Other major developers, including Sun Hung Kai Properties, New World Development and CK Asset Holdings, did not take part.

Against the backdrop of a sluggish economic recovery, industry observers had low expectations for the tender before the closing deadline, the SCMP learned.

Jeong, of CBRE Hong Kong, said the firm had expected all along a maximum of two bidders for the plot of land, adding that limited commercial feasibility and operational difficulties acted as the key deterrents for potential bidders.

“The three land sites in Hung Shui Kiu are either triangular or irregularly shaped,” she said, noting that these irregular layouts posed development challenges for firms.

She said that a major “operator gap” existed in meeting the enterprise and technology (E&T) requirements, noting that residential developers were struggling to identify suitable partners with a proven track record and the financial strength to meet the government’s stringent criteria, especially since the tender required the development of a site spanning about 10,190 square metres.

She added that overall returns became marginal once potential E&T losses were factored in, meaning the risk-adjusted return simply did not justify the massive capital and time commitment required.

Industry analysts and government officials viewed the final turnout of only two bidders as a clear success.

“Two bids for a project of this complexity and scale should be viewed as a qualified success, not a disappointment,” Vincent Cheung, managing director at Vincorn Consulting and Appraisal, said, noting that the pilot area was the first of its kind under an entirely new large-scale land disposal framework.

“There is no market precedent, no comparable benchmark transaction.”

He said the formation of a multiparty consortium bid showed that “the pairing of mainland state-owned enterprise capital, local developer execution capability and industrial anchors has now been validated as a workable structure”.

“It [the consortium bid] reflects a calibrated, rather than fearful, risk posture … in an environment of elevated interest rates and subdued residential sentiment, aggregating complementary competencies across housing, technology and logistics is a highly sophisticated response,” he said.

He added that rather than a sign of corporate weakness, multiparty risk distribution was a rational way to meet the government’s complex scoring criteria.

Conversely, he said, Henderson Land’s decision to bid unilaterally was strategically consistent with its position as one of the largest private landowners in the New Territories.

“This allows the developer to maximise the government’s premium offset mechanisms by leveraging its massive local land bank,” he said.

Cheung said that the broader outcome provided a constructive, if cautious, signal that the Northern Metropolis framework could successfully attract well-resourced consortiums.

Commenting a day after the tender closed, Secretary for Development Bernadette Linn Hon-ho said that unlike previous stand-alone residential or commercial land tenders, the new Northern Metropolis model had achieved the government’s goal of pairing traditional developers with industrial operators.

She said she was particularly pleased that the bids differed from past tenders for residential or commercial sites, which often attracted “homogeneous” bidders.

Government as matchmaker

However, asked whether the tender results had met Beijing’s expectations, a source said that the business sector had not faced much pressure as it was a free market and companies simply bid after calculating costs to ensure profitability.

The source added that the government was likely to have faced far more pressure than the private sector and authorities had been acting as matchmakers behind the scenes.

“They provided developers with lists of mainland artificial intelligence companies that have expressed interest in partnering on the project,” he said.

However, he said that because many of these tech firms were not household names and remained unfamiliar to the local commercial property sector, developers needed more time to assess them.

“The developers simply need more time to vet and get comfortable with these prospective tech partners,” the insider said.

He said that several major developers had conducted site visits and expressed initial interest in bidding, but many hit a bottleneck during discussions to create joint ventures.

He said that joint ventures with unfamiliar AI firms involved massive capital distribution, complex profit-sharing models and intricate legal agreements, processes that some consortiums simply could not finalise before the tight deadline.

He noted that some mainland AI firms were equally unfamiliar with Hong Kong’s large-scale land disposal frameworks, so many had adopted a “wait-and-see” approach, choosing to observe how this initial tender performed before committing to future tenders in the region.

Economist Simon Lee Siu-po said it was difficult for traditional developers to bid alone, adding that forming consortiums helped share the risk, especially for megaprojects.

He noted that back in the 2000s, several property developers tried to diversify and transform their businesses but ultimately failed.

“It is very difficult to get developers to pivot into industrial sectors because they are so accustomed to the traditional model of buying land, building properties and selling them,” he said, noting that developers were only now beginning to explore new business models.

Only the big boys will play?

Looking ahead to Fanling North and San Tin, the other two pilot areas earmarked for large-scale land disposal, the market expected the government to roll out the former first, given its more mature ancillary infrastructure.

“The objective conditions in San Tin are simply not mature enough yet,” another insider said, pointing out that critical transport infrastructure, such as some of the nearby proposed Northern Link railway stations, would not be ready until at least 2032.

He added that this prolonged infrastructure lag was expected to temporarily dampen developers’ appetite for bidding on San Tin plots.

The source said he expected the government would closely monitor the market’s response to the Hung Shui Kiu results and work to modify future tendering terms to better align with developer interest and financial realities.

“It was a highly, highly technical bid. Small players won’t have the appetite not just for risk but complexity; so it’s up to the big players to do their part,” the source said.

Kathy Lee, head of research and retail consultancy at Colliers, said the tender had successfully forced a structural evolution, drawing out cross-border joint ventures that were capable of executing long-term ecosystem building rather than conventional land banking.

Another source also warned that the market should brace for relatively few bidders, citing a total of fewer than five in the coming Northern Metropolis land sales, noting that the new tendering model represented a radical shift from traditional practices.

He added that historically, developers were only required to bid for land to build and sell flats. However, the current framework introduced a new, industry-driven style that bundles residential plots with information technology and industrial land, requiring property firms to partner directly with industrial operators.

“The industry needs time to adapt to this new model,” the source said.

The insider added that the sheer scale of these multi-use sites made it incredibly difficult for small to medium-sized developers to participate.

“These developers typically lack the immense capital required for such long-term, large-scale projects,” the source said, noting that the payback period for advanced industrial hubs was significantly longer compared with traditional residential developments, where profits were realised much faster through flat sales.

While forming consortiums offered a potential avenue for smaller developers to enter the Northern Metropolis market, the source noted they were likely to have very little say in the decision-making process.

As a result, smaller players were expected to pivot back to traditional investments such as urban redevelopment projects, where the development cycle was familiar and they could maintain full control over the asset.

“Only the major conglomerates can afford to enter the game now,” he said.

item.title: [SCMP] Anthropic, China and why Pax Silica architect thinks the US can keep the AI lead
item.description: The struggle between the US and China over who will lead in artificial intelligence is one of the defining competitions of our time. In this first part of our exclusive interview with a key player defining Washington’s policy on AI, US undersecretary of state for economic affairs Jacob Helberg discusses the ban on foreign access to Anthropic models and how the US can maintain its edge. The head of the US administration’s global initiative to exclude China from artificial intelligence supply chai
item.content:encoded:
The struggle between the US and China over who will lead in artificial intelligence is one of the defining competitions of our time. In this first part of our exclusive interview with a key player defining Washington’s policy on AI, US undersecretary of state for economic affairs Jacob Helberg discusses the ban on foreign access to Anthropic models and how the US can maintain its edge.

The head of the US administration’s global initiative to exclude China from artificial intelligence supply chains has defended Washington’s sudden restrictions on foreign access to Anthropic’s cutting-edge models, saying they were meant to safeguard the US and its allies.

“We do not want cyberattacks on our critical infrastructure or the critical infrastructure of our allies,” Jacob Helberg, US undersecretary of state for economic affairs, told the South China Morning Post in an exclusive interview.

Helberg, who leads Washington’s Pax Silica initiative – which also counts 22 other nations, plus the European Union, as signatories – said the administration had assessed how advanced AI models could be released safely.

“We undertook a process to make sure that we can have a sensible approach to releasing that technology in a way that is safe and secure for our critical infrastructure and our financial sector,” he said.

In early June, the Department of Commerce barred foreign nationals from using Anthropic’s Fable 5 and Mythos 5 models on unspecified national security grounds. Anthropic complied with the order, though access was restored three weeks later.

Washington is trying to balance preserving its AI lead over Beijing and addressing growing concerns over the security risks associated with the technology’s rapid development and deployment.

Under Pax Silica, members are supposed to work together to develop semiconductors and boost supply chains related to AI. While China is not explicitly a target of the programme, reducing “coercive dependencies” is listed as a key objective – widely seen as a reference to the country’s dominance in rare earths, which are critical to producing chips.

To further address security concerns, the White House on Tuesday launched Gold Eagle, a clearing house to coordinate the identification of cyber vulnerabilities with leading US AI firms.

The White House said the initiative brought together multiple government departments – including the Treasury, homeland security and defence – with industry partners to improve “exploit detection” and accelerate responses.

As Washington recalibrates its approach, Chinese President Xi Jinping is set to outline Beijing’s vision for global AI governance in a speech at the annual World Artificial Intelligence Conference in Shanghai on Friday.

Helberg said America’s private sector offered a strong path to technological leadership.

He argued that while China had pursued a state-led approach built around an “entirely in-house industrial strategy” of government-funded and government-operated projects, the US and its allies were leveraging “incredible companies that build transformative products that people really love”.

“We realise that the US government is not set up in a way to compete with the Chinese tit-for-tat on that same basis,” he added.

“We decided to lean into that strength and leverage the innovative, creative capacity and dynamism of our private sector.”

Helberg said the US continued to maintain an edge in AI, citing Chinese companies’ alleged use of “model distillation” to replicate the capabilities of top US firms.

“The US is still leading, and the evidence for that is that China is still distilling our models,” he said. “We obviously very strongly oppose it.”

The Trump administration – along with Anthropic and OpenAI – has accused Chinese companies including DeepSeek and Alibaba of using model distillation without authorisation to replicate cutting-edge models. The technique involves training smaller models with outputs generated by larger ones.

The Chinese government last week warned that Anthropic’s Claude Code posed “a serious threat” to Chinese users, while Alibaba barred employees from using the coding tool over security concerns.

Alibaba owns the South China Morning Post.

A senior Anthropic executive said on Wednesday that the United States held a six-to-nine month lead in frontier AI models and described his company’s Chinese rivals’ alleged use of model distillation as “adversarial”.

“We actually do have a pretty good handle on the lead. I think the six-to-nine-month figure is kind of an average if you look at models,” Tarun Chhabra, Anthropic’s head of national security policy, said during a discussion at the Aspen Security Forum.

Helberg acknowledged China’s growing technological capabilities, pointing to its progress in robotics and the strength of its manufacturing ecosystem.

“I think it’s very clear that China is very technologically formidable, and they have made very impressive inroads in robotics. They obviously have a very deep, vast manufacturing ecosystem,” he said.

The competition between the nations has also prompted Washington to adopt measures aimed at blocking China’s access to the most advanced AI chips.

The Trump administration has maintained tight restrictions on exports of high-end chips to China, while making a limited exception for Nvidia’s H200 graphic processing units – though Beijing has sought to discourage domestic companies from buying them.

A senior US official told lawmakers on Tuesday that Nvidia had shipped “very few” H200 chips to the Chinese mainland and Hong Kong.

“For the American people, the bottom line is very few shipments against licences for H200s and equivalents have taken place,” said Jeffrey Kessler, the US under secretary of commerce for industry and security, at a House Foreign Affairs Committee hearing.

Congress is also pursuing additional measures, with three major China-focused export control bills set to be included in the next National Defence Authorisation Act, the annual defence policy bill.

Helberg hoped that despite the intensifying rivalry, the expected US-China dialogue on AI governance would help identify areas for “constructive engagement” between the two sides.

“Our goal is to pursue these engagements in good faith and work to restate our views and hear Chinese views,” he told the SCMP.

The two countries agreed to launch formal discussions on AI governance following the meeting between Trump and the Chinese leader in Beijing in May.

However, few details have emerged since the announcement.

The Chinese embassy in Washington said last week that China and the US “need to work together” to advance AI governance and ensure the technology better served social progress and common welfare.

item.title: [SCMP] Does Beijing own Batanes? Why Chinese scholars’ claim is a warning shot to Manila
item.description: Chinese scholars have drawn fierce pushback from Manila by claiming Beijing should have sovereignty over the Batanes Islands, while analysts said the academic assertions served as a warning shot amid Japanese-Philippine maritime border talks. The archipelago, located between Taiwan and the northernmost tip of the Philippine island of Luzon, was the focus of an academic discussion on June 30 at Jinan University in Guangzhou. At the meeting, scholars from different institutions, including the Chin
item.content:encoded:
Chinese scholars have drawn fierce pushback from Manila by claiming Beijing should have sovereignty over the Batanes Islands, while analysts said the academic assertions served as a warning shot amid Japanese-Philippine maritime border talks.

The archipelago, located between Taiwan and the northernmost tip of the Philippine island of Luzon, was the focus of an academic discussion on June 30 at Jinan University in Guangzhou.

At the meeting, scholars from different institutions, including the Chinese Academy of Social Sciences, the country’s top think tank, agreed that Batanes legally belonged to Taiwan and therefore fell under Chinese sovereignty, according to a news release from the university published earlier this month, which has since been deleted. They also asserted that China held sovereign rights over the nearby Babuyan Islands.

Beijing sees Taiwan as part of China to be reunited by force if necessary. Most countries, including the United States and its ally the Philippines, do not recognise Taiwan as an independent state, but Washington is opposed to any attempt to take the self-governed island by force and is committed to supplying it with weapons.

The Chinese scholars also argued that Manila’s control over the archipelago lacked a historical and legal basis, citing territorial treaties and ethnic ties between people in Batanes and Taiwan.

According to the university, the discussion “served China’s key strategic interests” and was held against the backdrop of maritime border talks between Japan and the Philippines.

The intention and objectivity of such supposed scholarly assertions are suspect. They are manifestations of turbulence in the relations and should not be overplayed

Lucio Blanco Pitlo, Philippine Association for Chinese Studies

Manila’s foreign affairs department responded last Thursday, rejecting the claims and declaring Philippine sovereignty over the Batanes Islands “settled and not up for debate”.

Manila also argued that Beijing had de facto recognised the archipelagic province as Philippine territory when it included Batanes in the consular district of its Laoag consulate.

On Friday, the National Security Council of the Philippines said in a statement that the scholars’ claims had “no merit”, and that Manila should ignore such “false narratives”.

The same day, Philippine defence chief Gilberto Teodoro Jnr dismissed the Chinese scholars’ claim as “a joke” and “ludicrous”, saying that such claims validated China’s plan to “control the whole Pacific Ocean”.

Beijing has not officially commented on the matter.

Bao Yinan, an associate research fellow at the Huayang Centre for Maritime Cooperation and Ocean Governance in Hainan province, said the academic discussion was a reaction to recent attempts by Tokyo and Manila to delimit maritime borders east of Taiwan.

“Since Japan and the Philippines want to exploit the Taiwan issue and are colluding with each other, China is also attempting to find weak legal links in the Philippines’ claimed territorial sovereignty,” Bao said.

Manila and Tokyo agreed in May to launch formal negotiations to delimit the maritime boundary of the exclusive economic zones and continental shelves between the two countries, which overlap with areas claimed by China.

The announcement angered Beijing, which responded with diplomatic condemnations, legal warnings and maritime enforcement patrols in the region.

Although he called studying the sovereignty of the Batanes a “meaningful endeavour”, Bao said the spotlight on the islands was primarily a “diversion”, and it remained an open question whether Beijing would sustain or escalate pressure on the topic.

“I believe its significance is far more political than legal,” the scholar said.

The Batanes Islands sit along the Bashi Channel, a critical maritime chokepoint that would hold immense strategic value in the event of a conflict over Taiwan.

The debate over their sovereignty arises from their status in the 19th century. Some Taiwanese scholars have argued that the islands legally became Japanese territory following an 1895 treaty between Japan and Spain.

According to Taiwanese academics, following Japan’s defeat and surrender in World War II, the US exercised administrative jurisdiction – but not sovereignty – over Batanes, meaning Washington transferred only administrative control over the islands to Manila upon Philippine independence in 1946.

However, the Philippine side tells a different story. According to Manila, the Batanes Islands were already recognised as part of its territory during the 1896 Philippine Revolution against Spain.

Following the Spanish-American War, Spain ceded the Philippines and remaining outlying islands to the US through two separate treaties signed in 1898 and 1900.

Manila believes these two treaties legally brought Batanes under US colonial administration. The two treaties were also cited when the US handed over power to the Philippines.

However, some Taiwanese scholars have argued that neither US-Spain treaty mentions the Batanes Islands. They contend that in 1961, Manila used the alleged discovery of new islands as a pretext to invoke the principle of prior occupation, thereby claiming sovereignty over the archipelago and annexing it.

During the discussion in Guangzhou, the Chinese scholars said Japan should have returned Batanes, along with Taiwan, in line with the 1943 Cairo Declaration.

They also asserted that the Batanes Islands were under the jurisdiction of Taiwan prefecture during the Ming dynasty (1368-1644) and Qing dynasty (1644-1911).

According to Liu Fu-kuo, executive director of the Taiwan Centre for Security Studies at National Chengchi University, Taiwanese research from decades ago concluded that while Manila’s sovereignty over Batanes was not entirely baseless, its legal foundation remained “very limited”.

However, Liu said, Taipei was now prioritising regional cooperation under its New Southbound Policy, an initiative aimed at reducing reliance on Beijing by expanding trade, investment, and tourism with South and Southeast Asia. As a result, the Taiwanese government has tried to avoid strained ties with these neighbours, according to Liu.

“This approach has led to the perception that Taiwan has backtracked on the South China Sea issue in recent years,” he said.

“Ultimately, it is a matter of political trade-offs.”

Batanes became a major flashpoint in 2013 after the Philippine coastguard fired at a Taiwanese fishing boat in waters south of the islands. The confrontation led to the death of a 65-year-old fisherman and triggered a severe crisis in ties between Taipei and Manila.

Lucio Blanco Pitlo, president of the Philippine Association for Chinese Studies, said the academic discussion in Guangzhou might be “a way for Beijing to express dissatisfaction or apply pressure on target parties with deniability”.

“Regrettably, the irresponsible claims also coincided with the 10th anniversary of the South China Sea arbitration award, so it gave plenty of fodder to critics arguing that China’s claims are expanding from the disputed Spratlys to the uncontested Batanes,” he said.

Pitlo drew a parallel between Batanes and the Ryukyu Islands – an archipelago stretching between Kyushu and Taiwan that includes Okinawa, home to tens of thousands of US troops.

Chinese scholars challenged Japan’s sovereignty over the Ryukyus in 2013 after Tokyo nationalised the disputed Diaoyu Islands – known in Japan as the Senkakus. The argument has been revived in recent years amid a Beijing-Tokyo dispute over Japanese Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi’s remarks about Taiwan.

“Such claims do not arise from a vacuum,” he said.

“The intention and objectivity of such supposed scholarly assertions are suspect. They are manifestations of turbulence in the relations and should not be overplayed,” he said.

item.title: [SCMP] US hardens AI stance on China as Anthropic calls for extending lead
item.description: Washington is moving to strengthen its artificial intelligence posture towards China, with lawmakers advancing new export control bills and the Trump administration deepening cooperation with leading US AI firms as concerns grow that Beijing is rapidly closing the technological gap. A senior Anthropic executive said on Wednesday that the United States holds a six-to-nine-month lead in frontier AI models over Chinese competitors, while accusing Chinese companies of using unauthorised “distillatio
item.content:encoded:
Washington is moving to strengthen its artificial intelligence posture towards China, with lawmakers advancing new export control bills and the Trump administration deepening cooperation with leading US AI firms as concerns grow that Beijing is rapidly closing the technological gap.

A senior Anthropic executive said on Wednesday that the United States holds a six-to-nine-month lead in frontier AI models over Chinese competitors, while accusing Chinese companies of using unauthorised “distillation” techniques to replicate American advances.

The comments came as the White House launched a new initiative to coordinate cybersecurity vulnerabilities with AI companies, while Congress prepared legislation aimed at further restricting China’s access to advanced chips and semiconductor manufacturing equipment.

“We actually do have a pretty good handle on the lead. I think the six-to-nine-month figure is kind of an average if you look at models,” Tarun Chhabra, Anthropic’s head of national security policy, said during a discussion at the Aspen Security Forum.

Chhabra characterised what he described as China’s use of model distillation as “adversarial”, describing it as “extracting the most valuable IP from the models”. He added that the United States could have been “a year to 18 months ahead” if Chinese firms had not engaged in the practice.

“Virtually all of China’s leading labs now are distilling not only our models but those of our peers in the United States as well,” he said. “We are now shutting down accounts that are facilitating this distillation on the order of millions of accounts per week.”

Model distillation is a widely used AI training technique in which a smaller model learns from the output of larger ones.

Companies such as Anthropic and OpenAI, however, have accused leading Chinese AI firms including DeepSeek and Alibaba Group Holding of using the technique without authorisation to replicate the capabilities of their frontier models.

Alibaba owns the South China Morning Post.

According to Chhabra, Anthropic had new evidence that Beijing-based Zhipu AI’s latest GLM-5.2 model, which he described as “probably the most advanced Chinese model on the market now”, was distilled from both Anthropic and OpenAI models.

He also accused Chinese national champion DeepSeek of “continuing its adversarial campaign for distillation”.

Zhipu and DeepSeek did not immediately respond to requests for comment.

Last week, China warned that Anthropic’s Claude Code poses “a serious threat” to Chinese users, while Alibaba imposed a companywide ban on employees using the coding tool, citing security concerns.

The developments come as US-China AI competition intensifies, with both sides trading accusations over espionage, technology theft and the security risks posed by each other’s technologies.

Amid growing concerns, the White House on Tuesday launched “Gold Eagle”, a clearing house to coordinate cybersecurity vulnerabilities with leading American AI firms.

A White House statement said that multiple government departments, including the Treasury, Homeland Security and Defence, have worked closely with industry partners to enable “exploit detection” and develop faster responses to cyber vulnerabilities.

“Treasury, along with our partner agencies, will continue to harness frontier AI capabilities to stay ahead of our adversaries and defend the American people from emerging threats,” Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said.

The measure follows the Trump administration’s recent move to block foreigner access to Anthropic’s most advanced models, Fable 5 and Mythos 5, which forced the company to suspend all access to the models for almost three weeks.

While access was later restored, the episode raised questions about reliance on US AI providers and intensified calls among allies and partners to develop their own AI models.

Former US ambassador to China Nicholas Burns, speaking during the panel discussion, criticised the Trump administration’s decision to allow exports of Nvidia’s H200 chips to Beijing, calling it one of the “grave mistakes” alongside its policies on Taiwan, South China Sea and East China Sea.

“On the whole, I think we’re seeing a rather weak American policy towards China,” he said.

Chhabra of Anthropic advocated for maintaining export controls on advanced AI chips and manufacturing equipment, lauding the US Congress’s role in tightening the restrictions.

“I’m optimistic because of the bipartisan support in Congress for strengthening some of these controls right now,” he added.
