Chhabra’s remarks come as three major export control bills targeting China are set to be folded into the coming National Defense Authorisation Act (NDAA), the annual defence policy bill passed by the US Congress, marking a significant step in US efforts to restrict Chinese access to cutting-edge semiconductor chips and chipmaking equipment.

The AI Overwatch Act, the Match Act and the Chip Security Act will all be included in the manager’s amendment to the 2027 NDAA in the Senate, Washington news outlet Punchbowl News reported on Tuesday, citing multiple anonymous sources.

Senator Jim Banks, a Republican from Indiana, confirmed in a statement that the AI Overwatch Act he introduced would be included in the cornerstone of US defence policy legislation, “ensuring that the chips powering the next generation of artificial intelligence strengthen American innovation and national security”.

Introduced in the Senate in late April, the bipartisan bill would expand congressional oversight of AI chip exports to China, with the Commerce Department required to certify to Congress that exports of advanced AI chips will not undermine US supply or facilitate remote access by unauthorised parties.

The Commerce Department would also be required to develop an “American Artificial Intelligence Victory Strategy” outlining a plan to “win the AI race” against China.

“We are in an AI race with the Chinese Communist Party that’s powered by the advanced chips, and the winner will determine the future of global military superiority,” said Banks.

The Chip Security Act, introduced in May 2025 by Senator Tom Cotton, would further tighten US export control enforcement by requiring that advanced chip exports are equipped with location-verification mechanisms that can detect chip smuggling.

Meanwhile, the Match Act would restrict Chinese access to chipmaking equipment needed to produce semiconductor chips by requiring US allies to more closely align with US export controls.

The bill has received pushback from industry and allied governments including the Netherlands, home to Dutch firm ASML, which produces deep ultraviolet (DUV) and extreme ultraviolet (EUV) lithography machines, over concerns that it would hit their sales.

item.title: [SCMP] Philippines’ jeepneys sputter up costly climb to go electric
item.description: For generations, jeepneys – brightly painted minibuses that are among the Philippines’ cheapest and most widely used forms of public transport – have run on diesel, but recent fuel shocks are forcing thousands of small operators and drivers to weigh volatile prices against the debt needed to go electric. Diesel prices spiked by nearly 5 pesos this week as renewed tensions in the Gulf threatened to strain the country’s public transport sector, adding fresh urgency to the uneven shift towards “e-j
item.content:encoded:
For generations, jeepneys – brightly painted minibuses that are among the Philippines’ cheapest and most widely used forms of public transport – have run on diesel, but recent fuel shocks are forcing thousands of small operators and drivers to weigh volatile prices against the debt needed to go electric.

Diesel prices spiked by nearly 5 pesos this week as renewed tensions in the Gulf threatened to strain the country’s public transport sector, adding fresh urgency to the uneven shift towards “e-jeepneys”.

Since the Iran conflict began in late February, diesel prices have soared, prompting an exodus of jeepney drivers from the road. Fuel costs in Metro Manila hit a high of 114 pesos (US$1.90) per litre in March – nearly one-sixth of the minimum daily wage rate for non-agricultural workers in the capital region.

Inflation settled at 6.4 per cent in June, while diesel prices climbed to 76 pesos this week, up from 72 pesos the week before and well above the pre-conflict rate of 60 pesos per litre.

The latest increase has put the public transport sector on edge and exposed a deeper dilemma: electric jeepneys promise lower running costs, but remain out of reach for many small operators and drivers.

“We’re expecting diesel to climb again. The media has been saying that the stockpile will only last over 40 days,” said Liberty de Luna, national president of the Alliance of Concerned Transport Organisations (ACTO) in the Philippines.

“What about us? We might see another scenario where diesel climbs to 100 pesos per litre. This would be a huge blow to the transport sector.”

The country is especially exposed to price shocks from the Iran conflict, as it imports more than 95 per cent of its oil supply. Energy officials say it could take months before prices return to pre-conflict levels.

Another transport group, Pasang Masda, has urged the government to restore the 1-peso fare increase that was suspended earlier this year, citing the growing financial burden on the sector.

De Luna said more ACTO members were now considering a shift to e-jeepneys as diesel prices continued to spiral out of control.

“We’ve begun embracing e-jeepneys, especially now with the diesel crisis. Because if some of us are already on electric and others are still on diesel, at least if diesel becomes unaffordable, those on e-jeepneys can keep serving the public,” she said.

“If there’s no more affordable diesel and no electric alternatives either, how do we serve the riding public?”

In April, the Department of Trade and Industry launched a 2-billion-peso financing programme to help licensed small operators and drivers transition to electric vehicles amid the fuel crisis.

The scheme offers loans of up to 1.5 million pesos per EV, allowing operators to borrow as much as 3 million pesos, repayable over five years, with principal and interest waived for the first year.

Although only a small number of ACTO members have made the switch so far, de Luna said their experiences had been promising.

“With EV, we have lower spending. In two hours, we can fast-charge a vehicle good for 200km. If we’re spending 1,500 or 2,000 pesos for diesel, with electric, it will come around to 700 pesos. So the savings are huge,” she said.

Playing catch-up

The Philippines is fast catching up with its Southeast Asian neighbours on EV adoption as motorists look for ways to insulate themselves from rising fuel prices.

Sales of EVs, including battery and hybrid vehicles, surged by 133.5 per cent between January and May this year compared with the same period in 2025, according to data from the Chamber of Automotive Manufacturers of the Philippines and the Truck Manufacturers Association.

That trend appears to be spreading to public transport, with manufacturers keen to capitalise on rising demand.

Japan-based EV start-up Assemble Point opened a factory in Cavite province in February, just before the Gulf conflict led to the closure of the Strait of Hormuz.

The company, which builds 10-seater electric minibuses and aims to sell them nationwide, has seen a surge in interest, according to Japanese public broadcaster NHK, with a backlog of orders as it awaits imported components.

Not everyone, however, is keen to join the EV rush, however. Transport group Piston has pointed to gaps in the Philippines’ push to boost e-jeepney adoption, warning that the livelihoods of drivers and small operators who cannot afford the new units are at risk under the Public Transport Modernisation Programme, which forces them to consolidate franchises into cooperatives.

Sandy Mae Gaspay, an associate professor at the University of the Philippines’ College of Engineering, said the fuel crisis had raised awareness of e-jeepneys’ benefits, but added that this was only part of the picture.

“A bigger question is if jeepney operators can afford to buy new vehicles in the first place. This is why the EV discussion for jeepneys is difficult to separate from the Public Transport Modernisation Programme, which has its long list of issues,” she said, referring to the initiative to replace old vehicles with eco-friendly alternatives.

Aside from operators’ financial readiness, Gaspay pointed to a lack of government support for establishing EV infrastructure, including charging systems and electrical line adjustments.

“Those operating EVs need to set up these systems by themselves. This shifts what is usually the state’s burden to the operators,” she said.

The EV surge has also created new dependencies through battery acquisition and management systems that operators must finance privately.

“There are several who have opted to lease batteries from Chinese companies on a monthly basis,” Gaspay said. “While these arrangements are beneficial for operators with capital constraints, they create perpetual monthly expenses that operators must absorb indefinitely.”

Gaspay said the government should do more to support the public transport sector’s transition to EVs, including implementing dedicated “green routes” for electric minibuses and shared facilities such as charging or battery-swapping stations to lower the overall cost of ownership.

She also pointed to incentives such as service-contracting programmes, which could help EV drivers repay loans while allowing cooperatives to explore alternative income streams.

item.title: [SCMP] China is now world’s No 1 science power, top Russian scientist Artem Oganov says
item.description: Leading Russian chemist and materials scientist Artem Oganov, who received China’s top international science and technology award, said the honour from the world’s No 1 science power was a sign he was “on the right track”. Oganov, a distinguished professor at the Skolkovo Institute of Science and Technology in Moscow and long-time China collaborator, and eight other experts were awarded the International Science and Technology Cooperation Award by Chinese President Xi Jinping during a ceremony i
item.content:encoded:
Leading Russian chemist and materials scientist Artem Oganov, who received China’s top international science and technology award, said the honour from the world’s No 1 science power was a sign he was “on the right track”.

Oganov, a distinguished professor at the Skolkovo Institute of Science and Technology in Moscow and long-time China collaborator, and eight other experts were awarded the International Science and Technology Cooperation Award by Chinese President Xi Jinping during a ceremony in Beijing last week.

After Oganov visited China in 2008, he told colleagues and friends in Europe that the country was “on the way” to being No 1 in both industry and science, but said this was met with scepticism at the time.

He told the South China Morning Post on Monday that, 18 years on, “China is No 1 – in industry, in economy, and indeed also in science.”

Noting that America used to be the top scientific power, he pointed to the fact that China now accounts for a third of global scientific publications, adding that it was not just numbers, but “quality”.

China holds the top spot in the 2026 Nature Index – an analysis of over 125,000 research articles published in 178 leading publications in 2025 – with the US in second place.

“I knew that [China] will rise to the top position, but I did not expect that it will be so fast and so strong,” he said.

“We live now in very dramatic times when politicians, especially Western politicians, burn bridges.

“[The] future belongs to those who build the bridges, not those who burn them. And the most natural bridge between different cultures and nations is science,” he added.

Oganov said being recognised with the “highest award” of his decorated career gave him confidence that he was on the right path, and that his journey with China was not over yet.

“I keep coming to China every year, spending at least one month there. We have new plans, new ambitions. It’s going on an interesting upward trajectory,” he said.

Oganov, born in 1975, is a theoretical crystallographer, chemist, physicist, mineralogist and materials scientist.

He graduated from Moscow State University in 1997 and received a doctorate from University College London in 2002, after which he served as a senior scientist and group leader at ETH Zurich in Switzerland, where he received a postdoctoral qualification in 2007.

From 2008 to 2017, he was a professor at Stony Brook University in New York. And in 2015, he became a professor at the Skolkovo Institute of Science and Technology, according to the Universal Scientific Education and Research Network.

Oganov currently serves as the chief scientific officer of SberUniversity and is founding chairman of the scientific committee for the Vyzov Prize, Russia’s own science award.

He is well-known for developing the USPEX crystal structure prediction program, which has been used by researchers and companies around the world to make breakthroughs in new materials discovery.

Oganov said his relationship with China began in the late 1990s during his years at University College London, when he realised that his friends were mostly Russians and Chinese.

“For many Western people, Russians and Chinese were second-class people. I’m sorry to say it, but that is what it felt [like],” he said.

“I found that a lot of stereotypes that we were fed, especially through Western press, about China and Chinese people are completely wrong.”

In 2008, he was given the opportunity to be an invited professor at the Chinese University of Hong Kong for a month. During this trip to Hong Kong and later other cities in mainland China, Oganov said he learned more about Chinese history, including the century of humiliation.

He said he also learned about how China had, through “extremely clever strategic planning of its government, regained, little by little, its former glory”.

He said China achieved this “not by humiliating and destroying others like ‘Make America Great Again’”, but by building itself up.

In 2011, Oganov applied for and was selected to be a part of China’s 1,000 talents programme, a state-sponsored recruitment scheme to attract top global scientists.

He created his own laboratory at the Northwestern Polytechnical University in Xian in 2013, which later became the International Centre for Materials Discovery. Oganov served as the international institute’s founding director before delegating the centre in 2020 to his colleague and former PhD student, Professor Niu Haiyang.

After Covid-19 restrictions ended, he signed another contract with the Xinjiang Technical Institute of Physics and Chemistry, and said he now mostly went to Urumqi when visiting China.

The whole journey with China has been one huge surprise

Artem Oganov

In 2019, he received China’s Friendship Award, which is given to foreign experts who have made contributions to the country’s economic and social progress.

According to Northwestern Polytechnical, Oganov has been involved in building transnational collaborative research platforms and has jointly trained faculty members and graduate students, particularly in the field of materials genome engineering.

Among Oganov’s former postdoctoral students is Ma Yanming, now president of Zhejiang University and one of the country’s most influential physicists.

“The whole journey with China has been one huge surprise. I have worked with remarkably talented people and I’m extremely proud of them,” he said.

In the 2026 Nature Index Research Leaders, four of the top five leading institutions by research output are in China, including the Chinese Academy of Sciences (CAS) in first place, while Harvard University took third.

CAS leads in several subject areas, including biological, earth and environmental, physical sciences, applied sciences, and chemistry. Harvard took the top spot in health sciences and social sciences.

Oganov noted that Russia also held a leading position in certain fields and had its own school of thought that was different to those of China, Europe and America.

“When we search for the truth, it’s very important for us to see nature not from one angle, but from other angles. And this is where different schools of thought come into play,” he said.

“The future belongs to international collaboration. Science cannot be isolated. If science in a given country becomes isolated, it dies.”

Oganov also noted the China Initiative, where Chinese scientists or scientists who collaborated with China were targeted and discriminated against in American academia.

He said a fellow recipient of China’s science and technology award was Zhang Shoucheng, a physicist and professor at Stanford University who died in 2018, with the official cause of death being suicide.

“Because of his strong connections with China, he was bullied by [the US Federal Bureau of Investigation] and committed suicide,” Oganov said.

“For about six years before that, every year he was named as the most likely Nobel laureate in physics.”

Oganov said he believed the world was moving towards a third world war, with some regions in an informational or economic war rather than a hot war.

“It seems that one pool of countries wants to stop the progress of another pool of countries and redistribute world power from the current status quo,” he said.

“I think it will end in the redistribution of the world map, redistribution of the world power, like every world war does.”

But he expected that, in the end, “peace will prevail”.

Oganov said that while he was “just one small human being”, small people could make big changes.

“The bridges that I’m building are not only between myself and Chinese people and Chinese scientists but also between institutions.”

China’s International Science and Technology Cooperation Award has many prestigious recipients, including multiple Nobel laureates. This year, recipients came from Russia, the US, Belgium, Portugal, Australia, Germany, the Netherlands and France – including 1987 Nobel chemistry laureate Jean-Marie Lehn.

item.title: [SCMP] Mujun Planet: one man’s quest to revive Japan’s artisanal legacy
item.description: “There’s a snake.” Shinya Kobayashi points his phone camera at a sleek, black reptile, creating a trinity of S-shapes in the dirt, before calmly walking on. It is a remarkably cheerful day, with blue skies overhead and dense, emerald forest all around. The 39-year-old designer, artist and father is taking me on a virtual tour of his own mountain, in Japan’s Shimane prefecture, along Honshu island’s western fringe. Nearby are a recently built blacksmith’s forge, sawmill, woodworking workshop, cha
item.content:encoded:
“There’s a snake.”

Shinya Kobayashi points his phone camera at a sleek, black reptile, creating a trinity of S-shapes in the dirt, before calmly walking on. It is a remarkably cheerful day, with blue skies overhead and dense, emerald forest all around.

The 39-year-old designer, artist and father is taking me on a virtual tour of his own mountain, in Japan’s Shimane prefecture, along Honshu island’s western fringe. Nearby are a recently built blacksmith’s forge, sawmill, woodworking workshop, charcoal kilns and a renovated 200-year-old farmhouse, currently serving as storage space.

Mujun Planet, as Kobayashi has named the mountain and its tiny “village”, has been six years in the making. While the hardware might seem modest, he hopes its impact on the neighbouring town of Yunotsu, and the broader realm of Japanese craftsmanship, will be profound.

Metalworking is his focus for now. Blades from the forge made into knives are sold in Japan and around the world through Coelacanth Shokudo, his design company that, among other activities, manages the wholesale distribution of artisanal products.

All this and more are chronicled in the Hyogo native’s recently released, self-published book, Why Did Mujun’s Shinya Kobayashi Buy a Mountain?

“I have long worked to resolve the issue of artisan succession,” he tells me. “However, there are numerous problems that cannot be solved without entering the mountains to work, and I want people to understand why.”

To appreciate the seemingly tenuous connection between the two is to plunge into the depths of Kobayashi’s world – an adventure he has chosen for himself. On the surface, his mission revolves around self-sufficiency, breaking free from the dependence on imported tools necessary for various artisanal endeavours. His mountain and its surroundings serve as a natural reservoir of raw materials.

Take the knife – an essential tool across all crafts. It is why a forge was among the first components at Mujun Planet, operated by 29-year-old blacksmith Koharu Yamaguchi. As Kobayashi enters her workspace during our virtual tour, visible behind her is a tray of neatly arranged blades, their sharp edges catching the light.

Yamaguchi receives a steady supply of iron sand and charcoal from Yunotsu, historically renowned for tatara, the ancient Japanese method of ironmaking. For the knife handles, wood is harvested from the large branches of trees, as part of ongoing forest maintenance.

“The decline of craftsmanship has accelerated dramatically in recent years,” says Kobayashi. “I have always believed that nurturing successors for steelmaking artisans is vital to stemming this tide, and this conviction has now become a tangible reality.”

He is not alone in his beliefs. Growing numbers of young Japanese are eager to pursue careers in artisanal trades, and many are modernising traditional processes and finding innovative ways to share their stories. But few have adopted Kobayashi’s maverick methods.

His decision to buy a mountain is closely tied to his mission of promoting satoyama. Literally meaning “village” (sato) and “mountain” (yama), it originally described the landscape between these two realms.

Over time, it has evolved into a philosophy that deeply respects nature and tradition, advocating sustainability and living in rhythm with the seasons.

The past six years have been dedicated to laying a foundation for this vision. At Mujun Planet, overgrown trees have been cleared, mountain streams rejuvenated and neglected agriculture plots revived and nurtured.

As much as possible, local cedar is used, such as for the construction of buildings. In the farmhouse, Kobayashi’s pride is palpable as he highlights a smooth, 4.5-metre-long beam – crafted from a single trunk – supporting the roof. Plans are under way to turn the space into an atelier for emerging artisans.

“Sustainability is most crucial in resolving the issue of succession,” Kobayashi says. “It exists only through cycles and it is precisely because they have been practised for so long that we have traditional crafts. Within them lies an unbroken culture and way of life.

“That’s why I believe it is essential to revive the spirit of satoyama, which has been forgotten over the last 100 years or so.”

Compounding this challenge is society’s diminishing appreciation of Japan’s mountains. The costs associated with their management and maintenance have soared, leading many families to hesitate at the prospect of passing down or, indeed, inheriting such legacies.

Kobayashi says that after World War II, conifers such as cedar and cypress were planted as part of a new national policy for post-war reconstruction. Unfortunately, the initiative was quickly overshadowed by the influx of cheap, uniform meranti wood from Southeast Asia. This coincided with a population explosion in Japan that spurred the construction of inexpensive, simple houses throughout the country.

As a result, the conifers were left to languish and are now severely degraded. Undeterred by these challenges, however, Kobayashi seized an opportunity in 2020, when he learned from a local temple that a nearby 200,000 square metre mountain was being sold for just 1 million yen (US$6,230 at that time).

Today, the Mujun Planet community comprises seven members, three of whom are full-time “residents”, including Kobayashi. Their lodgings are a two-minute drive away, between the mountain and Yunotsu, and visitors are accommodated in a guest house behind his home.

Their workday begins at 8.30am and ends at sunset. In between caring for free-range chickens, the members engage in woodworking, grass cutting and tree-felling. Kobayashi himself travels far and wide occasionally – such as to design events in Europe and, recently, Singapore – to attract others to join him on his planet.

He also gives talks and holds workshops, including hosting a training and team-building retreat for a high-end blade-making brand.

Already, his efforts are beginning to bear fruit. Kobayashi speaks of young, like-minded nationals and non-Japanese finding their way to Mujun Planet, eager to become craftspeople or to experience satoyama. One, in particular, has embraced the lifestyle wholeheartedly: a Canadian-Chinese construction worker from the United States, who wants to build a house on the mountain, is currently working with Kobayashi on its design.

Then there’s the elderly Japanese blade maker whose speciality is scissor-making. Kobayashi tells how the man had given up on finding his successor only to change his mind after learning about Mujun Planet. He now plans to help train someone to take over his business.

While it might be easy to blame the dismal state of artisan succession on youthful indifference or the inability of senior craftspeople to think outside the box, the causes run deeper.

“The root of the problem lies in the social structure itself,” Kobayashi says, adding that one consequence is the homogeneity of many Japanese regions. “Japan became an economic powerhouse with astonishing speed, alongside its post-war reconstruction. Massive capitalist economies, and [the allure of] convenience, have ultimately devoured the regions and their cultures. Most Japanese regions now look identical – the same houses, the same shops, the same scenery.”

Moreover, issues of low profitability persist, stemming from inadequate distribution channels and ignorance around branding and marketing. Kobayashi has also heard of parents who choose not to support the continuation of their crafts. These elderly artisans want their children ­­– whose university education they funded ­– to find well-paid, city-based jobs, he says.

Kobayashi understands the push and pull personally, having witnessed similar issues in his own family’s traditional paper-mounting business in Ono city, Hyogo prefecture. He recalls a bustling business during his primary school years, in the 1990s, before Japan’s economic bubble burst and fortunes declined in the 2000s. “I increasingly saw my father looking rather idle. I’d often chime in with suggestions such as, ‘Why don’t you try sales?’ ­which led to frequent arguments.”

After his father, Michiaki, took over what would become a third-generation business, he made little effort to attract more customers, believing that they would appear if he simply waited. “I felt a strong sense of crisis seeing him just waiting like that,” recalls Kobayashi. “At the same time, I gradually came to understand that it was very difficult for him to suddenly adopt new ideas or put them into action.”

External factors compounded their challenges. Paper mounting involves replacing washi paper on sliding doors, folding screens and hanging scrolls marking significant ceremonies, such as weddings and funerals.

However, many of these events have moved away from the traditional settings of homes. “Culture has disappeared from communities,” Kobayashi says, adding that most Japanese now aspire to Western-style residences, rendering such time-worn Japanese features obsolete.

“The decline of regional culture, economy and local craftsmanship feels like just the tip of the iceberg. Perhaps I still want to do something about this today – or perhaps I just wanted to help my father. He passed away in March [2025].”

Kobayashi has the foundations for action. He studied product design at the Osaka University of Arts, graduating in 2010. The following year, he founded Coelacanth Shokudo to help revitalise traditional crafts.

“I was a young man who didn’t know his right from his left, a know-it-all with a head full of design education only applicable in the city,” he says. “But my enthusiasm was second to none. I was confident that with passion, ideas and skills, I could overcome any obstacle. My sense of urgency stemmed from a feeling that if I didn’t act quickly, it would be too late.”

Since 2013, Coelacanth Shokudo has focused on developing sales channels in Europe and America, collaborating with artisan businesses to generate income. This was formalised with the launch of the wholesale brand “Mujun” (meaning “contradiction”) in 2016.

From this initiative emerged a diverse range of products, including homeware, scissors, bags and wrapping cloth (furoshiki) made from vintage kimono fabric. Two years later, Mujun Workshop was established as a platform to nurture young craftspeople who would succeed retiring artisans.

Then there are the products he has designed for others, Kinjo Japan included. Kobayashi’s silicone cup collection, sold in Hong Kong at Wing On department stores, consists of silicone-rubber drinking vessels as transparent as regular glass and inspired by the Japanese kiriko tradition of glass cutting.

Kobayashi appears to have all his ducks in a row. Back on the mountain, he swings his phone from side to side, enthusiastically discussing upcoming projects.

Coils of hosepipes await connection to a small river about 200 metres up the mountain, promising an independent water supply. A barely visible track between tall hedges will soon become a road leading to fields where rice will be cultivated. He is also expecting the arrival of a Chinese-made dynamo to provide stable, independent power.

After the tour, he sits cross-legged on a dirt road with a colleague, who multitasks as his interpreter.

“Good internet connection here,” he says, when asked if he’d prefer moving to a more comfortable spot, such as the cedar-wood picnic bench and table built by the community.

He hopes to continue nurturing the satoyama way of life through Mujun Planet, while forging new connections domestically and internationally. On the family front, his wife, Misato, is learning the art of creating hanging scrolls from his mother, Misako, and he is exploring introducing the technique into his own art. Although his family’s paper-mounting enterprise ended with his father, some of the skills promise to live on.

“My ultimate goal is to sustain this satoyama life with my family. My dream is to be a sculptor, carving stone and wood using chisels I forge myself from iron sand,” he says. “I want to create a future where families engaged in various crafts live in these mountains, where children roam freely through the hills, drink mountain water and catch insects.”

item.title: [SCMP] Asia’s shaky food supply shudders as ‘super’ El Nino arrives
item.description: In the mist-wrapped hills of northern Thailand, where cacao trees erupt from the rich, nutrient-dense soil, three little words are spreading fear through a community of farmers. “There is no way to know for certain,” Koranut Rattanayanyong told This Week in Asia. “But it could be a total wipeout.” The weather event Koranut is dreading? A “super” El Nino. Earlier this month, the climate phenomenon officially started to form over the Pacific, with a bloom of ocean heat running 2.5 degrees Celsius
item.content:encoded:
In the mist-wrapped hills of northern Thailand, where cacao trees erupt from the rich, nutrient-dense soil, three little words are spreading fear through a community of farmers.

“There is no way to know for certain,” Koranut Rattanayanyong told This Week in Asia. “But it could be a total wipeout.”

The weather event Koranut is dreading? A “super” El Nino.

Earlier this month, the climate phenomenon officially started to form over the Pacific, with a bloom of ocean heat running 2.5 degrees Celsius (4.5 degrees Fahrenheit) above normal temperatures.

The forecasts emanating from meteorological agencies across the world in the days since have been almost uniform in their alarm.

Australia’s Bureau of Meteorology said on Tuesday that this year’s El Nino “could peak at levels among the highest observed since 1950”. Its US counterpart gave almost two in three odds that it would be “very strong”.

And UN Secretary General Antonio Guterres warned on June 2 that this year’s event “will hit even harder, travel even farther and cross borders with devastating speed”.

El Nino is a recurring disruption. In essence, one forms when the Pacific Ocean runs a fever, redistributing heat and moisture in ways that can shatter the climatic rhythms on which billions of people depend. It brings drought to some regions, catastrophic flooding to others and the kinds of climatic extremes – hot days, dry soils, then sudden deluges – that can overwhelm crops.

A “super” event, scientists warn, would do all of that and more, across a wider geography, with greater intensity, for longer. It has levelled harvests before: a decade ago, a super El Nino devastated Indian soybean yields, Thai rice, Vietnam’s Robusta coffee crop and the palm oil output of both Indonesia and Malaysia.

For Asia and its 4.7 billion people – hundreds of millions of whom still live off the land – what comes next could be devastating.

Fuel on the fire

Koranut has spent years building something unusual in the hills of Chiang Mai: a cacao economy.

Growers and grinders, processors and craft chocolatiers, all connected through his company Cocoa Baan Suan, operate a direct, farm-to-wrapper loop that ends in chocolate-coated nuts, dense brownies and bars of trendy “Dubai chocolate”.

This, he wants to show the world, is what rural Thailand could do if it moved up the value chain, from raw commodity to finished product, from subsistence to sophistication.

But cacao trees are highly sensitive to variations in heat and rainfall.

“Even if our water supply is good, if the heat above is that intense, the trees can’t survive,” Koranut said. “If there is waterlogging, the cacao trees will develop fungal problems. And if the yields are down … you can forget about all of this.”

Now, Koranut is waiting for a weather event that could erase it all. And he is far from alone.

In Pakistan, agriculture accounts for around 24 per cent of gross domestic product; in Laos, a similar share; in India, the government estimates that roughly 145 million people farm for a living, making up 16 per cent of the entire economy.

Even Southeast Asian industrial powerhouses such as Indonesia and Vietnam, not often thought of as agrarian economies, still depend on agriculture for 14 and 13 per cent of their output, respectively.

El Nino conditions will pour fuel on the fire of a warming world

UN Secretary General Antonio Guterres

This year’s super El Nino is arriving against a troubling backdrop. Sky-high fertiliser and fuel prices, a result of supply disruptions caused by the US-Israel war on Iran, led many Asian farmers to use less than needed this planting season.

Currencies across the region have also weakened, making food imports more expensive just as domestic production may be about to fall.

“The challenge is compounded … by the energy crisis, supply chain disruptions and the impact of weakening currencies on imports across markets from Indonesia to India,” said Vikash Sharma, director of Godrej International Trading and Investments, a Singapore-based commodity trading house.

“An El Nino of this nature will also have a ripple effect on places like Singapore and Hong Kong, both of which rely heavily on food imports.”

Nowhere, in other words, is likely to escape unscathed.

Ratings agency Fitch has warned of “sustained shortages” accelerating inflation. Futures prices for Arabica coffee, cacao and sugar have all climbed on commodity exchanges in anticipation of the fallout.

“Farm commodity markets are going to be highly volatile,” said Desh Ratna, a commodity market specialist operating out of Vietnam’s Ho Chi Minh City.

“Some parts of the world will experience dryness while others will see more rainfall. This, in turn, will impact yields and result in price spikes.”

That asymmetry may produce its own dislocations: if El Nino damages American soybean output – the foundation of the global animal feed supply – prices could rise across every food category that relies on it.

Some supply loss may be partially offset by farmers in India pivoting from water-hungry crops such as sugar cane and corn towards soybean, as officials there expect. But the overall direction of travel is clear.

From Bangkok to Tokyo, Asia’s cities were already adapting to a warming baseline. Cool rooms are being installed in public spaces. Outdoor workers are being sent home at midday.

Last year’s record temperatures extracted a grim toll in heatstroke deaths that governments are not eager to repeat.

The Philippines, perennially battered by Pacific storm systems, is steeling itself for a more violent typhoon season. Across Asia, conservationists are sounding alarms about biodiversity corridors that could be severed by wildfires if this year’s dry season turns extreme.

“El Nino conditions will pour fuel on the fire of a warming world,” UN chief Guterres warned this month. And the world is not ready.

Here it comes

In Nepal’s Chitwan district, not far from the flat agricultural plains that stretch south towards the Indian border, Ramesh Chandra Puri is worried about his summer rice crop.

“We have heard that this kind of weather is coming soon,” the 63-year-old said of the forecasts of unusually dry weather that have begun filtering through to his village. “But the government has not given us any support to prepare for it.”

His most recent harvest has been disrupted by weeks of heavy, unseasonal rain – too much water now, potentially too little later. The local traders paying to buy his grain are offering roughly half the government-mandated price of 3,600 Nepalese rupees (US$24) per 100kg.

That is rapidly eating away at the funds he needs to prepare his fields for the next crop, sown in late June or early July – without even taking into account the water needed to nourish it.

“If conditions are really dry this summer, we will have to resort to boring groundwater,” Puri said. “But if the hot and dry weather gets too intense, even boring does not work.”

A version of the predicament Puri finds himself in – caught between the immediate cruelty of low prices and the gathering threat of a climate event he cannot control – is now playing out among farmers across the continent.

For those who have irrigation, groundwater reserves or dams filled by last year’s rains may be enough to get through, according to Ajay Jakhar, who chairs Bharat Krishak Samaj, a farmers’ welfare organisation based in Punjab, one of India’s most productive agricultural states.

“The problem, however, is with farmers’ livelihoods in rain-fed areas such as Vidarbha in Maharashtra,” he said. Vidarbha, a region long associated in India with agrarian distress, severe debt and high rates of farmer suicides, does not need another bad season.

Roughly 45 per cent of the world’s most populous nation – some 660 million people – relies, directly or indirectly, on agriculture.

India’s Agriculture Minister Shivraj Singh Chouhan held a high-level meeting on Tuesday to outline a strategy for dealing with this year’s El Nino by redirecting resources and helping farmers pivot to more drought-resistant crops ahead of the monsoon planting season.

“Full preparations should be made in advance in those districts where there is a possibility of low rainfall or uneven rainfall,” he told the assembled officials present, adding that “weekly meetings will be held” to prepare.

India can boast community-led adaptation models, new irrigation techniques and a track record of feeding its population through shocks that would have caused crises a generation ago.

But the fertiliser shortage triggered by the Strait of Hormuz crisis – only now expected to ease as a US-Iran peace deal edges towards completion – will still be felt in the soil this season. And the heat has already been exceptional this year, even before El Nino arrives in earnest.

“The ongoing extreme heat conditions are causing alarm,” said Devinder Sharma, an independent food and trade policy analyst. “We are expecting El Nino, whose effect would be visible in July or August. This is a deadly combination for India.”

Should New Delhi’s confidence in its own resilience turns out to be misplaced, the consequences could spiral far beyond its borders.

“If there is any doubt about production, India will stop exporting food,” Jakhar said.

In a year when harvests are already under threat from Thailand to Indonesia to Vietnam, an export ban by India – one of the world’s largest food and agricultural exporters and its leading producer of rice – would land like a tonne of bricks on global markets.

‘We have to fight’

Back in Chiang Mai, Koranut’s son Pinyawat thinks about the ecosystem his father has spent years building as the rain patters down.

“Really, we’re just getting started,” he said, sharing his hope that one day, cacao could become a valuable cash crop for the region.

Thailand produces around 1,500 to 2,000 tonnes of cacao each year: a fraction of the 600,000 tonnes produced by Indonesia, the world’s No 3 producer behind only the Ivory Coast and Ghana.

“But if the climate works against us, the yields won’t justify the costs,” Pinyawat said.

His father is more defiant, though not naively so. He talks about planting shade trees to protect the smaller cacao plants from direct heat, about new irrigation technology that might carry the crop through the driest stretches.

“Every problem has a solution,” Koranut said. “We just have to keep solving it … We have to fight.”

item.title: [SCMP] Philippines’ iconic jeepneys face rocky road to go electric
item.description: For generations, jeepneys – brightly painted minibuses that are among the Philippines’ cheapest and most used forms of public transport – have run on diesel, but recent fuel shocks are forcing thousands of small operators and drivers to weigh volatile prices against the debt needed to go electric. Diesel prices spiked anew by nearly 5 pesos this week, as renewed tensions in the Gulf threatened to strain the country’s public transport sector, giving new urgency to the uneven shift towards e-jeepn
item.content:encoded:
For generations, jeepneys – brightly painted minibuses that are among the Philippines’ cheapest and most used forms of public transport – have run on diesel, but recent fuel shocks are forcing thousands of small operators and drivers to weigh volatile prices against the debt needed to go electric.

Diesel prices spiked anew by nearly 5 pesos this week, as renewed tensions in the Gulf threatened to strain the country’s public transport sector, giving new urgency to the uneven shift towards e-jeepneys.

Since the Iran conflict began in late February, diesel prices have soared, leading to an exodus of jeepney drivers from the road. Fuel costs in Metro Manila hit a high of 114 pesos (US$1.90) per litre in March – nearly one-sixth of the minimum daily wage rate for non-agricultural workers in the Philippine capital region.

Inflation settled at 6.4 per cent in June, while diesel prices rose to 76 pesos this week from 72 pesos a week ago – well above pre-conflict figures of 60 pesos per litre.

The latest increase has put the country’s public transport sector on edge while also highlighting a deeper dilemma: electric jeepneys promise lower running costs, but remain difficult for many small operators and drivers to afford.

“We’re expecting diesel to climb again. The media has been saying that the stockpile will only last over 40 days … what about us? We might see another scenario where diesel climbs to 100 pesos per litre. This would be a huge blow to the transport sector,” said Liberty de Luna, national president of the Alliance of Concerned Transport Organisations (ACTO) in the Philippines.

The country remains particularly vulnerable to continued price shocks from the Iran conflict because it imports more than 95 per cent of its oil supply. Energy officials say it could take months before the Philippines sees a return to pre-conflict prices.

Another transport group, Pasang Masda, urged the government to restore the 1-peso fare increase that was suspended earlier this year, citing the growing financial burden on the public transport sector.

De Luna said more ACTO members were now considering shifting to e-jeepneys as diesel prices continued to spiral out of control.

“We’ve begun embracing e-jeepneys, especially now with the diesel crisis. Because if some of us are already on electric and others are still on diesel, at least if diesel becomes unaffordable, those on e-jeepneys can keep serving the public,” she said.

“If there’s no more affordable diesel and no electric alternatives either, how do we serve the riding public?”

In April, the Department of Trade and Industry (DTI) launched a 2 billion-peso financing programme to help licensed small public transport operators and drivers transition to electric vehicles amid the fuel crisis.

The DTI began offering loans of up to 1.5 million pesos for an EV, allowing operators and drivers to borrow as much as 3 million pesos, and payable within five years. The repayment of principal and interest will be waived for the first year.

While only a small number of ACTO members had begun the EV shift, de Luna said their experiences were so far promising.

“With EV, we have lower spending. In two hours, we can fast-charge a vehicle good for 200km. If we’re spending 1,500 or 2,000 pesos for diesel, with electric, it will come around to 700 pesos. So the savings are huge,” she said.

Playing catch-up

The Philippines is now fast catching up to its Southeast Asian neighbours in EV adoption as motorists find ways to insulate themselves from rising fuel prices.

Sales for EVs, including battery and hybrid vehicles, surged by 133.5 per cent between January and May this year compared with the same period in 2025, according to data from the Chamber of Automotive Manufacturers of the Philippines and the Truck Manufacturers Association.

The trend appears to be crossing over to the public transport sector, with certain manufacturers keen to capitalise on the growing demand.

Japan-based EV start-up ASSEMBLEPOINT opened a manufacturing plant in Cavite province in February, just before the Gulf conflict led to the closure of the Strait of Hormuz.

The company, which builds 10-seater electric minibuses and aims to sell them nationwide, is dealing with a surge in interest, leading to a backlog in orders as it waits for imported components to arrive, according to an NHK report.

However, not everyone is keen to jump on the EV hype.

Transport group Piston has pointed out gaps in the Philippines’ bid to boost adoption of e-jeepneys, noting that the livelihoods of drivers and small operators who cannot afford the units are at risk due to the Public Transport Modernisation Programme that forces them to consolidate franchises into cooperatives.

Sandy Mae Gaspay, an associate professor at the University of the Philippines College of Engineering, acknowledged that the fuel crisis boosted awareness of e-jeepney benefits, but said that was only one aspect of the issue.

“A bigger question is if jeepney operators can afford to buy new vehicles in the first place. This is why the EV discussion for jeepneys is difficult to separate from the Public Transport Modernisation Programme, which has its long list of issues,” Gaspay told This Week in Asia, referencing the initiative to replace old vehicles with eco-friendly alternatives.

Aside from operators’ financial readiness, Gaspay pointed to gaps such as the lack of government support in establishing EV systems including charging and electric line adjustments.

“Those operating EVs need to set up these systems by themselves. This shifts what is usually the state’s burden to the operators,” she said.

The EV surge also created new technological dependency through battery acquisition and management systems that operators must finance privately, she pointed out.

“There are several who have opted to lease batteries from Chinese companies on a monthly basis. While these arrangements are beneficial for operators with capital constraints, they create perpetual monthly expenses that operators must absorb indefinitely.”

Gaspay said the government should boost support for the public transport sector’s transition to EVs, including implementing dedicated “green routes” for electric minibuses, and establishing facilities such as shared charging or battery-swapping stations to reduce the overall cost of owning e-jeepneys.

She also pointed to incentives such as service-contracting programmes to help EV drivers pay back their loans while allowing collectives to explore alternative income streams.

item.title: [SCMP] US-Iran war: is Trump falling into a Vietnam ‘quagmire’?
item.description: Five months and at least two ceasefires into the US-Israeli war on Iran, hostilities have intensified again, with US President Donald Trump reimposing a naval blockade on Iranian ports and American forces trading fresh strikes with Tehran. The renewed cycle is testing both Tehran’s resilience and Washington’s appetite for a prolonged conflict, prompting some analysts to draw uncomfortable parallels with the political tolls that past US presidents faced in drawn-out wars such as in Vietnam and Af
item.content:encoded:
Five months and at least two ceasefires into the US-Israeli war on Iran, hostilities have intensified again, with US President Donald Trump reimposing a naval blockade on Iranian ports and American forces trading fresh strikes with Tehran.

The renewed cycle is testing both Tehran’s resilience and Washington’s appetite for a prolonged conflict, prompting some analysts to draw uncomfortable parallels with the political tolls that past US presidents faced in drawn-out wars such as in Vietnam and Afghanistan.

Oil prices climbed on Wednesday as the renewed escalation disrupted shipping in the Strait of Hormuz, a strategic chokepoint that handled roughly a fifth of global oil and gas flows before the conflict began in late February.

The US military on Wednesday said it had launched a “90-minute wave” of precision strikes that had “further degraded Iran’s ability to attack commercial shipping in the Strait of Hormuz”.

Iranian officials reported more than 260 people wounded in the latest overnight US strikes, undermining an agreement reached last month.

Tehran, which insists it controls the strait, has launched retaliatory drone and missile attacks on US-linked targets in Jordan, Bahrain and Kuwait, vowing the waterway would stay closed “until the US ends its aggression”.

For the United States, the conflict has proved costly and inconclusive, presenting a complex yet familiar dilemma.

Trump has threatened to widen strikes to include power plants and bridges if Iran does not return to the negotiating table.

He has also drawn a parallel with the Vietnam war, telling CNBC in April that he “would have won Vietnam very quickly” had he been president then, contrasting its 19‑year duration with the Iran conflict.

Jodie Wen, a researcher at Tsinghua University’s Centre for International Security and Strategy, interpreted these remarks as both “a tough signal to Iran and reassurance to domestic audiences” ahead of the November midterm elections.

“The US does not want to get bogged down, but despite its overwhelming superiority, it has struggled to translate its strength into a decisive victory,” she said, noting US-led strikes had so far failed to topple Iran’s regime or resolve the nuclear issue.

Zhu Yongbiao, director of the Centre for Afghanistan Studies at Lanzhou University, also warned that Washington was showing signs of “sinking into a quagmire”, albeit one different from Vietnam or Afghanistan.

“It may not last as long, because from Trump to the military, there remains a certain level of vigilance within the US, and a reluctance to become deeply mired once again. This awareness helps prevent a repeat of past mistakes,” he said.

Yet disengagement remained difficult, he noted, citing Washington’s reluctance to concede while seeking gains in the Strait of Hormuz and hoping other maritime powers would share the burden.

Wen said: “The Vietnam quagmire analogy is not alarmist. If Iran does not play by US expectations, what will the US do next? It is indeed quite a headache.”

Both analysts noted that while the current conflict differed from Vietnam – particularly the absence of large-scale US ground deployments – key similarities remained: an adversary with strong ideological motivation, a high tolerance for costs and the ability to impose asymmetric pressure.

Zhu argued that Iran’s capacity to absorb sustained pressure reflected a combination of structural, ideological and institutional factors developed over decades of confrontation with the US.

“Iran is at least a middle power, arguably a major regional power, and such countries possess a natural, inherent resilience rooted in history, religion, national identity, as well as economic and geographic depth,” he said. “These factors provide Iran with significant endurance.”

Zhu highlighted the role of Shia political culture in the Islamic Republic, with its narratives of sacrifice and martyrdom, and the impact of long-term US-led sanctions in shaping what he described as a durable national temperament.

He added that incremental gains in successive phases of confrontation with Washington, especially perceived “victories” since the start of US-Israeli attacks on February 28, had reinforced Tehran’s determination and confidence to resist.

Wen pointed to Iran’s political institutions as another key source of resilience.

“Even after the targeted killing of senior leaders like Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, domestic politics remained stable and foreign policy coherent,” she said, adding that Iran’s legislative, executive and judicial systems were diverse and robust and leadership transitions had not led to collapse.

“A fundamental reason for this resilience is that neither domestic nor foreign policy relies on any single individual.

“There are relatively well-developed mechanisms that allow hardliners and pragmatists to quickly find a balance within the new power structure. These institutional mechanisms are the fundamental source of Iran’s resilience.”

Wen added that Iran also continued to maintain a strong influence in Lebanon, Iraq, Yemen and beyond through a network of regional allies and proxies, often referred to as the “Axis of Resistance”, such as Lebanon’s Shiite Muslim party Hezbollah.

“Although this network of proxies and alliances has been weakened by recent battlefield setbacks, it remains dispersed and low-cost, constituting a significant strategic asset for Iran,” she said, noting their abilities to complicate efforts by the US and its partners to weaken Iran’s regional reach.

Both Wen and Zhu also pointed to the Strait of Hormuz as central to Iran’s strategy.

“It is not just an energy corridor but a form of denial deterrence,” Wen said, noting that Iran did not need to fully close the strait to create strategic pressure. The mere risk of disruption could unsettle markets and raise costs for its adversaries.

Zhu characterised the waterway as a bargaining chip, arguing that it had become a focal point that served short-term strategic interests for both sides while giving Tehran extra leverage in negotiations.

He also cautioned against overestimating external backing, including China’s role, despite Beijing’s effort to position itself as a mediator while maintaining economic ties with Iran.

“The decisive factors are Iran’s own will to resist and its capabilities,” Zhu said, adding that external support played a largely auxiliary role, perhaps “as little as one-tenth”, in Iran’s overall resilience.

Wen echoed the view, describing China as an important economic partner and diplomatic advocate for dialogue, but “not a security guarantor”.

But she noted that Iran’s participation in the China-led Shanghai Cooperation Organisation and Brics was vital “because Iran has no other platforms to project its influence as a regional power”.

Pressure from Israel was further complicating US efforts to withdraw, he added, warning that “uncontrollable risks” such as accidental clashes could easily spark a wider escalation.

“Ultimately, this tests Trump’s resolve,” Zhu said. “If he acts decisively, the US has the capacity to withdraw quickly. But hesitation, or the pursuit of additional objectives, especially economic ones, will slow the process and risk dragging America deeper into difficulty.”

On Tuesday, Trump dropped plans to impose a 20 per cent charge on commercial cargo passing through the Strait of Hormuz, less than a day after announcing the proposal, though the US naval blockade targeting Iranian trade continued.

Trump said the fee would be replaced by trade and investment agreements with Gulf states following talks with regional leaders, writing on social media that “highly productive conversations” had led to the shift.

Additional reporting by Teresa Elena Frontado

item.title: [SCMP] Why South Korea’s submarine exports are running aground
item.description: South Korea’s failed bid for Canada’s next-generation submarine fleet has exposed a growing problem for one of Asia’s fastest-rising defence exporters: price, speed and technology may no longer be enough. Analysts said the loss of the Canadian Patrol Submarine Project (CPSP) to Germany’s ThyssenKrupp Marine Systems showed how Nato security ties and pressure on member states to favour allied industrial bases were increasingly outweighing commercial competitiveness in decisions over major purchase
item.content:encoded:
South Korea’s failed bid for Canada’s next-generation submarine fleet has exposed a growing problem for one of Asia’s fastest-rising defence exporters: price, speed and technology may no longer be enough.

Analysts said the loss of the Canadian Patrol Submarine Project (CPSP) to Germany’s ThyssenKrupp Marine Systems showed how Nato security ties and pressure on member states to favour allied industrial bases were increasingly outweighing commercial competitiveness in decisions over major purchases.

“The failure of the CPSP bid poured cold water on South Korea’s defence industry, which has performed strongly in recent years,” said Shin Jong-woo, secretary general of the Korea Defence and Security Forum, a Seoul-based think tank.

“As far as submarines are concerned, exports appear doomed for the foreseeable future, even though they are highly competitive.”

The setback has deepened concerns that South Korea’s submarine industry could soon face a shortage of new orders, forcing its defence firms to pursue more overseas production through joint ventures and local manufacturing partnerships as defence protectionism intensifies.

It was the second such blow in under a year. Hanwha Ocean, the shipbuilder behind the unsuccessful CPSP bid, also lost out on Poland’s Orka programme – a roughly US$4.8 billion contract for three air-independent propulsion submarines – when Warsaw chose Swedish shipyard Saab Kockums’ Blekinge-class design over both the Korean and German bids late last year.

Analysts suggest Hanwha Ocean, despite being the reserve supplier for the Canadian programme, may eventually have to restructure its submarine business to reduce excess capacity if new contracts become scarcer.

Last year, the European Union launched a €150 billion financing instrument known as SAFE (Security Action for Europe) to fund the joint procurement of ammunition, air defence systems and drones among member states via low-interest loans.

Shin said this was a key element in the growing hurdles facing South Korea and other non-European defence suppliers as the EU ramps up efforts to develop its own defence industrial base.

SAFE is designed to accelerate Europe’s defence readiness while strengthening its industrial base. Crucially, Canada also recently joined the framework, creating a powerful financial and strategic incentive for Ottawa to buy a European-designed platform.

Emptying order books

South Korea’s most successful submarine export market has been Indonesia, with three Nagapasa-class submarines, based on the KSS-I design, being delivered to the Indonesian Navy by Hanwha Ocean’s predecessor Daewoo Shipbuilding and Marine Engineering, under contracts worth more than US$1 billion, which also incorporated technology transfers and local construction.

A contract for a second batch of three submarines ultimately fell through, however, with Indonesia turning to France’s Naval Group for two Scorpene-class submarines instead.

South Korean shipbuilders have also submitted proposals to supply export versions of the KSS-III diesel-electric attack submarine to the Philippine Navy and are actively marketing their submarines to several countries considering fleet modernisation, including Peru, Egypt, Saudi Arabia and Greece.

Despite those efforts, industry analysts warn that South Korea’s submarine sector could run out of backlogged work after the current programme to build three 3,600-tonne upgraded versions of the KSS-III for the country’s navy is completed in 2029.

The first upgraded KSS-III submarine was launched in October last year, while the second and third vessels are undergoing testing or are under construction. No follow-on conventional submarine project has yet been announced.

Jang Won-joon, an associate professor of advanced defence technology and industry at Jeonbuk National University, said South Korea had benefited from Europe’s urgent demand for military equipment following Russia’s full-scale invasion of Ukraine.

“Four years on, however, European countries have largely returned to normal procurement practices, consolidating their defence industries through regionalised procurement centred on strategic alignment,” he said.

With Nato members now accounting for roughly 55 per cent of the global defence market and favouring suppliers who could contribute militarily in a conflict, Jang said South Korea increasingly found itself on the outside looking in.

His prescription is to build what he calls “quasi-alliance relationships” – covering joint production deals, technology partnerships and co-development arrangements – with Nato members and other potential customers rather than competing purely on merit.

Seoul and Nato have already agreed to begin negotiations on a procurement framework agreement that could open the door to South Korean firms joining the alliance’s joint defence procurement programmes.

Jang also dismissed concerns that deeper ties with the transatlantic security alliance would necessarily inflame tensions with Beijing and Moscow, arguing instead that “South Korea would be treated as a more equal partner by China and Russia through stronger cooperation with Nato”.

An autonomous future?

Industry insiders are increasingly anxious about what the future might hold for South Korea’s submarine industrial base.

An estimated 100 to 150 core suppliers make up the country’s submarine supply chain, expanding to as many as 300 companies once subcontractors and others are included.

“If left alone, I fear the entire submarine industrial ecosystem could collapse,” said one industry expert, speaking on condition of anonymity.

Large shipbuilders that also build surface warships and commercial vessels are expected to weather any downturn more easily, though smaller, specialised firms may not.

South Korea is planning to build nuclear-powered submarines, but Seoul first needs to renegotiate its nuclear cooperation agreement with Washington and industry officials expect years to pass before any contract materialises.

Experts also note that the industrial base required for nuclear-powered submarines is fundamentally different from that for conventional diesel-electric submarines.

“South Korea’s ambivalent diplomatic stance is also hindering the expansion of its defence exports,” the defence industry expert said.

Lee Il-woo, a senior analyst at the Korea Defence Network, said Seoul could simply place more orders to keep production lines alive, “but the government has shown little interest in procuring more conventional submarines”.

Shin suggested that South Korea’s shipyards could pivot towards large unmanned underwater vehicles instead, drawing on the country’s strengths in artificial intelligence.

“South Korea could gain a first-mover advantage in unmanned submarines, which are likely to become an increasingly important element of maritime security,” he said.

item.title: [SCMP] Chinese in the driver’s seat? India-China reset hits a bump on New Delhi’s streets
item.description: It was supposed to be just another hectic day on New Delhi’s roads. Instead, early this month, several e-rickshaws on the Indian capital’s crowded streets reportedly came to a halt mid-ride in separate incidents that left drivers stranded and passengers searching for answers. Videos of the disruption quickly went viral on social media, turning a local transport breakdown into a national debate. The alleged culprit was not a mechanical failure – it was software. And the trail led to China. Chines
item.content:encoded:
It was supposed to be just another hectic day on New Delhi’s roads. Instead, early this month, several e-rickshaws on the Indian capital’s crowded streets reportedly came to a halt mid-ride in separate incidents that left drivers stranded and passengers searching for answers.

Videos of the disruption quickly went viral on social media, turning a local transport breakdown into a national debate. The alleged culprit was not a mechanical failure – it was software. And the trail led to China.

Chinese battery management apps – including BAT-BMS, Epoch Li-ion and Lossigy – were said to have been used to remotely disable the vehicles, raising concerns over the security of connected electric vehicles and the possibility of Bluetooth-enabled tampering.

The apps did not respond to requests for comment from the South China Morning Post.

India’s IT secretary S. Krishnan later confirmed that a probe had been launched and that the government had ordered the removal of at least three Chinese battery management applications from app stores amid worries that connected EV systems could be exploited. Some reports mentioned up to seven apps being targeted.

The incident comes at a delicate moment in India-China relations. Years of tensions triggered by the deadly 2020 border clashes and India’s subsequent ban on Chinese apps such as WeChat and TikTok have given way to a cautious diplomatic thaw, with Delhi and Beijing seeking to restore economic engagement despite enduring strategic mistrust.

For a developing India, the challenge is increasingly one of balance. The country wants Chinese investment, manufacturing expertise and supply chains to support sectors such as electric vehicles, but it also wants to reduce vulnerabilities in areas where technology, data and connectivity could have security implications.

Analysts say the e-rickshaw episode has brought that contradiction into sharper focus. It is not only a question of whether India should engage with Chinese technology or move away from it. The larger challenge is how India manages dependence on China in critical sectors while protecting against potential strategic risks.

According to Amit Kumar of the Takshashila Institution, a think tank based in Bengaluru, “given the nature of India-China relations, the atmosphere is always charged, and episodes like these only make things worse”.

“The recent e-rickshaw incident shows how technology and apps can be weaponised, posing real security threats. The incident is reflective of the broader concern that India has. And it is exactly these kind of incidents that derail progress,” he added.

Kumar noted that the Chinese presence in India’s green energy transition was inevitable given China’s dominance of global supply chains.

Aadil Brar, a geopolitical analyst, said the case was a “reminder that dependence on Chinese technology is not always visible at the surface level” as it shows how a seemingly small mobility product can raise larger questions about surveillance, dependency and control.

“Even when a product is made in India, the more sensitive layers can still be Chinese, especially when it comes to batteries, software, connectivity and remote monitoring,” he added.

Kalpit Mankikar of the Observer Research Foundation, a think tank in Delhi, argued that the incident in the capital “feeds into the narrative of Chinese wielding a ‘kill switch’ in electronics systems”.

He added that it “will definitely impact the pace and scope of India’s economic normalisation with China, especially in sensitive sectors”.

EV battery experts, however, caution against viewing the issue only through the lens of geography. They argue that the underlying vulnerability lies in product security rather than the country where the technology is manufactured.

“As EV adoption grows, cybersecurity should be treated with the same importance as battery safety and vehicle reliability,” said Manju Verma, founder of Hyderabad-based EV diagnostics and battery intelligence company EVXpertz.

While such incidents “are not widespread”, Verma said they highlighted the need for manufacturers to strengthen security measures in connected battery systems.

Describing the Delhi disruption as “a product security issue rather than a Bluetooth issue”, Verma said the main concern was authentication.

Battery management systems are designed to monitor a battery’s voltage, temperature and current in real time. However, if these systems rely on weak or default passwords, they may also allow unauthorised users to send control commands.

“If a BMS uses default or commonly known passwords that are never changed, an unauthorised person with the appropriate BMS management app and within Bluetooth range could potentially access certain functions, including disabling the battery where that capability exists,” Verma said.

At EVXpertz workshops, Verma said the company regularly saw Bluetooth-enabled battery packs that could be discovered by nearby devices.

“Bluetooth visibility itself is not the issue,” she said, noting that the typical range was around 20 to 50 metres (65 to 164 feet).

The bigger concern, she added, was whether manufacturers had implemented proper authentication and access controls.

The debate in India echoes a broader discussion under way in the West, especially Washington, where policymakers have increasingly viewed connected vehicles as strategic technologies rather than simply consumer products.

A report from the American Enterprise Institute, a Washington-based think tank, argued that modern vehicles are no longer just machines but “connected computers” that collect data, communicate externally and rely on complex software systems.

The report, co-authored by Chris Miller, author of Chip War: The Fight for the World’s Most Critical Technology, warned that “packed with sensors, connected to the internet, and controlled by complex and opaque layers of software, modern cars represent a significant cybersecurity risk”.

In a separate email to the SCMP, Miller called requiring security reviews or “even prohibiting Chinese battery management systems a reasonable approach to mitigating these risks”.

He stressed that “connectivity software and hardware is another sphere where governments should carefully scrutinise reliance on Chinese components that could enable remote access”.

As some members of the European Union, and Canada, court Chinese investment and technology as part of their “reverse tech” strategy, the US is heading the other way, hitting Chinese EVs with 120 per cent tariffs and banning certain Chinese firms from selling new vehicles under the Commerce Department’s Connected Vehicle Rule.

In May, John Moolenaar, chairman of the House Select Committee on China, co-introduced the Connected Vehicle Security Act to ban Chinese vehicles and components from US roads. He has repeatedly slammed connected vehicles as “rolling data hubs”.

This week, Norwegian public transport operator Ruter announced plans to tighten anti-hacking measures after a test on new Chinese-made electric buses showed the manufacturer could remotely turn them off.

China, meanwhile, is moving in the opposite direction, making connected features such as remote monitoring, data recording and management increasingly central to its automotive framework.

Even as India finds itself at a crossroads after the e-rickshaw disruption, cybersecurity experts caution against reducing the debate to the question of Chinese origin alone.

“I believe this issue should be viewed from the perspective of cybersecurity practices rather than the country of origin,” Verma said.

The key question, she added, was whether manufacturers followed secure design principles “regardless of where the hardware is made”.

“Weak authentication and poor access control can create vulnerabilities in any connected device,” she said.

Kumar argued that India’s strategy with China must be to “manage dependencies, de-risk rather than reduce dependence, and create counter-dependencies”.

He said the problem was “not dependence or investments per se”, but creating a framework to de-risk trade and screen investments for strategic vulnerabilities.

“Once we get that right, imports and investments shouldn’t be a problem. And we must be open to it.”

item.title: [SCMP] Indonesia’s anti-LGBT education push raises concerns about stigmatising youth
item.description: Indonesia’s Ministry of Religious Affairs is drafting educational material aimed at discouraging what officials call the spread of “LGBT culture”, after a presidential regulation listed the issue among the country’s non-military security threats. Officials said the content, which was still under discussion, could be included in religious education in regular and Islamic schools, as well as Friday prayer sermons, family development programmes and other religious events. The government has said th
item.content:encoded:
Indonesia’s Ministry of Religious Affairs is drafting educational material aimed at discouraging what officials call the spread of “LGBT culture”, after a presidential regulation listed the issue among the country’s non-military security threats.

Officials said the content, which was still under discussion, could be included in religious education in regular and Islamic schools, as well as Friday prayer sermons, family development programmes and other religious events.

The government has said the presidential regulation is not meant to be the basis for criminalising any group, but experts and queer activists note that bringing the issue into classrooms risks deepening stigma around sexual orientation.

They also argue there is no scientific proof that anti-LGBT education can change a person’s sexual orientation, while warning that such teaching could harm children’s mental health.

Same-sex relations are not criminalised nationally in Indonesia, though LGBT people face deep social stigma. Aceh, the only province with special autonomy to enforce sharia law, punishes same-sex conduct.

Romo Muhammad Syafi’i, vice-minister of religion, said the material was a follow-up to the 2025 presidential regulation that included the spread of homosexuality as a non-military threat to national security, alongside separatism, terrorism and online gambling.

Coordinating Minister for Law, Human Rights, Immigration, and Corrections Yusril Ihza Mahendra has said the regulation is not a special rule targeting LGBT people and should not be read as a basis for criminalising any group.

“Currently, in accordance with the leadership’s directive, the Ministry of Religious Affairs is developing content related to LGBT in Indonesia. Discussions are still ongoing. We haven’t yet focused on more detailed content,” Abu Rokhmad, director general of Islamic community guidance at the ministry, told This Week in Asia.

“We must truly understand that LGBT [is caused] by many variations. Some are hormonal, meaning they have a hormonal predisposition [to being queer]. Some are influenced by their environment. Some are [gay] because of their belief related to freedom of sexual orientation. This requires different approaches.”

According to Rokhmad, there are suggestions that the anti-LGBT material should be taught starting in elementary school. He said teachers would also be trained on the matter but did not provide further details.

He vowed to view the issue “comprehensively”, particularly from the perspective of religious leaders.

“We must certainly be consistent with the views of religious scholars and figures regarding LGBT. Many religions generally prohibit it. We already have a regulation on this matter. Our culture also tends not to normalise LGBT,” Abu said.

No scientific proof

Social scientists and members of the LGBT community have criticised the decision, warning it could backfire with consequences for young people.

“The ministry’s position is based on the consensus of five religious figures, whom they claim disapprove of LGBT. In Indonesian society, where the majority [believes] in God, this religious-based argument has strong legitimacy and cannot be ignored,” said Wida Ayu Puspitosari, a sociologist at Brawijaya University.

“However, sexual orientation is not something that arises from exposure to information, it’s not something that can be prevented through education or counselling. Conversion efforts have also repeatedly been deemed ineffective and risk causing psychological harm.”

Wida warned that anti-LGBT teaching in schools could instead increase self-isolation among children.

“The debate about nature versus nurture is not yet fully resolved in the scientific realm, but today’s evidence does not support the idea that curriculum material can change a person’s [sexual] orientation,” Wida said.

Arisdo Gonzales, outreach director and campaigner at Pelangi Nusantara, an NGO that advocates for the LGBT community, called the anti-gay education plan an “inappropriate” approach because it discriminated against individuals with different sexual orientations.

“LGBT is an identity factor … and it cannot be spread, it is not a disease that when I poke someone, they will become gay,” he said.

For Andi*, a gay man living in Jakarta, the anti-LGBT teachings will “not have an impact and [are] simply not relevant” to an individual’s sexual orientation.

“As a gay man born into a devout Muslim family, I attended religious school from childhood to adolescence, but that doesn’t [alter] the fact that I am gay,” Andi said.

“And from my experience, there’s no connection between religion and my biological development. I continue to practise religious rituals, while at the same time, I’m gay.”

Digital persecution and raids

The anti-LGBT rhetoric has resurfaced with intensity in Indonesia in recent weeks, as members of the community faced raids, online harassment and public shaming.

Last month, a student body for psychology majors at the University of Indonesia published a post on social media, citing a 2008 study from the American Psychological Association that said homosexuality was neither a mental illness nor a psychological deviation. The post was removed after it went viral and sparked public backlash.
