Internationally, the rivalry between Beijing and Washington has served as a catalyst for Hong Kong’s IPO market, with analysts saying it offers an advantage over New York as a listing destination. However, appearing on the US radar is often accompanied with uncertainties.

Investor sentiment was further unsettled by comments in April 2025 from US Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent that “everything is on the table”, regarding the possibility of Washington delisting Chinese companies from American stock exchanges as part of its trade negotiations, adding that it was up to US President Donald Trump to decide.

This prompted a number of firms, including Alibaba and Baidu, to reassess cross-border listing strategies. Alibaba owns the South China Morning Post.

Jacky Tang, chief investment officer for emerging markets at Deutsche Bank in Hong Kong, said the proximity to mainland China’s corporate ecosystem and investor base had become increasingly valuable, with global capital markets becoming more fragmented and geopolitical considerations playing a larger role in listing decisions.

“Hong Kong is shifting from ‘backup venue’ to ‘default international venue’ for a growing group of Chinese issuers,” Tang said.

A moat fed by more sources

However, the persistent reliance on mainland issuers has raised questions over whether Hong Kong can preserve its status as a truly global financial centre. Market participants suggest that this concentration is far from sufficient, and that long-term resilience requires a more diversified issuer and investor base.

“The dominance of mainland Chinese companies in Hong Kong’s IPO market is likely to continue in the medium term,” said Tommy Ong, managing director of T.O. & Associates Consultancy, describing it as “a natural result” of Beijing’s 2024 capital market guidelines.

Mainland Chinese companies accounted for more than 90 per cent of both total funds raised and deal volume in the first half of 2026, according to a Deloitte research report published in June.

Ong recommended that Hong Kong diversify both the geographical mix of its issuers and the range of products available in its capital markets, attracting more companies from Asia, particularly the Asean bloc.

Beyond equities, Hong Kong should leverage its strengths in bond and foreign-exchange markets by expanding products such as tokenised bonds to broaden the investor base, while developing offshore renminbi-denominated commodity futures to meet rising demand for risk-hedging from Chinese companies, Ong added.

If policies are unpredictable and volatile, investors who commit significant capital will never feel secure

Lee Siu-po, economist

The predictability premium

Since the implementation of the National Security Law in 2020, debates over Hong Kong’s institutional autonomy have intensified, with some investors reassessing long-term exposure to the city’s markets.

For many market participants, the central concern is predictability, with regulatory consistency increasingly seen as a core determinant of investor confidence.

Economist Simon Lee Siu-po said Hong Kong’s strength has long rested on its stable and rules-based environment, which allows global capital to be deployed with clarity.

But he warned that Beijing’s recent regulatory interventions, when viewed alongside broader geopolitical uncertainties, have made that predictability less certain.

“International investors, fearing their capital could be trapped or affected by sudden policy interventions, are losing the confidence required to hold Hong Kong assets over the long term,” Lee Siu-po said.

More recently, tighter oversight of cross-border capital flows and enforcement actions targeting certain forms of offshore trading have reinforced a more cautious stance among parts of the global investment community.

The economist argued that maintaining Hong Kong’s status would depend on reinforcing a transparent and stable regulatory framework that reduces uncertainty for long-term investors, rather than relying on short-term market sentiment.

“If policies are unpredictable and volatile, investors who commit significant capital will never feel secure,” he said.

Additional reporting by Zoe Chan

item.title: [SCMP] Philip Chan: from singing inspector to Hong Kong cinema legend
item.description: I WAS BORN in Hong Kong in January 1945, during the Japanese military occupation. My parents were from Hong Kong, but my (paternal) grandfather was a general serving in the military in Guangxi. He had three wives and six children. During the civil war, he lost his command, retired to Guangzhou and then came south as the Japanese invaded. It was very tough. In fact, I read in my mother’s diary that when I was a baby, they had no money and she went to her father’s house to ask for a loan. Her fath
item.content:encoded:
I WAS BORN in Hong Kong in January 1945, during the Japanese military occupation. My parents were from Hong Kong, but my (paternal) grandfather was a general serving in the military in Guangxi. He had three wives and six children. During the civil war, he lost his command, retired to Guangzhou and then came south as the Japanese invaded. It was very tough. In fact, I read in my mother’s diary that when I was a baby, they had no money and she went to her father’s house to ask for a loan. Her father was a well-to-do businessman. He and his partners owned the Tabaqueria Filipina, which was a famous tobacco and wine company. He refused the loan because he didn’t like my father, who was just a foreman in the then-Sanitary Department. He also disliked my father because he was Cantonese and they were all Shanghainese. And my mother wrote in her diary that she was very disappointed.

SHE THEN TOOK my father’s watch and sold it on the waterfront to buy milk powder for me. The most touching part was when she wrote, “It was heartbreaking, but when I looked at my baby Kin, lying in my arms, I said to myself, ‘It’s all worth it.’”

I WAS THE FIRSTBORN, I have one sister and three brothers and we’re very tight. My most vivid memory of growing up is the riots in 1956. We lived in Mong Kok, on the rooftop of an abandoned factory, where there was a big covered area. There were about 12 or 13 households, all living there in separate wooden compartments – cubicles. Our family had two or three cubicles. It was crowded with people of various occupations – bonesetters, photographers and merchant sailors. I remember that day in October 1956, when the government tore down the Nationalist flag in Lei Cheng Uk Estate and there was an all-out riot. I was playing on the rooftop and we heard the sound of sirens. I looked down from our fourth-floor roof and I saw two armoured cars driving into Shantung Street, where our building was. British soldiers were chasing after a bunch of people dressed in black who were throwing rocks, bottles and other stuff at the soldiers. Suddenly there was a burst of machine-gun fire, then there was a boom, and a metal can shot up onto our rooftop and landed behind me. I was suddenly hit by the smoke and smell and I was in tears.

I WENT TO Good Hope School in Ngau Chi Wan, but after two years changed to La Salle College, where I stayed until I graduated in 1965. When I was in Form One or Two, I was in the school choir, and James Wong, who would later become the famous lyricist, was my senior. He played the harmonica and I admired him very much. Another schoolmate was a rhythm guitarist and part of a band called the Astro-Notes and they invited me to become the lead singer. It was the same era as the Kontinentals and Teddy Robin. We would perform at afternoon tea dances, from 3pm to 6pm, including at the Bayside, in the basement of Chungking Mansions. We also did a record with Diamond Records.

MY MOTHER’S BROTHER was a police inspector and I decided to try to enrol. I was a year younger than the minimum age requirement but Brother Casimir, my principal at La Salle, wrote a letter of recommendation, and they accepted me. I enjoyed my six months of training – firearms and combat, drills, discipline and all that. It was heaven for a young man of 19.

AT FIRST, I was sent to the Kowloon City Division as a police inspector, then I became a detective inspector very fast. In May 1974, when I was chief inspector in charge of CID (Criminal Investigation Department) in Mong Kok, there was a bank robbery. A gunman burst into the Po Sang Bank in Shanghai Street and demanded money. He fired a couple of shots inside as a warning to us, so it was quite intense. He got some money and then barricaded himself inside with 11 hostages. We had a lot of conversations with the gunman, asking him to give up. He asked for food. TVB sent a news team, and they put cameras on the rooftops. That’s when I was interviewed. Finally, the staff members jumped him, and that’s how the whole thing finished.

I HAD THREE MAJOR CASES in less than two years. The second one was the mutilation murder in August 1974 (of sex worker Lau Fu-man) in Mong Kok. When the woman’s body was found, sections of her face and other body parts had been cut off. These were found hidden between an air conditioner and a wall. We put those parts back onto her face and got an artist to draw a sketch of her to put in the newspapers. Her mother and her two children identified her from the sketch. We eventually found (the killer, Leung Siu-ping). He was a construction worker. He had strangled her. The third major case was a Taiwanese woman who, along with her lover, killed her husband, making it look like a robbery. I’ll save the details of that for my autobiography.

FOR 11 YEARS, including my time with the police, I sang on commercial jingles and as backing vocals on records, composed and written by Joseph Koo and James Wong. I did jingles for muesli, cognac and cigarettes. It was a different era. I was nicknamed the “Singing Inspector” because I wrote the lyrics and went on television to sing and promote road safety for children.

I RESIGNED FROM THE police in 1976, when acclaimed actress Siao Fong-fong and director Leong Po-chih asked me to be a researcher for their crime thriller, Jumping Ash. Coached by Siao, I learned to become a scriptwriter. She was my mentor and helped me transition to making movies. I joined the film team and learned about music selection, editing, production and sound effects. The film was a box office hit. Then, I emceed television shows.

I MET MY WIFE, Nona (Cheung Yung-yung), in 1969. She was an actress for Shaw Brothers and also Shanghainese. I have a special liking for the Shanghainese because of my mother’s side. We got married in 1973 and our first child was born in 1974. We’ve been married a long time. My wife suffers from dementia and has had that for more than 10 years. We have two daughters and we’re all very close.

THE MOST UNUSUAL PLACE I filmed in was Kowloon Walled City. That was for Long Arm of the Law (1984), directed by Johnny Mak. I wrote the script and it is my favourite film. The final gunfight scene was in the Walled City, which was infested with triads and drugs. The residents didn’t like people going in and disturbing their livelihoods. But Johnny Mak was a fantastic director and somehow we prevailed. That film left a valuable visual history of such a unique and mysterious place.

IN THE 1990s, I was in charge of Metro Broadcast, where I steered the station’s focus towards entertainment. I also worked on a lot of comedies with brothers Sam, Michael and Ricky Hui, as well as Richard Ng, John Shum and also Sammo Hung. When it comes to acting, I’m always a detective – scolding Chow Yun-fat or Jackie Chan. What I love most is directing comedies, like Chocolate Inspector (1986), with Michael Hui. I enjoy telling stories.

MY NEXT VENTURE is an autobiography, 陳欣健 – 我的風雲往事 (Philip Chan: Braving the Storms – Hong Kong 1945-1976), to tell the story of my life as an eyewitness to the tumultuous history of Hong Kong. It’s coming out on July 18 at this year’s Hong Kong Book Fair.

This interview has been edited and condensed for clarity

item.title: [SCMP] Is Japan a ‘spy paradise’ fuelling Russia’s war machine?
item.description: Concern that foreign spies can operate with impunity in Japan has deepened following a media report that dozens of Russian agents ordered to leave Western European countries have entered Tokyo and are buying components for Moscow’s war on Ukraine. The New York Times reported on Sunday that Moscow’s agents were taking advantage of Tokyo’s failure to enact a law specifically designed to combat espionage and the large number of companies manufacturing components critical to the Kremlin’s war machin
item.content:encoded:
Concern that foreign spies can operate with impunity in Japan has deepened following a media report that dozens of Russian agents ordered to leave Western European countries have entered Tokyo and are buying components for Moscow’s war on Ukraine.

The New York Times reported on Sunday that Moscow’s agents were taking advantage of Tokyo’s failure to enact a law specifically designed to combat espionage and the large number of companies manufacturing components critical to the Kremlin’s war machine.

The coveted parts include microchips, transmitters and the cutting-edge machinery required to make advanced weapons systems.

The report identified Maksim Vladimirovich Filchenkov, ostensibly an employee of Russian airline Aeroflot, as leading the operation in Japan.

James Brown, a professor of international relations specialising in Russian affairs at Temple University’s Tokyo campus, told This Week in Asia that what stood out in the report was how the alleged spy was unmasked, suggesting it could have been a tip-off from a foreign intelligence agency.

“There have been no direct flights to Russia for the last four years, so what was he doing here?” said Brown, author of the 2025 book Cracking the Crab: Russian espionage against Japan, from Peter the Great to Richard Sorge.

“I wonder if a foreign agency just got frustrated that there is so much espionage activity going on in Japan that is contributing to dual-use goods being funnelled to Russia and that Japan is not doing enough to stop it.”

He said the information sharing could have been a calculated move that “forces the Japanese authorities to take some action”, and warned that evidence of the technology Moscow was accessing was damning.

Vladyslav Vlasiuk, the Ukrainian official overseeing sanctions policy, told Kyodo News in late June that electronic components made by Japanese firms had been recovered from around 90 per cent of the Russian cruise and ballistic missiles fired at Ukraine, as well as from drones.

According to Yakov Zinberg, a professor of international relations at Tokyo’s Kokushikan University, the revelation that Russian agents have been able to enter Japan claiming to be diplomats, businessmen or working for Russian companies, such as Aeroflot, but then set about obtaining sensitive technology without intervention from Japanese authorities, will be “embarrassing” to Tokyo.

It comes just six months after Japanese police submitted documents to prosecutors identifying a former official of Russia’s trade representation in Japan as having violated unfair competition laws. The man, who was not named, allegedly paid a Japanese employee of a machine tool manufacturer for information on its products.

Japanese authorities only belatedly learned the Japanese employee had first met the Russian in 2023 and were unable to detain Moscow’s agent because he had left the country 10 months earlier.

The Times article claimed Japan had become “a crucial part of the Russian war effort”.

Quoting officials from five Western intelligence agencies, the report said the operation was being spearheaded by Filchenkov, who was in fact Moscow’s most senior 20th Directorate official in Japan. The agency is controlled by the GRU, or the primary intelligence directorate of the Russian armed forces.

Describing Japan as a “spy paradise”, the report claimed Filchenkov arrived in Tokyo in February 2024 and quickly set up links with logistics firms to help ship goods to Russia, either by using falsified shipping records or via third countries, frequently Vietnam.

Proco Air was identified as one of the firms carrying out shipments, although owner Takehiko Miki has denied being aware that Filchenkov was linked to Russian intelligence or that he sent prohibited items to Russia.

While US journalists had traced Miki and evidence of his company’s role in shipments to Russia, he said Japanese authorities had never contacted him.

Japan’s foreign ministry has not commented on the case.

Brown said Japan’s delay in acting against agents from Russia and other rival nations had historical roots, but the current administration was trying to rectify the issue.

“Japan was a police state up until 1945, and after that they wanted to have a democratic system so the security services could not have too much power again,” he said.

That “overcorrection” left Japan open to significant threats because current laws did not explicitly state that engaging in espionage was illegal, Brown added.

That is changing, however, with Prime Minister’s Sanae Takaichi’s government passing a bill in May to create a national intelligence bureau that will meet for the first time this month. The intention is to follow that with legislation outlawing spying and creating an external intelligence agency.

“There is going to be lots of pushback from the left to these initiatives and claims that Japan is ‘going back to the dark days of the 1930s’, so in that sense having this spy story break right now is quite helpful to the government,” Brown said.

Zinberg, who is originally from St Petersburg, agreed that Russian agents were “at ease here in Japan because there is still no legislation against espionage”.

“It is much easier and safer for them to operate here than in Europe and they have access to lots of companies making the parts they need for their missiles and drones,” he said.

According to Zinberg, the operation is likely linked closely to Moscow’s large embassy in Tokyo and may also involve individuals in various public and private positions in Japan, including those who could surreptitiously smuggle items out of the country using falsified paperwork.

Japan’s security allies had good reason to be alarmed at this latest revelation, he warned.

“Europe is going to be very worried indeed and something like this is going to leave a lasting impression,” he said.

“I think this is going to significantly affect Japan’s security relationships moving forward.”

Observers point out that partners are likely to become increasingly wary about secrets leaking from Japanese companies engaged in joint development of advanced weapons, such as the Global Combat Air Programme with Britain and Italy.

Ukraine could also use the unmasking of Moscow’s agents in Japan to its own advantage, Zinberg said.

“I see Ukraine making very good use of it,” he said. “They are going to propagate this news – as they have done previously – to encourage Japan and its companies to take better measures to stop the transfer of these items.”

item.title: [SCMP] Why ‘reverse tech transfer’ from China remains a non-starter in the US auto industry
item.description: For decades, Western carmakers complained that China forced them to hand over technology in exchange for access to the world’s largest auto market. Now the tables have turned. As Chinese carmakers capture global market share with cheaper, high-quality electric vehicles, countries including Canada and members of the European Union are embracing a “reverse tech transfer” strategy – by seeking Chinese investment and technology to strengthen their own industries. The United States, however, is takin
item.content:encoded:
For decades, Western carmakers complained that China forced them to hand over technology in exchange for access to the world’s largest auto market.

Now the tables have turned.

As Chinese carmakers capture global market share with cheaper, high-quality electric vehicles, countries including Canada and members of the European Union are embracing a “reverse tech transfer” strategy – by seeking Chinese investment and technology to strengthen their own industries.

The United States, however, is taking the opposite approach.

Tariffs on Chinese EVs have expanded, Chinese-owned auto companies are being banned or put on notice, and lawmakers are weighing new legislation to block the new global leader in electric vehicles.

Analysts say despite abundant scope for reverse tech transfer that could improve American carmakers’ competitiveness, Chinese companies would likely remain locked out of the market due to deep-seated barriers: geopolitical tensions, espionage concerns, economic protectionism, bipartisan opposition in Congress and resistance in local communities.

“The political aperture for such transfers has shrunk and is very narrow,” said Sourabh Gupta, resident senior fellow at the Institute for China-America Studies.

“There are meaningful opportunities for technology transfer from Chinese to American firms,” noted John Minnich, assistant professor at the London School of Economics.

“I remain sceptical that Washington will allow significant Chinese greenfield manufacturing investment in the US in the foreseeable future.”

Chinese EV technology faces steep barriers in the US

While US President Donald Trump has repeatedly suggested allowing Chinese carmakers to build manufacturing facilities in the US, his administration has aggressively moved to block their entry, citing espionage concerns, Beijing’s state subsidies and unfair competition.

It has maintained tariffs of more than 120 per cent on Chinese vehicles and tightened restrictions on US-assembled EVs containing Chinese software and hardware due to data security concerns.

Last month, the Trump administration banned Chinese-owned EV brand Polestar from selling new vehicles in the US under the Commerce Department’s Connected Vehicle Rule, finalised in the final days of the Joe Biden administration.

The rule prohibits the import and sale of vehicles containing China-linked hardware or software, including Bluetooth, Wi-fi, cellular connectivity and certain satellite communication technologies, citing national security concerns over data collection.

Polestar, owned by China’s Geely Holding Group, assembles vehicles in the US. Its sister brands, Volvo and Lotus, have so far been allowed to continue operating under heightened government scrutiny.

In response to the ban, Polestar said it would increase its “strategic focus” on Europe by localising the manufacturing of future car models.

“Forget about Chinese technology; even US-assembled, Chinese-owned EVs may never make it onto US roads for any durable period of time, as the recent case with Polestar demonstrates,” said Gupta of the Institute for China-America Studies.

In April, the US Trade Representative Jamieson Greer ruled out any relaxation in regulations even if Chinese auto companies attempt to invest in the United States.

“Those rules are effective,” Greer said.

Despite the recent improvement in bilateral ties following Trump’s visit to China, the administration has kept the strategic auto sector out of the newly created Board of Investment framework.

Congress has also moved to pursue tougher measures, with lawmakers from both parties introducing multiple House and Senate bills to shut out China-linked electric vehicles.

Republican Senator Bernie Moreno and House Select Committee on China chairman John Moolenaar have introduced identical versions of the “Connected Vehicle Security Act” in the Senate and House, which would “prohibit the importation, manufacture, and sale of connected vehicles, software, and hardware linked to China”.

On Monday, both lawmakers opposed joint ventures with Chinese automakers and dismissed the idea that their presence could drive innovation in the US market.

“If you have a joint venture between, for example, BYD and Ford … you would have a situation where you have an unbalanced deal because Ford has to live by US law and order, meaning private companies have to behave a certain way,” Moreno said at an event at the American Enterprise Institute.

“The Chinese model is very different. The Chinese government has the ability to dictate to BYD what to do.”

Moolenaar described the United States as an “innovator” and warned that Chinese companies would access American technology “legally or illegally” and use forced labour and unfair trade practices to drive US companies out of business.

“Not only is that an economic issue, but it’s also a national defence issue because you think of the arsenal of democracy – the manufacturing that we need for our defences,” he said.

“If we lose that capability in the United States, that puts our national security at risk.”

The Republican lawmaker from Michigan has also targeted Ford’s licencing agreement with China’s Contemporary Amperex Technology Co Ltd (CATL) – the world’s biggest battery maker – to manufacture energy storage systems in the state.

One of the few surviving US-China battery partnerships, the Ford-CATL plant in Marshall, Michigan, has also faced intense local opposition since its announcement in 2023.

Meanwhile, another multibillion-dollar EV battery plant proposed by Chinese company Gotion was abandoned in Michigan after years of persistent protests.

The Chinese embassy in Washington accused the lawmakers of overstretching “the concept of national security” to target Chinese companies, saying they had “politicised and instrumentalised” economic, trade and technology issues while making “fabricated allegations” such as forced labour claims.

“China firmly opposes such moves, will closely follow relevant developments, and will resolutely safeguard the legitimate rights and interests of Chinese companies,” embassy spokesman Liu Chang told the South China Morning Post.

Gupta said that a “China Derangement Syndrome”, stemming from Chinese auto leadership in the EV sector, was “compounding the stakes and incentivising short-term approaches”.

“It has less to do with revitalising manufacturing in the auto sector and more to do with politicians’ instinctive recourse to manufacture jobs behind protectionist barriers,” he added.

US legacy carmakers too have joined the political push to keep Chinese EV makers out. In March, five industry groups representing carmakers, including the Detroit Three – General Motors, Ford and Stellantis – as well as Volkswagen, Hyundai and Toyota, urged the Trump administration to act, calling Chinese EV companies’ presence in the US “a direct threat to America’s global competitiveness, national security and automotive industrial base”.

Protectionism may weaken American carmakers’ global competitiveness

Industry experts cautioned that this blanket isolationism could undercut the long-term global competitiveness of legacy carmakers and hurt consumers.

“If we protect the market for the next three years, I don’t think that the automakers will feel the urgency to have to reinvent themselves or disrupt themselves in a way that would be meaningful for their global competitiveness,” said Tu Le, founder of consultancy Sino Auto Insights.

Western carmakers are steadily losing ground in once-lucrative Chinese and European markets as domestic rivals gain traction, consumers shift towards EVs, and affordable non-Chinese alternatives remain limited.

Le also suggested that the Trump administration could address cybersecurity risks through a “data sovereignty framework” with adequate guard rails.

“If we want to force or restrict the use of Chinese suppliers on the connected hardware and software side, let’s do that,” he said.

“But if we are going to restrict certain components that might not pose a risk, then that’s unnecessary.”

The Trump administration’s rollback of electric vehicle tax incentives under the Inflation Reduction Act is also fuelling domestic complacency, according to Ilaria Mazzocco, a senior fellow at the Centre for Strategic and International Studies.

“Shielding from competition without creating pressure to innovate tends to create stagnant industries,” she emphasised.

To avoid stagnation, John Minnich of the London School of Economics noted that China introduced the so-called catfish effect policy in 2018 by allowing Elon Musk’s Tesla to build a wholly owned Gigafactory in Shanghai – a rare exception to its usual requirement that foreign companies partner with domestic firms.

He said the idea was to bring in a hypercompetitive foreign firm to both stimulate competition for local original equipment manufacturers and help nurture supplier ecosystems.

“It worked extremely well in China’s case and could probably benefit American automakers in the long run,” Minnich added.

According to Le, Canada’s approach of allowing limited Chinese EV imports offers a viable model for the US, though he conceded that it remains politically difficult.

“You can have them trickle in over a period of time,” he said.

“If they can give a financial commitment, let’s identify a handful of companies that want some sort of contract to build locally. Trump is going to force them to build in the United States, which is not necessarily a bad thing.”

But Gupta contended that intense political resistance and espionage concerns would prevent Chinese carmakers from entering the US market for the foreseeable future.

“The Chinese auto industry will essentially find itself on the outside, perpetually looking in, and excluded from the US market via both the direct and indirect routes.”

item.title: [SCMP] Trump to restore Iran blockade, charge fees for Strait of Hormuz shipping
item.description: US President Donald Trump said on Monday the United States would begin charging commercial vessels for safe passage through the Strait of Hormuz as Washington reinstates its blockade of Iran following a renewed escalation in the conflict. Trump said Iranian vessels and ships trading with Iran would be barred from using the strategic waterway, while other countries would retain access under a system that would impose a 20 per cent fee on eligible cargoes. “We are reinstating the THE IRANIAN BLOCK
item.content:encoded:
US President Donald Trump said on Monday the United States would begin charging commercial vessels for safe passage through the Strait of Hormuz as Washington reinstates its blockade of Iran following a renewed escalation in the conflict.

Trump said Iranian vessels and ships trading with Iran would be barred from using the strategic waterway, while other countries would retain access under a system that would impose a 20 per cent fee on eligible cargoes.

“We are reinstating the THE IRANIAN BLOCKADE, so named because it is only stopping Iran’s ships or customers from entering or leaving,” Trump said online. “All other countries will have fair and open use of the Strait.”

The US president said the charge would help pay for “any and all costs necessary to do the job of providing safety and security to this very volatile section of the World”.

As US forces struck Iran for a third consecutive night, Trump argued that the naval blockade had proven effective, particularly when paired with attacks on Iranian military infrastructure.

He also suggested Washington should be compensated for protecting Gulf allies and ensuring the flow of commerce through the region.

“We expect to be reimbursed because we are protecting a very rich part of the world ... Saudi Arabia, UAE, Qatar, Bahrain, Kuwait, they do very well,” Trump said on Monday afternoon.

“We are doing it to protect allies. We are doing it to protect them.”

Trump's proposal prompted questions about whether Washington could legally impose fees on vessels using one of the world’s most important international waterways.

The International Maritime Organization said it was seeking further details on Trump’s announcement and reiterated its long-standing opposition to mandatory charges for passage through international straits.

“IMO stands firmly against charging fees for passage through straits used for international navigation,” a spokesperson for the UN shipping agency said.

“There is no legal basis through which to introduce mandatory tolls simply to transit through a strait.”

International maritime law experts said, however, that Trump’s proposal could instead be interpreted as an optional charge for naval protection services rather than a fee for transit itself.

“This is a fee for services rendered (protection), not a fee for transit,” said James Kraska, chair of international maritime law at the US Naval War College and a visiting professor at Harvard Law School.

“The regime of transit passage would remain unaffected,” he said, adding that vessels would not be required to pay for passage through the strait but could choose to pay for American naval protection while transiting the waterway.

Kraska compared the proposal to anti-piracy arrangements used in waters off Somalia, where commercial vessels have at times hired private security companies or government protection detachments to safeguard voyages through high-risk areas.

Elisabeth Braw, a senior fellow at the Atlantic Council, said shipping companies would likely be reluctant to accept any arrangement that could be interpreted as a charge for access to international waters.

“If a country, in this case the United States, starts charging tolls for access to a strait, then other countries can say, ‘Well, in our strait, we will start charging tolls too,’” she said, warning that the move could undermine the principle of freedom of navigation that has governed global shipping for decades.

Braw argued that comparisons with anti-piracy measures off Somalia were misplaced because shipping companies pay private security providers or armed guards rather than governments for access to international waters.

Karen Young, a senior research scholar at Columbia University’s Centre on Global Energy Policy, said she viewed Trump’s proposal less as a concrete policy than as a negotiating tactic or warning directed at Tehran.

But she said the announcement risked further eroding confidence in the US security role in the Gulf.

“It lowers the already limited sentiment of trust between Iran and the US and will make regional states feel even more insecure about the US role and commitment in the region,” Young said.

Oil markets reacted sharply to the latest escalation, with Brent crude jumping 9.6 per cent and WTI rising 9.4 per cent on Monday as traders assessed the implications for energy supplies.

Young cautioned that markets may still be underestimating the risks to global energy supplies because disruptions in the Strait of Hormuz are coinciding with continued security concerns in the Red Sea shipping corridor.

“We are moving into another period of low traffic and threatened security in the Strait of Hormuz and this time the Red Sea corridor is under threat at the same time,” she said.

The latest escalation followed an Iranian attack on a container vessel in the Strait of Hormuz on Sunday, an incident that heightened concerns over shipping security and the stability of global energy markets.

The US military will begin enforcing the blockade from 2000 GMT on Tuesday, according to an advisory issued by the US Navy-led Joint Maritime Information Center.

The notice said the blockade would cover Iranian ports, oil terminals and coastal areas and apply to all vessel traffic regardless of flag.

“Any vessel suspected of entering or departing the blockaded area without authorisation is subject to interception, diversion, and capture,” the advisory said, adding that non-compliant vessels could be compelled “with force”.

The centre said, however, that neutral transit through the Strait of Hormuz to and from non-Iranian destinations would continue uninterrupted.

US Central Command (CENTCOM) said on Monday that American forces had struck a submarine and ship maintenance facility at Iran’s Bandar Abbas naval base using unmanned surface vessels, describing it as the first combat use of sea drones by US forces.

CENTCOM said the attack was intended to degrade Iran’s ability to continue targeting commercial shipping in the region.

Before the conflict began, about one-fifth of global oil and gas supplies passed through the narrow waterway linking the Gulf to international markets.

Iran has exercised effective control over the strait since the outbreak of hostilities, turning it into one of Tehran’s most important sources of leverage in its confrontation with Washington.

The prospect of new costs for transiting Hormuz could carry particular significance for Asian economies, including China, Japan and South Korea, which rely heavily on energy imports shipped through the waterway.

Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi mocked Trump’s proposal on social media, arguing that Tehran rather than Washington had long ensured the security of shipping through the strait.

“Whoever provides secure and safe passage of commercial vessels through the Strait of Hormuz should be compensated for this service,” Araghchi wrote.

“Iran has always been the GUARDIAN of the Strait and will remain so FOREVER. Twenty per cent is of course too much. We will be fair.”

item.title: [SCMP] Will Manila and Hanoi’s maritime deal challenge Beijing in the South China Sea?
item.description: A decade after Beijing rejected the ruling by The Hague’s Permanent Court of Arbitration on its South China Sea claims, rival nations continue to manoeuvre for control. In the second of our series on the anniversary, Alyssa Chen explores how deepening maritime cooperation between the Philippines and Vietnam could affect China’s regional strategy. With Vietnamese President To Lam by his side in Manila late last month, Philippine President Ferdinand Marcos Jnr sent a clear message of intent. “Main
item.content:encoded:
A decade after Beijing rejected the ruling by The Hague’s Permanent Court of Arbitration on its South China Sea claims, rival nations continue to manoeuvre for control. In the second of our series on the anniversary, Alyssa Chen explores how deepening maritime cooperation between the Philippines and Vietnam could affect China’s regional strategy.

With Vietnamese President To Lam by his side in Manila late last month, Philippine President Ferdinand Marcos Jnr sent a clear message of intent.

“Maintaining peace, stability and the freedom of navigation and overflight in the South China Sea remains non-negotiable,” Marcos said.

The two countries “remain resolute in their commitment to the peaceful resolution of disputes”, he said, adding that this approach would be grounded in international law.

The commitment emerged during Lam’s state visit to the Philippines, a trip that the two countries used to elevate their strategic partnership to an “enhanced” level.

As part of that upgrade, Manila and Hanoi also agreed to implement a hotline and improve coordination between their coastguards to prevent accidental clashes in disputed waters.

The move came at a symbolically important time – just weeks before the 10th anniversary of the ruling by The Hague’s Permanent Court of Arbitration that dismissed China’s expansive claims to most South China Sea features.

Manila had lodged the case with the tribunal but Beijing did not take part in the arbitration.

A decade later and China continues to reject the ruling but now, with last month’s state visit, two of its rival claimants in the South China Sea – the Philippines and Vietnam – are forging stronger ties.

For China, the alignment is concerning as it undermines Beijing’s South China Sea playbook of engaging each of its smaller challengers one by one. But analysts said the two nations’ divergent strategies meant the partnership’s symbolic value was bigger than its operational punch, at least for now.

The partnership could chip away at Beijing’s negotiating advantages, observers say, but Hanoi and Manila have fundamentally different approaches to managing long-standing maritime disputes.

“Beijing has generally preferred a bilateral approach to negotiations in the South China Sea, as this allows it to engage claimants individually and tailor arrangements to specific disputes,” said Ian Seow Cheng Wei, senior analyst at the China programme at Singapore’s S. Rajaratnam School of International Studies.

“As Vietnam and the Philippines deepen cooperation and expand information sharing, it may become more difficult for China to engage each country in isolation.”

While Beijing maintains a regular maritime dialogue mechanism with Manila, the countries’ vessels have still repeatedly clashed in the region.

In contrast, Vietnam has managed to contain its territorial disputes with China to diplomacy, with Beijing and Hanoi holding their first ministerial meeting of the “3+3” strategic dialogue on diplomacy, defence and public security in March.

While pursuing what observers describe as “struggling while cooperating” with China, Vietnam maintains “four noes”: no military alliances, no siding with one country against another, no foreign military bases and no use or threat of force.

However, Vietnam has also quietly expanded its construction efforts in the South China Sea, reclaiming an area of 11.2 sq km (4.32 square miles) in the Spratly Islands, according to the Washington-based Asia Maritime Transparency Initiative.

Seow said Hanoi viewed Beijing through a dual lens: an object of struggle in maritime disputes and an important partner in areas such as economic development and regime security,

Meanwhile, the Philippines under Marcos pursues “assertive transparency” – publicising Chinese grey-zone tactics to block Philippine access to the waterway to escalate reputational costs.

These differences are likely to shape the types of cooperation that are politically acceptable to both sides, according to Seow.

The countries’ coastguard cooperation would likely focus on “practical confidence-building measures and non-traditional security issues” rather than deeper interoperability or joint enforcement activities, Seow added.

He said the cooperation was still nascent – joint exercises involving the pair’s coastguards only began in 2024 – and developing interoperability, common procedures and institutional familiarity took time.

Joshua Espena, an international relations lecturer at Polytechnic University of the Philippines, said the practical challenges to the maritime partnership included disparate command structures, bureaucratic lines of command accountability and logistical priorities.

“Their political will, based on trust and patience, can serve as a formula to incrementally overcome differences,” Espena said.

“This is why continuous engagements at the strategic level, as well as education and training at the operational and tactical levels, are keys to making this happen.”

He added that such arrangements required time to develop, and both countries needed to first “stress test” these mechanisms.

There was still a long way to go before the countries’ coastguards could directly challenge China’s activities in the South China Sea, said Hanh Nguyen, a research fellow at the Yokosuka Council on Asia Pacific Studies.

Nevertheless, the symbolic value of their deepening cooperation should not be dismissed.

While Manila has been the most confrontational in its dispute with China, Hanoi has been quietly but steadily expanding its presence in the region.

According to Espena, Vietnam and the Philippines are the only countries within the Association of Southeast Asian Nations (Asean) to show “more visible resistance to Chinese harassment” at sea.

“The unilateral diplomatic preferences are already a given, but the strategic convergence is not,” he said, noting that this strategic pivot was being driven by both domestic capacity-building needs and “constant uncertainty posed by major power competition”.

Whether this model will spread to other South China Sea claimants remains uncertain.

Nguyen said overlapping maritime claims had prevented members of Asean from forming a consensus against China’s expanding maritime power.

But the hotline and maritime incident management mechanisms were positive steps towards reducing misunderstandings and defusing tensions, she added.

Luo Liang, deputy director of the Centre for Area Studies at National Institute for South China Sea Studies, said that building a broader coalition would be an uphill battle.

“Even as Vietnam and the Philippines scrambled to face a common challenge from China, they remain tangled in their own disputes,” Luo said.

For example, in May last year, Vietnam protested against separate incidents in which the flags of China and the Philippines were raised on Sandy Cay, a group of small sandbanks in the Spratly Islands that are claimed by all three countries.

The two countries also have overlapping extended continental shelf claims – the portion of the seabed and subsoil that extends beyond a country’s 200-nautical-mile exclusive economic zone.

Luo added that it could be hard to persuade countries which were prioritising economic ties with China to join any maritime bloc.

Bao Yinan, an associate research fellow at the Huayang Centre for Maritime Cooperation and Ocean Governance in southern China’s Hainan province, said the upgraded partnership between Manila and Hanoi was not a true military alliance as it lacked any mutual defence obligations or unified military command structure.

“Manila’s real geopolitical muscle still depends on external powers such as the United States and Japan.”

That partnership was on display in April and May when the US and the Philippines held their annual Balikatan joint military exercises, which included a record number of countries including Japan, Australia, New Zealand and Canada.

The Philippines is also negotiating with Tokyo about acquiring five Abukuma-class destroyer escorts when they are decommissioned from Japanese service. Japan’s Type 88 surface-to-ship cruise missile was used in this year’s Balikatan exercises for the first time. In addition, Australia provided surveillance drones to the Philippines in April.

“The best strategy for Beijing right now is to wait and see how the partnership plays out,” Bao said, as some details of the Hanoi-Manila deal were not yet public.

But on the water, the situation remained heavily stacked in China’s favour, Bao added, as its coastguard boasted a fleet that was “substantially larger and better equipped” than the combined forces of Vietnam and the Philippines.

Luo cautioned against reading the new maritime cooperation agreements as an immediate game-changer.

“The real turning point will be when Vietnam and the Philippines begin launching joint naval patrols in disputed waters, sharing sensitive intelligence or actively banding together to block Chinese law enforcement on the front lines.”

item.title: [SCMP] UK deal to serve as testing ground for India’s Western trade ambitions
item.description: India’s free-trade agreement with the United Kingdom takes effect on Wednesday, delivering immediate tariff relief to businesses on both sides while giving New Delhi an early test of the concessions, compliance demands and implementation challenges it may face in future deals with other Western powers. UK exports in sectors such as luxury vehicles and spirits are expected to benefit, while Indian textiles and leather products will gain as the agreement eliminates tariffs on 99 per cent of export
item.content:encoded:
India’s free-trade agreement with the United Kingdom takes effect on Wednesday, delivering immediate tariff relief to businesses on both sides while giving New Delhi an early test of the concessions, compliance demands and implementation challenges it may face in future deals with other Western powers.

UK exports in sectors such as luxury vehicles and spirits are expected to benefit, while Indian textiles and leather products will gain as the agreement eliminates tariffs on 99 per cent of exports from India and reduces duties on 90 per cent of British goods, analysts say.

“The earliest benefits will be tariff reductions on goods, simplified customs procedures, and improved market access for businesses in the UK and India,” said Christopher Blackburn, a London-based analyst.

“Bureaucratic hurdles that have caused headaches in the past will be removed or reduced.”

But analysts caution that the India-UK Free Trade Agreement’s longer-term value will depend on how effectively both governments and businesses handle customs compliance, product standards and other practical barriers to trade.

The agreement offered a “practical blueprint” for resolving issues such as tariffs, services, mobility of people and regulatory cooperation, Blackburn said. “It’s good news for both sides.”

Total bilateral trade rose 10 per cent year on year to £47.9 billion (US$64.2 billion) by the end of 2025, making India the UK’s 11th-largest trading partner, according to the British government.

The deal is being implemented at a crucial juncture for India as a long-pending agreement with the United States, one of its largest trading partners, is still being finalised.

For the UK, the deal is equally important strategically because it eases access to one of the world’s largest consumer markets, analysts say.

“Businesses on both sides will benefit at a moment when the global economy is growing increasingly fragmented and unpredictable. The main challenge will be implementation,” said Uday Chandra, a political-science professor at India’s Ashoka University.

Indian industry has had a mixed record with free-trade agreements: labour-intensive sectors such as textiles and pharmaceuticals secured greater market access, while manufacturing industries dealt with import pressures.

The main challenge for smaller Indian companies with the UK agreement would be adapting to new criteria such as determining product origin and customs procedures concerning the application of tariffs and export quotas, Chandra said.

“Indian exporters will also have to meet British regulatory, environmental and quality standards. British firms will have to work through the complexity of the Indian market,” he said.

India’s trade and foreign investment regulations are often seen as intricate, requiring navigation of sector-specific caps and strict foreign exchange rules.

“The success of the [free-trade agreement] will, therefore, depend on the extent to which both governments resolve everyday obstacles to trade,” Chandra said.

Indian Prime Minister Narendra Modi and his British counterpart Keir Starmer signed the deal in July last year. British government estimates at the time suggested the pact could add £4.8 billion, or about 0.13 per cent, to the country’s gross domestic product.

Starmer resigned as UK prime minister and leader of the ruling Labour Party last month, but has continued to serve as a caretaker prime minister until a new Labour chief is selected.

Analysts say the political changes are unlikely to affect the trade agreement because it enjoys broad cross-party support in the UK.

Starmer has cited the trade pact as an important political achievement with one of the world’s largest and fastest-growing economies, Chandra pointed out.

Despite this, Chandra said Labour would still need to show that the deal “produces investments, export orders and jobs” to boost support for the party after its recent defeats in local elections.

Biswajit Dhar, an economics professor at Delhi’s Council for Social Development, said how the agreement was implemented would be equally or more important for India, though “on the face of it there are a lot of market opportunities for both countries”.

More importantly, he said Indian industry had to be ready to meet environmental and labour standards to fully tap the market opportunities offered by the trade deal.

India’s exports to the UK will be subject to a Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism – a charge on carbon-intensive imports such as cement – that is scheduled to launch next year and scale up gradually, similar to the one implemented by the European Union.

Dhar said sectors in India such as iron and steel, fertilisers, cement, hydrogen and aluminium could be affected.

“We don’t have the preparedness. Our industries have not really looked at the global emission norms very seriously,” he said.

For Dhar, such readiness is particularly important because India and the EU are also expected to implement their free-trade agreement soon. The two sides concluded negotiations in January and are reviewing the treaty’s legal provisions.

Indian exports to the UK and the EU could also increasingly face stringent labour standards and sustainability requirements, driven by recent free-trade agreements and other laws, Dhar said.

The local industry would need to be sufficiently prepared to deal with such issues because India was set to conclude trade pacts with other major powers such as Canada and Australia that could enforce similar standards, Dhar said.

“All these issues have implications for other markets as well,” he said.

Delhi and Canberra have already implemented an interim deal called the Australia-India Economic Cooperation and Trade Agreement and announced plans for a broader free-trade pact.

Earlier in March, India and Canada formally relaunched trade negotiations for their Comprehensive Economic Partnership Agreement, following a diplomatic thaw after ties were strained three years ago over Ottawa’s accusations of Indian involvement in the assassination of Canadian Sikh activist Hardeep Singh Nijjar.

Chandra said the UK-India trade agreement would help Indian industry assess what it needed to do to take advantage of such deals.

“The UK agreement gives India valuable experience in implementing a comprehensive trade deal with an advanced Western economy. It also demonstrates that Delhi is willing to make significant concessions on tariffs, services, and government procurement when it receives meaningful market access in return,” he said.

“For British firms, there is a potential first-mover advantage. They will receive preferential access to India before the EU agreement is likely to enter into force later in 2026-27.”

item.title: [SCMP] Wombs for hire: the painful price of Asia’s baby trade
item.description: As the car bounces along a rural Thai road, a baby just a few months old watches the emerald green countryside rush by from Nicha’s* lap, oblivious to the countdown that has already begun. Soon the child will be handed over to her father to be raised in China by grandparents whose own hopes of a larger family were curtailed by decades of state population control. Nicha may never see the baby she gave birth to again, but she knows that is the painful price of commercial surrogacy. This is her thi
item.content:encoded:
As the car bounces along a rural Thai road, a baby just a few months old watches the emerald green countryside rush by from Nicha’s* lap, oblivious to the countdown that has already begun.

Soon the child will be handed over to her father to be raised in China by grandparents whose own hopes of a larger family were curtailed by decades of state population control.

Nicha may never see the baby she gave birth to again, but she knows that is the painful price of commercial surrogacy.

This is her third time around the carousel of pregnancy, love, nurture and eventual separation that always ends with the same abrupt emotional wrench.

“It’s a deep bond, just like carrying and raising your own child,” Nicha told This Week in Asia, sharing videos taken on her phone of the smiling baby.

“My whole family has fallen in love including my own parents and children. It will be so hard to give up this baby, but I have to.”

Commercial surrogacy is illegal in Thailand, yet a shadow economy thrives in its place, one where women like Nicha are paid US$10,000 to US$15,000 to help strangers become parents.

Neighbouring Laos and Cambodia enforce similar bans. China, meanwhile, punishes medical professionals who facilitate the trade at home, though surrogacy itself is not explicitly outlawed there – a legal grey zone that lets surrogate-born children claim Chinese citizenship.

Legal constraints mean embryo transfer and fertilisation can span several territories, leaving surrogates and prospective parents to travel the world in search of safe legal ground.

If they get caught, the outcomes can be severe.

Earlier this month, a court in Bangkok handed down sentences of up to 15 years to four Thai obstetricians and four brokers, convicted over their roles in a transnational surrogacy ring.

Prosecutors traced the operation to Zhao Ran, a Chinese national in detention since 2020 for recruiting surrogate women and paying them for their eggs.

Days after the verdict, Thai police detained two more Chinese nationals at a hotel in the seaside town of Pattaya – a man and woman in their thirties and forties – who were suspected of running an illegal surrogacy business , according to Thailand’s Central Investigation Bureau.

None of which has done much to deter demand. Money, it turns out, still makes babies.

“The pathway to surrogacy has always existed,” said Jun*, a gay Chinese man in his thirties and the father of Nicha’s baby. “The only difference between people who can do it and those who can’t is financial means.”

For Jun, surrogacy offered a way around the stigma that still surrounds sexuality and childlessness in a culture where family remains paramount.

His egg donor was a Russian woman he met once, over a hurried meal, before their paths diverged for good. “After that we went our separate ways and never spoke again,” he said.

Less than a year later, Jun had a child.

Wombs for hire

Estimates from the United Nations put the global surrogacy market at nearly US$15 billion in 2023 – a figure that was expected to multiply six- or seven-fold within the following decade, as populations decline and people put off having children until later in life.

But surrogate mothers “receive only a small fraction” of the money the industry generates, according to a report by the UN special rapporteur on violence against women and girls published last year, with the bulk going to intermediaries such as clinics, brokers, recruiters and agents.

Legal precarity leaves surrogate mothers at risk of being punished, or of going unpaid under contracts that are unenforceable precisely because the trade is illegal. By its very nature, the UN special rapporteur notes, commercial surrogacy is defined by “harmful power imbalances”. Paying for a baby, in other words, is an ethical minefield.

Thailand banned commercial surrogacy for foreign parents in 2015, after a Thai surrogate had twins for an Australian couple who then refused to accept one of the babies, named Gammy, who was born with Down’s syndrome. Cambodia and Laos followed suit as allegations of exploitation of poor women emerged across the Mekong region.

After Thailand’s laws tightened, Russia and Ukraine became global surrogacy hubs, until war meant that Georgia and, increasingly, Armenia, have taken over as bases for clients from the US, China, Israel and India.

Given the industry’s shadowy nature, there are no comprehensive statistics on how many surrogate babies are born each year. But experts say China drives a large share of global demand.

Without an outright ban, “the domestic industry has been pushed underground or overseas”, said Dr Yingyi Luo, a research fellow at the University of Melbourne, adding that Chinese clients often preferred foreign surrogates seen as more likely to hand over the baby “cleanly”, without complication.

Luo, whose research spans the legal, ethical and cultural dimensions of surrogacy supply chains, says Chinese demand is diverse, from LGBTQ couples to people who married late or lost children and those with fertility challenges.

But she cautioned that surrogate mothers had few protections under the law and were for all intents and purposes “invisible” to authorities, “with little need to consider their consent, financial situation or psychological support”.

Still, the supply of surrogates from Southeast Asia shows no sign of drying up.

Poor, mainly rural women are enticed in by lump-sum payments to pare down debts, fund their children’s education or buy homes for elderly parents.

Recruitment happens through closed Facebook groups, said Nicha, a mother of two from a Thai farming community where the average monthly wage is US$270 to US$300 at best.

Lao, Filipino and Vietnamese women also form part of the global surrogacy supply chain, many making similar zigzagging journeys to evade authorities.

Nicha recalls a Thai mother who had an embryo from China transferred in Boten, on the Laos-China border, returned to Thailand for the first trimester, then travelled back to the mainland with the pregnancy hidden, but timed so the birth would happen close to the family paying for it.

The Zhao Ran case pushed the industry further underground, according to Thai police.

“Illegal surrogacy operations became far more complex,” a senior detective who has worked on such cases told This Week in Asia, speaking anonymously as he was not authorised to talk to media.

“The entire process, from sourcing and storing sperm and eggs, to embryo implantation and finally childbirth, became much more sophisticated, making it harder for investigators to uncover wrongdoing.”

Calls for legal reform are mounting in Thailand, to protect women from exploitation while bringing the trade in babies into a regulated space for surrogates and parents alike.

Reproductive healthcare should be a right

Nattawut Buapratum, Thai opposition lawmaker

Fertility clinics across the Thai capital would welcome the surge in demand a legal change might bring. But decriminalisation may still be years away.

“Reproductive healthcare should be a right,” said Nattawut Buapratum, an opposition lawmaker and former member of Thailand’s Committee for the Protection of Children Born Through Assisted Reproductive Technology. Under current law “in practice, access is largely limited to people who can afford it”, he said.

But surrogacy is a complex issue and even under a ban, Thai women are exposed to exploitation in commercial arrangements, according to Nattawut.

“We therefore need to carefully balance several competing considerations when amending the law: advances in medical technology, women’s rights and the rights of the child.”

As she prepares for the heartbreak of handing over a baby yet again, Nicha says this will be her last time.

“But the ones who do it, do it again and again,” she said. “We have no choice. There is nothing better we can do for money.”

*Names changed to protect interviewees’ identity

item.title: [SCMP] How Philippines is reshaping South China Sea defence, 10 years after Hague ruling
item.description: A decade after a tribunal in The Hague ruled on July 12, 2016, that China’s sweeping South China Sea claims had no legal basis, Philippine officials and analysts say Manila remains heavily outmatched at sea but has begun turning its landmark legal victory into a more credible form of deterrence. The Philippines brought the case in 2013 under the United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea (Unclos), challenging Beijing’s nine-dash line and other claims. The tribunal ruled that China’s nine-da
item.content:encoded:
A decade after a tribunal in The Hague ruled on July 12, 2016, that China’s sweeping South China Sea claims had no legal basis, Philippine officials and analysts say Manila remains heavily outmatched at sea but has begun turning its landmark legal victory into a more credible form of deterrence.

The Philippines brought the case in 2013 under the United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea (Unclos), challenging Beijing’s nine-dash line and other claims.

The tribunal ruled that China’s nine-dash line had no legal basis and that it had violated Manila’s sovereign rights in its exclusive economic zone, though it did not decide which country owned the disputed reefs and features.

China rejected the proceedings and the ruling, saying the tribunal had no jurisdiction and insisting it had sovereignty over the disputed features. It has since built and militarised artificial islands on reefs also claimed by the Philippines, a development critics cite as proof that the award did little to restrain Beijing.

Speakers at a Manila forum on Friday said the past decade had seen the Philippines strengthen its ability to raise the cost of what they described as Beijing’s “creeping invasion” in the West Philippine Sea – Manila’s term for waters it claims in the South China Sea.

Realistically, the country could not really “take on China because we will never have parity with China in terms of military, but at least [we will have the capability] to be able to inflict enough pain to deter, to prevent, and for China to at least hesitate”, former military chief General Emmanuel Bautista told the forum.

The forum, entitled “A Decade of the Arbitral Award: Credible Deterrence in Defence of the West Philippine Sea”, was sponsored by Stratbase, a local think tank established by the late Philippine foreign secretary Albert del Rosario, who led Manila’s arbitration case.

“I think when it comes to building deterrence, clearly there has been a lot of improvement, especially in the last couple of years and the air force is in a much, much better place now than it ever was before, even though there’s still a lot that needs to be done,” said Professor Jay Batongbacal, director of the University of the Philippines Institute for Maritime Affairs and Law of the Sea.

“We know the odds are not clearly in our favour, but yet we stay the course,” Defence Secretary Gilberto Teodoro Jnr said in his keynote address.

Teodoro pointed to several ways forward, saying the military arsenal should no longer concentrate on storing small-calibre ammunition and “should not be outsourced”.

He said the scope of military operations was being reoriented to active defence covering an additional 2 million km of maritime domain – a result of a partial shift under the Comprehensive Archipelagic Defence Concept, which treated the Philippines’ maritime zones, islands and surrounding waters as a single defence space.

General Romeo Brawner Jnr, the current Armed Forces chief of staff, said “one of the most important lessons” was maintaining a sustained and constant presence in the maritime domain area by the armed forces, the coastguard and the Bureau of Fisheries and Aquatic Resources.

For both Brawner and Teodoro, the armed forces have been able to leverage the arbitral victory by getting like-minded nations to support the award, engage in multilateral maritime activities and assist the military in upgrading its war assets.

On Friday, 14 countries sent their envoys to the forum and jointly signed a statement affirming that the arbitral award remained “final, legally binding and definitive”.

They also rejected China’s nine- and 10-dash lines and opposed its use of coercion to harass Manila’s lawful operations in the South China Sea.

China reiterated on Sunday that the ruling was “null and void and has no binding force” and Beijing “neither accepts nor recognises it”, Associated Press reported.

Deterrence also extended into cyberspace, said retired navy Rear Admiral Roy Vincent Trinidad, spokesman for Philippine operations in the South China Sea.

Trinidad said online messaging, which he attributed to Chinese influence operations, sought to “destroy the will to fight of the Filipino people, to create divisions internally, politically, economically, everything, to cause us to fight among ourselves”.

He added that such online operations constantly promoted the message that the arbitral victory was but “a mere scrap of paper” – a quote by former president Rodrigo Duterte as an excuse to temporarily shelve the dispute in favour of billions of dollars’ worth of concessionary loans from China – and that other Southeast Asian countries viewed the arbitral victory with disfavour.

Critics have long argued that the “scrap of paper” description appeared to be borne out by events on the water.

After Manila filed the arbitration case in 2013, Beijing built six more military islands on Philippine-claimed Fiery Cross Reef, Subi Reef, Johnson Reef, Gaven Reef, Hughes Reef and Cuarteron Reef, in addition to its structures on Mischief Reef in the mid-1990s.

“I was in the office of the [foreign] secretary at the time. It took us completely by surprise,” said Evan Garcia, former Permanent Representative of the Philippines to the United Nations Office at Geneva and ambassador to the United Kingdom, who spoke on the issue for the first time.

“After we protested, the Chinese said, don’t worry, this is a fisherman’s shelter. Especially because they said they’d never militarise Mischief Reef. And now we can see what has happened and we can see the full extent of Beijing’s ambitions,” Garcia said.

“So you have to understand what the Philippines had faced since 1995, since all the attempts to talk bilaterally and multilaterally had failed.”

As for online posts saying that no other Asean state backed the arbitration and its ruling, Garcia stressed of the regional bloc: “Asean has never supported the Chinese [nine or 10-dash] line as a group. Just because you’re silent doesn’t mean you support it.”

Garcia said the 2016 ruling made clear that “nothing on the nine-dash line is legitimate. So it’s all Philippine EEZ as far as we’re concerned”.

item.title: [SCMP] DAP’s election loss in Malaysia’s Johor prompts soul-searching ahead of next key poll
item.description: As Malaysian political coalition Barisan Nasional (BN) basks in the afterglow of its landslide win in the Johor state election, the soul-searching within Prime Minister Anwar Ibrahim’s defeated Pakatan Harapan (PH) bloc could run deepest within its largest group – the Chinese-majority Democratic Action Party (DAP). BN improved its grip on the country’s southernmost state, securing 48 out of 56 seats. PH managed to win only eight seats, and within the coalition, DAP lost 11 of the 17 seats it con
item.content:encoded:
As Malaysian political coalition Barisan Nasional (BN) basks in the afterglow of its landslide win in the Johor state election, the soul-searching within Prime Minister Anwar Ibrahim’s defeated Pakatan Harapan (PH) bloc could run deepest within its largest group – the Chinese-majority Democratic Action Party (DAP).

BN improved its grip on the country’s southernmost state, securing 48 out of 56 seats. PH managed to win only eight seats, and within the coalition, DAP lost 11 of the 17 seats it contested, including four previously held spots in Johor Jaya, Tangkak, Jementah and Perling. It had won 10 seats in the 2022 election.

The Johor election was the second state poll loss for DAP in the last eight months.

In November last year, DAP suffered a crushing defeat in the 17th Sabah state election, losing all eight seats it contested under the PH banner – the first time since 2004 that it had no representation in the Sabah State Legislative Assembly.

The losses have turned the Negeri Sembilan election on August 1 into a critical test of whether DAP’s setbacks in Sabah and Johor were an aberration or a sign of a deeper erosion in voter support, with the party’s national congress on August 16 likely to see demands for changes in strategy, messaging and grass-roots engagement.

“DAP is seen as being overly cautious and has not sufficiently explained to its supporters the compromises it has made within the unity government,” said Awang Azman Awang Pawi, a sociopolitical analyst from Malaysian-based Universiti Malaya.

He said DAP was facing the consequences of promised reforms moving slowly and persistent cost-of-living pressures, as the party appeared less vocal in defending its principles.

“Some supporters chose not to vote or cast protest ballots,” Awang added.

The coming election in Negeri Sembilan could see DAP hold on to its previous gains, with experts saying that it still possesses a strong base in the state run by a PH minority government.

“The greatest threat may not be the loss of many seats, but a decline in vote share and majorities because supporters do not turn out to vote,” Awang warned.

Azmi Hassan, a senior fellow at the Nusantara Academy for Strategic Research, said the negative perception of DAP had become apparent in Sabah and resurfaced in Johor, but the party had not adequately addressed it.

“With so little time before the Negeri Sembilan election, it will be difficult for DAP to change that perception, and the same trend may recur,” he said.

The minimum benchmark for DAP would be to retain all 11 seats in the 36-member state assembly it won in 2023, most of which were secured with healthy majorities of more than 2,000 votes, Azmi said.

“Retaining the 11 seats with larger majorities will be an uphill task, but DAP has to defend all of them to show that it has responded to what happened in Sabah and Johor,” he said.

For DAP Secretary General Anthony Loke, the party’s objective was to defend its seats in Negeri Sembilan, and he was confident it could still form a PH state government following the election.

“The main objective is to win a majority, and that is achievable. We just need to win at least a few more [seats] to form a stable government,” Loke told This Week in Asia last week.

“The election is still very much about the future of the state, bread-and-butter issues, development [and] progress.”

Analysts say if DAP loses just two or three more seats, pressure would mount on the party to reassess its strategy, candidates, voter outreach and position within the unity government.

“If it becomes extremely marginal or DAP loses four or more seats, the result could trigger a crisis of confidence in the leadership,” Awang added.

Kalimulah Hassan, a Malaysian political observer, said the blow for the DAP came as “promises made during the polls kept being broken” in Johor.

“The largest in Anwar’s coalition and for 60 years the loudest in criticising and preaching against corruption, power abuse and good governance – what did they do? Kept silent and revelled, enjoying the perks of power and fame,” he said in a social media post.

DAP’s purported Chinese-first strategies to gain voters’ support in Johor did not work out well, according to analysts.

Columnist Joceline Tan, in an opinion piece published by The Star, said PH had focused heavily on winning Chinese voters as it had largely accepted that Malay support was out of reach.

“DAP’s campaign was more realistic than that of PKR. It made no pretence of wooing the Chinese vote to survive,” Tan wrote, referring to the People’s Justice Party.

Multiple factors contributed to DAP’s losses, including its choice of candidates and party machinery, BN’s dominance, the Malaysian Chinese Association’s resurgence and multi-cornered contests, according to analysts.

“It would therefore be inaccurate to conclude that all the losses were solely caused by weaknesses in DAP’s central leadership,” Awang said.

DAP has long been branded as Chinese-first by Malay-led parties such as the United Malay National Organisation and the opposition party Bersatu.

Analysts say its national congress next month would be a platform for demands for a firmer strategy.

The convention could steer DAP in a different direction as it looked to erase negative perceptions of DAP among the Chinese voters, Azmi said.

“From Sabah to Johor, nothing has been done [to improve the perception], so during the convention, I believe something will be proposed to counter the negativity,” he said.

Ahmad Mohsein Azman, a senior analyst at BowerGroupAsia, pointed to DAP’s weaknesses in urban constituencies, saying that voters there were more aware of current criticisms of DAP and PH, a factor that had dented the coalition’s support in Johor.

While DAP’s losses in Sabah alone were not an accurate gauge of public sentiment towards the party, the losses in Johor should serve as a warning that its current campaign approach might be “too removed from reality”.

“By staying largely silent on such issues, the party has become an antithesis of its own manifestos,” Ahmad said, adding that DAP’s traditional Chinese and urban seats could no longer be treated as safe havens.

item.title: [SCMP] Beijing hits out as US-led coalition marks 10th anniversary of South China Sea ruling
item.description: Beijing has cast US-led military deployments as the “primary threat” to peace in the South China Sea, hitting back at a multinational declaration that marked the 10th anniversary of an arbitral ruling it does not recognise. In a strongly worded statement on Sunday, the Ministry of Foreign Affairs asserted China’s “historic rights” while condemning “militarising and coercive behaviour” from the US and its allies as the “foremost challenge currently facing the South China Sea”. “It is the continuo
item.content:encoded:
Beijing has cast US-led military deployments as the “primary threat” to peace in the South China Sea, hitting back at a multinational declaration that marked the 10th anniversary of an arbitral ruling it does not recognise.

In a strongly worded statement on Sunday, the Ministry of Foreign Affairs asserted China’s “historic rights” while condemning “militarising and coercive behaviour” from the US and its allies as the “foremost challenge currently facing the South China Sea”.

“It is the continuous military deployments, reckless manoeuvres, and instigations by external powers like the United States that constitute the primary threat to regional stability,” the statement said.

“China will take decisive measures to defend its rights and interests in a reasonable, lawful, professional and restrained manner in response to infringements and provocations by relevant countries.”

Beijing also traded barbs with Tokyo over the matter in a separate statement, after a Japanese minister criticised its stance on the ruling. Foreign Minister Toshimitsu Motegi released a statement saying China’s refusal to accept the 2016 decision is “against the principle of peaceful settlement of disputes”, prompting Beijing to summon a senior official of the Japanese Embassy to protest.

Earlier on Sunday, a 14-nation coalition led by the United States and the Philippines issued a joint statement reaffirming their “unwavering commitment” to a rules-based, free and open Indo-Pacific, marking a decade since an international tribunal at The Hague invalidated most of Beijing’s South China Sea claims.

Other signatories to the statement included US allies such as Japan, Australia and Britain.

Beijing’s sweeping claims of sovereignty over nearly all islands and rock features in the South China Sea and rights to their adjacent waters have locked it into long-standing disputes with rival claimants, including the Philippines, Vietnam, Malaysia and Brunei.

In 2013, Manila took the unprecedented step of launching an arbitration case under the UN Convention on the Law of the Sea (Unclos). The tribunal at The Hague ultimately ruled on July 12, 2016, that although it could not rule on questions of sovereignty, Beijing’s claims to historical and economic rights over most of the features in the South China Sea were invalid.

Beijing refused to recognise the tribunal’s authority to hear the case and has rejected the ruling. It has also said it would not implement its findings.
