Sunday’s 14-nation statement described the 2016 award decision as a “significant milestone” that was “final, legally binding, and definitive”. It added there was no legal basis for Beijing’s sweeping maritime claims, including those based on historic rights.

“We reaffirm our strong opposition to the use of coastguard, military and maritime militia forces to harass, obstruct, or intimidate lawful operations by other states at sea or in the air, and in so doing endanger the safety of personnel and fishermen and seriously degrade regional peace and security,” the statement said.

The Chinese foreign ministry described the waterway as one of “the safest maritime corridors in the world”, where freedom of navigation and overflight had never encountered any issues.

The 2016 ruling has only worsened geopolitical friction rather than settling it, according to the ministry.

The arbitration had failed to resolve the maritime dispute and instead become a “stumbling block” for China-Philippines relations and regional stability, it cautioned.

“If the standards set by this ruling were to be applied globally, many nations would lose the legal basis for claiming maritime rights around their own islands and reefs. We must ask: have those individual states that champion the award voluntarily surrendered the maritime entitlements of their own corresponding features?”

In a separate statement on Sunday, the ministry accused Tokyo of repeatedly “attempting to interfere” in the South China Sea. The rebuke followed remarks by the Japanese foreign minister that Beijing’s rejection of the arbitration award undermined the international rule of law.

“For some time, Japan has steadily intensified its collusion with the Philippines, exporting weapons and military equipment to Manila. Tokyo has repeatedly dispatched military forces abroad and test-fired offensive missiles – actions that far exceed the scope of ‘self-defence’,” the Chinese statement said.

Japan and the Philippines are both US treaty allies. The US currently has nine military bases in the Philippines while Japan has emerged as a strategically significant partner for Manila.

Building upon a reciprocal access agreement for joint military exercises, Manila and Tokyo are negotiating an intelligence-sharing pact and the potential transfer of up to five Japanese Abukuma-class destroyer escorts to the Philippine Navy.

The Chinese foreign ministry’s sharp rhetoric was mirrored by Beijing’s top diplomat in Manila. In an article published in People’s Daily on Sunday, Jing Quan, China’s ambassador to the Philippines, said clearing the arbitration’s “lingering toxic legacy” was a matter of utmost urgency.

“The most important task for Manila today is to … abandon illusions and remove this stumbling block to allow bilateral relations to return to the right track,” Jing wrote in the Communist Party mouthpiece.

“The South China Sea arbitration was a thoroughly illegal affair from start to finish.”

China stepped up its campaign against the ruling in the run-up to the anniversary, with a series of articles in the People’s Daily and reports from a Ministry of Natural Resources think tank, including one about Unclos warning of “abusive practices”.

Jing said the 2016 decision had created immense hurdles for managing disputes and improving ties with the Philippines.

“It has become a painful ‘thorn’ embedded deeply between China and the Philippines, and a convenient pretext for extra-regional forces to interfere in regional affairs,” he wrote.

Jing argued that the dispute was fundamentally about territorial sovereignty over maritime features – something that lay outside the scope of Unclos.

He also said Beijing had excluded itself from the convention’s compulsory dispute resolution mechanisms via a formal declaration in 2006, something allowed under Article 298.

However, the convention also stipulates that if two nations fail to resolve a maritime dispute through negotiation, one party can unilaterally take the other before an international judicial body or tribunal.

Jing warned that further “infringements and provocations” by Manila would meet a firm response from China to protect its rights.

“China’s decision not to accept, participate in or recognise the arbitration and its award is a stance not only to defend our own sovereignty and interests, but also to uphold the authority and dignity of international law.”

Sunday’s anniversary follows three years of escalating tensions between China and the Philippines, marked by a string of maritime confrontations in the disputed waters.

Since becoming president in 2022, Ferdinand Marcos Jnr has reversed the policy of his predecessor Rodrigo Duterte, who downplayed the South China Sea dispute to secure better relations with Beijing.

The Marcos administration has an “assertive transparency” policy of publishing footage of confrontations, including collisions between ships and China’s use of water cannons, to expose what it describes as aggressive tactics from Beijing.

item.title: [SCMP] Flights and rail services resume across eastern China as Typhoon Bavi weakens
item.description: Rail and air services began returning to normal across large areas of eastern China on Sunday as Typhoon Bavi weakened and moved inland, away from some of the country’s largest cities. As of 9pm on Sunday, the storm was over Chuzhou city in the central province of Anhui, according to the National Meteorological Centre. Bavi, which had been downgraded to a tropical storm earlier in the day, was slowly moving to the north at speeds of around 15km/h (9mph), the centre said. The system is expected t
item.content:encoded:
Rail and air services began returning to normal across large areas of eastern China on Sunday as Typhoon Bavi weakened and moved inland, away from some of the country’s largest cities.

As of 9pm on Sunday, the storm was over Chuzhou city in the central province of Anhui, according to the National Meteorological Centre. Bavi, which had been downgraded to a tropical storm earlier in the day, was slowly moving to the north at speeds of around 15km/h (9mph), the centre said.

The system is expected to veer northeast over Anhui province on Monday, cross the Shandong Peninsula into the northern waters of the Yellow Sea on Tuesday, and eventually transition into an extratropical cyclone.

The storm had earlier brought strong winds and heavy rains to large swathes of the east coast, prompting the evacuation of around 2.4 million people in the path of the storm and causing thousands of flights and rail services to be cancelled.

But as the conditions began to ease along the east coast, and some local authorities downgraded their weather warnings, services slowly began to return to normal. However, high winds and heavy rainfall were expected to continue across many parts of the country.

High-speed rail services along several major corridors connecting cities from Beijing to Hong Kong – as well as major eastern transport hubs such as Shanghai, Nanjing and Hangzhou – began a phased resumption on Sunday afternoon.

In Shanghai, a total of 653 flights were expected to be cancelled on Sunday, 458 at Pudong International Airport and 195 at Hongqiao International Airport, the local airport authority said earlier.

By Sunday afternoon, planes had started arriving at and departing from both hubs as Bavi moved inland. Flights also resumed at major airports in Hangzhou and Wenzhou in Zhejiang, and in Hefei, the capital of Anhui.

Xuanzhou Airport in Wuhu, the city at the centre of the storm on Sunday afternoon, cancelled all flights except one.

Air services were also returning to normal in Taiwan, which experienced heavy rainfall on Saturday when the storm brushed by the northern tip of the island.

The Taoyuan airport transit line – which connects major cities to the island’s primary international airport – also resumed full operations

Spanning 1,000km (600 miles) at its widest point – roughly the width of mainland France – Typhoon Bavi first came ashore in the coastal city of Taizhou in Zhejiang just after 11.10pm on Saturday. It made a second landfall in Wenzhou city around midnight, with maximum sustained winds near its centre of around 108–117km/h (67–72mph).

Regular bus, taxi and ride-hailing services in both cities gradually resumed from Sunday afternoon, with only a few routes still suspended due to the weather.

The National Meteorological Centre warned on Sunday that parts of the country would experience heavy to torrential rain, with Anhui receiving up to 250-350mm (10-14 inches) in a 24-hour period.

Provinces likely to be affected stretched from Fujian in the southeast to Liaoning in the northeast, taking in large parts of the east coast, including Zhejiang and Jiangxi, as well as Hebei in northern China.

Strong winds with sustained speeds of 75-88 km/h, and gusts reaching 89-117 km/h are expected in the Yangtze River estuary, along with coastal areas of central and southern Jiangsu, Shanghai, and Zhoushan in Zhejiang.

Local authorities in affected areas remain on high alert for flooding.

On Sunday afternoon, the Ministry of Water Resources issued a Level IV flood prevention alert – the lowest level – for Jiangsu, Anhui, Jiangxi and Shandong, with the heaviest rains expected over the next two days.

It said torrential rain was expected in several areas and significant rises in water levels were expected on major rivers, while smaller ones may also see dangerous levels of flooding.

So far no local authorities have reported serious casualties or economic damage, but many videos of severe flooding in Wenzhou, where Bavi made its landfall, have been circulating online.

Some of the footage showed rescuers using inflatable boats to evacuate residents after the ground floors of many houses were inundated by floodwaters.

Other videos showed that strong winds had caused serious damage to the windows in many residential buildings in Zhejiang and Fujian, leaving rooms in a mess.

Rescuers were also seen braving the rain and wind to clear fallen tree branches and clear roads.

In just one part of Wenzhou, Yueqing city, the winds had downed more than 1,300 trees, according to the China News Service.

More than 2.2 million people were evacuated in Zhejiang and nearly 200,000 in Fujian, along with thousands of others in the neighbouring provinces and Shanghai, according to state media.

Schools, work and outdoor activities have been suspended in the affected provinces.

As of Sunday morning, the authorities had not reported any casualties.

The National Marine Forecasting Centre issued a red alert – the highest level in a four-tier system – for high waves and an amber alert for storm and tidal surges on Saturday, warning local governments to stay alert to these risks.

According to CCTV, strong winds caused huge waves up to 8 metres (26 feet) high along Fujian’s coast.

But as Bavi headed north on Sunday, some areas began to lower their weather warnings.

Fujian’s provincial meteorological observatory downgraded its typhoon and rainstorm warning to Level IV. The provincial government also officially ended its flood response emergency preparations and services in the provincial capital Fuzhou resumed.

Zhejiang province also lowered its typhoon emergency response at noon and subway trains started running again in major cities such as Hangzhou, Ningbo and Shaoxing.

Bavi began as a super typhoon, battering Guam and the Northern Mariana Islands last Monday with wind speeds of 290km/h.

After sweeping through a chain of remote Japanese islands, it brought heavy rainfall to Taiwan.

No fatalities or severe injuries have been reported on the island so far, but 113 people were injured – mainly as a result of falling off motorbikes or bicycles due to strong winds and slippery roads, or from accidents while preparing for the typhoon.

The storm also knocked out power supplies to 234,481 households as of 8pm yesterday, according to local government reports.

Landslides triggered by the storm have also killed at least 17 people in the Philippines.

Parts of southern China are still reeling from the devastation brought by Typhoon Maysak earlier this week.

Maysak left at least 39 people dead and killed large numbers of livestock. It also caused two rare tornadoes in the landlocked central province of Hubei.

item.title: [SCMP] South Korean ticket touts to face the music when new penalties come into play
item.description: For many K-pop fans in South Korea, the hunt for concert tickets has turned into a high-stakes battle, one where logging on early is no longer enough and ordinary fans find themselves outmatched by organised resellers, bots and shadowy workarounds that can send prices soaring far beyond face value. Previous solutions have failed to rein in the problem, but the government is hoping that the threat of massive fines may finally free ticket seekers from the grips of predatory scalpers. From August 2
item.content:encoded:
For many K-pop fans in South Korea, the hunt for concert tickets has turned into a high-stakes battle, one where logging on early is no longer enough and ordinary fans find themselves outmatched by organised resellers, bots and shadowy workarounds that can send prices soaring far beyond face value.

Previous solutions have failed to rein in the problem, but the government is hoping that the threat of massive fines may finally free ticket seekers from the grips of predatory scalpers.

From August 28, amendments to the National Sports Promotion Act and Performance Act will allow authorities to impose penalties of up to 50 times the resale price of a ticket, a sharp escalation from the maximum fine of 10 million won (US$6,600) introduced under laws that took effect in March 2024.

Earlier efforts to curb scalping focused largely on “macro” programmes – software tools that can click through ticketing pages and buy seats faster than any human user. But recent cases show the problem has moved beyond simple bot-buying and into more elaborate attempts to game systems that require a verified identity.

In March, police arrested 16 members of a scalping ring accused of reselling more than 33,000 tickets for about 190 concerts between October 2022 and the end of 2025, generating 7.1 billion won (US$4.7 million) in illegal profits.

The group allegedly used AI-powered macros to stockpile tickets before ordinary fans could access them.

Investigators said the gang also used a technique known as “holding transfer”: they released the tickets they had reserved late at night, when fewer people were online, and instantly repurchased them through buyers’ own accounts using automated tools. That allowed the tickets to appear under the individual buyers’ names, helping resellers bypass real-name verification checks.

The group also allegedly built fake webpages mimicking Government24, South Korea’s official online public-services portal, to display false mobile ID cards and pass on-site identity checks at venues.

Tickets were resold at huge mark-ups, including one with a face value of 200,000 won (US$152) that sold for 5 million won – 25 times the original price. One gang member alone secured 126 tickets for a single concert, according to police.

The problem is not limited to K-pop. In June, the Ministry of Culture, Sports and Tourism referred 15 alleged scalpers to police after tracking suspected bulk resale activity between January and June, according to Yonhap News Agency.

One seller moved 110 professional baseball tickets during that period. Another resold a ticket for a Seoul concert by S.Coups and Mingyu – two members of the K-pop boy band Seventeen – for 1.2 million won, more than eight times its face value.

Out of options

For Ren Renle, a 28-year-old Chinese graduate student at Korea University, the March case sounded familiar.

In early 2022, she paid 30,000 won for an official fan club membership for boy band NCT Dream, hoping it would give her priority access to tickets for the group’s July 29 concert in Seoul.

Fan club presales are meant to reward members with an early chance to buy tickets, but Ren still came away empty-handed.

When tickets opened to the general public, she tried again, this time going to a PC cafe in the hope that its faster internet connection would help. “I still failed to get a ticket,” she said.

Ren then turned to a scalper introduced by other fans and contacted him through WeChat. The scalper asked for her ticketing website username and password, telling her the ticket would be secured through holding transfer – the same workaround later described in the March police case.

Ren paid the face value of about 100,000 won, plus a 1,600 yuan (US$235) service fee via Alipay. The mobile wallet is operated by Ant Group, an affiliate of Alibaba Group Holding, which owns the South China Morning Post.

The concert was cancelled due to Covid-19 but the scalper refunded only the ticket’s face value. “I lost 1,600 yuan in service fees with no way to get it back,” she said.

Despite the loss, Ren said she would still consider using scalpers again if official channels failed. “If Korea cracks down harder, I might have to pay even more,” she said.

Ren’s dilemma points to the central challenge facing South Korean authorities: tougher penalties may deter organised scalpers, but they do not automatically give desperate fans a safe way to buy tickets once official channels sell out.

Lee Hong-joo, a professor of consumer economics at Sookmyung Women’s University, said stricter enforcement could push scalpers onto private messaging apps such as Telegram or overseas resale platforms, making fraud harder to track.

“The real solution is not an outright ban on resale but creating official platforms where fans can transfer tickets at face value with ID verification and dispute resolution,” he said.

Gambling with strangers

Organised scalping damaged South Korea’s “fan economy” and cultural soft-power exports by undermining trust in fair access to performances, said Kwon Byeong-ung, an arts management professor at Chung-Ang University.

“If fans cannot trust the ticketing system, it damages the entire cultural export model that South Korea has built,” Kwon said.

South Korea’s approach is among the toughest in the region, especially because the incoming penalties scale with resale price.

Japan has introduced imprisonment of up to one year to combat scalping, but its fixed fine of 10 million yen (US$66,000) lacks South Korea’s economic deterrent.

In Hong Kong, the maximum fine for scalping is HK$2,000 (US$255), though venues used for large-scale performances are generally government-managed and exempt from the law.

Kate Wong, a 20-year-old university student, bought a ticket to the 2025 MAMA Awards, one of K-pop’s biggest annual music awards shows, on Carousell for HK$1,200 – nearly 50 per cent above the HK$800 face value – after failing to secure one through official channels.

The two-day awards show was held at Hong Kong’s Kai Tak Stadium in November, marking its return to the city after seven years.

“I was very worried about being scammed. Even though I insisted on meeting face-to-face, I was still afraid it might be a fake ticket,” she said. “I didn’t even know it was illegal. Many of my friends buy from scalpers, some even from mainland Chinese platforms. It feels normal.”

She said Hong Kong should introduce stricter enforcement to ensure tickets reach genuine fans rather than scalpers.

But she also said heavier penalties alone were not sufficient. “What fans really need is a safe, regulated resale platform. Right now, we have no other choice but to gamble with strangers.”

item.title: [SCMP] BN cruises to victory in Malaysia’s Johor, in blow to Anwar and PH
item.description: The coalition of Malaysian Prime Minister Anwar Ibrahim was dealt a blow on Saturday after state rival Barisan Nasional (BN) trounced Pakatan Harapan (PH) in the Johor election. BN cruised to victory with 48 seats out of 56 up for grabs, while Anwar’s PH managed only eight. BN’s result was better than the 40 seats it secured in the 2022 race. The two-thirds majority it retains gives it absolute power to pass constitutional amendments and redraw electoral boundaries in Johor. The state was turned
item.content:encoded:
The coalition of Malaysian Prime Minister Anwar Ibrahim was dealt a blow on Saturday after state rival Barisan Nasional (BN) trounced Pakatan Harapan (PH) in the Johor election.

BN cruised to victory with 48 seats out of 56 up for grabs, while Anwar’s PH managed only eight. BN’s result was better than the 40 seats it secured in the 2022 race. The two-thirds majority it retains gives it absolute power to pass constitutional amendments and redraw electoral boundaries in Johor.

The state was turned into a battleground between the federal partners, with both coalitions fielding candidates in every constituency and a voter turnout nearing the expected 70 per cent mark for an electorate of more than 2.7 million.

The result now casts doubt on Anwar’s unwieldy coalition government and could have implications for whether a snap national election is called.

Ahmad Zahid Hamidi, deputy prime minister and BN president, earlier thanked Johoreans for once again choosing BN to govern the state, stressing that the party “will not be arrogant in victory” and would work hard to fulfil its campaign pledges.

“We also hope this victory will trigger a blue wave in the elections to come,” he said.

The election saw 172 candidates vying for all 56 seats, with the turnout significantly higher than the 54.9 per cent in 2022, driven by high public interest and intense campaigning.

Fourteen seats had straight fights, 27 featured three-cornered contests, 12 were four-way battles and three had five candidates.

BN entered polling day defending the 40 seats it held before the state assembly was dissolved on June 1, while PH had 12, opposition Perikatan Nasional three and youth-centric Muda one.

The result is not expected to change Anwar’s parliamentary majority, but a sweeping BN victory strengthens Umno’s – the dominant Malay party in BN – hand inside his national coalition while exposing PH’s inability to match its federal influence on state ground.

Anwar had earlier said he would consider a snap election if tensions within his ruling coalition worsened.

BN had ruled Malaysia for six decades before losing power in 2018 to PH. After the 2022 national election ended in a hung parliament, BN joined Anwar’s bloc to form the present unity government.

Wong Chin Huat, a political scientist at Sunway University, said the Johor election underscored PH’s “under-competitiveness against BN”.

He said BN, with clear aims of leading the next federal coalition government, must win 70 of the 165 federal seats, exceeding its current 23 seats by a large margin.

“If PH is strong, BN would train its gun at the opposition, but now PH is weak, it might enter a deal with PAS to divide the Malay heartland and focus on winning most of PH’s seats,” he said.

In Bekok, a town in the northern Johor district of Segamat, BN’s victory was warmly received by locals such as Kumutha Ramasamy, who paid tribute to incumbent state legislator Tan Chong’s hands-on approach to constituency work.

The BN candidate won by 64 per cent, or 10,230 votes, against his Democratic Action Party (DAP) opponent, according to official results from the Election Commission.

“Whatever help is needed, Tan Chong is always the first to arrive on the spot to help the people of Bekok,” Kumutha, 41, said.

She hoped the new state government would build a road linking her small town to Kluang to boost employment access beyond Bekok’s limited job market.

Earlier in the day in Johor Jaya, where Chinese voters form the largest electoral bloc, a woman who wanted to be known only as Melanie, said she and her sister completed the voting process in about five minutes.

“We have done our part as citizens,” the 50-year-old told This Week in Asia. “As Malaysians, we have a responsibility to come and vote. It matters for the country, the economy, everything.”

Voter Afya Ghazali, 26, who works in Singapore and commutes across the Causeway every day, said she wanted the next government to improve infrastructure and create more jobs in the state.

“We need better infrastructure and more job opportunities in Johor so that more of us can serve our own state,” she said. “There is still a shortage of jobs in the city, especially for us.”

Pollster Ilham Centre on Friday before the race attributed BN’s advantage to its stronger political machinery, familiar candidates and support in Malay-majority zones.

PH, meanwhile, was relying on Chinese-majority urban and semi-urban seats, where Ilham said turnout of at least 65 per cent would be needed for the coalition to hold its ground.

Johor, Malaysia’s southern gateway to Singapore, has attracted vast investment promises, yet the campaign repeatedly returned to wages, housing and living costs, along with the distrust between Umno and Chinese-majority DAP that their alliance in Putrajaya has never fully buried.

The state contributed about 14 billion ringgit (US$3.7 billion) to federal coffers between 2023 and 2025, according to finance ministry figures cited by Anwar, who said Putrajaya returned 16 billion ringgit to Johor over the same period.

In a fishing village in Iskandar Puteri, Sabri Abdullah had little patience for the intrigue in Kuala Lumpur. The 58-year-old measured governments by the roads, street lights and running water that had reached his community during his lifetime.

“Put simply, this place started from zero. There was nothing,” Sabri told This Week in Asia on Friday. “Now, alhamdulillah, people in the village can see the development.”

That record, he said, was why his vote remained with BN.

Izuddin Zulhaidat, who runs a food business, was coy about his vote, but pointed to the range of developments in Bekok.

These include the completion of a new railway station as part of the Electric Train Service that runs along the west coast of Peninsular Malaysia from the Thai border to Johor Bahru, and new franchises and consumer brands opening up in Bekok.

“Overall, I am satisfied with the state government’s performance, because we also get to enjoy the benefits of development from southern Johor,” the 29-year-old said.

Former DAP state legislator Gan Peck Cheng urged the local government to develop other Johor towns to boost overall growth, even as she lauded initiatives such as the Johor-Singapore Special Economic Zone.

“We are not asking [the state government] to slow down Johor Bahru’s development, but we are asking that the rest of Johor be brought along so that they don’t miss out,” she said.

In the lead-up to polling day, caretaker chief minister Onn Hafiz Ghazi and BN sold continuity, political stability and Johor’s investment record, while promising that the state would remain aligned with Putrajaya even without DAP in its government.

Anwar and PH pressed wages, affordable housing and the cost of living, while DAP chief Anthony Loke warned against “one-party dominance” earlier this week.

item.title: [SCMP] At Indonesia’s sharia frontier, 21 lashes for a TikTok kiss ‘is our right’
item.description: Footage of the punishment quickly went viral: an unmarried couple each receiving 21 lashes of the cane in Indonesia’s Aceh province after being accused of kissing in a car and live-streaming it on TikTok. As each blow landed on their backs, the unnamed man, 22, and woman, 25, visibly grimaced. The woman later burst into tears, wailing in pain as the public punishment continued. The caning was carried out on July 2 in Banda Aceh, the provincial capital. Aceh is the only Indonesian province to enf
item.content:encoded:
Footage of the punishment quickly went viral: an unmarried couple each receiving 21 lashes of the cane in Indonesia’s Aceh province after being accused of kissing in a car and live-streaming it on TikTok.

As each blow landed on their backs, the unnamed man, 22, and woman, 25, visibly grimaced. The woman later burst into tears, wailing in pain as the public punishment continued.

The caning was carried out on July 2 in Banda Aceh, the provincial capital. Aceh is the only Indonesian province to enforce sharia law under its Islamic Criminal Code, known locally as Qanun Jinayat.

It was far from the first such case to gain international attention and the response has become familiar: footage spreads online, rights groups condemn the punishment as inhumane and Aceh’s status as the Muslim-majority country’s sole sharia-enforcing province is thrust back into the spotlight.

But inside Aceh, caning is not viewed solely through a human rights lens. For some residents, it is bound up with religion, local autonomy and Acehnese identity.

Support is neither universal nor uncomplicated – even those who back the punishment often disagree over when and how it should be applied.

Aceh was historically one of the most powerful Islamic sultanates in Southeast Asia and for centuries observed informal Islamic law before it was formally codified in 2004. The law was enacted as part of a peace deal granting Aceh semi-autonomous status following a long and bloody civil conflict that left thousands of Acehnese dead in their struggle for independence from the rest of Indonesia.

Rights groups have long argued the punishment violates international human rights law. In a statement issued the same day as the flogging, Amnesty International said all forms of corporal punishment constituted torture or other “cruel, inhuman or degrading punishment”, while its co-regional director, Montse Ferrer, called the caning of the couple “a horrifying act of discrimination”.

Amnesty also said the case showed Aceh authorities were expanding enforcement of sharia law online, with sharia police appearing to intensify digital monitoring to punish acts deemed to violate Islamic law, including public displays of intimacy outside marriage.

The July case followed several other public canings in Aceh that have drawn attention in recent months.

On May 12, the Southeast Aceh Prosecutor’s Office flogged 15 people, administering more than 200 strokes of the rattan cane collectively – the highest number ever carried out in a single session in the regency. One woman received 100 lashes for adultery, while 14 men received between six and 10 strokes each for gambling.

In January, a couple were caned 140 times each – 100 for having sex outside wedlock and 40 for consuming alcohol – in what was thought to be the most severe caning punishment ever handed down in Aceh. The woman collapsed afterwards and had to be escorted to a waiting ambulance.

Yet the outrage that follows Aceh’s canings often travels further than the views of those who live under the law. In Banda Aceh, residents who spoke to This Week in Asia described the punishment in more practical, personal and sometimes conflicted terms.

‘It is our right’

Nurjani, a 62-year-old housewife in Banda Aceh, said she supported caning because it “was about religion” and in line with sharia law. Personal responsibility was involved, she said, adding if people were worried about being caned, they should not commit a caning offence.

“If you do not do anything wrong, then there is nothing to fear. I support caning and I believe that if you lead your life in the right way and walk the right path, no one will question you.”

Muhammad Nasir, 42, also a Banda Aceh resident, said he supported caning because of Aceh’s semi-autonomous status.

“It was agreed that Aceh could enact sharia law as part of the peace deal, so it is our right. Daily life in Aceh is closely woven with religion, not just regarding the law, but at all levels. It is part of the identity of Acehnese people.”

It is our right … It is part of the identity of the Acehnese people

Muhammad Nasir, Banda Aceh resident

He considers caning a deterrent that was put in place to protect the province’s people.

“If someone is caned, it will be a lesson for them so that they do not do the same thing again. It has a physical element to it, but it is mostly social and designed to make the person ashamed of their crimes.”

Clear standard operating procedures govern the punishment, he added, with the person administering the flogging, known as an algojo, prohibited from raising their arms above their shoulders when administering each blow.

Caning in Aceh uses a thin rattan cane and is carried out over clothing while the perpetrator stands or kneels before the algojo, who wears a hooded robe to protect their anonymity. All algojo are drawn from the ranks of the Wilayatul Hisbah, Aceh’s religious police.

The Legal Aid Institute in Banda Aceh told This Week in Asia that, broadly, it found caning was supported by most of Acehnese society, though support sometimes depended on the specific case.

“At the Legal Aid Institute, we fundamentally oppose the use of caning as a punishment for perpetrators of sexual violence. In our view, caning is too lenient a penalty for sexual violence and it fails to act as a deterrent and lacks a victim-centred perspective,” said Muhammad Qodrat, Banda Aceh institute’s operational head.

Because caning sentences could be “carried out within days or even hours”, he said perpetrators were often quickly able to return to their communities, potentially encountering their victim again and retraumatising them.

“In some instances, perpetrators have even persecuted or intimidated the victims whom they hold responsible for their punishment.”

Especially in cases involving sexual violence, “imprisonment is considered a wiser and more victim-oriented approach” to enforce physical separation, Qodrat said, noting that the perpetrator was often “someone close to the victim who lives in the same or a nearby neighbourhood”.

While Aceh regularly makes headlines when public canings grab global attention, it is not the only place in Southeast Asia to officially sanction the punishment.

In neighbouring Malaysia, flogging is used as punishment for a range of offences including sexual assault, financial crimes and drug-related crimes. Unlike in Aceh, whipping in Malaysia is administered against bare skin, though women and men over 50 are typically exempt.

On May 6 this year, Singapore’s government announced caning could be meted out to male students found bullying classmates as “a last resort”, potentially affecting children as young as nine. Singapore also prescribes caning for more than 35 offences, including rape, drug trafficking, robbery and financial crimes.

Nasir Husein, a 57-year-old videographer based in Lhokseumawe on Aceh’s east coast, told This Week in Asia that views on caning were subjective: some people supported it unconditionally, while others backed it only for certain crimes.

“There are some people who support caning, but who question why it is used for crimes like gambling and not for other crimes like theft or corruption which some people feel are more serious,” he said.

“Others feel that it is more negative than positive as, when footage goes viral like in the most recent case, there is backlash against both Aceh and Islam in general. Some people feel it causes a lot of stigma.”

item.title: [SCMP] Reading the Sino-US rivalry: how a quiet literary relationship has shaped views on China
item.description: As the United States marks the 250th anniversary of its founding, it confronts a new world order dominated by its relationship with China. In this wide-ranging series, we examine the pressure points and possibilities in those ties, from hard tech to soft power. Here, Khushboo Razdan looks at how books shape public perceptions and inspire lifelong connections between the US and China even as their geopolitical rivalry intensifies. Alexander Boyd was 14 when a book about China altered the course o
item.content:encoded:
As the United States marks the 250th anniversary of its founding, it confronts a new world order dominated by its relationship with China. In this wide-ranging series, we examine the pressure points and possibilities in those ties, from hard tech to soft power. Here, Khushboo Razdan looks at how books shape public perceptions and inspire lifelong connections between the US and China even as their geopolitical rivalry intensifies.

Alexander Boyd was 14 when a book about China altered the course of his life.

Raised in a house overflowing with books in the small Massachusetts town of Acton, Boyd spent hours browsing his parents’ shelves in search of his next read.

One title in particular caught his attention – China Wakes by journalists Nicholas Kristof and Sheryl WuDunn – a book chronicling China’s transformation under former leader Deng Xiaoping’s economic reforms and opening up to the world.

Boyd said the book affected him profoundly.

“The impact of reading it was immediate and shocking,” he recalled. He read it on the school bus and between classes, absorbed by the story of a country undergoing one of the most dramatic transformations in modern history.

“It just made me hungry to know more … to figure out what the hell is going on in China,” Boyd said, observing that “the more you learn, the less you know”.

That curiosity never left Boyd.

He studied Chinese in college, travelled to Beijing as a student, and today, at 30 years old, works in New York as the associate editor at China Books Review, a digital magazine introducing English-language readers to China-related literature.

Boyd is not alone in tracing a lifelong fascination with China back to a single book.

For Canadian-American author and long-time China journalist Robert Delaney, the spark came through martial arts. After taking up kung fu as a teenager, he devoured Mark Salzman’s Iron and Silk, the memoir of an American studying martial arts in China.

“I simply wanted to follow in Salzman’s footsteps,” Delaney said.

That ambition eventually took him to China, where he lived for more than a decade. He later published The Wounded Muse, a thriller set in the run-up to the 2008 Beijing Olympics.

Delaney, 58, is now working on a second, still untitled book that returns to his early kung fu training and years in China.

Along the way, he said works such as Maxine Hong Kingston’s The Woman Warrior deepened his understanding of the lived histories behind modern China.

“I always gravitate to authors who have lived within Chinese culture, and who write from the heart and not the head.”

As the United States marks the 250th anniversary of its founding, its relationship with China is often framed through trade disputes, military competition and geopolitical rivalry. But another history has unfolded more quietly.

For generations, books have been among the first places Americans encountered China – shaping public perceptions as well as inspiring students, scholars, journalists and writers to travel there, study its language and build careers around understanding the country.

Even as the bilateral relationship has grown more contentious, that literary bridge has endured.

From The Good Earth, Pearl Buck’s bestselling 1930s novel of rural Chinese life, to Edgar Snow’s Red Star Over China, which introduced Mao Zedong and Chinese Communists to Western readers, and later Wild Swans, Jung Chang’s sweeping family memoir of 20th century China, these works have shaped perceptions, sparked curiosity, and inspired countless literal and intellectual journeys towards a country that has long fascinated the American imagination.

As US-China ties have shifted from cooperation to strategic competition, and America has turned inward under the “Make America Great Again” movement, the market for China books has widened.

American bestseller trends reveal that readers in the US are polarised yet highly engaged when it comes to books about China.

Politically charged titles such as Peter Schweizer’s Blood Money and US Senator Tom Cotton’s Seven Things You Can’t Say About China became New York Times bestsellers in 2024 and 2025, respectively, tapping into anxieties around US-China competition and national security.

“It’s quite interesting,” Boyd said of the heightened attention, adding that he sometimes joked that it was “heartening to see that American right-wingers are reading so much”.

On a more serious note, he said there “are three or four big things that Americans want to know about China”.

“There’s conspiracy theories, there’s communism, and there’s cookbooks, but there’s increasingly also fiction,” he said. “Chinese fantasy is becoming much more popular.”

This year, Chinese-inspired epic fantasy The Poet Empress topped the April bestseller list for the China Books Review. The story centres on a woman’s struggle to save her family’s village and navigate power, politics and destiny in a richly imagined imperial world.

Among the notable recent releases, Boyd highlighted Adrift in the South, a powerful work following a migrant labourer’s harsh life on factory assembly lines in southern China’s manufacturing hubs and the city of Shenzhen during the country’s economic boom.

According to Boyd, the “only consistent thing” in China-themed books is that the ones built upon “deep personal experience, expertise and of course excellent writing do the best”, even when they offer more complex or mixed views.

In 2023, conspiracy titles like Mao’s America and cookbooks such as Invitation to a Banquet led the charts.

From 2024 onwards, tech-focused books like Breakneck and Apple in China became long-running successes, while stories such as Daughters of the Bamboo Grove – a non-fiction work about identical twin sisters in China who were separated at birth to evade the one-child policy – likewise gained traction.

Yet, books have also served another purpose: helping members of the Chinese diaspora make sense of their own identities while navigating Western perceptions of China.

For Karen Ma, a New York-based writer, film scholar and author raised in Hong Kong and Japan, books were less a gateway to China than a way of finding her own place within it.

Living in Japan as a teenager, Ma was frequently reminded that she was an outsider and asked when she would “go home”.

Later, in the US, she found herself fielding simplistic questions about China, such as about feng shui, kung fu or whether all Chinese people belonged to the Communist Party.

That experience, Ma said, left her with both a curiosity and an urgency to understand her own roots more intimately.

“I was always treated as ‘the other’,” she said. “I had an unexpected, lingering burden to know my roots – and a deep shame when I couldn’t answer everyone’s silly assumptions.”

Writing became a way to find her roots and better understand her identity. Early guides included Cao Xueqin’s Dream of the Red Chamber and Jonathan Spence’s The Gate of Heavenly Peace, books that helped her connect with China’s literary and historical traditions.

Ma, who has since written the novels Excess Baggage and China’s Millennial Digital Generation, observed that another obstacle to Americans’ understanding of China lay not in the books being written but in how they were translated.

“High-quality translation is a major issue for Chinese literature,” she said.

“Many Chinese novels lack the adequate and eloquent English translations seen in Japanese literature. Chinese literature could attract a much wider readership if publishers were willing to invest more money into securing top-tier translators.”

Ma argued that the best books about China come from writers with deep immersion, regardless of nationality.

“Many Western writers risk misreading China due to language barriers and a lack of on-the-ground immersion,” she said, while some Chinese authors could also suffer from a kind of literary “tunnel vision” that German sinologist Wolfgang Kubin attributed to decades of “historical and cultural isolation”.

Delaney pointed to the increasing competition books faced in shaping perceptions from short videos that were often not well researched.

“So many of our perceptions are now shaped by sound bites on social media,” he said. “Books give us the depth and context that we need to counteract this. I just hope that novelists are not fighting a losing battle.”

Boyd also hoped books could help fill the gap and spark new interest at a time when American student numbers in China have plummeted.

According to data from the Institute of International Education’s Open Doors report, the number of American students studying in China stood at over 11,600 before the coronavirus pandemic.

Following a steep decline owing to strict policies and geopolitical tensions, US official estimates indicate that the number began a modest recovery, ticking up to about 800 during the 2023-2024 academic year.

“That was my experience,” Boyd said of his life trajectory. “I read a book about China, and I got obsessed … books can absolutely play that role. I hope so.”

“I wish books could solve US-China diplomatic problems,” he added.

“I hope that there’s room for more exchange of books between both cultures. America wants to know more about China. China should want to know more about America. I believe it does.”

item.title: [SCMP] China-India diplomats are shaking hands, but it’s no holds barred for web users. Why?
item.description: The Chinese ambassador to India’s attempt at bridge-building has triggered a fierce nationalist backlash back home, including accusations that he has lost sight of his role. “The ambassador seems to have forgotten his actual job,” one social media user wrote this month of Xu Feihong, arguing that the envoy’s role was to make Indians more friendly towards China – not the other way round. Another took a xenophobic turn, accusing Xu of “letting hundreds of thousands, even millions, of Indian ‘cockr
item.content:encoded:
The Chinese ambassador to India’s attempt at bridge-building has triggered a fierce nationalist backlash back home, including accusations that he has lost sight of his role.

“The ambassador seems to have forgotten his actual job,” one social media user wrote this month of Xu Feihong, arguing that the envoy’s role was to make Indians more friendly towards China – not the other way round.

Another took a xenophobic turn, accusing Xu of “letting hundreds of thousands, even millions, of Indian ‘cockroaches’ flood into China” and “polluting the country”.

In June, Xu described China-India ties as being among the “most important” bilateral relationships in the world”, saying they had moved from a “reset and fresh start” to a “new level of development”.

Yet the online outrage extends far beyond Xu’s specific comments. It reflects deep-seated frustration over Beijing’s recent diplomatic overtures to New Delhi, with Xu – as China’s public face in India – becoming a lightning rod for the public anger.

But even as online commenters vent their fury, official state policy and public demand are moving in the opposite direction.

Six years after clashes on their disputed Himalayan border left 20 Indian and four Chinese soldiers dead, the two sides are finally moving to rebuild their shattered relationship.

The Chinese embassy in New Delhi reported issuing 85,000 visas to Indian passport holders in the first quarter of 2026. The surge comes as the neighbours work to mend diplomatic ties, alongside growing curiosity among many Indians regarding China’s rapid technological and economic growth.

Amid the thaw, China has reopened the Kailash Manasarovar pilgrimage route through Tibet, sacred to followers of Hinduism, Buddhism, Jainism and Bon, while India has eased some restrictions on Chinese investment.

New Delhi is also considering allowing Chinese journalists to return for the first time since 2023, when the last Xinhua reporter left the country after being denied a visa extension. However, it has yet to reciprocate on travel permits, as Chinese nationals continue to face significant hurdles in securing Indian visas.

But a different battle is unfolding online, revealing the gap between government-led attempts at normalisation and decades of entrenched public hostility on both sides.

Observers say rebuilding public trust could prove far harder than restoring flights, visas and diplomatic engagement. Still, some pragmatic voices in China have pushed back against the backlash, warning that sensationalist portrayals of the other country – often drawn from isolated negative incidents – risk entrenching stereotypes. They argue that such narratives are driven less by patriotism than by the pursuit of online attention and traffic.

A series of viral incidents has further inflamed online sentiment. Since May, multiple videos circulating on Chinese social media have shown Indian tourists engaging in disruptive behaviour, including arguing loudly with hotel staff, dancing on the Great Wall, and placing their feet on seats aboard high-speed trains.

Many Chinese web users condemned such “uncivilised” conduct, saying such behaviour harmed the country’s social environment.

Meanwhile, clips of Indian tourists just walking through Chinese cities have prompted online questions about whether they had enough money to spend.

Another widely shared video clip from a visiting Indian influencer drew criticism for asking passers-by stereotypical questions, such as mocking the quality of China’s infrastructure and asking if they were wearing fake Gucci products.

A recent commentary in Chinese state-owned outlet The Paper warned about the rise of biased social media content exaggerating the “outrageous” or bizarre aspects of life in India, while amplifying alleged misconduct by Indians in China. This risked creating a fragmented and stereotypical view of the country, it cautioned.

“Although these posts may appear to be expressions of patriotism, they are actually designed to generate traffic and attention by stoking hostility and xenophobia,” the article said. “We cannot negate an entire country because of a few individuals.”

Last month, a heated online debate erupted between Indian and Chinese web users over social hierarchies, with some Indians arguing that China had a “caste-like system” comparable to India’s hereditary social structure.

To support their argument, they pointed to ancient China’s Shi-Nong-Gong-Shang social hierarchy and the modern hukou household registration system, arguing that both had fostered deep, enduring urban-rural disparities for migrant workers.

“Born rural? Tough luck. You’re basically a second-class citizen locked out of proper education, healthcare, housing subsidies, and decent jobs,” posted one user from India. “It’s a caste system … that traps hundreds of millions in poverty.”

A YouTube video on the issue, titled “Indians expose China’s caste system” and produced by an Indian influencer with 1.7 million subscribers, drew nearly 550,000 views.

Chinese social media users hit back, with one highlighting that in China’s administrative system “the children of farmers can become officials, merchants, or workers through their own efforts”.

“All countries in the world have these occupational divisions. Indians, with your level of understanding, it’s better not to come out and embarrass yourself.”

He Xianqing, an associate researcher at the National Institute for South China Sea Studies, noted there was “no doubt” that online and media-driven nationalism had had a negative impact on the positive development of China-India relations.

“If the peoples of the two countries do not understand each other and public support is weak, the momentum for cooperation between the two sides will be limited, which may eventually backfire on the cooperative relationship,” he warned.

But he also emphasised that such digital friction had its limits. “Nationalistic remarks or sentiments are irrational after all, and they are an unsystematic public opinion reaction,” he said, arguing that they would fail to derail the diplomatic momentum between the two governments.

“There are also some rational voices on both sides, and we should listen more to these rational voices.”

He also called on both sides to organise more Track 2 dialogues and cultural exchange activities, “using the power of experts to provide a rational voice for public opinion in both countries”.

Sourabh Gupta of the Institute for China-American Studies said nationalist rhetoric had long been a feature of the bilateral relationship, but largely on the Indian side. “The novel feature is that the sentiment is now beginning to be shared both ways.”

Gupta argued that the geopolitical disruption caused by US President Donald Trump had provided a “tailwind to the calculus of tentative rapprochement”. Just as New Delhi had largely learned to look past public opinion in managing ties with China, Beijing was also likely to see beyond nationalist rhetoric and press ahead.

“It is not as if the two governments are racing ahead of public opinion. Their bilateral relationship is no longer at the failing stage, but that’s about the best that can be said about the state of ties currently,” Gupta noted.

“China is not the first country in the Asia-Pacific region to see Indian tourists or workers in such dim light, and there might be a lesson for travelling Indians to internalise too in this regard.”

item.title: [SCMP] Chinese AI labs pursue custom chips to lower costs but heavy upfront investment a risk
item.description: Chinese AI labs are increasingly pursuing proprietary chips, mirroring a global trend towards software-hardware integration, but industry insiders and analysts warn that the strategy carries risk due to the massive upfront investments required. “The core motivation for choosing in-house chips lies in pursuing [greater] hardware-software synergy and lowering long-term operating costs,” said Arisa Liu, chief director and research fellow at Taiwan Industry Economics Services. Paul Triolo, a partner
item.content:encoded:
Chinese AI labs are increasingly pursuing proprietary chips, mirroring a global trend towards software-hardware integration, but industry insiders and analysts warn that the strategy carries risk due to the massive upfront investments required.

“The core motivation for choosing in-house chips lies in pursuing [greater] hardware-software synergy and lowering long-term operating costs,” said Arisa Liu, chief director and research fellow at Taiwan Industry Economics Services.

Paul Triolo, a partner and technology policy lead at DGA-Albright Stonebridge Group, said in his personal newsletter AIStackDecrypted that the proprietary efforts underscored how China’s leading model developers increasingly viewed silicon as a strategic extension of the model stack rather than simply another infrastructure input.

DeepSeek has been quietly hiring chip-design talent without posting public job openings, according to two people familiar with the situation, who declined to be named as the matter was private.

The Hangzhou-based start-up’s plans for a customised AI inference chip began roughly a year ago and remained at an early stage, according to a Reuters report on Tuesday.

Meanwhile, Zhipu AI, the Beijing-based developer of the high-performance GLM-5.2 model, was in early talks with domestic chip-design companies about tailored AI processors amid a sharp increase in its daily token usage, The Information reported on Tuesday.

The chip design aspirations of Chinese model makers come amid surging computing demand, rising inference costs and continued restricted access in China to Nvidia’s most advanced AI chips.

While processors from external suppliers like Huawei Technologies were available, Liu said custom silicon allowed for hardware optimisation tailored to specific architectures, such as the DeepSeek-R1 model.

Triolo added that the goal was not to replace Nvidia or even Huawei, but to reduce the rapidly rising cost of AI inference as token volumes explode and enterprise deployments accelerate.

“Software-hardware integration is an inevitable trend,” said Hui He, semiconductor research director at Omdia. “For model companies, running their own models on customised chips can improve efficiency and help build a moat around their ecosystem.”

US tech giants Google, Amazon, Microsoft and OpenAI have all pursued custom or semi-custom AI chips to reduce reliance on off-the-shelf processors and tailor hardware to their own workloads.

JP Morgan estimated in June that shipments of AI application-specific integrated circuits, or ASICs, and XPUs to major US hyperscalers would exceed graphics processing unit (GPU) shipments from Nvidia and AMD by 2027, accounting for 53 per cent of AI accelerator units.

Custom AI chips are not necessarily more powerful than Nvidia’s GPUs across all workloads, but JP Morgan said they offered better performance per dollar and per watt when designed around a hyperscaler’s own software stack and data-centre architecture. Such projects require large, stable workloads and high-volume deployments to justify the cost.

Model developers’ efforts do not always have to take the form of a fully internal chip-design unit, Omdia’s He said. They could also build chip teams, invest in chip start-ups or co-develop processors with specialist designers, sharing the results of such projects.

Still, building in-house-design capabilities did not necessarily mean a company had already taped out a chip, she said. Companies have to decide whether and when to tape out based on their expected deployment scale, future shipment volumes and whether those volumes can cover the high upfront cost. Tape out is the final stage of chip design when it is sent to the foundry for fabrication.

However, Nvidia chips would still be necessary for general-purpose AI computing, He added, describing its GPUs as a “token generator” for the industry. Custom chips, by contrast, are more likely to be used for proprietary or dedicated workloads, as AI companies seek multiple sources of computing power amid persistent supply constraints.

The trend is also expected to benefit ASIC design partners and related supply-chain companies, including Broadcom, Marvell and MediaTek, while also becoming a key driver of Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Co’s CoWoS advanced-packaging expansion and high-bandwidth memory demand.

DeepSeek, which closed a 51 billion yuan (US$7.5 billion) fundraising round in June that valued it at around US$60 billion, has been aggressively expanding its team. Its latest V4 model has been widely deployed for inference on Huawei’s Ascend 950 chips.

China’s major cloud service providers, including Alibaba Group Holding, Baidu and ByteDance, have also been pouring resources into chip units.

At the same time, as demand for computing power surges and access to advanced foreign semiconductors remains constrained, Chinese AI companies are increasingly sourcing processors from domestic chip designers such as Cambricon, Iluvatar CoreX and MetaX to diversify their supply. Alibaba owns the South China Morning Post.

“The integration of domestic chips and domestic algorithms or models is inevitable,” said Wei Shaojun, vice-president of the China Semiconductor Industry Association and founder and chief executive of Dongfang Suanxin, a Shanghai-based chip-design unicorn.

Wei said his company was working with model developers to build large models “based on domestic chips,” rather than developing them first on foreign processors and then adapting them to Chinese hardware – a process he said inevitably resulted in efficiency losses.

Triolo said US export controls had pushed the Chinese AI ecosystem “to co-develop and optimise solutions across the manufacturing, hardware integration, software integration, model development, and harness or application development stack all at once in parallel.”

Meanwhile, analysts also warned that the pivot from software to hardware carried immense risks, as the heavy upfront investment might take years to generate positive returns.

Even if a chip successfully replaced external hardware and significantly lowered electricity and computing costs, it generally took two to three years of mass production to recoup the high R&D costs, Liu said.

“For AI software companies such as DeepSeek, the initial financial and time investment required to develop in-house chips is undoubtedly a huge gamble,” Liu said. The recruitment of scarce talent in chip design and wafer engineering could be intensive, she added.

Additional reporting by Howard Liu and Coco Feng

item.title: [SCMP] Cabo Verde’s Sal makes a comeback: the tide is turning as its diaspora returns to the island
item.description: “Just remember, the sharks can touch you, but you can’t touch them,” warns João, raising his hand with the tip of his middle finger curled away, a mock amputation delivered with such casual theatre that, for a moment, I believe the island really did claim it. He produces a pair of Crocs bleached thin by salt and sun and adds, “Oh, and no open blisters on your feet, either. That tends to excite them, too.” João is part of a quiet reversal happening across Cabo Verde, an archipelagic country off t
item.content:encoded:
“Just remember, the sharks can touch you, but you can’t touch them,” warns João, raising his hand with the tip of his middle finger curled away, a mock amputation delivered with such casual theatre that, for a moment, I believe the island really did claim it. He produces a pair of Crocs bleached thin by salt and sun and adds, “Oh, and no open blisters on your feet, either. That tends to excite them, too.”

João is part of a quiet reversal happening across Cabo Verde, an archipelagic country off the coast of West Africa that was, until 1975, a Portuguese colony. After decades defined by departure, Cabo Verde is experiencing return.

The guide had planned to stay in Lisbon, Portugal, for five years. He stayed for 12. Hotel maintenance in Bairro Alto paid well, the work was steady and like hundreds of thousands of Cabo Verdeans before him, he’d followed his grandparents away from his island – in this case, Sal – towards somewhere with more possibilities than salt, sand and sea could offer.

Then, three years ago, he came back to guide tourists into these shallow Atlantic waters where lemon sharks learn to hunt.

“They always come back,” he says, nodding towards the pale blue of Shark Bay. “The young ones. This is where they learn.” He smiles. “A bit like us.”

Although the Cabo Verdean diaspora still outnumbers the domestic population of just over 500,000, net migration has narrowed significantly in recent years.

The economy expanded by around 7.3 per cent in 2024, with tourism creating the kinds of opportunities that once required a plane ticket to find.

We step into the water at low tide, when the surf breaks farther out and the shallows warm into a pale nursery blue. Within seconds of João slapping the surface with a fish spine, the bay answers: metre-long shadows slide past my calves, blunt snouts nosing forward with soft urgency. I am standing in a cradle, a shallow bowl of warmth and safety where juvenile lemon sharks learn to be apex predators before facing the open Atlantic.

“The mama and papa sharks stay out there,” João says, gesturing towards deeper water. “They can’t easily come in here when the tide is low. This part is just for the young ones.”

It’s an apt metaphor for what’s happening across the island. Sal – the name means “salt” in Portuguese – once had a singular economic purpose: extract, dry, export. But as tourism has grown and the economy has diversified, Sal, which attracts more than half of all international visitors to Cabo Verde, is becoming a place worth staying for. Along with neighbouring Boa Vista, it acts as the hub for the country’s tourism industry, largely due to its white-sand beaches, reliable sunshine and abundance of all-inclusive resorts.

Shark Bay lies on the eastern shoulder of Sal. A rusting mini-tanker crouches on the rocks, its hull oxidising patiently into the same cinnamon colour as the desert that spills across the plain to meet it, as if land and wreck were slowly learning to resemble each other. Nothing here looks hurried. Even decay seems to have found a comfortable rhythm. “No Work, No Stress” is the island’s T-shirt motto.

From the air, Sal appeared less an island than a thoughtful doodle in rock and sand: pale, economical, faintly aloof, as if someone began sketching a tropical paradise and then stopped early, deciding that too much greenery might distract from the more serious business of light and space. On the ground, the palette remains disciplined: dunes the colour of shortbread, skies in extravagant blues and an ocean that practises every shade of turquoise like a master stained-glass painter.

There are no jungles here to wrestle, no waterfalls throwing tantrums. Instead, Sal traffics in trade winds, sea breezes, long horizons and the peculiar theatre of time slowing down. Even the ever-present street dogs nap with intent. Everything, from the rare palm tree to your own ambition, eventually agrees to lie flatter. A planned hike becomes a long stare at the sea; a resolved itinerary dissolves in the face of a slow conversation.

The towns are dispersed like points on a clock around this 216 sq km island and there are two main ways to travel between them. Taxis can be flagged down easily, but it’s advisable to set a price before getting in, and shared minibuses, called aluguers – typically white Toyota Hiace vehicles, occasionally modified pickup trucks – run on set routes and leave only when full.

The distance between the two main population centres, Santa Maria and Espargos, is 20km. The former is where most of the tourist resorts stand but the latter, Sal’s modest capital, is where the island gathers itself, schoolchildren threading their way past motorbikes; laundry fluttering from balconies; and brightly painted breeze-block houses offering gentle defiance against the dun landscape. From open windows comes the melancholic acoustic music known as morna: guitar and cavaquinho lute braided with violin, dripping with saudade, or longing, and moving at the pace of a slow strum, the way music sounds when it belongs to a place rather than performs for it.

The ever-present influence of Portugal lingers not only in the cuisine – the bread rolls that arrive with every meal, the bica espresso drunk standing at a counter, the Sagres and Super Bock beer sweating in the afternoon heat – but beneath your feet in the calçada portuguesa: mosaic pavements of limestone and basalt that stitch Sal into the wider Lusophone world, all the way to Macau.

The newly built houses here are too large for single families. Many belong to returnees from Lisbon, Boston, Rotterdam … built to function as guest houses, small hotels, futures. Some of these returnees speak little or no Crioulo, the national language, and some have never lived on Sal before. But they’re investing in an ancestral geography known through family stories and remittance transfers.

I stay for a few nights at the Académico do Sal hostel, above the city’s public 25-metre swimming pool. The building carries the cheerful pragmatism of that arrangement: part-school dormitory, part-old-fashioned American roadside motel, the rooms offer exactly what they promise and nothing they don’t.

One morning, I descend to the pool before the heat has fully committed to the day. I am midway through my third length when I notice a man watching from the edge, his attention fixed not on me but on a girl of maybe 12 years old cutting clean lines up and down the lane beside mine. Adilson took an aluguer up from Santa Maria with his daughter for her morning training session.

“The resorts down there would rather you were a guest paying for the sunbed,” he says, without particular bitterness. “Here, the pool is open to everyone. She gets proper lanes, proper conditions. She learns what a race actually feels like.”

By mid-afternoon, the pool has transformed entirely. A local football team has taken over the terrace for their annual gathering, members arriving in ones and twos with dishes wrapped in foil and plastic containers smelling of cumin and slow cooking. The pool bar does steady business, music appears from somewhere. The Académico do Sal, it turns out, is less hostel, more civic living room.

I meet João again between tours, at Espargos’ Girasol e Museu restaurant, which stands on a backstreet and doubles, as its name suggests, as a small museum, its walls hung with local art and artefacts that give the place the feeling of a family home that quietly decided to become important. The menu is short and unambiguous and depends on what’s available: cachupa, the archipelago’s signature stew, made here from recipes guarded by avós, grandmothers, and spoken of with the reverence due to any secret kept long enough to become identity; fresh tuna grilled over charcoal; roast chicken; crisps fried from real potatoes rather than extruded from a factory; whatever came in on the last ship from the Canary Islands or was grown in the thin, salt-tolerant soil of the island.

The food arrives on clay dishes glazed in strong colours – deep blue, ochre, a particular shade of terracotta red – and the effect, against the spare whitewashed walls, is similar to that of those brightly painted breeze-block houses out on the street: an insistence on colour in a landscape that otherwise declines to offer much of it.

“In Lisbon, I had a job. Here, I have a life,” says João. The difference, he says, wasn’t just about money; it was about what kind of future felt possible. His daughter speaks Crioulo. She learns morna music at school. “She knows where she’s from. I didn’t, not really, until I came back.”

There are no roads to Shark Bay, only wandering rutted tracks in the dust left by the daily pilgrimages of tour-group Toyota Hiluxes with bench seats bolted into their beds. Leaving the bay, you bounce up-island as the land rises slightly, then hollows itself into a vast volcanic bowl, towards Pedra de Lume and its historic salinas.

Inside the crater, salt pans shimmer rose white with a light so fierce it seems to erase edges. Tourists of varying hues of pink bob like penguins in water that is below sea level and mineral-rich, with more than 20 times the salinity of the open sea. João’s warning about blisters suddenly feels less whimsical: even the smallest abrasion blooms instantly into pain. A second warning follows: don’t, whatever you do, touch your eyes.

West towards Palmeira, the ground reddens and the vicious erosion cuts deeper furrows as the land eases down to the island’s harbour and ferry terminal, which links Sal to Boa Vista, São Nicolau and Santiago (Cabo Verde’s most populous island and home to the Chinese-built national football stadium). Fishing boats and private catamarans lean into the quay like animals at a trough. Men mend nets under tin roofs and the Atlantic breathes steadily against the kaleidoscope-coloured homes. Some of the landed tuna, another of the island’s main exports, ends up on the charcoal grills that overlook the marina.

Under a tin awning at the harbour, where lengths of sail cloth – vivid blues, sun-bleached whites, panels of red and orange – are draped over the port railings and the balustrades of the ferry terminal for mending, I meet Steven. He is from southern Nigeria, from a Christian farming town where work thinned and wages never quite arrived.

“I came for a job on the boats,” he says, referring to the tuna-fishing vessels. “I stayed because it felt calm. Because people greet you. Because you can breathe.”

As tourism proliferates, Steven says, finding somewhere to live has become his hardest battle.

Rents rise with every new resort opening. Building cranes and scaffolding abound on the island, but entire buildings disappear into short-term holiday listings as soon as the paint dries.

North of Palmeira lies the bay of Buracona, where the Atlantic has spent millennia arguing with volcanic rock, finally punching through and leaving a cathedral-sized window into darkness. At midday, when the sun reaches its apex, the “blue eye” opens: a circle beamed down into the cenote below of a colour so impossible, it seems like a flaw in reality. Had anyone lived on these islands before the Portuguese turned them into slaving ports, this blue eye might have felt like the genesis of belief itself.

I stand at the edge and watch it glow. For perhaps 10 minutes, the light holds, then shifts and the magic closes again like a pupil contracting. It feels important that the wonder is brief, that you can’t schedule it or guarantee it – you can only arrive and hope and sometimes witness.

Down the ambitious four-lane motorway that is being built to bisect the island, Murdeira Bay opens on the left, a protected crescent yawning beneath Monte Leão. The “Lion Mountain” rises 165 metres from the surrounding basalt flatness and watches over the calm water below, its cliffs hosting the few birds found on Sal: kestrels and ospreys that look outward to the ocean for sustenance rather than inward to the sparse land. The small resort and scatter of holiday rentals almost dissolve into a landscape purpose-built for watching sunsets.

By the time you reach Santa Maria, the island has changed its posture entirely. Here, Sal stands upright, opens its shoulders and smiles.

I sit one evening near the crumbling pier as the light turns amber, watching a German family photograph the sunset while a Cabo Verdean guitarist plays morna for a cluster of swaying French tourists. Behind them, bougainvillea spills over pastel walls. The beach curves in long pale ribbons. More music drifts from somewhere unseen.

The hotels are here, the flights arrive daily from a dozen European cities – Sal’s Amílcar Cabral is the busiest of Cabo Verde’s four international airports – but something of the island’s original temperament persists beneath the welcome. You see it in the local staff running the kitesurf schools, in the Crioulo spoken in hotel hallways, in the menus that list cachupa alongside burgers and club sandwiches.

The same winds that once carried salt ships now beckon kitesurfers. The same desert that meant death is now flown across on zip lines and traversed by quad bikes. And the same beaches that once cradled only turtle eggs now embrace sunbathers.

From June to October, when the moon lifts silver over the dunes and the beaches empty of people, loggerhead sea turtles haul themselves ashore. They return not to just any coast, but to the exact sands that once saw them take their first steps, their ancient bodies guided by a geography written somewhere deeper than memory.

Decades ago, their numbers here were collapsing. Eggs were taken, beaches were unguarded, the future uncertain. Today, local patrols walk the shoreline at night, nests are protected, hatchlings are shepherded to the sea and Sal has become one of the most important loggerhead nesting sites in the entire Atlantic.

This island once did little more than dry and trade salt to keep passing sailors alive, but now, it quietly protects the fragile arithmetic of tomorrow itself. Like the young sharks in Shark Bay, Sal is learning how to belong.

item.title: [SCMP] Bhutan’s heritage arts become the frontline of cultural preservation as modernity dawns
item.description: Bhutan today doesn’t look quite like the Bhutan of even just four years ago. In monasteries, laminated QR codes for digital payments are displayed next to worn donation boxes. In Thimphu, the capital, there are solar panels on roofs, Chinese-made electric cars on roads, construction projects just paces apart – six storeys sprouting from the ground in humdrum blocks. Even the hand-painted road signs, once quippy rhymes and memorable alliterations, have become mundane: “No hurry, no worry” replace
item.content:encoded:
Bhutan today doesn’t look quite like the Bhutan of even just four years ago.

In monasteries, laminated QR codes for digital payments are displayed next to worn donation boxes. In Thimphu, the capital, there are solar panels on roofs, Chinese-made electric cars on roads, construction projects just paces apart – six storeys sprouting from the ground in humdrum blocks. Even the hand-painted road signs, once quippy rhymes and memorable alliterations, have become mundane: “No hurry, no worry” replaced with “Don’t look at your mobile phone while driving”.

How did the Land of the Thunder Dragon, a nation whose reputation for idyllic, down-to-earth, lo-tech living was built on its reclusion from the outside world, get here?

Since it emerged from the Covid-19 pandemic, Bhutan has launched, in quick succession, a slew of initiatives designed to bring the once-withdrawn kingdom into the 21st century – and the people of the 21st century into its mountainous reaches. From the biennial Snowman Race ultra-marathon and the restoration of the ancient Trans Bhutan Trail as a hiking attraction to the opening of the Bhutan Innovation Lab and even a reduction of its Sustainable Development Fee – the daily levy paid by travellers visiting the country – from US$200 to US$100 per night, Bhutan is embracing the new, the foreign and the lucrative.

In June, it hosted the inaugural Bhutan International Travel Mart, to bolster its position as an alluring getaway, but its biggest leap is still to come: the Gelephu Mindfulness City, a 2,600 sq km (1,000 square miles) special administrative region in which governing policies and business practices from around the world will be adopted and tested; those that prove successful will eventually be rolled out across the country. Gelephu International Airport, handling up to 123 flights daily, is slated to open in 2029.

All this forward momentum raises the question: how does Bhutan balance the realities of progress with the preservation of its traditions, arguably the one quality that makes this destination stand out for travellers? One possible solution: double down on the foundations of your unique culture – with a twist.

On a balmy day in late May, Kinley Tshering sits cross-legged on the cement porch of the Exotic Art Gallery, in a village on the edge of Punakha district, a short stroll from famed hilltop fertility temple Chimi Lhakhang.

He is hunched over, with his back to the rest of the world, his attention trained on a stretched canvas frame propped against the wall, unbothered by the flies zipping around his head, landing on his bare shoulder, inching around on his paint-splattered jeans.

Kinley Tshering deftly layers white paint on top of a black background, colouring tiny, exposed slivers of negative space between delicate, embossed linework applied in shimmering gold, his brush strokes so precise and small he looks as though he is hardly moving.

He is painting a thangka. Sacred illustrated scrolls bearing motifs of divine beings, mandalas (concentric designs representing the universe) and scenes from Buddhist texts, thangkas are ubiquitous in Bhutan’s religious sites and dzongs (fortresses). Just steps away from Kinley Tshering’s in-progress Vajrakilaya – a wrathful deity of Vajrayana Buddhism, Bhutan’s state religion – the workshop’s full collection is on display, a cacophony of colour and pattern.

Leaning against the doorframe, watching Kinley Tshering at work, is his older brother Kinga Tshering, the owner of the Exotic Art Gallery. “You see those two dragons? He is combining them with Vajrakilaya for strength and purity,” explains Kinga Tshering. “He’s putting a lot of different ideas into this. He will even wait for his dreams. Whatever idea he gets, whatever dream he dreams, he will add those elements.”
