Lhazo devotional painting – one of the 13 traditional arts and crafts of Bhutan – is in the brothers’ blood: the thangka trade is a family speciality that has been passed down through three generations. Kinga Tshering studied under his father from a young age and went to the National Institute for Zorig Chusum, in Thimphu, to continue his education. He opened the Exotic Art Gallery “around seven, eight years ago”, taking orders for thangkas from temples and private households; the rest of his income is earned through the gallery. Kinley Tshering is one of seven brothers and cousins he has in his employ.

Before starting a thangka, the artist must prepare their pigments. Botanical and mineral ingredients are crushed and blended with water and animal glue, to protect colour intensity and repel insects.

“Sometimes, we use pure 24-carat gold. But that costs a lot, so we only use that for paintings for temples,” says Kinga Tshering. He nods at the painting of Vajrakilaya. “We get black from the charcoal of oak trees. Gold, from crushed pyrite; we get it from the mountains.

“Once a thangka is done, it’s done; it is difficult to repeat. Colours that we got two, three years ago, it’s not always possible to replace them; it all depends on what nature can give. I can mix pink again, but not the exact same; that’s the beauty of handmade painting.”

Despite being a creative art form, thangka work does obey strict fundamentals. A mandala has to be a mandala, Kinga Tshering stresses. Depictions of deities must adhere to prescribed postures and hand gestures. Deviations are considered “bad deeds”.

Even within these restrictions, the brothers’ approach to the art is attuned to their passions, emotions, imagination and “vibes”, unlike that of their father, who didn’t paint thangkas for creation’s sake and whose practice was entirely tied to commissions.

“We cannot force ourselves to paint; the energy has to come from the heart,” says Kinga Tshering. “If your mind is too busy, if you’re in pain – you don’t paint. Good moods give the best results.

“Around 60 per cent of the artwork we make comes from us thinking about what we want to paint; we discuss, we wait for our dreams.” Motifs of mythical creatures, flora or fauna that come to them are melded with the immutable central figure of their thangkas. In their blending of the traditional, the devotional and the personal, the brothers keep lhazo relevant.

The 13 forms of arts and crafts Bhutan recognises as part of its cultural identity are known collectively as zorig chusum (zo meaning “the ability to make”, rig meaning “science” or “craft” and chusum meaning “13”). Some, like lhazo, survive thanks to continued demand from Bhutanese traditionalists. Others, like cane- and bamboo-weaving (tsharzo) and blacksmithing (garzo), are struggling to maintain their place in a rapidly modernising country.

“When it comes to zorig chusum, tourists are more inclined towards painting and [textile] weaving because they see them in our daily lives, in our buildings and monasteries, as paintings on the walls, in the national dress,” says Kuenzang Choden, marketing manager for Asia-Pacific and Oceania at the tourism department. “But our mission is to promote all 13 arts and crafts, including the ones that receive less public attention, like tsharzo, garzo, parzo [wood carving], shagzo [wood turning], lugzo [bronze casting] and dozo [stonework and masonry].”

Khampa, project management specialist for the Handicrafts Association of Bhutan, an organisation comprising some 7,500 members who practise zorig chusum, believes that the determination to preserve heritage starts from within.

“We are not looking to promote craft as a ‘thing’. We want people to understand the significance of it and educate them so they can find value in living that culture,” he says.

“Our art is alive; it lives as skills in the hands of our artisans. As an outsider, we just see them as someone who makes a ‘product’. But they practise that art because it defines their life; there is meaning in weaving a piece of cloth. It defines their survival. It defines their purpose. It defines their identity,” he adds. “For us, these artisans are how we can achieve our vision of living Bhutanese culture and traditions.”

A sharp, clacking noise rings out across a room at the Royal Textile Academy (RTA), in Thimphu, bouncing off the walls: repetitive, insistent, percussive. A calloused hand reaches for a wide wooden slat, suspended horizontally, pressing it down against rows of woven thread to tighten the weave. Rods of bamboo, threaded through the warp, clatter hollowly.

Two master weavers are teasing cloth into existence, one weft at a time, working silently save for the sound of their equipment: a pair of formidable pangthag backstrap looms, constructed of timber. A waterfall of filaments cascades from the top of the apparatus, the fibres kept taut by a belt wrapped around each of the weaver’s hips.

“Among the 13 traditional arts and crafts, weaving is considered especially important because of its integral role,” says Chimi Lhakyi Dolma, assistant programme officer of the RTA, which was established in 2005, under the patronage of queen mother Sangay Choden Wangchuck, to preserve thagzo, the art of weaving. She is referring to the national dress, gho for men and kira for women, which must be worn in schools, government offices, religious sites and dzongs, at official ceremonies and on formal occasions.

As a domestic skill, thagzo is passed down from mother to daughter, Dolma explains, and a weaver’s ultimate test is the mastery of the exquisite kishuthara, a highly intricate silk made using trima, a labour-intensive technique of hand-coiling individual threads around the base yarn to create a raised effect that mimics embroidery. Beyond being an example of ability and endurance – weaving a garment using this technique can take anywhere from eight months to two years – a treasured kishuthara kira can be handed down the matrilineal line, creating generational bonds.

However, “home-based weaving traditions are declining day by day”, says Dolma, who is also the founder of textile brand Dakini by Jyenn. “Although their mothers weave, younger generations do not take an interest in the skill; to them, weaving is a chore. [RTA’s] mission is to promote weaving as an art.”

While the museum at the RTA functions as a repository of ancient fabrics, the institute also runs educational programmes and houses a studio in which five master weavers, from eastern Bhutan, ply their craft. One of them creates textiles for the royal family while the others weave for research and development purposes, and for the gift shop.

Nowadays, the RTA is exploring how time-honoured techniques can be applied in novel ways. Craftswomen are encouraged to expand beyond tried-and-tested colour combinations and experiment with shapes and motif placement.

New thinking is also revolutionising an indispensable tool. “A traditional backstrap loom is constraining and not comfortable for weavers who work on it for a prolonged time; it affects their health,” says Dolma. “We are working with the Jigme Namgyel Engineering College to semi-mechanise the loom to ease their workload.”

Several initiatives run by the RTA, including the annual National Design and Arts Competition and community programmes such as livelihood skills training, also support the thagzo craft.

“Sometimes, [rural artisans] weave for months, but when their textiles can’t be sold, it’s discouraging,” Dolma says. “So, we buy it from them. If their textiles are good, we sell them in our gift shop. Otherwise, we transform them into pencil bags or small souvenirs, and we give them to the students who visit us.”

Dolma and the RTA are not alone in shining a light on the plight of hand-woven arts. Independent labels such as She Bhutan – the brainchild of self-taught designer Gaymit Sada Lepcha – are also presenting thagzo through a contemporary lens.

“It started as a celebration of women through fashion and stories,” says Lepcha, who launched She Bhutan in 2013. “I wanted to do something for women, support women artisans and work closely with them.”

Her vision: jackets, coats and capes that blend modern silhouettes and fashion sensibilities with yathra, a colourful weave native to Bumthang district, in central Bhutan.

Made of sheep’s wool and yak’s hair, yathra is historically woven into blankets, throws, rugs, outerwear and scarves – dense, practical items suited to higher altitudes, long winters and punishing temperatures – using the thrithag horizontal-frame loom, a mechanism introduced to Bhutan in the early 20th century. Bold, geometric motifs make up the distinctive yathra weave, and producing just one panel of fabric, around 63.5cm in width and 2.5 metres in length, can take up to two weeks.

Lepcha, who was born in Samtse, a small town in southern Bhutan, had no background in thagzo or design, so getting She Bhutan off the ground was a leap of faith.

“Initially, it was difficult to find manpower,” she says. “Most of the women knew only how to weave traditional gho and kira. I needed people who were familiar with pattern-making for Western garments.”

Eventually, she established a rapport with a yathra specialist and a community of weavers in Bumthang who could apply their traditional skills to her modern designs. Using yathra as the “hero element”, Lepcha creates patterns and swatches for her craftspeople to follow. After the panels of fabric are woven, a seamstress stitches them together to create the final product.

“Before I started combining yathra with modern jackets, people didn’t think they could wear this textile in a different shape or form, because it’s so old and traditional,” Lepcha says. “Nobody had done it before.”

But Covid-19 threw a spanner in the works and She Bhutan was forced to go on hiatus.

In October 2025, when Lepcha felt ready to return, help came from an unexpected quarter: the first-ever Bhutan Fashion Week, a government-supported event celebrating the kingdom’s textile heritage. Twenty designers and labels presented their collections; Lepcha used the opportunity and the national platform to mount She Bhutan’s comeback. In January, the brand went back to bricks and mortar, opening a boutique in downtown Thimphu, to bring the Bumthang art form to the high street.

“Preserving heritage art doesn’t mean doing things in only the strictest, most traditional way. Preservation happens when an old art form is given new life,” she says. “By adding a contemporary touch to yathra, it is kept alive because people continue to wear it.”

With all this craftsmanship in the air, it was perhaps inevitable that other practices not previously undertaken in the kingdom have entered Bhutan. In 2019, the Bhutan Wine Company brought Western vinification to the small town of Yusipang. It resulted in all 21 lots of the inaugural 2023 vintage being auctioned at Bonhams in April last year for a total of US$74,250 – an unheard-of price for an upstart wine label – expanding alcohol-making possibilities beyond the home-brewing tradition of ara, Bhutan’s answer to moonshine.

Coffee culture is also knocking on Bhutan’s door. Home-grown bean farms and roasters such as Bhutan Mountain Coffee and independent cafes, like Ambient Cafe in Thimphu and CCH2O Cafe in Bumthang’s Ura valley, are finding footholds in a country traditionally married to butter tea (suja). Most of these are entrepreneurial endeavours started by returnees who have been influenced by their time abroad.

Initiatives such as the Voluntary Artists Studio, Thimphu, aka VAST Bhutan, a non-profit organisation supporting contemporary art, creativity and innovation, and the Kabjisa artist retreat-cum-art village, led by Asha Kama Wangdi, a co-founder of VAST Bhutan and one of the kingdom’s most respected modern artists, are part of a movement to nurture “a uniquely Bhutanese voice in contemporary art”.

Choden notes opportunities for zorig chusum to flourish among this proliferation of old-meets-new ingenuity: “We’re seeing tourists – who used to come to Bhutan for sightseeing – increasingly looking for authentic and immersive experiences. Many visitors now want to understand our culture more deeply and connect with local communities, so there’s a growing interest in Bhutanese crafts. It reflects a broader global trend of travellers seeking meaningful, culturally enriching moments.”

Attention, however, can be a mixed blessing. “When tourism grows, there is pressure to produce more and adapt to market demands. We already see it happening in Lhuentse [district], with kishuthara textiles. With the demand of kishuthara exceeding what the weavers can actually produce, there are machine-made kira being sold, [diluting] the authenticity of kishuthara and the hard work that goes into it,” says Choden.

Commercialisation and commodification are notable concerns, too. “We’re not in the business of arts and culture; we don’t want to sell our arts and culture,” says Khampa. “Bhutanese arts and crafts are deeply rooted in the spiritual teachings of Buddhism. A master craftsman can become an enlightened being in that field because they have practised it, they have lived that meaning of art. But if you are learning a skill just to sell it, you’ll never understand it. You would only understand markets, money, customers, challenges.

“If you are not living the art and the culture, you are not being true to yourself.”

Cultural conservation is proving to be a struggle between generations as well.

“Like any other country, young people are focused on digital progress and pursuing further education,” says Choden. “In western Bhutan, we have more job opportunities, so they leave their villages and towns in the east.” As a result, ageing generations are unable to pass down their know-how.

“Bhutan is known for living the culture, breathing the culture. With a younger generation less likely to pursue traditional arts and crafts as a profession, it diminishes our rich cultural heritage. When a master artisan passes away, we will have difficulty reviving this craft.”

Khampa says he believes in the evolution of arts and culture: “We don’t want it to be frozen in time. We want it to flow like a river, rather than be like a reservoir. But to make people understand, to live that particular arts and culture, we need to speak their language. We don’t want them to forget their culture.”

It’s true, Bhutan is changing. For those who romanticise the Buddhist kingdom in the Himalayan mountains as a mystical Shangri-La whose appeal is intrinsically tied to its “entrenched in the past” way of life, it’s time to lose the rose-tinted glasses. But while there are practical and ideological obstacles to overcome, Bhutan’s quest for modernity hasn’t taken the shine off its national cultural heritage. If anything, it has added fuel to the fire of legacy preservation, because to understand the Bhutan of 2026, it must still be experienced through its folk arts and crafts.

People such as Choden, who promote Bhutan to the world, are keenly aware of the tightrope they must walk between the loss of identity and being left in the dust.

“We cannot compete with machines in terms of production but we can make people understand that whatever a machine can produce, hands can do it far better the traditional way. We need to blend modernity and tradition so one does not overpower the other. We need to document the traditional techniques and raise awareness among locals and tourists about the value of authentic handmade products so they can cherish them.”

When it comes to the fate of zorig chusum, Khampa takes a guarded view: “It all depends on the people, if they are committed to continuing to live that art and culture. But we are optimistic that whatever we are doing right now [to educate and promote] will have a bearing on the future passion of Bhutanese people.”

Kinga Tshering feels hopeful about the path that lies ahead for lhazo. “Now, there are a lot more painters, compared with the last 10 years. It is going to increase again; because of schools like Zorig Chusum, anyone can learn to paint. People get to learn about the religion that they practise and there is work available, so they can see a future in this kind of art.”

Lepcha sees modernisation as motivation: “It gives me encouragement to preserve our textiles and our culture because I don’t want them to vanish into thin air someday; the more modern we become, the more I want to preserve thagzo. But we can do a lot more than just preserve; we can modernise and make tradition relevant to people today.”

item.title: [SCMP] Typhoon Bavi: Shanghai, Hangzhou brace for impact as nearby cities start evacuations
item.description: Shanghai raised its local typhoon warning on Saturday afternoon as eastern China braced for the arrival of Typhoon Bavi, cancelling flights and ferry services and evacuating thousands of people. As of 6pm, Shanghai transport authorities had limited the speed on the overground sections of four subway lines, and warned they might impose further limits or partially suspend services on the rest of the network on Sunday and Monday. The city government has also imposed a 60km/h (35mph) speed limit on
item.content:encoded:
Shanghai raised its local typhoon warning on Saturday afternoon as eastern China braced for the arrival of Typhoon Bavi, cancelling flights and ferry services and evacuating thousands of people.

As of 6pm, Shanghai transport authorities had limited the speed on the overground sections of four subway lines, and warned they might impose further limits or partially suspend services on the rest of the network on Sunday and Monday.

The city government has also imposed a 60km/h (35mph) speed limit on all highways and cancelled all ferry services.

Community workers in Shanghai patrolled the streets, using loudspeakers to remind residents to close windows and doors and secure objects indoors after the city’s typhoon warning was raised from blue to yellow, the third highest level in a four-tier system.

Dozens of events were cancelled, including a fireworks display in the Pudong district and a tourism festival along the banks of the Suzhou River.

Shanghai Disney Resort has pushed back its opening time on Sunday from 10am to 12pm, according to a statement.

The municipal government said China Eastern Airlines and Shanghai Airlines had cancelled 188 flights serving airports in Shanghai and Zhejiang, Fujian and Jiangxi provinces. Juneyao Airlines cancelled 116 domestic and international flights on Saturday, with further cancellations expected on Sunday.

China Southern Airlines has cancelled 43 flights to or from Shanghai, while Spring Airlines has cancelled 16.

The neighbouring province of Zhejiang raised its typhoon emergency preparations to the highest level, which allows it to relocate large numbers of people, close schools and workplaces, and mobilise all resources needed.

In Zhejiang’s capital of Hangzhou, the 35.7km (over 22-mile) Hangzhou Bay Bridge sea crossing was closed to traffic from 7.30pm on Saturday, while subway authorities said all services would be suspended from 6am on Sunday until further notice.

Railway authorities in the province have suspended 29 high-speed trains and nine other services, while temporary stoppages are planned on several other lines.

Cangnan county, a possible landing point for the typhoon in Zhejiang’s Wenzhou city, has relocated 150,000 people to safety and set up temporary shelters in local schools.

Sanmen county in Taizhou city, another place where the typhoon may land, has stopped all outdoor activities and construction work, and closed down schools, tourist sites and public transport. It has also advised local residents not to leave their homes unless necessary.

In Yuhuan, a coastal city between the two landing points, local media posted footage online of heavy rain and waves more than 10 metres (33 feet) high caused by high winds as the storm approached.

Zheng Siyu, a resident of Yueching on the Zhejiang coast, said the wind and rain was already “very heavy” on Saturday afternoon and only a few cars and motorbikes could be seen on the roads.

“I have experienced typhoons before but I’m still very worried,” Zheng said, adding that she was stocking up on food and water and had charged her electronic devices in preparation. She has also moved her car to higher ground in case of flooding.

She added that the neighbourhood authorities had been pruning trees to lower the risk of falling branches and had made all parking free.

In Hong Kong, over 130 flights to and from Taiwan, mainland China and Japan have been cancelled while 10 high-speed rail services have also been axed.

In its 10am bulletin on Saturday, the mainland National Meteorological Centre (NMC) kept an orange typhoon warning in place, while upgrading the rainstorm alert to red – the highest level in the four-tier system and the first such warning this year.

Taiwan bore the brunt of the storm on Saturday, with hundreds of flights and ferry services being cancelled. More than 14,000 people were forced to evacuate, over 150,000 households were left without power and 36 people were injured.

Taiwan Taoyuan International Airport cancelled 913 flights over two days, including 760 on Saturday. Airlines were expected to gradually resume services after 4am on Sunday as the typhoon moved away.

The cancelled services included flights to Hong Kong, Singapore, Tokyo, Shenzhen, Bangkok, Osaka, Ho Chi Minh City, San Francisco, Vancouver, Milan, Paris, Los Angeles and Amsterdam.

Ferry services were also hit, with 116 sailings on 13 routes suspended on Saturday. Maritime authorities urged people to avoid travelling to Taiwan’s outlying islands in case they became stranded.

Taiwan’s weather authorities said the greatest threat would last until Saturday evening as the centre of the typhoon passed closest to the island and brought the strongest winds and heaviest rainfall.

The sea and land warnings could be lifted as early as Sunday morning as the storm moved towards the Chinese mainland, the Central Weather Administration said.

Mountainous areas of northern and central Taiwan were warned of torrential rain, while low-lying areas faced a risk from landslides, rockfalls and flash floods.

At least 36 people were injured, mostly in scooter accidents caused by strong winds or slippery roads, or after falling or being struck by debris while preparing for the storm. No deaths or serious injuries were reported.

More than 14,200 people had been evacuated, with Hualien, Taichung and Kaohsiung the worst-affected areas.

The Taiwan Power Company said it had mobilised around 16,000 personnel for repairs after over 150,000 households were hit by power cuts, and had restored supplies to more than 110,000 of those affected.

Despite the severe weather, more than 1,000 Pokemon Go players gathered at Taipei Main Station for the game’s global annual event after outdoor festivities planned in the capital were suspended because of the typhoon.

Hundreds of players also crowded inside and around Central Park metro station in Kaohsiung, braving the wind and rain to join Pokemon Go group raids.

Taiwan’s military placed more than 20,000 personnel on disaster-response standby, with around 2,000 vehicles and pieces of equipment prepared for rescue operations. Amphibious assault vehicles and inflatable rescue boats were pre-positioned in Suao, Yilan county, one of the areas most exposed to the storm.

As of 5pm, the centre of the storm, a severe typhoon with maximum sustained winds of 40 metres per second (Force 13), was located about 165km off Wenling city of Zhejiang, moving northwest at 30 km/h towards southern Zhejiang.

The storm is now forecast to make landfall in Zhejiang late Saturday night or early Sunday morning, with winds of 33 to 40 metres per second (Force 12 to 13), slightly less than earlier forecast. It will then move to the northwest and gradually weaken.

The red rainstorm warning, effective between 2pm on Saturday and 2pm Sunday, forecasts torrential downpours of up to 800mm (31 inches) in parts of eastern Zhejiang, northeastern Fujian and north-central Taiwan over the same 24-hour period.

Elsewhere, rainfall of 250 to 500mm is expected in eastern and southern Zhejiang and northern Fujian, and 250 to 280mm in parts of Beijing and Hebei.

Gale-force winds of Force 9 to 11, with gusts of up to Force 12 to 13, are expected across coastal areas from Shanghai to Fujian province, as well as the East China Sea. Winds of Force 12 to 13, plus gusts of up to Force 14 to 16, are expected at the typhoon’s centre.

In its 6am bulletin on Saturday, the NMC said Bavi had regained strength – reaching severe typhoon status at midnight.

Other areas of mainland China have also stepped up their emergency preparations, mobilising emergency response teams – including firefighters and crews to repair power grids – and volunteers.

Fujian raised its typhoon emergency response to Level 2 on Friday afternoon, ordering all schools and training institutions to halt outdoor activities and construction work.

In the southern province of Guangdong, 21 passenger ferries and 117 construction vessels have been taken out of service, with 108 workers evacuated from offshore wind platforms as of 2pm on Friday.

The storm has also forced shows and concerts to be cancelled in affected areas. The Hong Kong singer G.E.M. cancelled three concerts in Hangzhou, while Taiwanese singer Jolin Tsai has delayed Saturday’s show in Nanchang in Jiangxi province until Monday.

On Friday, Premier Li Qiang chaired an executive meeting of the State Council to coordinate flood-control and disaster-relief efforts.

It called on local authorities to carry out inspections for hidden risks, step up checks on vulnerable dykes and rivers where the waters had passed warning levels, and maintain strict oversight of dangerous reservoirs.

Authorities were told to improve forecast accuracy, issue timely “call-and-response” warnings and evacuate people in high-risk areas – especially vulnerable groups – when danger loomed.

The meeting also called on all regions and departments to uphold their political responsibilities, prepare for the worst-case scenario and guard against complacency.

In 2021, the impact of typhoons reached inland as far as the landlocked central province of Henan, causing extreme rainfall and floods that killed almost 400 people.

item.title: [SCMP] Hong Kong and Shenzhen the perfect end points for modern Silk Road: leading economist
item.description: Prominent economist Jeffrey Sachs says he chose Hong Kong and Shenzhen as the final stop on an overland journey retracing the footsteps of Marco Polo because of their significant role in powering the world’s green and digital age. The Columbia University professor set off from Rome on June 13 for a 43-day, 15,000km (9,300-mile) journey, the Marco Polo Drive of Peace, Culture and Sustainable Development. The former adviser to the UN secretary general said the journey was designed to send a messag
item.content:encoded:
Prominent economist Jeffrey Sachs says he chose Hong Kong and Shenzhen as the final stop on an overland journey retracing the footsteps of Marco Polo because of their significant role in powering the world’s green and digital age.

The Columbia University professor set off from Rome on June 13 for a 43-day, 15,000km (9,300-mile) journey, the Marco Polo Drive of Peace, Culture and Sustainable Development.

The former adviser to the UN secretary general said the journey was designed to send a message of peace, connectivity and sustainable development through green technology, as well as to highlight the importance of the modern equivalent of the Silk Road.

While the eastern end of the ancient Silk Road was Xian in northwest China, Sachs said Hong Kong and Shenzhen now played that role.

“Shenzhen and Hong Kong are our final stops because together they form the world’s greatest innovation cluster – the one that will power the world’s green and digital age,” he said in an email.

“If Venice was the western gateway from which Marco Polo set out, Hong Kong and Shenzhen are the eastern gateway of our own time: the great innovators and interconnectors of East and West, North and South.”

He said the Hong Kong also served as a connecting point between the overland Silk Road and the maritime Silk Road.

“There is a deep historical resonance as well. In the era of Marco Polo and after, the Yuan and Ming dynasties were the world’s greatest seafarers of their age – Zheng He’s astounding voyages reached the coasts of Arabia and East Africa decades before the Europeans rounded the Cape of Good Hope,” he said.

“The maritime Silk Road began on these shores. So ending in Hong Kong brings us from the overland Silk Road to the ancient and modern maritime one, and from the age of caravans to the age of green and digital technology.”

The expedition commemorates the 750th anniversary of the Venetian merchant’s arrival at the court of Kublai Khan. Polo then spent the next 17 years at court and travelling around China before returning to Venice.

His account of his travels gave Western audiences their first in-depth insight into the country and has become a symbol for centuries of cultural and commercial exchanges between East and West.

Sachs is travelling with his wife Sonia, Patrick Zhong, founding managing partner of private equity firm M31 Capital, and Zhong’s son Jimmy.

They are travelling in a vehicle provided by the Chinese electric car giant BYD. Sachs said green infrastructure was improving far more rapidly than commonly understood, with charging stations available along the entire route.

“The route is very comfortable and welcoming. It is only needless wars and bloc politics that threaten to sever these ancient connections. Our journey demonstrates that the corridor is open, safe, and full of promise,” he said.

In a post on his website last month, Sachs wrote that he had set off from Rome, rather than Polo’s native city of Venice, because “like the Polos before us, we are carrying a message from the Pope, in our case Pope Leo XIV’s new encyclical [official proclamation], Magnifica Humanitas, a plea for peace and for the wise and humane use of our powerful new technologies”.

“The journey seeks to carry forward a mission of friendship, mutual understanding, and peaceful coexistence at a time when conflict, mistrust, and division continue to shape many parts of the world,” a brochure about the trip said.

Sachs travelled through Croatia, Serbia, Bulgaria, Turkey, Georgia, Azerbaijan and Kazakhstan before reaching Uzbekistan last week. He will cross into China through the Khorgos land port in Xinjiang around July 15.

The travellers will then pass through several major mainland Chinese cities, including Urumqi, Xian, Suzhou, Shanghai and Shenzhen, before reaching Hong Kong around July 26.

The brochure said several governments along the route had extended a warm welcome, including meetings with the presidents of Croatia, Slovenia and Serbia and the prime minister of Georgia. Sachs is also expected to meet a mainland Chinese government minister and the chief executive of Hong Kong, John Lee Ka-chiu.

Upon reaching Uzbekistan, Sachs described the journey as “a wonderful, fascinating trip”.

Sachs is also using the journey to hit out at US foreign policy and the belief that “the civilisations of East and West are fated to clash rather than cooperate”.

“Marco Polo knew better. At the far end of the longest road to the East, he found not enemies but a brilliant, sophisticated civilisation eager to trade ideas as readily as goods,” he wrote on his website on June 19.

In a second “postcard” posted on Tuesday from Khiva, Uzbekistan, Sachs wrote that as the United States marked its 250th anniversary, it continued to antagonise ancient civilisations such as Iran – a nation whose “statecraft and poetry and science reach back some 10 times longer than America’s own short national life.”

He added: “There is a wisdom in the old city walls and ancient madrasas of Khiva that Washington would do well to relearn: that great and ancient nations are not to be bullied into submission but should be met in trade as equals.”

He also described the overland journey as a testament to human unity, saying it was designed to celebrate “the long, patient, borderless collaboration of human minds across every frontier of nations, faiths and languages”.

The comments echoed a pre-departure interview Sachs gave to the Italian newspaper Il Fatto Quotidiano, where he called for a multipolar world.

“No one should be viewed intrinsically as an enemy,” Sachs said. “For every other power, there are issues that are real. Security issues come first. Economic issues come next.”

He urged world powers to adopt a standard of “indivisible security” where states accommodate rather than dismiss the core concerns of rivals.

“Multipolarity means not only the reality of multiple power centres, but a different kind of geopolitics, where every power centre relates in a prudential way with every other power centre,” he said. “We respect your red lines. You respect our red lines. We discuss. We make what used to be called indivisible security.”

He also restated his long-standing view that Europe should engage with Russia independently rather than following the US lead.

“Europe, as Europe, should be having diplomacy with Russia as Russia,” Sachs told the publication. “In other words, not hugging [Ukrainian President Volodymyr] Zelensky for the 10,000th time, but sitting down with [Russian] President [Vladimir] Putin for the first time in years.”

item.title: [SCMP] Southeast Asia’s scammers’ new disguise: your leader’s face
item.description: There is a bogus version of Singapore’s prime minister that exists only to steal money. It connected to a Zoom call, thanked a stranger for his time and helped relieve him of US$3.8 million – with the real Lawrence Wong, for a time, none the wiser about what had happened. The victim had received a WhatsApp message purporting to be from the secretary to the cabinet, inviting him to a private meeting. What followed was not some doctored photo or a 15-second clip designed to fool a distracted dooms
item.content:encoded:
There is a bogus version of Singapore’s prime minister that exists only to steal money. It connected to a Zoom call, thanked a stranger for his time and helped relieve him of US$3.8 million – with the real Lawrence Wong, for a time, none the wiser about what had happened.

The victim had received a WhatsApp message purporting to be from the secretary to the cabinet, inviting him to a private meeting.

What followed was not some doctored photo or a 15-second clip designed to fool a distracted doomscroll. It was an entire fabricated summit, complete with assembled officials including Wong, President Tharman Shanmugaratnam, cabinet minister Indranee Rajah and foreign dignitaries, all rendered convincingly enough to believe they were discussing funding tied to the Strait of Hormuz.

The synthetic Wong closed the meeting by thanking the victim for attending. Then a fake lawyer took the call and S$4.9 million (US$3.8 million) vanished into an account that no longer exists.

Southeast Asia’s scam industry has found a powerful new weapon, and it isn’t malware, cunning or even greed – it’s borrowed faces.

Criminals are increasingly cloning the region’s most trusted leaders using generative AI, wrapping fraud in the unimpeachable authority of a president’s voice or a prime minister’s visage and cashing in on the thing no security system can patch: human trust.

In Indonesia, a similar con surfaced within days of Prabowo Subianto’s 2024 election win, when an Instagram clip began circulating in which he appeared to ask viewers: “Who hasn’t received aid from me? What are your needs right now?”

The incoming president had said no such thing. But the voice was his, the face was his and for many, that was enough. Across 20 provinces, an unknown number of Indonesians each wired scammers “administrative fees” of up to 1 million rupiah (US$55), chasing government aid that was never coming and never had been.

In the Philippines, the fraud wore Ferdinand Marcos Jnr’s face, superimposed onto footage endorsing an online trading scheme with a polish good enough to convince one dentist to hand over 93 million pesos (US$1.5 million) before the illusion broke.

Three countries. Three cons. One unsettling realisation: artificial intelligence has stopped merely helping scammers write better emails. It has started manufacturing their credibility from scratch.

“If you’re not trained to look at it and you don’t have the discernment to know that it’s impossible for the president to be endorsing investment schemes, you will believe it,” said Art Samaniego, a Manila-based tech journalist and co-founder of ScamWatch Pilipinas. “Because you can clearly see the president’s face and he appears to be speaking.”

Old con, new face

An Interpol report released in June identified AI-driven crime as one of Southeast Asia’s most urgent emerging threats, warning of criminal networks that are growing leaner and more organised as technology races ahead of regulation.

From January 2024 to March 2025, it found “phishing” attacks – where scammers send fake emails, messages, or links to trick victims into sharing sensitive data – and related swindles were the costliest form of cybercrime across Asia and the South Pacific, with a third of countries logging more than 10,000 cases apiece.

“These include the use of deepfake videos to impersonate public figures, celebrities, or business executives,” it said. “Such schemes have resulted in significant financial losses and have targeted both individuals and institutions.”

On criminal forums and Telegram channels, chatter about deepfakes surged 600 per cent in just one five-month period in 2024, the report found.

But strip away the technology and the con itself is nothing new. All the AI does, experts say, is play on the same old human weaknesses – fear, hope, desperation, deference to power – but with a more believable face.

“Many people are raised to respect authority,” said Jun Angelo Suglao, a clinical psychologist who chairs the Psychological Association of the Philippines’ mental health support group.

“So if someone calls and can impersonate someone we inherently perceive as good or trustworthy, our default instinct is to comply, not to doubt them.”

The elderly made for some of the easiest targets, Samaniego said, as many had been conditioned over long lifetimes to trust a phone call at face value.

But no one is immune from scammers, especially as the technology becomes more advanced. Voice cloning adds a crueller dimension still, mimicking the reassuring tones – or panicked cries – of a loved one.

“Hearing someone you care for cry for help triggers an immediate fight-or-flight response, completely shutting down your rational thinking,” Suglao said. “You act on pure emotion.”

Nobody understands that better than a Filipino domestic worker identified only as Maria, who reportedly believed she had found love with a Dubai prince on a dating app.

He wrote constantly. He placed video calls, warm and attentive, present in every way that mattered. But he was never real.

By the time Maria saw through the facade, she had sent 100,000 pesos – a year’s wages – for a “marriage certificate” and a fictitious “royal membership card” meant to secure her a job in Dubai, Agence France-Presse reported earlier this month.

“They prey on isolated individuals,” Suglao said of the scammers. “Slowly building a deep emotional connection with the goal of scamming them out of money.”

AI has not changed the criminals’ cruelty. It has simply given it a face worth falling for.

Tricking the experts

Jeremy Douglas, deputy director of operations at the UN Office on Drugs and Crime, confirmed to This Week in Asia that AI and other tech tools were increasingly being integrated into scam operations across Southeast Asia and further afield.

“It’s classic organised crime behaviour, innovating to accelerate business and stay ahead of law enforcement,” he said. “The use of deepfake images and videos, and mimicking or copying voice intonation and patterns of partners and contacts are on the rise and we can expect more sophisticated use going forward.”

Neal Jetton, who leads Interpol’s cybercrime directorate, pointed to estimates that global cybercrime losses hit US$10.5 trillion in 2025, with a 142 per cent rise forecast by the end of this decade.

“Cybercrime exists wherever anyone is using a smartphone or laptop … it is a global problem,” Jetton said, adding that AI had become “one of the main drivers”.

What makes the scams so effective, according to Interpol, is their reliance on social engineering techniques, exploiting human trust rather than technical vulnerabilities.

This has transformed so-called romance baiting into a booming subsector all of its own, part of a cyberfraud economy the UN Office on Drugs and Crime estimates has cost the world up to US$37 billion and counting.

The method rarely changes: weeks or months of patient, tender contact, before the big ask arrives.

Analysts are careful not to overstate the novelty, however. AI has not built a new criminal underworld, it has retooled an old one – at speed.

“These capabilities are enabling organised crime syndicates to radically change scam and associated money-laundering businesses,” Douglas said, adding that such operations were “professionalising, using fewer people to run or perpetuate scams” and sometimes switching jurisdictions or shifting from the notorious large scam compounds “into a model of networked locations”.

Samaniego has watched and reported on the retooling happening in real time.

“Before, if an email had wrong grammar, you could automatically tell it was a scam,” he said. “Now you can no longer identify it that way, because their grammar has become so good, because they’re using AI to create fake links and fake websites.”

“AI is not replacing cybercriminals, it’s making them more efficient … Their efficiency has doubled or tripled.”

AI is not replacing cybercriminals, it’s making them more efficient

Art Samaniego, ScamWatch Pilipinas

Even seasoned professionals occasionally fall foul of the scammers’ tactics.

Jetton recalled hearing of a cybercrime expert who clicked on a fraudulent email disguised as an Uber receipt, simply because he had recently used the e-hailing app.

“Awareness campaigns are helping, but we’re still seeing the human element – desperation, need, naivety. And the phishing emails are so good now,” he said, citing a Microsoft report that found a human was 4½ times more likely to click on a phishing email created by AI.

John Wojcik, senior threat researcher for Asia at cybersecurity firm Infoblox, said there had been “rapid acceleration” in the kinds of AI tools that scammers were using.

“From deepfakes, lifelike chatbots and complex, nuanced machine translation, to automated scam outreach, domain registration and phishing attacks, it’s clear this technology is being rolled out at scale within this criminal ecosystem,” he said, fed by a surge in “malware-as-a-service” kits sold on the dark web.

Doubting what you see

There is no silver bullet solution, Jetton concedes – only what he calls a “whole-of-society effort”.

And as AI-generated content edges towards the indistinguishable, old methods of detecting fakes, such as listening for a stilted phrase or watching for an unnatural blink, are fast losing relevance.

“As AI reaches human-level output, traditional visual or audio cues are becoming unreliable,” Samaniego said.

The only defence that still held, he said, was verification through a separate channel entirely.

“If you receive an urgent or high-stakes request, even if it looks or sounds exactly like someone you know, do not respond to the message or caller directly,” Samaniego said.

“If someone claiming to be your friend asks for money on social media, call him. If he texts, use other channels. Best if you can verify in person.”

His best advice for countering the scammers? Forcing them “off script”.

“AI models and scam scripts are excellent at handling expected flows but often falter when faced with unpredictable, human-centric interruptions,” he said. “Ask a question that other people won’t know the answer to.”

Suglao’s solution, meanwhile, was more community-focused.

“If a suspicious investment opportunity or a sudden emergency request comes up, run it by a trusted peer first,” he said. “Talking it out disrupts the high-pressure environment the scammer relies on.”

For ScamWatch Pilipinas’ Samaniego, pre-emptive measures such as strong cyber literacy are the best defence.

“Hearing or seeing someone talk online is no longer sufficient proof that what you’re seeing is genuine or real,” he said. “Because of AI, we all need new ways to verify identity.”

What his cybersecurity advocacy movement wanted, Samaniego said, was for people to already be educated “before the scam occurs” – so they never had the misfortune to become victims in the first place.

item.title: [SCMP] US opens door for Turkey’s return to F-35 stealth jet programme – but Israel’s not happy
item.description: The United States is set to resume military sales to Turkey, Nato’s second-largest military power, after President Donald Trump said sanctions imposed against Ankara over its procurement of Russian air defence systems seven years ago would soon be lifted. “We don’t want to sanction friends,” Trump told journalists at the Nato summit in the Turkish capital on Wednesday, while emphasising his “good chemistry” with President Recep Tayyip Erdogan. Trump further said he would soon take a final decisi
item.content:encoded:
The United States is set to resume military sales to Turkey, Nato’s second-largest military power, after President Donald Trump said sanctions imposed against Ankara over its procurement of Russian air defence systems seven years ago would soon be lifted.

“We don’t want to sanction friends,” Trump told journalists at the Nato summit in the Turkish capital on Wednesday, while emphasising his “good chemistry” with President Recep Tayyip Erdogan.

Trump further said he would soon take a final decision on restoring Turkey’s participation in the Joint Strike Fighter (JSF) programme – raising expectations among observers of a “face-saving” deal in which Ankara would transfer its Russian S-400 systems to a third country in return for US F-35 stealth warplanes.

Turkey was ejected from the F-35 programme – for which it was manufacturing some components – in 2019 over US concerns that the cutting-edge jet’s unrivalled stealth capabilities could be compromised by its exposure to Russian technology.

The understanding reached between Washington and Ankara “is a strategic reset in spirit but not yet in practical terms”, said Asli Aydintasbas, director of the Turkey project at the Brookings Institution in Washington. “Clearly, President Trump values his relationship with Erdogan and wants Turkey to be a central player in his Nato, Europe and Middle East policies.”

After years of turmoil, “this is the closest Erdogan has come to the reset with Washington he has been seeking” – but it is “not full normalisation yet”, she said. “The congressional part of the equation is still not there.”

While Trump can use his executive power to waive sanctions imposed on Turkey’s defence production industry under the Countering America’s Adversaries Through Sanctions Act (Caatsa), Congress must be convinced that the threat posed by the Russian S-400s has been removed before its access to the JSF can be reinstated.

For Erdogan, the lifting of Caatsa sanctions, movement on engines for the Kaan fighter, and the prospect of returning to the F-35 conversation “all signal that Turkey is being brought back into the Western defence-industrial fold after years in the cold”, Aydintasbas said. “It strengthens his argument that Turkey is an indispensable ally with a direct line to Trump.”

Israeli factor

Nonetheless, Turkey’s acquisition of the F-35 is bound to face fierce opposition from Israel’s many supporters in Congress.

In the build-up to the Nato summit, Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu made multiple appearances on US television to voice his opposition to the lifting of sanctions and the F-35 sale to Turkey.

With Trump saying he had “an obligation” to supply Turkey with up to 80 US-built F110 engines for its indigenous Kaan stealth fighters, Netanyahu complained that Israel’s air force could lose its technological superiority over rivals in the Middle East.

“Everybody understands that notwithstanding the personal friendship that President Trump has with Erdogan, it doesn’t make Turkey a friendly state to the United States,” Netanyahu said on CNN on Tuesday. “To the contrary … he threatens to destroy my country!”

Washington is legally bound, under the Naval Vessel Transfer Act of 2008, to ensure that any arms sale in the Middle East does not erode Israel’s military edge over its neighbours.

The US president responded to Netanyahu’s claims in Wednesday’s press conference, saying: “I hear some people saying about their relationship with Israel.”

If Turkey had wanted to clash with Israel, it “could have gotten into the fight on the other side”, Trump said, describing the Nato member as ‘a very powerful military nation”.

“Maybe they didn’t do that because of me,” he added.

At no point during the Middle East’s conflicts since October 2023 has war ever appeared likely between the two countries, despite exchanges of harsh rhetoric.

Israel is the “biggest impediment to a reset” of defence cooperation between US and Turkey, according to Aydintasbas.

The Israeli government “seems determined to use its access to Trump and US Congress” to prevent Turkey’s return to the F-35 programme, she said. “Israel wants to preserve its own military edge in the region and views Turkey as its top regional competitor.”

Both countries are locked in a rivalry for regional influence and view each other as expansionist.

“The larger issue is political and strategic,” she said, with Israel increasingly viewing Turkey not just as a critic of the Gaza war or its occupation of southwest Syria, but as a long-term regional competitor.

Turkey, for its part, sees Israel as “becoming too dominant after the Iran war and wants to prevent a regional order shaped entirely by Israeli military power”, Aydintasbas said.

Convincing Congress

However, former Pentagon official David Des Roches said Israel “is not the important issue” in determining Turkey’s participation in the JSF programme.

“The resistance will come from within the US government. It is very, very, very protective of the multi-hundred-billion-dollar investment we’ve made in stealth,” said Des Roches, a senior fellow at the Washington-based Gulf International Forum.

Having agreed last year to sell F-35s to Saudi Arabia, Trump also wanted to offer the JSF to the United Arab Emirates, but “he can’t do that because of our requirements to protect stealth”.

Similarly, Washington does not share Israel’s view that the acquisition of fifth-generation fighters by Turkey and other US partners in the region poses a threat.

“The American view is that if a region is able to defend itself, the US does not have to,” Des Roches said. “We do not regard any of our partners as countries that spread instability. We regard them as countries that enforce stability, that benefit from the rules-based order, and that benefit from free trade.”

Trump’s decision to bring Turkey back in from the cold was motivated by the need for Ankara’s cooperation against Russia in the Black Sea, and against Iran and its proxies in Syria and Iraq, according to Des Roches.

“Right now, Iran is the biggest issue in the region, and whatever beef the US has with Turkey is much, much smaller,” Des Roches said. All the same, Turkey would “have to get rid of the S-400s if it’s going to get the JSF”.

item.title: [SCMP] Who cares for Singapore’s carers? Recent deaths highlight urgent need to tackle burnout
item.description: When Karasi Chandramogan’s father died four years ago, the 35-year-old Singaporean was left with the overwhelming responsibility of being the sole carer for her brother, who has autism, and mother, an amputee. “Me and my dad were a tag team,” the freelance behavioural therapist said. “When he passed on, I couldn’t mourn too much because I had to handle the funeral situation as well as my mum and brother.” She recalled an incident in April, when her brother, Bala, suffered seizures during lunch.
item.content:encoded:
When Karasi Chandramogan’s father died four years ago, the 35-year-old Singaporean was left with the overwhelming responsibility of being the sole carer for her brother, who has autism, and mother, an amputee.

“Me and my dad were a tag team,” the freelance behavioural therapist said. “When he passed on, I couldn’t mourn too much because I had to handle the funeral situation as well as my mum and brother.”

She recalled an incident in April, when her brother, Bala, suffered seizures during lunch. “It was very scary for me to handle because I was terrified that he would choke. So I try not to be away for too long, check the CCTV regularly, I’m always on the lookout,” she said.

The recent sentencing of a man who had been the long-term carer for his 56-year-old brother before strangling him to death last year hit close to home for Chandramogan and other carers in Singapore, where families are taking on increasingly complex care needs amid a rapidly ageing population.

Abdul Rani Md Ariffin, 59, had been looking after his brother, who suffered from chronic illnesses and depression. A psychiatric assessment produced in court revealed that Abdul Rani was diagnosed with adjustment disorder, a depressed mood state and irritability, as well as carer stress. He also had difficulties regulating and expressing emotions.

Initially charged with murder, Abdul Rani was sentenced to eight years in jail after he pleaded guilty to a reduced charge of culpable homicide not amounting to murder.

Karasi said such cases affected her greatly. “I think most carers would resonate with such tragic cases, because we know it can get very isolating, and they must have been stretched way beyond the point of burnout.”

She used to blame herself if Bala, who has a history of epilepsy, suffered seizures. “I would always think that it was because I didn’t take care of him properly,” she said. “It’s taken me a few years to remind myself that these things are not in my control.”

Cases where carer burnout leads to death are, unfortunately, uncommon, and highlight the immense toll and demands placed on them.

In 2022, a father killed his 11-year-old twin sons who were diagnosed with autism as he believed it would take away his wife’s burdens. He made an attempt on his own life after strangling his sons.

Such situations are often a result of stress, isolation and a lack of timely support, and highlight the “invisible burden” as carers navigate complex or long-term conditions, said Tim Oei, chief executive of Mindfull Community, a non-profit which supports carers.

“Many carers continue to cope quietly, and distress may only surface at a crisis point.”

‘Barely coping’

The cases underscore the importance of carers’ well-being as the city state transitions to a super-aged society, according to social welfare agencies and experts. They urged early identification and support for carers, and noted that while Singapore had strengthened support in recent years, lack of accessibility and awareness posed challenges.

A 2022 study by the National Council of Social Service, which surveyed over 4,500 carers in Singapore, found that more than half of the respondents were “burdened” or “barely coping” with their caregiving responsibilities and felt they had less control over their lives.

Increased financial assistance, community care services and respite options demonstrated “growing recognition of the critical role” of carers, said Steven Yeo, executive director of Caregiving Welfare Association.

Under Singapore’s Home Caregiving Grant, eligible carers can receive monthly payouts of up to S$600 (US$465) – depending on per capita household income – which can be used to hire a helper or buy healthcare items. Carers can also apply for a grant which subsidises training courses.

Millie Su, a senior lecturer at the Singapore University of Social Sciences’ SR Nathan School of Human Development, said though help was available, many carers were not aware of the policies or found the services inaccessible, she said.

She pointed to the study, which revealed that fewer than three in 10 carers used support services.

Many had difficulties navigating paperwork and applications when applying for subsidies, grants and nursing homes, she said, urging the streamlining of such processes. “Some are being ping-ponged between different agencies, and making the system more integrated and connected will make care more accessible.”

Carers should be identified early for timely support to be given, she added. “As long as someone is taking care of someone who has a chronic condition, they are vulnerable, and we’ve been ignoring this because there’s so much focus on patient care.”

As Singapore becomes a super-aged society, carer support is even more critical, experts say. The city state crossed the threshold of being super-aged this year, with more than 21 per cent of its population now aged 65 or above.

“We are seeing more and more aged couples living on their own and looking after each other, both as frail as the other,” said Oei, urging holistic and sustained support, including expanded access to respite care, and strengthened coordination across healthcare and community services.

Healthcare professionals should also be trained to identify informal carers to provide relevant information, resources and support, she added.

Yeo urged employers to foster carer-friendly workplaces through flexible work arrangements and greater understanding. “Ultimately, caregiving should never rest solely on one individual’s shoulders. Preventing such situations requires a whole-of-society approach,” he said.

Overcoming guilt

A decade ago, Edgar Tang had to step into the role as a carer for his parents after his father’s Parkinson’s disease worsened rapidly, leaving him wheelchair-bound.

“There were times I was breaking down due to work and these duties, and I felt like both ends of the candle were burning out on me,” said Tang, 50, whose mother is diagnosed with a mental condition.

He was eventually forced to move his father into a nursing home, a decision which tore him up with guilt. “It was really tough because I felt like such a horrible child,” he said. “How could I have put my father in a nursing home?”

Filial piety and a stigma associated with seeking help to care for family members – values prevalent in Asian societies such as Singapore – may also prevent carers from seeking timely support.

“We tend to not talk about it and think that caregiving is a family matter, a show of filial piety, a duty and a responsibility, and this cultural norm may prevent people from seeking help,” Su said.

Many didn’t recognise themselves as a carer in need of support. “They simply see themselves as a spouse, son, daughter or sibling doing what they should, and therefore delay seeking help until they are already overwhelmed,” Yeo said.

Tang managed to rationalise his emotions after attending a get-together session for carers organised by a welfare group. “I realised that many are feeling these emotions and it’s OK to feel this way,” he said.

His father also started to walk again in the nursing home – a development which Tang hailed as a “miracle”.

As Tang’s physical and mental health improved, he was able to be fully present for his father, allowing him to shed the guilt. “Now I’m not turning up exhausted. I’m present and we chat, walk, draw our memories, do some writing and listen to music,” he said.

item.title: [SCMP] In Malaysia’s Johor, Chinese voters prize affordability over politics
item.description: Koo can see Johor changing around him, even if the promised wealth has not reached his bank account. The 30-year-old service worker has lived for 25 years in Skudai, a busy Johor Bahru suburb anchored by universities, older housing estates and the kind of Chinese shoplot economy that helped make the Democratic Action Party (DAP) a force in Malaysia’s southern state. He sees cranes, rail works and projects built by Chinese companies. But before Saturday’s state election, and as the China-linked b
item.content:encoded:
Koo can see Johor changing around him, even if the promised wealth has not reached his bank account.

The 30-year-old service worker has lived for 25 years in Skudai, a busy Johor Bahru suburb anchored by universities, older housing estates and the kind of Chinese shoplot economy that helped make the Democratic Action Party (DAP) a force in Malaysia’s southern state.

He sees cranes, rail works and projects built by Chinese companies. But before Saturday’s state election, and as the China-linked boom loses its political shine, the question nagging at him is more prosaic than geopolitics: whether Johor can still offer a decent living to Malaysians who do not want to chase the Singapore dollar just next door.

“My primary concern is skyrocketing housing prices and living costs,” Koo, who asked to be identified only by his surname, told This Week in Asia. “Parties need to prove that the economy in Johor can support those who work in Johor, instead of forcing them to go into Singapore to make a living.”

That unease cuts into one of the most closely watched fights in Johor’s election: the Chinese vote.

DAP, the Chinese-majority party in Prime Minister Anwar Ibrahim’s Pakatan Harapan (PH) coalition, is trying to defend its urban base against Barisan Nasional’s (BN) Malaysian Chinese Association (MCA), BN’s long-time Chinese partner, which is leaning on its Johor business and state links to regain ground.

Mainland Chinese buyers and China-linked investment still carry political meaning in Johor, analysts say, especially in places shaped by the Iskandar development corridor. But among local Chinese voters, the argument has moved from questioning whether China money should come in, to whether locals can afford the boom it helps create.

Recent China-linked investments in Johor include TikTok parent ByteDance’s 1.5 billion ringgit (US$368 million) data centre expansion in Kulai and Tianjin-based Rianlon Corporation’s 1.27 billion ringgit research and development and manufacturing base.

Last year, Johor drew 110 billion ringgit in total approved investments.

Johor, home to just over 4 million people, is Malaysia’s southern gateway to Singapore. Bumiputera, Malaysia’s official category for Malays and indigenous groups, make up about 60 per cent of the population, with Chinese nearly 33 per cent and Indians 6.6 per cent. Its 56-seat election has drawn 172 candidates.

DAP Secretary General Anthony Loke told This Week in Asia on Monday that his party’s minimum target was to defend the 10 seats it won in 2022, even as it contests 17 this time.

No longer a China scare

Forest City, the Country Garden-backed mega development on reclaimed islands off Johor’s coast near Singapore, was once the perfect political villain: vast, China-funded and widely cast by critics as a luxury enclave built for outsiders rather than Malaysians.

In the 2018 general election, it fed the narrative that BN, under jailed former prime minister Najib Razak, had allowed too much foreign money to shape Malaysia’s future.

That anger has not disappeared, but it has lost its old potency. Forest City has fewer sharp edges now, while the Johor-Singapore Special Economic Zone (JS-SEZ), data centres, manufacturing and logistics have made Chinese and Singapore-linked capital part of the growth pitch for both BN and PH.

Francis E. Hutchinson, principal fellow at the ISEAS – Yusof Ishak Institute, said mainland Chinese presence was “not politically salient” in this campaign because cost of living was dominating the race, while both the Johor government and Anwar’s federal administration supported new investment into sectors with some China participation.

Samuel Tan, founder of property consultancy Olive Tree, said Forest City “does not appear to be the electoral flashpoint it was in 2018”, when it became a lightning rod for attacks on Umno and BN over claims of selling out to foreigners.

Tan said the backlash then was sharper among Malay voters than their Chinese counterparts.

Among Chinese voters now, he said, the mood was more pragmatic, with cost of living, jobs and business opportunities weighing more heavily than party loyalty or old arguments over Forest City.

“Forest City is now more of a governance or economic-delivery symbol, tied to JS-SEZ credibility, than an ethnic wedge issue,” Tan said.

Forest City’s early buyer base, along with newer arrivals, tenants and investors from China, does not form an electoral bloc because most are not Malaysian voters. But their presence still colours how voters read Johor’s rapid transformation.

Peter T.C. Chang, former deputy director of Universiti Malaya’s Institute of China Studies, said recent mainland Chinese migrants mattered politically “more as a symbolic presence than as a direct electoral constituency”.

“They have become a proxy for wider debates about China-linked investment, housing affordability, local employment and Johor’s rapid transformation,” Chang said.

The Singapore-dollar trap

Voters are now weighing grand projects against their own household maths.

In its Johor election manifesto, PH has promised 250,000 high-paying jobs, a 30 per cent rise in median wages, 80,000 affordable homes and a Balik Johor programme to bring home professionals working in Singapore or abroad by 2031. BN countered with a plan to create 200,000 quality jobs and expand Johor’s economy to 260 billion ringgit by 2030.

Malaysia and Singapore last year signed the JS-SEZ agreement, targeting 50 projects and 20,000 skilled jobs in the first five years, along with smoother movement across one of the world’s busiest borders.

Loke, who is also transport minister, said the SEZ, RTS Link and border improvements depended heavily on federal ministries, making PH’s presence in Johor crucial.

“We have been losing a lot to Singapore,” he said, naming manufacturing, services, shipping and bunkering as sectors that Malaysia could have held more strongly.

“Instead of our people crossing the border every day, they can still work in Johor, but at the same time with better-paying jobs.”

MCA could still lean on its old image as the business-minded Chinese party in Johor. But Chang said mainland Chinese investors were a “double-edged sword” for the party, especially when projects such as Forest City were seen as helping foreign investors more than local communities.

Chin Yee Mun, director of Universiti Tunku Abdul Rahman’s Tun Tan Cheng Lock Institute of Social Studies, said Chinese support for DAP had become “more conditional”, shaped by high expectations after years of reform slogans and disappointment when delivery fell short.

“Main issues remain bread-and-butter concerns, governance and equal treatment,” Chin said.

Voters like Koo are still unconvinced. “Salary in Johor is definitely not as high as Kuala Lumpur,” he said, “but the living cost in Johor Bahru is as high as, if not higher than, KL.”

item.title: [SCMP] Why Citigroup CEO Jane Fraser left the US-China summit feeling optimistic
item.description: For Citigroup’s global CEO Jane Fraser, her time in Beijing last month meant far more than a handshake and photograph with heads of state. Fraser was among 17 business leaders US President Donald Trump invited to China as part of his three-day visit to Beijing from May 13 to May 15. Standing on the western steps outside the Great Hall of the People – an enormous building near Tiananmen Square that is the primary venue for China’s legislative activities, diplomatic receptions and state banquets –
item.content:encoded:
For Citigroup’s global CEO Jane Fraser, her time in Beijing last month meant far more than a handshake and photograph with heads of state.

Fraser was among 17 business leaders US President Donald Trump invited to China as part of his three-day visit to Beijing from May 13 to May 15. Standing on the western steps outside the Great Hall of the People – an enormous building near Tiananmen Square that is the primary venue for China’s legislative activities, diplomatic receptions and state banquets – Fraser took in one of China’s most iconic panoramas.

Amid analyst caution about the structural tensions shaping the China-US relationship, some saw the meeting as little more than good optics. To Fraser, such takes missed the big picture, one that was broader and more encouraging.

“Two important powers in the world were engaging with each other, making sure there was an understanding of each other’s points of view,” she said in an exclusive interview.

As China courted foreign investment and its companies pursued global expansions, the summit reaffirmed stabilisation and collaboration as shared priorities, Fraser said. That meant China would remain a strategic imperative for Citi and many other firms.

“In the room, it felt much more like a meeting of equals,” Fraser said during an Asia trip late last month, before flying to Beijing again to meet clients and senior government leaders. There was recognition of China’s importance as a leader in many industries, and therefore “the discussion was very much about stabilisation”, she said, adding that there was “real investment” on both sides towards improving the economic relationship.

The number of US leaders at the summit, the support of the Trump administration for US companies engaging with China and the support of the Chinese government were all strong positive signs, she added.

The summit marked the seventh face-to-face meeting between Trump and President Xi Jinping. Focused on the US-Israel war on Iran, trade and the status of Taiwan, among other issues, it was also the first trip by a US leader to China since 2017. Following the meetings, both nations committed to establishing a “constructive relationship of strategic stability”, and the two leaders agreed to meet in the US in September.

While Fraser did not deny tensions between the two countries, she said she was encouraged to see their commitment to healthy ties.

“There are areas where there is a decoupling that has occurred, but instead of the word ‘de-risking’, we heard the word ‘collaboration’ all the time on both sides,” Fraser said. “I think that’s good for the world.”

Fraser, 58, is often referred to as the most powerful woman on Wall Street. Born in St Andrews, Scotland, she studied economics at Cambridge University. Before joining Citi, she was a partner at McKinsey & Company. She was named chair of Citi’s board in October. She is married with two children.

Last year, her pay climbed to US$42 million, elevating her into the ranks of the best-paid bosses on Wall Street as investors cheered her efforts to restructure the group’s businesses and streamline its management. Citi’s stock has gained 101 per cent since she took the helm in March 2021, and the company’s value is now around US$238.7 billion. In the latest recognition of her services to the global financial sector, she was awarded a damehood during King Charles III’s birthday honours last month.

Fraser’s presence at the Beijing summit highlighted the strategic importance of the Chinese market for the global bank, as well as its active pursuit of a wholly owned securities licence in China. The licence would permit Citi to directly operate an onshore brokerage and investment banking business in mainland China, allowing it to compete against already licensed peers like JP Morgan, Goldman Sachs and UBS.

Beijing reportedly approved the licence during the summit, after a four-year process, but it has not yet been issued.

“I’m very hopeful we do get it, because the role we play in connecting China and the world, the world and China, it requires more of the breadth and depth of the local capital market and capabilities,” Fraser said. “It would be beneficial for our Chinese clients.”

Fraser said the banking group was focused on serving Chinese companies looking to expand overseas as well as clients with cross-border needs, adding that many of her bank’s international clients, including those from the US, had been showing growing interest in China and its world-leading companies in many industries.

In the first four months of this year, 20,113 new foreign-invested enterprises were established in China, representing a 6.8 per cent year-on-year increase, according to data from the Ministry of Commerce. By source, investment flows into China grew significantly from Luxembourg (up 110 per cent), Switzerland (61 per cent), France (58 per cent) and the US (25 per cent).

“We see a lot of the American companies recognising that if you don’t know what’s happening in China and don’t understand where that is … then you are going to be irrelevant,” she said. “It doesn’t necessarily mean to say that they’ll be as competitive as the Chinese companies, but they want to understand.”

Citi is known as the “flower flag” bank in China, a nickname that originated in 1902 when locals thought the stars on the American flag flown above the first Shanghai branch of Citi predecessor International Banking Corporation looked like a representation of flowers.

The bank has shifted its strategy to primarily focus on institutional banking, capital markets and corporate services. It has largely completed an exit from 14 international consumer markets, including mainland China, India and Taiwan. Two years ago, it completed the sale of its mainland consumer wealth portfolio to HSBC.

Citi has advised on a number of big cross-border transactions in the last 12 months, including acting as an exclusive financial adviser to US-based General Mills in the sale of its Haagen-Dazs shops in mainland China to an investor group including China-based lemon tea chain Ningji.

Today, Citi serves 70 per cent of the Fortune 500 companies operating in China, as well as over 300 local enterprises. In the last five years, the bank helped raise more than US$300 billion for Chinese clients from global capital markets, according to data from analytics platform Dealogic.

China stepped up policy support for cross-border financing in May by raising the leverage ratios for overseas lending by foreign banks in the country. The move aimed to promote trade and investment as well as accelerate the global use of the yuan amid rising demand for cross-border financial services as Chinese companies quicken their pace of overseas expansion.

The People’s Bank of China, the National Financial Regulatory Administration, the China Securities Regulatory Commission and the State Administration of Foreign Exchange announced major financial measures separately at the Lujiazui Financial Forum last month.

“Obviously, for Chinese clients – as China goes more global – it is very useful to have the renminbi internationalised and to make sure they have the asset base behind the needs and ability to hedge,” Fraser said. “Given the size of China and the globalisation of Chinese companies, the internationalisation of the renminbi is something clients want and need, and we’ll be there to support them.”

While at the moment the US dollar was “in debt” it would remain the reserve currency “for a long time”, she said.

Looking ahead, Fraser said the increase in cross-border trade and Chinese companies going global was “a major trend that will not change”. Changes in money flows would create more opportunity in Asia, including mainland China and Hong Kong, she added.

The world was seeing increasing flows between China and other regions – including the Middle East and Latin America – mediated through Hong Kong, as well as important flows to the United States and Europe, according to Fraser.

“China has enormous scale, but it’s still young in developing that scale,” she said, adding that clients wanted to use Hong Kong as a springboard to mainland China, ensuring the city would “play an absolutely critical role” in the new phase of globalisation.

Fraser emphasised the bank’s long commitment to the city, which she said had a special place in her heart. “We’re unique in having a 124-year history in Hong Kong,” she said.

Hong Kong had a “long and deep history of being one of the big global hubs and [financial] centres,” Fraser said, highlighting the city’s talent base, infrastructure and regulatory framework as a solid ecosystem.

The city was “not just a gateway to China” but a “China plus”, providing access to where “all the new innovations are coming from and how they are being applied”, she said.

Last year, Citi took part as a primary liquidity provider in the enhanced offshore yuan bond repo scheme introduced by the Hong Kong Monetary Authority (HKMA) and the PBOC, acting as the only US bank among the 11 dealers in the programme.

As Citi continues to develop digital asset solutions to meet the market’s needs, Fraser praised Hong Kong’s technological innovations and regulatory framework to support such developments. With Beijing’s support, she said the HKMA was doing well in establishing frameworks for many innovations that would shape the future financial system, citing the successful tokenised bond issuances.

“What I really appreciate here is the regulation is forward-looking,” she said.

The city made history by launching the world’s first tokenised government green bond in 2023. This was followed by the issuance of the world’s first multicurrency digital bond in 2024, as well as the largest digital bond issuance at the time of issuance and the first digital bond to integrate tokenised central bank money in the form of e-CNY and e-HKD in 2025.

A lot of Chinese wealth is flowing through Hong Kong, as the city’s market offers sophistication, breadth and depth, as well as investment access to different global opportunities in the region and beyond, Fraser said.

She noted that family offices from China and around the world were setting up in Hong Kong, and said Citi planned to expand its wealth-management business to serve them.

During her trip to Hong Kong in late June, Fraser met Victor Fung Kwok-king, group chairman of the Li & Fung group of companies, which Citi has served for 117 years. They discussed the next phase of reinvention, including how the bank could support the group on topics including blockchain, logistics, AI and digitisation.

In the first quarter of this year, Citi’s net income jumped 42 per cent year on year to US$5.8 billion on US$24.6 billion in revenue, the highest quarterly trading haul in a decade.

“What we have proven in the last four years is not only could we do all of that restructuring of the organisation, but we grew,” she said, adding that there was a lot of growth and upside ahead, which should make investors “very happy”.

“Our competitors should be fearful,” Fraser said, adding she planned to celebrate her damehood with her family following her packed Asia trip.

item.title: [SCMP] Hong Kong graduates at crossroads as AI takes over entry-level jobs, experts warn
