Much has been written about how large countries shape global norms by leveraging market power or through coercion, from the “Brussels Effect” to American and Chinese strategic dominance. In contrast, Singapore positions itself as a “neutral facilitator”, pioneering agreements that are flexible enough to accommodate countries with different strategic interests, but concrete enough for legal norms to crystallise and proliferate.

A notable example of Singapore’s norm entrepreneurship in international trade law is the substance and structure of its digital economy agreements (DEAs). Early DEAs include the Digital Economy Partnership Agreement (DEPA) with New Zealand and Chile in 2020, followed by the Australia–Singapore DEA the same year.

Unlike traditional e-commerce clauses in older trade agreements, which were mainly concerned with the online trade of goods and services, Singapore’s DEAs prioritise implementing rules to regulate the data-driven economy itself, including regulations on digital identity, e-payments, artificial-intelligence governance and digital competition.

‘Soft law’

Some scholars have noted that these provisions tend to take the form of “soft law” commitments, such as endeavour clauses or broad statements of intent, rather than more specific or binding commitments. However, this does not diminish their legal or political significance, nor are all such provisions equally soft.

This is evident, for example, in the contrast between DEPA’s provision on digital identity, which states that parties shall “endeavour” to cooperate, and the stronger Australia–Singapore DEA commitment, which requires that parties “shall pursue the development of mechanisms to promote compatibility between their respective digital identity regimes”.

The significance of these provisions lies not only in what they do bilaterally, but in how they socialise emerging expectations about digital trade governance, laying the groundwork for harder law commitments.

Elements from Singapore’s DEAs have already influenced other digital agreements. The recent Joint Statement Initiative (JSI) at the World Trade Organization in March, co-led by Singapore, Australia and Japan, incorporates similar language and topics from previous DEAs.

The JSI established interim commitments adopted by 66 WTO members as a pathway to bring the WTO Agreement on Electronic Commerce into force, which would create the world’s first baseline set of global digital trade rules, including binding commitments on electronic signatures, electronic contracts and e-invoices, among other areas.

Singapore-led DEAs have also supplied the building blocks for the Asean Digital Economy Framework Agreement, which includes binding rules covering many of the same policy areas in Singapore DEAs, such as digital trade, cross-border e-commerce and AI.

Singapore’s approach to essential supply chains follows a similar logic to its digital trade approach: using softer arrangements to test and socialise norms before pursuing harder commitments with other partners. Softer law antecedents to AOTES can be seen, for instance, in Singapore’s memorandum of understanding with South Korea, which requires both countries to convene an emergency meeting in the event of supply-chain disruptions.

Like DEPA, AOTES is also meant to be a template others can copy or join. However, the diffusion of norms under AOTES will be harder than in digital trade agreements because essential supplies touch on the more politically sensitive issue of resource scarcity in times of crisis. Were bigger states to join such an arrangement, it is unclear how their larger essential supply demands would be accommodated.

Nonetheless, the model set by AOTES could adopt the modular structure of DEPA, allowing new partners to join selected areas of cooperation rather than accept an all-or-nothing bargain. This would make it easier to accommodate different national vulnerabilities, whether in fuel, food, medical supplies or logistics, while preserving the broader norm that trusted partners should keep essential goods moving, even during crises.

This pragmatic and incrementalist approach is not new for Singapore; it has been carried over from traditional trade agreements to forge partnerships in novel policy domains. Other than AOTES, Singapore is also adopting this trade agreement model to address emerging challenges such as decarbonisation and sustainable finance, as seen in the launch of the Green Economy Partnership Agreement with DEPA partners New Zealand and Chile.

Singapore’s approach should not be thought of as a turn away from multilateralism, but rather as a pragmatic response to the limits of the WTO system. Countries cannot wait for a consensus to address pressing issues that old trade rules do not adequately cover.

The question is not whether plurilateral agreements will be entered into, but what kind. Indeed, the devil is in the details, and not all bilateral or plurilateral agreements will reinforce the liberal international vision of world trade.

Singapore’s plurilateral agreements, however, do not represent an abandonment of the system, but rather, an attempt to strengthen it.

As Singapore’s first foreign minister, S. Rajaratnam, described in the country’s accession speech to the United Nations: its commitment to the international law system is out of “practical self-interest and not vague idealism”. In international trade law, it is this self-interest as a major beneficiary of the rules-based order that pushes Singapore to innovate within, rather than undermine, the system.

Tristan Eng is a practising lawyer in Australia and Associated Junior Research Fellow with the Stockholm Centre for South Asian and Indo-Pacific Affairs. This article was first published by the ISEAS - Yusof Ishak Institute’s commentary website fulcrum.sg

item.title: [SCMP] From starry-eyed to sceptical: why young Chinese are turning away from the American dream
item.description: As the United States marks the 250th anniversary of its founding, it confronts a new world order dominated by its relationship with China. In this wide-ranging series, we examine the pressure points and possibilities in those ties, from hard tech to soft power. In this article, Jane Cai and Yuanyue Dang examine Chinese people’s changing attitudes towards the US. As the United States prepares to celebrate its 250th anniversary this month – marking its journey from a revolutionary experiment to a
item.content:encoded:
As the United States marks the 250th anniversary of its founding, it confronts a new world order dominated by its relationship with China. In this wide-ranging series, we examine the pressure points and possibilities in those ties, from hard tech to soft power. In this article, Jane Cai and Yuanyue Dang examine Chinese people’s changing attitudes towards the US.

As the United States prepares to celebrate its 250th anniversary this month – marking its journey from a revolutionary experiment to a global beacon of opportunity – few relationships have mattered more in modern history than the one between America and China.

For generations, the US represented not just a distant power but an aspirational ideal: a land of innovation, individual freedom and boundless possibility that contrasted with China’s own turbulent path through dynastic decline, republican hopes, invasion, revolution and rebirth.

Nowhere is the transformation more vivid than across two generations of Chinese families. Parents who once viewed America as the ultimate destination – a symbol of modernity and escape from scarcity – now have children who approach it with pragmatic respect or detachment, national confidence and sometimes outright scepticism.

What was once emulation has become selective engagement; what was aspiration has shifted towards equality and, in some quarters, rivalry.

“I’ve got such a headache,” said Zhang Mengyao, 48, a former banker in the northern port city of Tianjin. “My daughter doesn’t want to study in the US, even though I’ve made all the preparations for her. I really don’t understand what’s on her mind. I’ve dreamed of this chance my whole life, yet she won’t even consider it.”

Zhang’s frustration runs deep. In the 1990s, as China flung open its doors, her sister landed a coveted job on Wall Street. Videotapes mailed from across the ocean revealed American streets, offices and vibrant city life that moved Zhang to tears in her modest Tianjin bedroom. Fired up by those images of prosperity, she applied for a student visa – only to face three rejections over suspected immigration intent.

For her daughter, a third-year public administration student, the plan was postgraduate study in the US. But the young woman has rejected the idea. She aims to take the civil service exam and aspires to become a foreign ministry spokeswoman – one who can confidently push back against powers such as the US.

Zhang’s story reflects a seismic shift in Chinese perceptions of America: from starry-eyed admiration to a more complex, pragmatic and self-assured stance. As the US approaches its semiquincentennial, this psychological evolution may prove as significant as any trade deal or diplomatic pact.

A century of shared history

For more than a century, the US stood apart in Chinese eyes as a power distinct from, and often less predatory than, European colonial forces.

In the late Qing dynasty (1644-1911), America returned much of its share of the Boxer Indemnities – penalties imposed on the Qing following the Boxer rebellion. These returned funds helped establish institutions including the prestigious Tsinghua University and enabled generations of Chinese students to study in the US.

That schooling shaped a generation of leaders, according to Rana Mitter, S.T. Lee Chair in US-Asia Relations at the Harvard Kennedy School.

“American education was central to shaping the views of the elites of the Republican era,” he said. “Even for many who did not go to America, American education in China was central to shaping their wider understanding of the world.”

The US was the first Western power to recognise the Republic of China in 1911. Chinese intellectuals praised American ideals, and the US-China wartime alliance against Japan in World War II, including the Flying Tigers pilots, cemented bonds of shared sacrifice.

Later, Cold War hostilities gave way to Chinese paramount leader Deng Xiaoping’s “reform and opening up”. The Deng-era slogan “to get rich is glorious” aligned with the American dream.

Hollywood, the NBA, Starbucks and American universities became emblems of the future. Tens of thousands of Chinese students flocked to American campuses. For that generation, the US embodied progress.

Today, younger Chinese often have limited exposure to this rich shared history, which was once a source of affinity, according to a historian at a university in Jiangsu province who spoke on condition of anonymity because of the sensitivity of the topic.

He said Generation Z, those born roughly between 1997 and 2012, generally had “scant interest in digging into the details of past Sino-US ties”, and this history was given modest coverage in China’s textbooks.

Official narratives around events such as last year’s 80th anniversary of Victory Day increasingly cast China as the main bastion against fascism in Asia, and portray the West as neglecting or downplaying this contribution.

The historian said that while there continued to be gestures of respect and people-to-people warmth, such as invitations to Flying Tigers veterans and their families by President Xi Jinping, the broader framing positioned China as “an equal partner rather than a pupil”.

The wartime partnership ran deep both ways, according to Mitter. “Without China’s resistance until 1941, Japan might have conquered Asia,” he said.

“However, without the US bringing finance and materiel to China, China could not have defeated Japan on its own.”

Mitter said the relationship “became warm and deep in many ways during the war”. In recent years, as ties have soured, “that history is less well-remembered than it used to be – but there are good reasons to recall the period when the two worked together”, he added.

From ‘sea turtles’ to self-reliance

Many of China’s reform-era elites were “sea turtles” – citizens who returned home after living and studying abroad.

Figures such as former vice-premier Liu He, who earned a master’s degree in public administration from the Harvard Kennedy School, and former central banker Yi Gang, who holds a PhD from the University of Illinois, brought back advanced ideas in economics, finance, management and governance.

Liu, one of Xi’s most trusted confidants, was in charge of a huge portfolio – from China’s hi-tech drive to state enterprise reforms and broader industrial policies – until his retirement from all official positions in the government and the Communist Party in 2023. Despite his retirement, he is still regularly consulted on important financial and economic affairs – particularly on issues related to the US.

From 2018 to 2023, Yi led financial reforms and debt reduction while maintaining a prudent monetary stance as governor of the People’s Bank of China. Yi was also responsible for raising China’s profile on the global stage and continuing its opening to the world. He is unusual among Chinese bureaucrats in that he earned his doctorate in economics in the US and taught the subject at Indiana University-Purdue University Indianapolis. He worked at the institution for about eight years before returning to China in 1994 and has called the university his “second home”.

For decades, China learned from other countries, adapting technologies and models while often playing catch-up. In the early 1990s, China’s nominal gross domestic product (GDP) was roughly 5 to 10 per cent of America’s. In recent years, it has reached around 65 per cent. This transformation has reshaped global dynamics and prompted Washington to view China as a systemic challenger.

China’s economic ascent has fuelled domestic confidence. Having gone through a copycat phase, the country is now a leader in electric vehicles, high-speed rail, 5G and artificial intelligence (AI). Home-grown tech giants such as Huawei Technologies and online platforms including Douyin offer alternatives. Public sentiment has shifted accordingly.

Over 70 per cent of 2,083 Chinese adults surveyed in 2021 had “unfavourable feelings” towards the US, according to a research paper published in the April 2023 issue of the Journal of Current Chinese Affairs. In contrast, just 17 per cent of Chinese respondents reported having unfavourable feelings towards the US in 2019, according to a study conducted by the Eurasia Group.

This dramatic rise in animosity over just two years was highlighted in the 2023 paper by Adam Liu of the National University of Singapore, Xiaojun Li of the University of British Columbia and Songying Fang of Rice University. They found that compared with the older generation, younger Chinese survey respondents were less likely to express positive feelings towards the US. In addition, more “nationalistic respondents” and party members viewed the US less favourably.

When US President Donald Trump blamed China for the Covid-19 pandemic and repeatedly called it the “China virus”, his rhetoric fuelled a surge in nationalism in China and reinforced the widespread perception that the US had mismanaged the crisis.

Meanwhile, the trade war and technological restrictions imposed by Washington have prompted Beijing to respond in kind, intensifying its focus on supply-chain security and the development of home-grown technology.

The end of history?

Since the Covid-19 pandemic, Chinese families have become far more pragmatic. According to Jason Zhu, a Beijing-based overseas study consultant, seminars that were once packed with anxious parents planning undergraduate studies in the US are now half-empty.

“Many now prefer to keep their children in domestic universities. They believe in China’s bright future and worry about safety and stability in the US,” Zhu said.

While China remains the largest source of undergraduate international students in the US, the numbers have dropped sharply. Undergraduate enrolment from China fell around 10 per cent year over year to around 78,583 in the 2024-25 academic year, down from its peak of 148,900 in the 2018-19 academic year.

Zhu said multiple forces were at work, including the trade wars, technology export controls, bitter debates over the origins of Covid-19 and vivid images of American political chaos, inequality and social division.

The economic insecurity facing ordinary Americans was highlighted in the “kill line” meme on Chinese social media, which featured stories of how a single illness, accident or job loss could push people into poverty. The online trend, whose name comes from video game slang, helped shatter illusions about life in the US.

“Also, parents, many of them being civil servants, believe in the state media narrative of ‘Eastern rise, Western decline’,” he said. “I’m now considering scaling down my business.”

This framing, promoted since around 2020, portrays China and Asia as ascending while the West, led by the US, faces decline.

“Beijing today retains many of the instincts of a vulnerable power even though in reality it is a very strong power,” said Harvard’s Mitter.

He said that the China of today had “much more agency than in the past and could be more confident about coexisting with the US in Asia and beyond”. This, he added, was “what most other Asian countries would take as the best legacy of the war era”.

As China continues to thrive, Western observers are rethinking old assumptions about the country’s future, which has buoyed Beijing’s confidence.

American political scientist Francis Fukuyama, long a champion of liberal democracy and author of the “end of history” thesis, has offered a more nuanced assessment of China’s system.

In recent interviews, he acknowledged that China had created “a pretty impressive system” that had proved highly effective at marshalling new technologies and innovating in areas once thought beyond its reach.

Fukuyama contrasted this with American democracy, which sometimes appears to be stumbling, noting that he was reconsidering some of his earlier assumptions about the inevitability of the triumph of Western-style democracy.

Mitter remains wary of both narratives. “America and China tell apocalyptic stories about each other – either that the other one wants to dominate the world, or that one of them is about to decline or collapse,” he said.

“Actually, both powers will have to live with each other for a very long time to come. Apocalyptic stories rarely come true.”

New pragmatism, new anxieties

Li Yuelin, a university student in the southern province of Guangdong, exemplifies the new pragmatism of the younger generation.

“I’d still love to study cutting-edge technology in the US and hopefully land a Silicon Valley job, but attractive, high-paying opportunities in Shenzhen or Hangzhou make staying home an equally strong choice,” Li said. “Many of my fellow [computer science] majors think the same way.”

Zhang’s daughter, Wang Yiyi, prioritises stability and the conveniences of life in China rather than trying her luck in the US.

“If that path [being a civil servant] doesn’t work out, I would consider a stint in Hong Kong,” Wang said.

“It would give me some international exposure before going to Beijing or Shanghai to look for a job. If I cannot find a job, as the competition is cutthroat nowadays, I would like to enjoy a comfortable ‘lying flat’ life back home with my parents’ support,” she said.

“Lying flat” is a popular slang term among Chinese youth that refers to a conscious rejection of intense societal pressure to strive, compete and achieve.

Instead of chasing the conventional path of relentless hard work, home ownership and career success, many young people are choosing to do the bare minimum necessary to get by – essentially “lying flat” and living a low-desire, low-stress life.

“Rather than stressing over high-stakes opportunities in the US, I’m happy just exploring the world through my screen at home. After seeing all those viral cost-of-living comparisons on [social media platform] RedNote last year, I realised we can actually enjoy a much lower-stress, lower-cost life in China,” she said.

Xi has taken a firm stance against the “lying flat” mindset, repeatedly urging young Chinese to contribute actively to national rejuvenation. In official discourse, the Chinese leader has explicitly called for avoiding “lying flat”, a passive attitude he views as incompatible with the country’s ambitions for greatness.

“The United States was built on the idea of America,” said Xu Guoqi, the David H.Y. Chang Professor of Chinese History at the University of Hong Kong.

“The Chinese have never thought about the idea of China in a big way. We need a deep soul-searching: What do we mean by China? Who are the Chinese? What do we want to be?” he said. “Just as the Americans did in 1787 with their constitution.”

Wang Yuanchong, associate professor of history at the University of Delaware, said: “The year 1900 was a turning point. For China, it marked the Boxer rebellion and the Boxer Indemnity; for the United States, the [American] civil war had ended, ushering in an era of reconstruction and the Gilded Age, a period of steady growth.”

“Back then, the United States faced the challenge of shaping itself based on European experience; today, China faces the challenge of preserving its identity amid globalisation,” he added.

“From this perspective, the two are similar.”

item.title: [SCMP] Global attractions that ooze innovation and imagination
item.description: By their very nature, museums, heritage sites and other tourist favourites tend to be backwards-looking, but that doesn’t mean there aren’t global attractions that flip the script to imagine what lies ahead – or what could exist in alternate universes. From interactive museums to cities built on tomorrow’s green tech and imagination-firing immersive-art zones, the following destinations offer a passport to innovative worlds, be they future or imaginary ones. Shenzhen Science & Technology Museum
item.content:encoded:
By their very nature, museums, heritage sites and other tourist favourites tend to be backwards-looking, but that doesn’t mean there aren’t global attractions that flip the script to imagine what lies ahead – or what could exist in alternate universes.

From interactive museums to cities built on tomorrow’s green tech and imagination-firing immersive-art zones, the following destinations offer a passport to innovative worlds, be they future or imaginary ones.

Shenzhen Science & Technology Museum

Inspired by a “colossal interstellar spacecraft” and located in the Guangming Science City, the Shenzhen Science & Technology Museum is an eight-storey (two subterranean) architectural masterpiece designed by Zaha Hadid Architects that opened last May. It features an eye-catching U-shaped stainless-steel facade and this year was named one of the world’s seven most beautiful museums by the compilers of the Prix Versailles lists.

This immersive, tech-driven playground is packed with hands-on artificially intelligent robotics, simulation technologies and state-of-the-art cinemas. Outdoor terraces and a sunlit central atrium integrate the building with the surrounding Science Park and the museum is easy to reach across a walkway from the Guangming Metro Station, a mere 90 minutes from Hong Kong’s Central MTR station (depending on connections and border-crossing queues).

Chengdu Science Fiction Museum

Another Zaha Hadid Architects masterpiece, the Chengdu Science Fiction Museum “floats” on Jingrong Lake and boasts a “fluid starburst” roof that reflects the expanding nebula of the universe to viewers on the outside.

Enter and you’re treated to a deep dive into the history and future of Chinese and global science-fiction in multimedia-heavy exhibition spaces, art galleries and a multifunctional theatre that in 2023 hosted the Hugo Awards, widely considered the Oscars of the sci-fi literary world. Look out for the life-size replica of Benben, the hyper-intelligent, easily frightened utility droid/mechanical dog from Andy Lau Tak-wah’s sci-fi blockbuster The Wandering Earth 2 (2023).

The surrounding complex blends the futuristic, fluid indoor spaces with lakefront pedestrian pathways, green parklands and outdoor viewing platforms complete with panoramic photo opportunities.

The quickest way to get to the Chengdu Science Fiction Museum is to set your time machine for the year 2100, then be whisked over to Sichuan province from any of Hong Kong’s major teleport stations.

City in Nature, Singapore

Singapore’s City in Nature initiative aims to weave rich, functional ecosystems directly into the urban fabric, the goals being to boost biodiversity, manage climate resilience and improve human well-being.

At the heart of this vision is the Supertree Grove at Gardens by the Bay. Despite being 14 years old, these 12 structures are still considered futuristic and are fully functional biophilic systems. Standing up to 50 metres tall, they serve as vertical beds for more than 160,000 plants, use photovoltaic cells to harvest solar energy and act as exhaust and cooling receptacles for the park’s conservatories.

As in every good ecosystem, the Supertrees do not exist in a vacuum, and are connected to an island-wide network of living infrastructure, exemplified by three other tourist-friendly sites that can be easily reached from Gardens by the Bay on public transport: the Singapore Botanic Gardens, the CapitaSpring Green Oasis and Sky Garden – two huge green spaces that break up the density of the 280-metre-tall skyscraper – and the Forest Walk, part of the 10km Southern Ridges Trail, which links several hilltop parks and crosses the Henderson Waves, Singapore’s highest pedestrian bridge.

Toyota’s Woven City, Japan

The Woven City is “weaving the future”, according to the website. Located at the foot of Mount Fuji, the 175-acre “living laboratory” is designed to test technologies such as autonomous driving, robotics, smart homes and AI in a real-world community.

The city gets its name from its layout and the weaving together of three distinct street types: one dedicated to fast automated vehicles, one for lower-speed personal mobility and pedestrians, and a third consisting of park-like paths exclusively for walkers.

Toyota is planning to open public tours as early as this year, on which visitors can enter the community as “one-day weavers” to test out the city’s mobility tech and interact with smart-home features. They will also be encouraged to provide feedback to the resident inventors.

A key stop on these tours is likely to be the Inventor Garage, which was opened in April to serve as the primary hub for tech showcases, AI demonstrations and collaborative innovation events.

Museu de les Ciències, Valencia, Spain

The Science Museum opened in 2000 as a focal point of Valencia’s City of Arts and Sciences and is housed in a building that is still considered futuristic, even though it resembles the skeleton of a prehistoric whale. Spanish architect Santiago Calatrava’s innovation here involved making a heavy concrete and steel structure look fluid, weightless and alive. Roughly 220 metres long, 80 metres wide and 55 metres high, the vast exhibition halls are largely free of internal supporting pillars.

Operating under the motto “Forbidden not to touch, not to feel, not to think”, the museum is 26,000 square metres of interactive exhibits devoted to everything from genetics and space exploration to climate change.

Echoing Singapore’s Supertrees, here there is the Chromosome Forest, 23 glowing “trees” each representing a pair of chromosomes in the human genome. Visitors have to pull levers, press buttons and trigger interactive modules built directly into the “bark” of a tree to discover what specific genes control.

Outside a curtain wall consisting of 4,000 panes of glass – the entire north facade of the museum – seasonal concerts take place around turquoise reflection pools. Next door is the Hemisfèric IMAX cinema and the Oceanogràfic, Europe’s largest aquarium.

Futurium, Berlin, Germany

Standing on the banks of the River Spree, in central Berlin, the energy-sustainable Futurium is a space designed for both public debates and lectures and as an exhibition hall in which the possible worlds of tomorrow are viewed through the lenses of humanities, nature and technology.

The Futurium’s sculptural exterior features more than 8,000 diamond-shaped cassettes made of cast glass and hidden metal reflectors, and admission to its three floors of interactive exhibits, hands-on subterranean lab and AI installations is free of charge.

In the centre of the building is an energy-storage system packed with paraffin-candle wax combined with water. As the building absorbs heat from the “solar sea” of panels on the roof, the wax melts, soaking up energy. When the building cools down at night, the wax solidifies again, releasing that stored energy back into the museum. This giant wax battery is housed behind glass that allows visitors to check the state of the melting or hardening wax to see exactly how hard the building is working to power itself.

The highlight for many visitors, though, is the Skywalk on the roof, which pairs an inspection of the building’s solar-energy collectors with panoramic views of the Berlin skyline, including the Reichstag dome and the Federal Chancellery.

TeamLab, Tokyo

Created by an interdisciplinary international art collective that came together in 2001, Tokyo’s permanent teamLab exhibitions – including “Borderless”, in Azabudai Hills, and “Planets”, in Toyosu – are large-scale, multisensory digital art museums that dissolve the boundaries between the viewer and the artwork.

The spaces are designed to be “museums without a map”, allowing visitors to freely explore shifting, digital landscapes that change in real time based on human interaction – a walk barefoot through knee-deep water filled with digital projections of koi fish, a wander through a mirror maze of thousands of hanging LED crystal lights or a lie-down under a dome of floating orchids – and even sample flower-infused vegan ramen inside an active art installation.

Not visiting Tokyo? TeamLab operates between 15 and 20 long-term or permanent exhibitions globally, including full-scale museums, permanent collaborations and open-air parks. The nearest to Hong Kong is teamLab SuperNature Macao, a permanent, 5,000-square-metre immersive digital museum inside The Venetian Macao.

Meow Wolf, various locations across the United States

Another outfit riding the modern immersive-art wave is Meow Wolf.

Part-escape room (or, more accurately, linger room), part-immersive exhibition, Meow Wolf started in 2008 as a DIY punk artist collective in Santa Fe, New Mexico, and now operates five experiences across the US, with a sixth to open soon, in Los Angeles.

The company hires hundreds of local painters, sculptors, technologists and writers to build permanent, larger-than-life dreamscapes in which visitors are encouraged to touch everything, explore freely and play.

Each is unique. Santa Fe’s House of Eternal Return is based on a multidimensional Victorian house in which a family has vanished after performing an experiment with time and space; Omega Mart, in Las Vegas, takes its cue from a weird American supermarket that serves as a front for a corrupt, interdimensional corporation harvesting a mysterious cosmic substance. Similar freakiness unfolds at the Convergence Station in Denver, Colorado, and two experiences in Texas, The Real Unreal and Radio Tave.

There are narrative puzzles to solve in each experience, says Meow Wolf public relations manager Michael Duffield, but visitors don’t have to do so to fully enjoy the experience, which lasts as long as they wish. Some may just want to find the well-hidden speakeasy bar.

T-PARK, Hong Kong

Sludge may not sound like everyone’s idea of fun, but T-Park, a state-of-the-art waste-to-energy treatment facility in Tsang Tsui, Tuen Mun, is a must for the eco-minded.

One of the largest and most technologically advanced self-sustained facilities of its kind in the world, T-Park processes the concentrated, semi-solid by-product left after waste water is treated elsewhere in the city. That sludge is fired in incinerators that in part heat the water for a free public bathhouse, T-Spa, featuring three indoor pools maintained at different temperatures. Also on-site are 9,800 square metres of landscaped regenerated outdoor wetlands designed to support biodiversity, and a self-service cafe.

It’s a bit of a trek out to Tsang Tsui, granted – even if you take the free shuttle from the Tuen Mun MTR station – but as far as green days out go, it rivals another hike along the Dragon’s Back trail.

item.title: [SCMP] Asean at 60: peace, prosperity and polite paralysis
item.description: Dan Rae Hugo has never heard of the Asean Petroleum Security Agreement. All he knows is that his diesel costs have doubled, his profit margins have evaporated and the rice on his neighbours’ tables now costs 20 per cent more than it did before the Iran war. The 43-year-old has been farming the fields of Iloilo, the Philippines, for 18 years. He has never worked harder for less. “It’s all the inputs: the diesel, the labour for operating the machines, the transportation when we harvest … It’s the
item.content:encoded:
Dan Rae Hugo has never heard of the Asean Petroleum Security Agreement. All he knows is that his diesel costs have doubled, his profit margins have evaporated and the rice on his neighbours’ tables now costs 20 per cent more than it did before the Iran war.

The 43-year-old has been farming the fields of Iloilo, the Philippines, for 18 years. He has never worked harder for less.

“It’s all the inputs: the diesel, the labour for operating the machines, the transportation when we harvest … It’s the highest I’ve ever seen,” Hugo said.

Some 150km (93 miles) from his farm, in the convention halls of Cebu, leaders of the Association of Southeast Asian Nations were meeting to address the very crisis threatening Hugo’s livelihood: a global energy shock affecting all 11 member states.

They agreed – as they have agreed before – to accelerate an energy security framework and push forward a regional power grid that could, in theory, stabilise electricity prices for the nearly 700 million people who call Southeast Asia home.

Philippine President Ferdinand Marcos Jnr gave voice to the urgency required. “We needed it last month. Forget about next month, six months, a year from now,” he said. “We needed it yesterday, if not sooner.”

His remarks struck at the heart of a long-standing critique of the bloc, that its consensus-driven approach hampers its ability to respond swiftly to crises and renders it little more than a talking shop.

As Asean approaches its 60th anniversary next year, analysts and former diplomats told This Week in Asia that the bloc had, by almost any historical measure, been a remarkable success.

It has kept the peace in a region that was tearing itself apart when it was founded. It has presided over one of the most spectacular economic ascents in human history. It has woven together 11 of the world’s most diverse nations into a functioning diplomatic community that punches well above its collective weight.

And yet, as it prepares to mark six decades of existence, a cluster of compounding crises – from the Iran war energy shock and an unresolved military coup to the disintegrating world order – is putting Asean’s limitations on full display.

Born of distrust

Asean was born in August 1967 in Bangkok, the product of five governments – Indonesia, Malaysia, the Philippines, Singapore and Thailand – who had every reason to distrust one another and chose, improbably, to cooperate.

The Cold War was at its height. Communism was spreading through the region. Indonesia and Malaysia were barely past a military confrontation that had spilled into Singapore, which had itself only just been expelled from the Federation of Malaya.

Save for Thailand, every founding member had recently emerged from colonial rule – under different powers, with different grievances, different languages and different faiths.

That they held together at all was, as former Philippine foreign affairs secretary Delia D. Albert put it, “something to be proud of”.

That they have now held together for 60 years, expanding from five members to 11 and becoming the world’s fifth-largest economic bloc – projected to rank fourth by 2030 – is an even greater achievement.

“Judging it [Asean] according to what it was meant to do and what it has achieved is a healthier way to do it,” Albert told This Week in Asia. “Of course everybody wants it to do more, but one has to understand why it was set up, what it was meant to do and to see what it has done these past 60 years.”

Since 1991, there has been no major armed conflict between Asean members, with the notable exception of the recent Thailand-Cambodia border clashes. The bloc has sealed landmark trade agreements with China, India, Japan and South Korea. And its foundational compact of mutual respect and non-interference, the Treaty of Amity and Cooperation, has been signed by 58 countries.

“It has fostered regional norms of peace and dialogue which have allowed the region to dramatically raise living standards,” said Hunter Marston, director of the Lowy Institute’s Southeast Asia programme, of Asean. “It has created a diplomatic community … which has deepened regional integration.”

But the same principles that made Asean possible – consensus, non-interference, sovereign equality – are now the source of its most persistent failures, analysts say. Decisions that require unanimity tend to arrive late, diluted or not at all.

Agreements signed at summits gather dust as implementation founders on the shoals of competing national interests. The energy security framework Hugo has never heard of has existed, in various forms, for years. The power grid has been in the planning stages for decades.

Asean needs to experiment with some innovative ways to improve decision-making processes

Sharon Seah, foreign policy analyst

“Asean needs to experiment with some innovative ways to improve decision-making processes,” said foreign policy analyst Sharon Seah, a former diplomat who now serves as a principal fellow at the ISEAS–Yusof Ishak Institute in Singapore.

She described the way the bloc currently makes decisions as slow and cumbersome, bogged down by years of expanding red tape.

Albert, who served as the Philippines’ first female foreign secretary under former president Gloria Macapagal Arroyo, is clear-eyed about the trade-off Asean embodies.

“You have to remember that we’re not like the EU, with a supranational body that tells us what to do,” she said. “We are still independent on our own in many ways and that’s the only way it would work.”

Asean, at its heart, was designed to include, not to compel – and inclusion, at scale, across this degree of diversity, demands patience that crises rarely permit.

The Myanmar impasse

Few crises have tested the bloc’s patience more severely than Myanmar.

When the country’s armed forces, known as the Tatmadaw, seized power in February 2021, arrested Aung San Suu Kyi and turned their guns on protesters, Asean moved with uncharacteristic speed.

Within weeks, it had brokered a “five-point consensus” plan with junta chief Min Aung Hlaing as a framework for ceasefire, dialogue and humanitarian access.

For a bloc defined by non-interference in each other’s affairs, it was an unprecedented step. It was also, in the years since, almost entirely ineffective. Min Aung Hlaing prevaricated. Asean barred him from summits. The killing continued.

Thinzar Shulei Yi was barely 20 years old when she first started organising protests against the military dictatorship that had ruled Myanmar for nearly five decades until 2011. She was motivated to act after teaching orphans whose parents had been killed in the long years of civil conflict.

Now 34, she has watched soldiers fire on her friends at protests and seen others disappear into interrogation rooms. Her parents have been harassed by the Tatmadaw.

“Asean has covered its crimes internationally,” she said of the military, “which will make Asean lose trust and legitimacy, not just among Myanmar people but among Asean citizens.”

Tun Khin carries a different wound. The president of the Burmese Rohingya Organisation UK was stripped of his citizenship under Myanmar’s 1982 Citizenship Law, which classified nationality by ethnicity, effectively rendering the Rohingya stateless.

Since the 1970s, his people have been expelled from Rakhine state, denied education and forbidden to marry freely, with hundreds jailed for doing so without permits. He now lives in Britain.

He described the junta’s use of Asean membership as a “protective shield against international pressure”.

“When Asean allowed Myanmar to join, it argued membership would encourage change and reform,” he said, referring to the country’s accession to the bloc in 1997. “Instead, we have seen another coup, genocide and conflict worse than ever.”

Both activists rejected the framing of Myanmar’s crisis as a domestic matter: the refugee flows, the narco-networks and the spillover criminality are already regional problems.

Thinzar Shulei Yi called on Asean to abandon the five-point consensus, end any platforming of the Tatmadaw and channel humanitarian aid directly through civil society. Tun Khin added that measurable, time-bound targets for human rights and democratic progress were required, backed by the credible threat of Myanmar’s expulsion if the benchmarks were not met.

Albert argued against expulsion but conceded that the current approach was failing.

Her solution is to dust off a provision the bloc has long overlooked: the “High Council” – a formal dispute-settlement mechanism embedded in the Treaty of Amity but never activated, which she envisions as a body of respected Asean elder statesmen and women empowered to act without the paralysing constraint of their political positions.

“If Asean wants to be able to solve its own problems, then create that High Council,” she said. “Everybody tells me ‘you are too ambitious’ and ‘you are too naive’, but I say we can try.”

US-China squeeze

Myanmar may be the bloc’s thorniest issue, but it is far from the only one. The international rules-based order that Asean grew up in and shaped to fit its own needs is coming under serious strain.

US-China rivalry has increasingly pushed member states towards bilateral deals on tariffs, investment and security that risk “eventually hollowing out the enduring regional body”, according to foreign affairs analyst Lucio Blanco Pitlo III of the Manila-based Asia-Pacific Pathways to Progress Foundation think tank.

The bloc’s edge lay in its collective clout, he said. If that was fragmented into 11 separate transactional relationships, the institution would lose any power it once had.

“Asean creates a platform to make its voice heard on the international stage and be an active shaper of norms to govern its neighbourhood,” Pitlo said.

Peace is being overtaken by a tendency towards economic and military coercion

Muhammad Faizal bin Abdul Rahman, security analyst

Meanwhile, the global security outlook is more volatile today than it has been in decades.

Muhammad Faizal bin Abdul Rahman, a security analyst at the S. Rajaratnam School of International Studies in Singapore, noted that the regional norms of cooperation Asean had spent decades building were coming under pressure from a new logic.

“Peace is being overtaken by a tendency towards economic and military coercion and cooperation is being undermined by growing strategic distrust,” he said. “It remains to be seen how Asean can improve its platforms while maintaining its neutrality and cohesion.”

Invisible to millions

Back in Cebu, running parallel to the leaders’ summit, the Foreign Policy Community of Indonesia (FPCI) think tank organised “Asean for the Peoples Week” in an attempt to bridge a gap that even the bloc’s former diplomats acknowledge has never been bridged.

Barry Desker, who previously served as Singapore’s representative to the Asean Intergovernmental Commission on Human Rights, identified it as far back as 2017.

Genuine commitment to Asean’s ideals existed largely among policymakers and professionals, he wrote in a commentary.

“For most of the diverse peoples living in Southeast Asia, the idea of an Asean community with shared values and a common identity looking towards a common destiny is a wish still to be fulfilled,” Desker said.

Asean has an official anthem, though the overwhelming majority of the region’s 700 million citizens are unaware of it.

Few take pride or even show interest in which country currently holds the Asean chair, according to Faizal. The institution that has lurked in the backgrounds of their lives for years remains, to most of them, invisible.

“These are symptomatic of the lack of awareness and appreciation of Asean among ordinary citizens in Southeast Asia,” Faizal said.

That invisibility may be the bloc’s deepest long-term vulnerability. Marston suggests that tangible results could change the dynamic, however.

“While its primary dealings are more esoteric and less germane to the everyday lived experience of the people of Asean, if it can successfully mediate disputes like that between Thailand and Cambodia or mitigate rising fuel prices via a strategic petroleum reserve, it may come to be seen as having a more material impact at the grass-roots level,” he said.

Dino Patti Djalal, a former Indonesian envoy and minister who runs the FPCI, said that civil society needed to be woven into the Asean conversation more deliberately, especially in an increasingly multipolar world.

“The people-centred Asean community,” he told a Cebu town hall during the Peoples Week event, “is driven and shaped not just by policies of government … but by the peoples of the region”.

Hugo is one of those people. He does not know what decisions were taken 150km away from his farm last month. But he does know that his diesel costs have never been higher, and he is waiting – as many Southeast Asians have waited before – to see whether the agreements reached in his name will find their way to his fields.
